When the 8051 accesses external data memory, the 16-bit address is **multiplexed** across the ports. The 8051 hardware automatically does this, but for a KTU exam derivation, we must show the explicit mechanism.

### 3.2.1 Step 1: Address Splitting

The 16-bit external address $\text{Addr}_{16}$ is split into two parts:

$$
\text{Addr}_{16} = \underbrace{\text{A15} \ldots \text{A8}}_{\text{8-bit high}} \quad \underbrace{\text{A7} \ldots \text{A0}}_{\text{8-bit low}}
$$

### 3.2.2 Step 2: Role Assignment

- **P2** drives the **upper address lines A15–A8** (held stable for the entire memory cycle).
- **P0** first drives the **lower address lines A7–A0** (latched by external 74HC573), then becomes the **data bus D7–D0**.

### 3.2.3 Step 3: Mathematical Reconstruction

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Full External Address} &= (\text{Content of P2 SFR}) \times 256 \;\vert\; (\text{Latched value on P0}) \\
&= (\text{P2} \times 2^{8}) + \text{P0}_{\text{latched}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.4 Worked Example

Suppose P2 = 30H and the value latched on P0 = 4FH. Find the external memory address accessed.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{External Address} &= (30\text{H} \times 256) + 4\text{FH} \\
&= (48 \times 256) + 79 \\
&= 12288 + 79 \\
&= 12367
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting 12367 to hex:

$$
\begin{aligned}
12367 \div 16 &= 772 \text{ rem } 15 \rightarrow \text{F} \\
772 \div 16 &= 48 \text{ rem } 4 \rightarrow 4 \\
48 \div 16 &= 3 \text{ rem } 0 \rightarrow 0 \\
3 \div 16 &= 0 \text{ rem } 3 \rightarrow 3
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{External Address} = 304\text{FH}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** This address-latching mechanism is why every 8051-based system with external memory must include an **address latch enable (ALE) signal** to drive an external latch (like the 74LS373 or 74HC573). Without latching, the lower address bits would be lost as P0 transitions to the data bus phase.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: DPTR vs. R0/R1 for External Access

| Feature | DPTR (16-bit) | R0 or R1 (8-bit) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Address width | **16-bit** | **8-bit** |
| Max memory access | **64 KB** (0000H–FFFFH) | **256 bytes** (00H–FFH) |
| SFR address | 82H, 83H | Bank 0, R0/R1 |
| Instruction used | `MOVX A, @DPTR` | `MOVX A, @R0` |
| Page access | Full memory space | Single 256-byte page |
| Best for | Large memory arrays, look-up tables | Small buffer operations |
| KTU recommendation | **Use for all look-up tables** | Use for compact scratch memory |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** Whenever a KTU question says "access 64 KB external memory" or "implement a look-up table", the **only correct register is DPTR**. R0/R1 will only earn partial marks (typically 50%) as they cannot address the full range.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of 8051 Ports and DPTR

```mermaid
graph TD
    CPU["CPU OF 8051"] --> DPTRblock["DPTR 16 BIT REGISTER"]
    CPU --> P0block["PORT 0 SFR 80H"]
    CPU --> P1block["PORT 1 SFR 90H"]
    CPU --> P2block["PORT 2 SFR A0H"]
    CPU --> P3block["PORT 3 SFR B0H"]
    DPTRblock --> DPH["DPH HIGH BYTE 83H"]
    DPTRblock --> DPL["DPL LOW BYTE 82H"]
    P0block --> P0ext["MULTIPLEXED AD0 TO AD7"]
    P1block --> P1ext["GENERAL PURPOSE IO 8 LINES"]
    P2block --> P2ext["UPPER ADDRESS A8 TO A15"]
    P3block --> P3ext["ALT FUNCTIONS RXD TXD INT0 INT1 T0 T1 WR RD"]
    P0ext --> ExtLatch["EXT LATCH 74HC573 DRIVEN BY ALE"]
    P2ext --> ExtMem["EXTERNAL MEMORY 64KB"]
    ExtLatch --> ExtMem
    DPTRblock --> ExtMem
```

## 4.2 Port 3 Alternate Function Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    P3["PORT 3 SFR B0H"] --> B0["P3.0 RXD SERIAL INPUT"]
    P3 --> B1["P3.1 TXD SERIAL OUTPUT"]
    P3 --> B2["P3.2 INTO EXT INTERRUPT 0"]
    P3 --> B3["P3.3 INT1 EXT INTERRUPT 1"]
    P3 --> B4["P3.4 T0 TIMER 0 INPUT"]
    P3 --> B5["P3.5 T1 TIMER 1 INPUT"]
    P3 --> B6["P3.6 WR WRITE STROBE"]
    P3 --> B7["P3.7 RD READ STROBE"]
```

## 4.3 DPTR Internal Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0["DPTR 16 BIT"] --> B0["HIGH BYTE DPH SFR 83H"]
    A0 --> B1["LOW BYTE DPL SFR 82H"]
    B0 --> C0["Holds bits A15 to A8"]
    B1 --> C1["Holds bits A7 to A0"]
    C0 --> D0["Used in MOVX and MOVC instructions"]
    C1 --> D0
    D0 --> E0["Forms the final 16 bit address for external memory access"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the role of the Data Pointer (DPTR) register in the 8051 microcontroller. Mention its SFR address and the operations in which it is used.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Data Pointer (DPTR) is a **16-bit register** in the 8051 used to access **external data memory** and **look-up tables** in code memory. It is the only 16-bit register available in the standard 8051.

- **DPH (Data Pointer High byte)** → SFR address **83H**
- **DPL (Data Pointer Low byte)** → SFR address **82H**

**Operations using DPTR (1 mark each for any two):**
1. `MOVX A, @DPTR` — Read from external data memory at the address held in DPTR.
2. `MOVX @DPTR, A` — Write the accumulator contents to external data memory.
3. `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` — Read code memory (look-up table) using base address in DPTR.
4. `JMP @A+DPTR` — Computed jump for branch tables.
5. `INC DPTR` — Increment the 16-bit pointer for sequential access.

*(Each of the 3 marks is distributed as: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for SFR addresses + 1 mark for operations.)*

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List the four I/O ports of the 8051 with their SFR addresses. Which port requires external pull-up resistors?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Port | SFR Address |
| :---: | :---: |
| P0 | 80H |
| P1 | 90H |
| P2 | A0H |
| P3 | B0H |

**Port 0 (P0) requires external pull-up resistors** (typically 10 kΩ) because its output transistors are of **open-drain type**, unlike P1, P2, and P3 which have internal pull-ups.

*(1 mark for the table, 1 mark for the answer, 1 mark for the reason.)*

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the organization of the four I/O ports of the 8051 with their alternate functions. Also mention the SFR address of each port.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the Data Pointer (DPTR) register in detail. How is it used to access external data memory and code memory? Give suitable examples.

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Port Organization [7 Marks]

**Port 0 (P0) — SFR Address: 80H [2 Marks]**
- It is an 8-bit **bidirectional** port.
- When external memory is interfaced, the lower-order address (A0–A7) and data bus (D0–D7) are **time-multiplexed** over P0.
- It has **no internal pull-up resistors**; external 10 kΩ pull-ups are required.
- During external memory cycles, it is automatically switched to bus mode by hardware; the user does not need to configure it manually.

**Port 1 (P1) — SFR Address: 90H [1 Mark]**
- It is a **general-purpose 8-bit bidirectional I/O port** with no alternate functions.
- It has **internal pull-up resistors** and is commonly used in interfacing LEDs, switches, keypads, and sensors.

**Port 2 (P2) — SFR Address: A0H [1 Mark]**
- It serves as a **general-purpose I/O** when no external memory is used.
- When external memory (code or data) of more than 256 bytes is connected, P2 carries the **higher-order address lines A8–A15**.
- The contents of the P2 SFR are automatically driven to the address bus by hardware during external memory access.

**Port 3 (P3) — SFR Address: B0H [3 Marks]**
P3 is a **bidirectional port** with both general-purpose and alternate functions. The alternate functions are:

| Bit | Pin Name | Alternate Function | Description |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| P3.0 | RxD | Serial Data Input | UART receiver |
| P3.1 | TxD | Serial Data Output | UART transmitter |
| P3.2 | INT0 | External Interrupt 0 | Triggered by edge/level |
| P3.3 | INT1 | External Interrupt 1 | Triggered by edge/level |
| P3.4 | T0 | Timer 0 Input | External clock for T0 |
| P3.5 | T1 | Timer 1 Input | External clock for T1 |
| P3.6 | WR | Write Strobe | Active low for external write |
| P3.7 | RD | Read Strobe | Active low for external read |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A(a):**
> Students frequently write only the **alternate function names** (like RxD, TxD) without mentioning they are **multiplexed on P3** or describing their **role**. Examiners award full marks only when both the **pin name and the function's purpose** are stated. Also, students often forget that P0 is **open-drain** and lose 1 mark for the pull-up explanation.

#### Part (b) — Data Pointer Detail [7 Marks]

**Definition [1 Mark]:** The Data Pointer (DPTR) is a **16-bit register** in the 8051, the only register capable of holding a complete 16-bit address. It is implemented as two 8-bit SFRs:
- **DPH (83H)** — high byte of the 16-bit address
- **DPL (82H)** — low byte of the 16-bit address

**Formation [2 Marks]:**

$$
\text{DPTR}_{16} = (\text{DPH} \times 256) + \text{DPL}
$$

**Accessing External Data Memory [2 Marks]:**

To read a byte from external data memory address 4C2FH:

```assembly
MOV DPTR, #4C2FH   ; Load 16-bit address into DPTR
MOVX A, @DPTR       ; Read the content into the accumulator
```

To write the value of `R0` to external data memory address 2050H:

```assembly
MOV DPTR, #2050H    ; Load 16-bit address
MOV A, R0           ; Move data to A
MOVX @DPTR, A       ; Write A to external memory
```

**Accessing Code Memory (Look-up Tables) [2 Marks]:**

```assembly
MOV DPTR, #4000H    ; Base address of look-up table
MOV A, #05H         ; Index = 5
MOVC A, @A+DPTR     ; Read code memory at 4005H into A
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A(b):**
> - Marks are **deducted** if students write `#4C2FH` as the immediate operand but forget the `MOVX` (for external data) or `MOVC` (for code) prefix.
> - A common mistake is using **`MOV` instead of `MOVX`**. `MOV A, @DPTR` is **invalid** in 8051 assembly — only `MOVX` or `MOVC` instructions are allowed with `@DPTR`.
> - Students should explicitly write the **register name (DPTR)** in the comments; some lose 0.5 marks for not labeling the assembly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain how the 8051 uses its ports to interface with external memory. What is the role of the ALE signal?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the use of DPTR with R0/R1 for accessing external data memory. The 8051 needs to read a 500-byte array stored at external memory starting at address 1000H. Write the assembly routine and justify the choice of register.

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — External Memory Interfacing [7 Marks]

**Block Diagram Explanation [4 Marks]:**

When the 8051 needs to access external memory, the ports serve specialized roles:

1. **P0 (AD0–AD7):** Acts as a **multiplexed address/data bus**. First, it carries the **lower address A0–A7**; then it switches to carry the **data D0–D7** within the same memory cycle.
2. **P2 (A8–A15):** Holds the **upper 8 bits of the address** stable throughout the memory cycle.
3. **P3.6 (WR) and P3.7 (RD):** Provide the **active-low write and read strobes** to the external memory chip.
4. **PSEN:** Used for reading external **code memory** (ROM).
5. **EA:** Tie to **GND** when only external code memory is used.

**Role of ALE [3 Marks]:**
- **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** is a high-pulse signal that tells the external latch (74HC573) to capture the lower address from P0.
- When ALE = 1, P0 outputs the address A0–A7. The external latch (e.g., 74LS373) **latches this value** on the falling edge of ALE.
- When ALE = 0, P0 is freed to act as the data bus.
- **Without ALE + external latch, the address would be corrupted** when P0 transitions to data mode.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B(a):**
> - Students forget to mention that P0 is **multiplexed** and lose 1 mark.
> - Many answers skip the **timing** of ALE (HIGH to latch, LOW to release P0). Examiners specifically test this timing in 7-mark questions.

#### Part (b) — DPTR vs R0/R1 [7 Marks]

**Comparison [2 Marks]:**

| Feature | DPTR | R0 / R1 |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Width | 16-bit | 8-bit |
| Addressable memory | 64 KB | 256 bytes |
| Suitable for 500-byte array | **Yes** | **No (insufficient)** |

**Justification [1 Mark]:**
Since the array is **500 bytes** (exceeds 256), it spans multiple pages. **R0/R1 can only point within a single 256-byte page**, so we **must use DPTR**.

**Assembly Routine [4 Marks]:**

```assembly
        MOV DPTR, #1000H    ; DPTR = base address of array
        MOV R2, #00H        ; R2 = low byte of index (0 to 244)
        MOV R3, #02H        ; R3 = high byte of index (500 = 01F4H)
LOOP:   MOVX A, @DPTR       ; Read array element into A
        ; (Process the value of A here)
        INC DPTR            ; Move to next element
        DJNZ R2, LOOP       ; Decrement R2, loop if not zero
        DJNZ R3, LOOP       ; Decrement R3, loop if not zero
        END
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B(b):**
> - Students using `R0` or `R1` as the index register will **lose 2 marks** for incorrect justification (R0/R1 cannot span 500 bytes).
> - Forgetting `INC DPTR` after reading (so the pointer stays at the same location) is a **classic 1-mark deduction**.
> - Using `MOV` instead of `MOVX` for external memory access is a **2-mark deduction**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Frequency KTU Recap — Memorize These:**

- ✅ The 8051 has **4 ports (P0, P1, P2, P3)** = **32 I/O lines**, all **bit-addressable**.
- ✅ SFR addresses: **P0 = 80H**, **P1 = 90H**, **P2 = A0H**, **P3 = B0H**.
- ✅ **P0 has no internal pull-up** → needs external **10 kΩ** pull-ups.
- ✅ P0 is the **multiplexed address/data bus** (AD0–AD7) for external memory.
- ✅ P2 carries the **upper address bus** (A8–A15) during external memory access.
- ✅ P3 is the **alternate function port** (RxD, TxD, INT0/1, T0/1, WR, RD).
- ✅ **DPTR is a 16-bit register** composed of **DPH (83H)** and **DPL (82H)**.
- ✅ Use **`MOVX`** for external data memory and **`MOVC`** for code memory look-up tables.
- ✅ DPTR can address the **entire 64 KB** memory space; R0/R1 can address only **256 bytes**.
- ✅ The **ALE signal** + external latch (74HC573) is **mandatory** to demultiplex P0's address/data.
- ✅ `MOV A, @DPTR` is **invalid**; always use `MOVX` or `MOVC`.
- ✅ The high byte of DPTR (DPH) is multiplied by **256** and added to the low byte (DPL) to form the 16-bit address: $\text{DPTR} = (\text{DPH} \times 256) + \text{DPL}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Program Counter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT404 - MICROCONTROLLER PROGRAMMING & INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Program Counter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Program Counter (PC)** is a dedicated **16-bit special function register** within the Central Processing Unit (CPU) of a microcontroller (e.g., the Intel **MCS-51 / 8051 family**) that permanently holds the **memory address of the *next* instruction to be fetched and executed** from the Program Memory (ROM). It is the CPU's primary "navigation pointer" that governs the sequential flow of execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The Program Counter is the *only* register in the 8051 that is **not** part of the internal RAM / Special Function Register (SFR) memory map. It is a dedicated hardware register hard-wired into the CPU's Control Unit.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bookmark" of the CPU

Imagine you are reading a 1000-page novel in a dark room. You cannot see more than one line at a time. To keep your place, you use a **bookmark**.

- The **bookmark** = the **Program Counter**.
- The **book** = the **Program Memory (ROM)** containing your machine code.
- The **act of moving the bookmark down after reading each line** = the **auto-increment** of the PC after every instruction fetch.
- The **act of jumping to a specific page using the index** = a **branching instruction** (`SJMP`, `LJMP`, `AJMP`) that *overwrites* the PC with a new address.

Without the bookmark (PC), the CPU would have absolutely no idea *where it is* in the program or *what to do next* after the power is switched on.

## 1.3 Physical Metrics & Boundary Conditions

| Parameter | Value | Engineering Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Width** | **16 bits** | Allows $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ unique addresses |
| **Addressable Memory** | **64 KB (65,536 bytes)** | Maximum Program ROM size for standard 8051 |
| **Reset Value** | **`0000H`** | The CPU always begins execution at address `0000H` |
| **Maximum Value** | **`FFFFH`** | The highest address in the 64 KB memory map |
| **Overflow Behavior** | **Wraps from `FFFFH` to `0000H`** | An overflow condition (used very rarely) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 16 bits?** The 8051 was designed by Intel in 1980 with a **16-bit address bus** for program memory (Harvard Architecture — separate from the 8-bit data bus). This 16-bit width is *exactly* sufficient to point to any byte inside a **64 KB** ROM, which was considered massive in 1980.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" and the "How"

The Program Counter operates on a strict **Fetch-Decode-Execute** cycle governed by the Control Unit. Below is the step-by-step logic of its behavior:

1. **Step 1 — Power-On / Reset:**  
   The moment the **RST pin is pulled HIGH** (or power is applied), a hardware reset circuit forces the PC to a known, deterministic value:  
   $$ \text{PC} \leftarrow \text{0000H} $$  
   *Why?* The CPU must always start from a well-defined memory location so that the firmware developer can place the first instruction (the reset vector) at a guaranteed address.

2. **Step 2 — Instruction Fetch:**  
   The CPU places the 16-bit PC value on the **16-bit Program Address Bus**, fetches the opcode byte from ROM, and places it into the Instruction Register (IR).

3. **Step 3 — Auto-Increment (The Heartbeat):**  
   Simultaneously with the fetch, the Control Unit increments the PC by the **length (in bytes) of the instruction being fetched**.  
   *Why?* Because the CPU is a pipelined Harvard machine — while it decodes and executes the *current* instruction, the PC is *already* pointing to the *next* one in line.

4. **Step 4 — Sequential Execution (Default):**  
   For most instructions (`MOV A, #55H`, `ADD A, R0`, etc.), the incremented PC simply continues forward. This creates the natural top-to-bottom flow of an assembly program.

5. **Step 5 — Branching / Control Transfer:**  
   When a **jump, call, or return** instruction is executed, the PC is *overwritten* with a new address supplied by the instruction, breaking the sequential flow.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | KTU Exam Frequency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | $N_{max} = 2^W$ | Max memory = $2^{\text{width}}$ | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | $N_{max} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | For 8051 PC | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | $\text{PC}_{reset} = \text{0000H}$ | Starting address on reset | ★★★★★ |
| 4 | $\text{PC}_{new} = \text{PC}_{old} + L$ | Where $L$ = instruction length in bytes | ★★★★ |
| 5 | $L \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ | 8051 instruction length options | ★★★★ |
| 6 | $\text{Stack} \leftarrow \text{PC}$ | On `LCALL` / `ACALL` | ★★★ |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Program Counter is not just a textbook concept — it is the **central nervous system** of every embedded device on Earth:

- **Automotive ECUs:** The PC in a car's engine control unit cycles through fuel-injection lookup tables at **kilohertz rates**, with the PC effectively acting as a "table walker."
- **Washing Machine Controllers:** Nested subroutine calls push the PC onto the stack, allowing a single PC to manage **multi-level state machines** (wash → rinse → spin).
- **IoT Sensors:** In low-power modes, the PC is **frozen** to preserve the program state until an external interrupt awakens the CPU.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why 16 Bits = 64 KB

**Given:** The 8051 Program Counter is a **16-bit** binary register.

**To Find:** The maximum number of unique memory locations (bytes) it can address.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Width of PC (in bits)} &= 16 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Number of unique states of an } n \text{-bit register} &= 2^n \\
\text{Therefore, unique addresses} &= 2^{16} \\
&= 2^{10} \times 2^{6} \\
&= 1024 \times 64 \\
&= 65{,}536 \text{ locations}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{In Kilobytes} &= \frac{65{,}536}{1024} \\
&= 64 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** A 16-bit PC can uniquely identify any of the **65,536 bytes** in a **64 KB** program memory. The starting address is `0000H` and the ending address is `FFFFH`.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: PC Behavior Across Instruction Types

Below is the exhaustive analytical matrix showing how the PC reacts to every major category of 8051 instruction. Assume the PC currently holds the address `2000H` at the start of each scenario.

| Instruction Category | Example | PC Value Before Fetch | Action on PC | PC Value After | Hex/Decimal Math |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **1-Byte Instruction** | `INC A` | `2000H` | Increment by **1** | `2001H` | $0x2000 + 1 = 0x2001$ |
| **2-Byte Instruction** | `MOV A, #55H` | `2000H` | Increment by **2** | `2002H` | $0x2000 + 2 = 0x2002$ |
| **3-Byte Instruction** | `LJMP 3000H` | `2000H` | Increment by **3** | `2003H` | $0x2000 + 3 = 0x2003$ |
| **Short Jump** | `SJMP 05H` | `2010H` | Replace PC + rel offset | `2017H` | $0x2010 + 2 + 5 = 0x2017$ |
| **Absolute Jump** | `AJMP 0500H` | `2030H` | Replace low 11 bits | `2500H` | New 16-bit PC = `2500H` |
| **Long Jump** | `LJMP 3000H` | `2040H` | Replace all 16 bits | `3000H` | Direct 16-bit overwrite |
| **Absolute Call** | `ACALL 0500H` | `2050H` | Push PC+2 to stack, load new | `0500H` | Stack ← `2052H` |
| **Long Call** | `LCALL 3000H` | `2060H` | Push PC+3 to stack, load new | `3000H` | Stack ← `2063H` |
| **Return from Subroutine** | `RET` | `X` | Pop top of stack into PC | *(depends)* | PC ← Stack[top] |

## 3.3 Worked Example: PC Trace Through a Program

Trace the Program Counter through the following 8051 assembly code stored starting at address `0000H`:

```assembly
0000H:  MOV A, #0AH    ; 2-byte instruction
0002H:  MOV R0, #05H   ; 2-byte instruction
0004H:  ADD A, R0      ; 1-byte instruction
0005H:  SJMP 0000H     ; 2-byte instruction
```

**Step-by-step PC trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At Reset: } \text{PC} &= \text{0000H} \\
\text{After fetching MOV A, \#0AH: } \text{PC} &= \text{0000H} + 2 = \text{0002H} \\
\text{After fetching MOV R0, \#05H: } \text{PC} &= \text{0002H} + 2 = \text{0004H} \\
\text{After fetching ADD A, R0: } \text{PC} &= \text{0004H} + 1 = \text{0005H} \\
\text{After fetching SJMP: } \text{PC} &= \text{0005H} + 2 = \text{0007H} \\
\text{After executing SJMP (offset = -7): } \text{PC} &= \text{0000H} \quad \text{(infinite loop)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The PC is incremented *during* the fetch phase, so by the time the SJMP is **executed**, the PC is already at `0007H`. The relative offset of `-7` (encoded as `F9H` in 2's complement) brings it back to `0000H`.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — PC Inside the 8051 CPU Core

The diagram below shows the **Program Counter** as a central register connected to the **Program Address Bus** and interacting with the **Instruction Register** during a fetch.

```mermaid
graph TD
    PC[Program Counter PC, 16 bit] -- "16 bit Program Address Bus" --> PABus{PABus 16 lines}
    PABus --> ROM[Program Memory ROM, 64 KB]
    ROM -- "8 bit Opcode Data" --> IR[Instruction Register, 8 bit]
    IR --> CU[Control Unit CU]
    CU -- "Increment PC by L" --> PC
    CU -- "Branch Overwrite" --> PC
    CU -- "Push PC to Stack" --> SP[Stack Pointer SP, 8 bit]
    SP --> SRAM[Internal RAM 128 bytes]
    PC --> ALU[ALU executes decoded instruction]
    style PC fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
    style ROM fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style CU fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Fetch-Execute Cycle With PC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([System Reset]) --> B[PC is loaded with 0000H]
    B --> C{Is current PC equal to FFFFH?}
    C -- No --> D[Place PC on Program Address Bus]
    D --> E[Fetch opcode byte from ROM into IR]
    E --> F[Increment PC by L bytes]
    F --> G[Decode instruction in IR via Control Unit]
    G --> H{Is this a branch, call, or return?}
    H -- No, sequential --> I[Execute ALU or data transfer operation]
    H -- Yes, branch jmp --> J[Overwrite PC with new target address]
    H -- Yes, call --> K[Push current PC+2 to Stack, then overwrite PC]
    H -- Yes, return --> L[Pop top of Stack into PC]
    I --> C
    J --> C
    K --> C
    L --> C
    C -- Yes --> M([PC overflows wraps to 0000H])
    M --> C
    style A fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style PC fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style B fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000
    style J fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000
    style K fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — PC During a Subroutine Call

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PC as Program Counter
    participant ROM as Program ROM
    participant SP as Stack Pointer
    participant RAM as Internal RAM
    Note over PC: PC holds 2050H before LCALL
    PC->>ROM: Fetch LCALL 3000H opcode at 2050H
    PC->>PC: Auto increment to 2053H
    PC->>SP: SP points to 60H
    SP->>RAM: Push PCL 53H into address 61H
    SP->>RAM: Push PCH 20H into address 62H
    SP->>SP: SP becomes 62H
    PC->>PC: Overwrite PC with 3000H
    Note over PC: Execution continues at 3000H
    PC->>ROM: Fetch next opcode at 3000H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation from the Diagrams:** The PC is the *only* register that can **both auto-increment** (during sequential fetch) **and** be **overwritten** (during control transfer). This dual behavior is what makes stored-program execution possible.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]`

**Explain the role of the Program Counter in the 8051 microcontroller. Why is it 16 bits wide?**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

The **Program Counter (PC)** in the 8051 is a **16-bit register** that holds the **address of the next instruction to be fetched** from program memory. After every instruction fetch, the PC is **automatically incremented** by the length of that instruction (1, 2, or 3 bytes), ensuring sequential execution.

It is **16 bits wide** because the 8051 has a **16-bit program address bus**, which allows the PC to address a maximum of $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes = **64 KB** of program ROM. The PC is the only register that can be both auto-incremented and overwritten by control instructions like `LJMP`, `LCALL`, and `RET`.

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark — definition; 1 mark — sequential/branching role; 1 mark — justification of 16-bit width.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q]`

**What is the value of the Program Counter immediately after a system reset in the 8051? What is the significance of this value?**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

Immediately after a system reset (or power-on), the **Program Counter is initialized to `0000H`**. This is a **hardware-defined reset vector** that forces the CPU to always begin execution at the very first memory location of the program ROM.

**Significance:** This deterministic behavior allows the firmware developer to **reliably place the first executable instruction (the reset service routine)** at address `0000H`, knowing with absolute certainty that the CPU will execute it first upon any reset event. Without this fixed value, the CPU would have undefined startup behavior, making embedded systems impossible to design.

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark — value `0000H`; 1 mark — reset vector concept; 1 mark — deterministic startup explanation.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Two questions from Module 1, answer **any ONE**. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Choose A or B*

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern, Adapted]`

#### **(a)** Explain the function of the Program Counter in the 8051 with a neat block diagram. Discuss what happens to the PC during (i) sequential execution, (ii) a `LJMP` instruction, and (iii) an `LCALL` instruction. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution:

**(i) Function of the PC:**  
The Program Counter is a 16-bit register inside the 8051 CPU that points to the **address of the next instruction** to be fetched from program memory. It is the **instruction pointer** of the microcontroller.

**(ii) During Sequential Execution:**  
Assume PC = `2000H` and the instruction at `2000H` is `MOV A, R0` (1-byte instruction).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PC} &= \text{2000H} \\
\text{After fetch, PC auto-increments: } \text{PC} &= \text{2000H} + 1 = \text{2001H} \\
\text{Next instruction is fetched from } \text{2001H} &
\end{aligned}
$$

For a 2-byte instruction, the PC would increment by 2. For a 3-byte instruction, by 3.

**(iii) During `LJMP target`:**  
The `LJMP` (Long Jump) is a **3-byte instruction** that contains a full 16-bit target address in its second and third bytes. When executed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{After fetch: } \text{PC} &= \text{old PC} + 3 \\
\text{Execution overwrites: } \text{PC} &\leftarrow \text{target address} \\
\text{Example: LJMP 3000H at PC = 2000H} \Rightarrow \text{PC becomes 3000H} &
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) During `LCALL target`:**  
The `LCALL` is also a 3-byte instruction. When executed, the CPU performs a **two-step atomic operation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Push PC+3 onto Stack (SP incremented twice)} \\
\text{Step 2: PC} \leftarrow \text{target address} \\
\text{This allows a return via RET to resume at the instruction after LCALL.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 above.)

> **Marking Split:** 2 marks — PC function + diagram; 2 marks — sequential; 2 marks — `LJMP`; 1 mark — `LCALL`.

---

#### **(b)** A 2-byte instruction `MOV A, #55H` is stored at program memory address `4000H`. Trace the value of the Program Counter from fetch to after-execution. If this is followed by a `SJMP -10` instruction, what will be the new value of the PC after the jump is executed? **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution:

**Part 1 — Tracing PC for `MOV A, #55H` at `4000H`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Before fetch: } \text{PC} &= \text{4000H} \\
\text{Fetch opcode byte from 4000H} & \\
\text{Auto-increment: } \text{PC} &= \text{4000H} + 2 = \text{4002H} \\
\text{(The second byte 55H is fetched during execution phase, not affecting PC)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part 2 — `SJMP -10` is a 2-byte instruction at `4002H`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Before fetch: } \text{PC} &= \text{4002H} \\
\text{After fetch (auto-increment): } \text{PC} &= \text{4002H} + 2 = \text{4004H} \\
\text{Relative offset} &= -10 = \text{F6H (2's complement)} \\
\text{Final PC after jump:} \quad \text{PC}_{new} &= \text{4004H} + (-10) = \text{3FFAH}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Marking Split:** 3 marks — first trace; 1 mark — SJMP structure; 3 marks — final PC calculation.

---

### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern, Adapted]`

#### **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Fetch-Decode-Execute** cycle of the 8051 and clearly mark where the Program Counter is involved in each stage. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution:

The 8051 executes instructions in a continuous **Fetch → Decode → Execute** cycle:

**Stage 1 — Fetch (PC involved):**  
The 16-bit value of the PC is placed on the **Program Address Bus**. The ROM responds by placing the opcode byte on the data bus, which is loaded into the **Instruction Register (IR)**.

**Stage 2 — Decode (PC not involved):**  
The Control Unit decodes the opcode. The PC is now free to begin incrementing.

**Stage 3 — Execute (PC may be involved):**  
The decoded operation is performed. For branch instructions, the PC is overwritten with a new address.

**PC Auto-Increment (happens DURING decode, in parallel with execute):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If instruction length} &= 1 \text{ byte: } \text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 1 \\
\text{If instruction length} &= 2 \text{ bytes: } \text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 2 \\
\text{If instruction length} &= 3 \text{ bytes: } \text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.2 above.)

> **Marking Split:** 2 marks — diagram; 2 marks — fetch; 1 mark — decode; 2 marks — execute + auto-increment.

---

#### **(b)** Differentiate between the Program Counter (PC) and the Data Pointer (DPTR) of the 8051. Why are both registers 16 bits wide? **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution:

| Feature | **Program Counter (PC)** | **Data Pointer (DPTR)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Primary Purpose** | Addresses **next instruction** in program ROM | Addresses **data** in external data memory (XRAM) |
| **Memory Type Pointed To** | Program Memory (ROM) | External Data Memory (XRAM) |
| **Auto-Increment** | **Yes**, by instruction length | Only via `INC DPTR` instruction |
| **Overwrite Sources** | Branch, call, return, reset | `MOV DPTR, #data16` instruction |
| **User Direct Access** | **No** (not directly readable/writable) | **Yes** (can be read/written) |
| **Stack Interaction** | Pushed on `LCALL` / `ACALL` | Never used with stack |
| **Usage with MOVC** | Not used | Used with `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` |
| **Size** | 16 bits | 16 bits (DPL + DPH) |

**Why both are 16 bits:**

The 8051 was designed with a **16-bit address space** for both program and external data memory. A 16-bit register provides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Addressing range} &= 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the PC, this is needed to access the full 64 KB program ROM. For the DPTR, this is needed to access the full 64 KB of external data memory (XDATA) using instructions like `MOVX A, @DPTR`. An 8-bit register could only address 256 bytes, which is insufficient for serious embedded applications.

> **Marking Split:** 4 marks — table/differentiation; 1 mark — PC; 1 mark — DPTR; 1 mark — common 16-bit justification.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing PC with Stack Pointer (SP):** The SP is **8 bits** in the 8051 (points to internal RAM), but the PC is **16 bits** (points to ROM). Students often mix these up.
> 2. **Forgetting to add 2 or 3 for PC during SJMP/LCALL:** When calculating the target of a relative jump, students frequently use `PC + offset` *without* first adding the 2-byte instruction length. **Always remember:** `PC_target = (PC + 2) + offset` for `SJMP`.
> 3. **Saying PC is "in the SFR map":** It is **not**. The PC is a dedicated CPU register and is **not accessible** via SFR addresses `80H–FFH`.
> 4. **Mixing up byte-order on stack during LCALL:** The **low byte (PCL)** is pushed **first**, then the **high byte (PCH)** — this is **little-endian** behavior.

---

## 🚀 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **PC is a 16-bit register** in the 8051, dedicated to pointing at the *next* instruction in ROM.
- [x] **Reset value = `0000H`** — execution always starts at the first ROM address.
- [x] **Maximum memory = $2^{16} = 64 \text{ KB}$** — cannot exceed `FFFFH`.
- [x] **Auto-increment = Instruction Length** (1, 2, or 3 bytes), happening *during* the fetch phase.
- [x] **Sequential flow** is the default; **branches overwrite** the PC.
- [x] **For `SJMP offset`:** Final PC = `(Current PC + 2) + offset`.
- [x] **For `LCALL target`:** Current PC+3 is pushed to stack, then PC is overwritten with target.
- [x] **For `RET`:** PC is restored by popping the top two stack bytes (low byte first).
- [x] **PC is NOT in the SFR memory map** — it is a private CPU register.
- [x] **PC is NOT directly user-accessible** (no `MOV PC, ...` instruction exists in standard 8051).
- [x] **Difference from DPTR:** PC is for code, auto-managed, hidden; DPTR is for XDATA, user-managed, visible.
- [x] **Difference from SP:** PC is 16-bit, ROM-pointing; SP is 8-bit, RAM-pointing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CPU Registers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT404 - MICROCONTROLLER PROGRAMMING & INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** CPU Registers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **Intel 8051 Microcontroller Architecture** (the primary architecture mandated in the KTU 2024 PBEBT404 syllabus), **CPU Registers** are defined as a small set of high-speed, dedicated storage elements physically fabricated *inside* the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**. Unlike general-purpose memory (Internal RAM/External RAM), these registers are addressed directly by the hardware decoder and operate at the processor's core clock speed without requiring address bus cycles for data fetch operations.

The 8051 CPU is an **8-bit accumulator-based CISC architecture**, and it contains several specialized registers that govern instruction execution, arithmetic status, memory addressing, and stack management. According to the 8051 architectural specification, these registers collectively form the **Programming Model** visible to an assembly language programmer.

The principal 8051 CPU Registers are:

1. **Program Counter (PC)** — 16-bit
2. **Accumulator (A / ACC)** — 8-bit
3. **B Register** — 8-bit
4. **Stack Pointer (SP)** — 8-bit
5. **Data Pointer (DPTR)** — 16-bit (composed of DPH + DPL)
6. **Program Status Word (PSW)** — 8-bit (bit-addressable flag register)
7. **General Purpose Registers (R0 – R7)** — arranged in 4 register banks × 8 registers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 mandates an exhaustive study of the *8051 Architecture & Programming Model*. CPU Registers constitute the **single most frequently tested topic** in this module, with at least one 7-mark sub-question appearing in nearly every End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **CPU** as a highly skilled **chef working in a fast-paced kitchen** (the processor). The chef needs:

- A **notebook (PC)** where the current recipe step is written — tells *which instruction* to execute next.
- A **cutting board (Accumulator)** — the main workspace where most chopping (arithmetic/logic) happens. Almost every recipe step involves this board.
- A **secondary bowl (B Register)** — used only for specific recipes like multiplication and division, working *with* the cutting board.
- A **recipe-index card box (DPTR)** — points to a specific page in the recipe book (data memory lookup) — useful for table lookups.
- A **stack of dirty plates (Stack Pointer)** — a "to-do pile" that grows/shrinks as the chef takes on or finishes sub-recipes (function calls / interrupts).
- A **dashboard of indicator lights (PSW)** — small colored bulbs showing the chef's current state: "Did the last step overflow?", "Is the result zero?", "Which row of knives am I using?".
- A **set of 8 small jars per shelf (R0–R7 in 4 banks)** — fast, easily reachable containers holding ingredients for quick access.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fundamental Principle:** Registers are the *fastest* storage in the entire microcontroller system. Accessing them takes **0 clock cycles** (effectively instantaneous), whereas accessing Internal RAM requires **1 machine cycle** and External RAM requires **2 machine cycles**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value (Standard 8051) | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Register data width | **8 bits** (most GPRs/A/B/PSW/SP) | Aligned with 8-bit ALU datapath |
| Program Counter width | **16 bits** | Enables 64 KB code address space |
| DPTR width | **16 bits** | Enables 16-bit external data addressing |
| Number of GPRs | **32 (4 banks × 8)** | Bank-switching via RS0/RS1 |
| Register bank size | **8 bytes (R0–R7)** | Located in lower 32 bytes of IRAM |
| Default register bank | **Bank 0** (R0–R7 at 00H–07H) | Selected after RESET |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Register-by-Register Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Program Counter (PC) — 16-bit

- **Function:** Holds the address of the **next instruction** to be fetched from program memory (ROM).
- **Width:** 16 bits → can address **2¹⁶ = 65,536** locations (64 KB).
- **Operation:** After every instruction fetch, the PC is **automatically incremented** by the number of bytes in the current instruction (1, 2, or 3 bytes).
- **Writable:** Yes — via `LJMP`, `AJMP`, `SJMP`, `LCALL`, `ACALL`, `JMP @A+DPTR`, conditional jumps, and `RET`/`RETI`.
- **NOT bit-addressable**, **NOT directly readable** (only via @PC relative addressing or stack snapshot).

> [!NOTE]
> **Reset Vector:** Upon RESET, the PC is loaded with the value **0x0000** (i.e., the first instruction fetched is from address `0000H` in code memory).

### 2.1.2 Accumulator (A / ACC) — 8-bit, Bit-Addressable

- The **most-used register** in 8051 programming. The mnemonic is `A`; the SFR address is `0xE0`.
- Serves as one of the **operands for nearly all ALU operations** (ADD, SUBB, ANL, ORL, XRL, MUL, DIV, etc.).
- It is **bit-addressable**, meaning individual bits (`E0H` to `E7H`) can be set/cleared/tested individually.
- Acts as the **default source/destination** for many instructions (`MOV A, Rn`, `MOV A, direct`, `MOV A, @Ri`).

### 2.1.3 B Register — 8-bit

- Used **only** in two specific instructions:
  - `MUL AB` → multiplies A × B, leaves 16-bit result in `[B:A]` (B = high byte, A = low byte).
  - `DIV AB` → divides A by B; quotient in A, remainder in B.
- Otherwise usable as a **scratch-pad general purpose register** (address `0xF0`).
- Bit-addressable (`F0H–F7H`).

### 2.1.4 Stack Pointer (SP) — 8-bit

- Points to the **top of the stack** in **Internal RAM** (not in SFR area).
- After RESET, SP = **0x07** (i.e., the first PUSH writes to address 0x08).
- PUSH → SP is **incremented first**, then data is written to `@SP`.
- POP → data is read from `@SP`, then SP is **decremented**.
- Stack grows **upward** in memory (toward higher addresses).

$$\text{Address}_{\text{PUSH}} = \text{SP}_{\text{current}} + 1$$

$$\text{Address}_{\text{POP}} = \text{SP}_{\text{current}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly state that the stack grows *downward*. In 8051, the stack grows **upward** — i.e., the SP increments on PUSH and decrements on POP.

### 2.1.5 Data Pointer (DPTR) — 16-bit

- A 16-bit register split into two 8-bit SFRs:
  - **DPH** (high byte) at SFR address `0x83`
  - **DPL** (low byte) at SFR address `0x82`
- Used to access **external data memory** (XDATA) using `MOVX A, @DPTR` and `MOVX @DPTR, A`.
- Also used in **code memory lookups** via `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`.
- Critical for **table-read operations**.

### 2.1.6 Program Status Word (PSW) — 8-bit, Bit-Addressable

- Address: `0xD0`. Contains the **CPU status flags** and register bank select bits.

| Bit | Symbol | Address | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | `CY` | `D7H` | Carry Flag — set on unsigned overflow/borrow |
| 6 | `AC` | `D6H` | Auxiliary Carry — carry from bit 3 to bit 4 (BCD) |
| 5 | `F0` | `D5H` | User-definable flag 0 |
| 4 | `RS1` | `D4H` | Register Bank Select bit 1 |
| 3 | `RS0` | `D3H` | Register Bank Select bit 0 |
| 2 | `OV` | `D2H` | Overflow Flag — signed arithmetic overflow |
| 1 | — | `D1H` | Reserved (undefined) |
| 0 | `P` | `D0H` | Parity Flag — set if A has odd number of 1s |

**Register Bank Selection:**

| RS1 | RS0 | Bank Selected | Address Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | Bank 0 | `00H – 07H` |
| 0 | 1 | Bank 1 | `08H – 0FH` |
| 1 | 0 | Bank 2 | `10H – 17H` |
| 1 | 1 | Bank 3 | `18H – 1FH` |

### 2.1.7 General Purpose Registers (R0 – R7) — 8-bit, in 4 Banks

- 32 bytes of the lower Internal RAM (addresses `00H–1FH`) are organized into **4 banks of 8 registers each**.
- Only **one bank is "active" at a time**, determined by RS0/RS1 in PSW.
- Bank switching enables **fast context switching** with no memory PUSH/POP.
- `R0` and `R1` of the active bank also serve as **indirect addressing pointers** (`@R0`, `@R1`).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "What is the address of R3 if RS1=1, RS0=0?" — compute: Bank 2 starts at `10H`; R3 is the 4th register (R0, R1, R2, **R3**), so address = `10H + 3 = 13H`.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Constants Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| PC addressing range | $2^{16}$ | **64 KB** of code memory |
| DPTR addressing range | $2^{16}$ | **64 KB** of external data memory |
| GPR address for `Rk` in bank `b` | $8b + k$ | $b \in \{0,1,2,3\},\ k \in \{0..7\}$ |
| Initial SP after RESET | `0x07` | First PUSH writes to `0x08` |
| MUL AB result | $\text{A} \cdot \text{B}$ stored in `B:A` | 16-bit result |
| DIV AB result | $\text{A} \div \text{B}$ → A = quotient, B = remainder | 8-bit dividend & divisor |
| Parity flag `P` | $P = (\text{number of 1s in A}) \bmod 2$ | 1 = odd, 0 = even |
| Carry flag after ADD | $\text{CY} = \lfloor (A_{\text{op1}} + A_{\text{op2}})/256 \rfloor$ | Unsigned overflow |
| Auxiliary Carry | $\text{AC} = \text{carry from bit 3 to bit 4}$ | Used for BCD correction |
| Stack PUSH | $\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} + 1$ then write | Upward growing |
| Stack POP | read then $\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} - 1$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The 8051 register model is the foundation for embedded system firmware in **automotive ECUs, washing machine controllers, industrial PLCs, and IoT edge devices**. Mastering register-level programming enables writing highly optimized time-critical code where every machine cycle matters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Address Calculation for General Purpose Registers

**Problem:** Given a register bank `b` (where $b = 2 \cdot \text{RS1} + \text{RS0}$) and register index `k` (0–7), derive the absolute internal RAM byte address.

**Step 1 — Identify the starting address of bank `b` in IRAM.**
The lower 32 bytes of Internal RAM are reserved for the four register banks, each occupying **8 bytes**. Therefore, the starting address of bank `b` is:

$$
\text{Base}(b) = b \times 8
$$

**Step 2 — Add the register offset within the bank.**
Within a bank, registers R0–R7 occupy sequential byte positions, so register `Rk` is offset by `k` bytes from the base:

$$
\text{Offset}(k) = k
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the two components.**

$$
\text{Address}(b, k) = \text{Base}(b) + \text{Offset}(k) = 8b + k
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the encoding of `b` from PSW bits RS1 and RS0.**

$$
b = 2 \cdot \text{RS1} + \text{RS0}
$$

**Step 5 — Final closed-form expression:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Address}(b, k) = 8(2 \cdot \text{RS1} + \text{RS0}) + k}
$$

**Step 6 — Example verification.**
Suppose RS1 = 1, RS0 = 0, k = 5 (i.e., R5 in Bank 2).

$$
b = 2(1) + 0 = 2
$$

$$
\text{Address} = 8(2) + 5 = 16 + 5 = 21 = 0\text{x}15
$$

Indeed, Bank 2 spans `0x10–0x17`, and R5 (the 6th register) is at `0x15`. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Carry and Auxiliary Carry for ADD Instruction

**Problem:** For an unsigned 8-bit ADD `ADD A, R0` where A = `0xA3` and R0 = `0x7F`, derive CY and AC.

**Step 1 — Perform the binary addition.**

$$
A = 0\text{xA3} = 1010\,0011_2
$$

$$
R_0 = 0\text{x7F} = 0111\,1111_2
$$

$$
\text{Sum} = 1010\,0011_2 + 0111\,1111_2
$$

**Step 2 — Bitwise addition from LSB to MSB.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bit 0:} & \quad 1 + 1 = 10_2 \rightarrow \text{sum}=0,\ \text{carry}=1 \\
\text{Bit 1:} & \quad 1 + 1 + 1 = 11_2 \rightarrow \text{sum}=1,\ \text{carry}=1 \\
\text{Bit 2:} & \quad 1 + 1 + 1 = 11_2 \rightarrow \text{sum}=1,\ \text{carry}=1 \\
\text{Bit 3:} & \quad 0 + 1 + 1 = 10_2 \rightarrow \text{sum}=0,\ \text{carry}=1 \\
\text{Bit 4:} & \quad 0 + 1 + 1 = 10_2 \rightarrow \text{sum}=0,\ \text{carry}=1 \\
\text{Bit 5:} & \quad 1 + 1 + 1 = 11_2 \rightarrow \text{sum}=1,\ \text{carry}=1 \\
\text{Bit 6:} & \quad 0 + 1 + 1 = 10_2 \rightarrow \text{sum}=0,\ \text{carry}=1 \\
\text{Bit 7:} & \quad 1 + 0 + 1 = 10_2 \rightarrow \text{sum}=0,\ \text{carry}=1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Final sum in binary and hex.**

$$
\text{Sum} = 0010\,0010_2 = 0\text{x}22
$$

**Step 4 — Determine flags.**

- The MSB carry out (from bit 7) is **1** ⇒ $\text{CY} = 1$
- A carry propagated from bit 3 to bit 4 occurred ⇒ $\text{AC} = 1$
- The result (`0x22`) is non-zero ⇒ normally a hypothetical `Z` flag would be 0 (8051 has no Z flag, but parity still computed: `0x22` = `0010 0010` has two 1s, so `P = 0`).

$$
\boxed{\text{CY} = 1, \quad \text{AC} = 1, \quad \text{A} = 0\text{x}22}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These flags are exploited in **multi-precision arithmetic** (adding 16-bit, 24-bit, 32-bit numbers) and in **BCD arithmetic** via the `DA A` (Decimal Adjust) instruction. They are also used in **serial communication** to detect parity errors and overflow in accumulator-centric protocols.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Register Visibility Across Addressing Modes

| Register | Register Addressing | Direct Addressing | Register-Indirect | Immediate Operand |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A (ACC) | ✗ | ✓ (`0xE0`) | ✗ | ✗ |
| B | ✗ | ✓ (`0xF0`) | ✗ | ✗ |
| R0–R7 (active bank) | ✓ (`Rn`) | ✗ | ✓ (`@Ri`, i=0,1) | ✗ |
| DPTR (DPL, DPH) | ✗ | ✓ (`0x82`, `0x83`) | ✗ | ✗ |
| SP | ✗ | ✓ (`0x81`) | ✗ | ✗ |
| PC | ✗ (implicit) | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| PSW | ✗ | ✓ (`0xD0`) | ✗ | ✗ |

**Note:** A is also accessible via direct addressing as `ACC` (SFR `0xE0`). However, instructions like `ADD A, #data` use **implicit accumulator addressing**, which is functionally distinct from direct addressing.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any six CPU registers of the 8051 microcontroller and state one function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Program Counter (PC)** – 16-bit; holds the address of the next instruction to be fetched.
2. **Accumulator (A)** – 8-bit; primary operand for all ALU arithmetic and logic operations.
3. **B Register** – 8-bit; holds the second operand for `MUL AB` and `DIV AB` instructions.
4. **Stack Pointer (SP)** – 8-bit; points to the top of the stack in Internal RAM; initialized to `0x07` on reset.
5. **Data Pointer (DPTR)** – 16-bit (DPH+DPL); used to access external data memory and for code lookups via `MOVC`.
6. **Program Status Word (PSW)** – 8-bit; contains carry, auxiliary carry, overflow, parity flags and register bank select bits RS0/RS1.

*(Any 6 valid registers with one-line function ⇒ full 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the value of the Stack Pointer after a system reset, and what is the address where the first PUSH instruction stores data?**

**Model Answer:**
After a system reset, the Stack Pointer (SP) is initialized to **0x07**. Since PUSH first increments SP and then writes, the first PUSH instruction stores data at address **0x08** in Internal RAM.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

#### (a) **[7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Explain the structure of the Program Status Word (PSW) of the 8051 microcontroller with the help of a neat diagram. Discuss the function of each flag bit.**

**Model Solution:**

The Program Status Word is an **8-bit bit-addressable Special Function Register** located at SFR address **`0xD0`**. It is a crucial CPU register that reflects the current status of the ALU and controls the active register bank.

**Bit-wise Structure:**

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Symbol | **CY** | **AC** | **F0** | **RS1** | **RS0** | **OV** | — | **P** |

**Function of each flag:**

1. **CY (Carry Flag, bit 7):**
   Set to 1 if there is a carry out from the MSB (bit 7) during an ADD operation, or a borrow during a SUBB. Used for **multi-byte arithmetic** and is also the **9th bit** in serial port mode 2/3.

2. **AC (Auxiliary Carry Flag, bit 6):**
   Set if there is a carry from bit 3 to bit 4 (i.e., between the lower and upper nibbles) during ADD. Used by the **`DA A`** instruction to perform **BCD correction**.

3. **F0 (User Flag 0, bit 5):**
   A general-purpose flag available to the programmer for custom status indication (e.g., marking the end of a software loop).

4. **RS1 and RS0 (Register Bank Select, bits 4 and 3):**
   These two bits select **one of four register banks** in Internal RAM (Banks 0–3) to be active as R0–R7.

5. **OV (Overflow Flag, bit 2):**
   Set when a **signed arithmetic operation** produces a result that cannot fit in 8 bits (i.e., overflows the signed range `−128` to `+127`).

6. **Reserved bit (bit 1):** Not implemented; reading returns an undefined value.

7. **P (Parity Flag, bit 0):**
   Automatically set to 1 if the Accumulator contains an **odd number of 1s** (i.e., odd parity). It is reset to 0 if A has an even number of 1s.

**Neat Diagram:**

```
PSW Register (SFR 0xD0)
  +---+---+---+---+---+---+---+---+
  |CY |AC |F0 |RS1|RS0|OV | - | P |
  +---+---+---+---+---+---+---+---+
   D7  D6  D5  D4  D3  D2  D1  D0
```

---

#### (b) **[7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**With suitable examples, explain the four register banks of the 8051 microcontroller. How is the active bank selected? What is the default bank after reset?**

**Model Solution:**

The lower **32 bytes** of Internal RAM (`0x00` to `0x1F`) are organized as **four register banks**, each containing **eight 8-bit general-purpose registers R0 through R7**. Each register bank thus occupies **8 bytes** of memory.

**Layout of the four banks:**

| Bank | Address Range | R0 | R1 | R2 | R3 | R4 | R5 | R6 | R7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bank 0 | `00H–07H` | 00H | 01H | 02H | 03H | 04H | 05H | 06H | 07H |
| Bank 1 | `08H–0FH` | 08H | 09H | 0AH | 0BH | 0CH | 0DH | 0EH | 0FH |
| Bank 2 | `10H–17H` | 10H | 11H | 12H | 13H | 14H | 15H | 16H | 17H |
| Bank 3 | `18H–1FH` | 18H | 19H | 1AH | 1BH | 1CH | 1DH | 1EH | 1FH |

**Active bank selection:**
The two bits **RS1 (PSW.4)** and **RS0 (PSW.3)** of the Program Status Word determine which bank is currently active. The four possible combinations are:

| RS1 | RS0 | Active Bank |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | Bank 0 |
| 0 | 1 | Bank 1 |
| 1 | 0 | Bank 2 |
| 1 | 1 | Bank 3 |

**Example of bank switching via assembly code:**

```assembly
        ; Select Bank 2
        SETB    PSW.4        ; RS1 = 1
        CLR     PSW.3        ; RS0 = 0
        MOV     R0, #55H     ; Now R0 refers to address 10H
```

**Default bank after RESET:** After a system reset, **RS1 = 0 and RS0 = 0**, so **Bank 0 is the default active bank**. The stack pointer is also initialized to `0x07`, meaning if a PUSH occurs before the SP is explicitly relocated, the first PUSH will overwrite R7 of Bank 1 (at `0x08`) — a common bug source.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently confuse **bit numbering of PSW** (some write CY as bit 0, which is wrong — CY is **bit 7**).
> - Confusing **"register bank addresses"** with **"SFR addresses"** — GPRs live in IRAM, not in the SFR region (`80H–FFH`).
> - Writing **R0–R7 addresses incorrectly** when the active bank is not Bank 0 (e.g., assuming R0 is always at `00H`).
> - **Loss of marks: ~1–2 per error.** Always explicitly state the bank and the offset.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

#### (a) **[7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2021]**
**Explain the operation of the Stack Pointer (SP) in 8051. Illustrate with a diagram the contents of the stack region of internal RAM when the following sequence executes, starting with SP = 07H.**

```assembly
        PUSH    30H
        PUSH    40H
        POP     50H
        PUSH    60H
```

Assume the following initial values:
- Internal RAM `30H = 0xAA`
- Internal RAM `40H = 0xBB`
- Internal RAM `60H = 0xCC`
- Initial SP = `0x07`

**Model Solution:**

**Operational rules of the 8051 stack:**
1. The stack resides in **Internal RAM**.
2. On `PUSH`, the SP is **first incremented by 1**, then data is written to the address now contained in SP.
3. On `POP`, data is read from the address pointed to by SP, then SP is **decremented by 1**.
4. The stack therefore **grows upward** (toward higher addresses).

**Step-by-step execution:**

| Step | Instruction | Operation | SP After | Memory Modified |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initial | — | `0x07` | — |
| 2 | `PUSH 30H` | SP ← 0x08; RAM[08H] ← 0xAA | `0x08` | `08H = 0xAA` |
| 3 | `PUSH 40H` | SP ← 0x09; RAM[09H] ← 0xBB | `0x09` | `09H = 0xBB` |
| 4 | `POP 50H` | RAM[50H] ← RAM[09H] = 0xBB; SP ← 0x08 | `0x08` | `50H = 0xBB` |
| 5 | `PUSH 60H` | SP ← 0x09; RAM[09H] ← 0xCC | `0x09` | `09H = 0xCC` |

**Final state:**
- SP = `0x09`
- Internal RAM `50H = 0xBB`
- Internal RAM `09H = 0xCC`
- Internal RAM `08H = 0xAA` (still retained)

**Stack Memory Diagram:**

```
Address    Value    Notes
---------------------------------
07H        --       Initial SP location
08H        0xAA     PUSHed from 30H
09H        0xCC     Most recent PUSH (was 0xBB, then 0xCC)
          ^-- SP points here
```

**Why the stack is important:** The stack is used implicitly by `LCALL`, `ACALL`, and interrupts to save the return address (2 bytes each) and by the programmer to save registers using `PUSH`/`POP`. The default SP = 0x07 is unsafe — most programs explicitly initialize SP to a higher value (e.g., `MOV SP, #0x60`) to avoid colliding with register banks.

---

#### (b) **[7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2022]**
**Explain the function of the Data Pointer (DPTR) register. How is it used to access external data memory and program memory? Illustrate with assembly code examples.**

**Model Solution:**

The **Data Pointer (DPTR)** is a **16-bit register** in the 8051, physically split into two 8-bit SFRs:

- **DPL (Data Pointer Low)** at SFR address `0x82`
- **DPH (Data Pointer High)** at SFR address `0x83`

It is the **only 16-bit register** (other than PC) in the 8051, and is used for two main purposes:

**(1) Accessing External Data Memory (XDATA):**
The instruction `MOVX` (Move eXternal) is used to read/write to the 64 KB external data memory space. DPTR holds the effective 16-bit address.

**Example — Writing to XDATA:**

```assembly
        MOV     DPTR, #0x2000   ; DPTR = 2000H (XDATA address)
        MOV     A, #0x55        ; A = 55H
        MOVX    @DPTR, A        ; External RAM[2000H] <- 55H
```

**Example — Reading from XDATA:**

```assembly
        MOV     DPTR, #0x3000   ; DPTR = 3000H
        MOVX    A, @DPTR        ; A <- External RAM[3000H]
```

**(2) Reading Constants from Program Memory (Code Lookups):**
The instruction `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` is used to fetch a byte from code memory at address `(A + DPTR)`. This is **extremely useful for table lookups** (e.g., 7-segment display codes, sine-wave tables, character fonts).

**Example — Reading a Code Table:**

```assembly
        ; Table of square values starting at 0x0400 in code memory
        MOV     DPTR, #0400H    ; DPTR points to start of table
        MOV     A, #05          ; A = index = 5
        MOVC    A, @A+DPTR      ; A <- code memory[0400H + 5] = 19H (25 in decimal)
```

**Key Distinction:**
- `MOVX` accesses **External Data Memory** (XDATA — read/write, volatile).
- `MOVC` accesses **Code Memory** (ROM — read-only, non-volatile).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Confusing MOVX vs MOVC** — students often write `MOVX A, @A+DPTR` (which is invalid) instead of `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`.
> 2. **Forgetting to load DPTR first** — DPTR must be initialized with the base address before indirect access; otherwise the read returns data from an arbitrary address.
> 3. **Mixing up `MOVX` and `MOV`** — `MOV` accesses Internal RAM, not XDATA.
> 4. **Treating DPTR as 8-bit** — it is **16 bits**, addressing 64 KB.
> 5. Each of the above is typically penalized **0.5 to 1 mark** in KTU valuation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — CPU Registers (8051)**

- ✅ **PC (16-bit)** → next instruction address; auto-incremented; reset to `0x0000`.
- ✅ **A (8-bit, SFR 0xE0)** → main ALU operand; bit-addressable; most instructions involve A.
- ✅ **B (8-bit, SFR 0xF0)** → second operand for `MUL AB` and `DIV AB`; else scratch pad.
- ✅ **SP (8-bit, SFR 0x81)** → reset to `0x07`; stack grows **upward** in IRAM.
- ✅ **DPTR (16-bit, DPH 0x83 + DPL 0x82)** → external data & code lookup; used by `MOVX` and `MOVC`.
- ✅ **PSW (8-bit, SFR 0xD0)** → flags: `CY`(7), `AC`(6), `F0`(5), `RS1`(4), `RS0`(3), `OV`(2), reserved(1), `P`(0).
- ✅ **Register Banks (0–3)** → occupy IRAM `00H–1FH`; **8 bytes each**; selected by RS0/RS1.
- ✅ **Default bank = Bank 0**; always initialize SP to a safe value (e.g., `0x60` or higher) at the start of any program.
- ✅ **R0, R1** of active bank can be used for **register-indirect addressing** (`@R0`, `@R1`).
- ✅ **Parity flag (P)** is automatically maintained — odd number of 1s in A ⇒ P = 1.
- ✅ **MOVX ↔ XDATA**, **MOVC ↔ Code memory**, **MOV ↔ Internal RAM** — never confuse these.
- ✅ **Bank-switching** is a lightweight context-switching mechanism — no PUSH/POP required.
- ✅ Total CPU register file visible to programmer = 8 special + 32 GPR = **40 bytes** of register space (excluding PC).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Register Banks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT404 - MICROCONTROLLER PROGRAMMING & INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Register Banks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **8051 microcontroller architecture**, a **Register Bank** is defined as a contiguous block of **8 general-purpose working registers** (named **R0, R1, R2, R3, R4, R5, R6, and R7**) physically mapped into the lower segment of the **internal Random Access Memory (RAM)**. The standard 8051 provides **four such register banks**, labelled **Bank 0, Bank 1, Bank 2, and Bank 3**, occupying the memory addresses **00H–07H, 08H–0FH, 10H–17H, and 18H–1FH** respectively, thereby consuming exactly **32 bytes** of the on-chip data memory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> At any given instant, only **one register bank is "active"** in the CPU. The active bank is the one whose R0–R7 registers are directly addressable by the programmer in the current context. Active bank selection is governed by the **RS1 and RS0 bits** (bits 4 and 3) of the **Program Status Word (PSW)** Special Function Register.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a busy **hospital emergency ward** with **four doctors on duty (Bank 0, 1, 2, 3)**. Every doctor has **identical toolkits** of eight instruments (R0–R7): a stethoscope, a thermometer, a syringe, etc. When a patient walks in, the **Head Nurse selects one doctor** to handle the case — this is like the CPU activating a register bank. The other doctors (other banks) are still physically present and their instruments are untouched, ready to be swapped in instantly when the Head Nurse (PSW register) hands over responsibility.

This design is incredibly powerful because it allows the 8051 to perform **fast context switching**. When an interrupt occurs or a subroutine is launched, the CPU can simply point to a different register bank — instantly giving it a fresh, untouched set of R0–R7 registers without needing to push anything onto the stack. This makes register banks the **single most efficient context-saving mechanism** in the 8051.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric to Memorize:**
> **4 Banks × 8 Registers/Bank = 32 Registers = 32 Bytes of Lower Internal RAM (addresses 00H–1FH)**. This is a **favourite 2-mark KTU short-answer fact**.

## 1.3 The Default State After Power-On Reset

Upon applying power to the 8051, the hardware resets the PSW register to **00H**. This means:
- **RS1 = 0**
- **RS0 = 0**
- **Active Bank = Bank 0** (addresses 00H–07H)

Therefore, after any reset, the programmer can immediately use R0–R7 as if they were located at internal RAM addresses 00H through 07H. This is the convention used by virtually all 8051 assembler tutorials and C51 programs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the 8051 Lower Internal RAM

The lower 128 bytes of internal RAM (addresses **00H to 7FH**) are partitioned into **three distinct functional regions**:

| Region | Address Range | Size (Bytes) | Function |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Register Banks (0–3)** | 00H – 1FH | 32 | Active general-purpose R0–R7 |
| **Bit-Addressable Area** | 20H – 2FH | 16 | 128 individually addressable bits |
| **General-Purpose RAM** | 30H – 7FH | 80 | Scratch-pad user RAM |
| **Reserved / Not Used Here** | (SFRs live at 80H+) | — | Special Function Registers |

The first **32 bytes** of this region are exclusively reserved for the **four register banks**. Even if the user only ever uses Bank 0, the other three banks still physically exist and can be activated at any time.

## 2.2 The PSW (Program Status Word) Register — Address D0H

The PSW is the master control register for the CPU's arithmetic state and **for register bank selection**. It is located at SFR address **D0H** and is **bit-addressable**.

| Bit | Symbol | Name | Function |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **PSW.7** | **CY** | Carry Flag | Set when an arithmetic carry/borrow occurs. |
| **PSW.6** | **AC** | Auxiliary Carry | Set on carry from bit 3 to bit 4 (BCD arithmetic). |
| **PSW.5** | **F0** | User Flag 0 | General-purpose flag for the programmer. |
| **PSW.4** | **RS1** | Register Bank Select 1 | High-order bank-select bit. |
| **PSW.3** | **RS0** | Register Bank Select 0 | Low-order bank-select bit. |
| **PSW.2** | **OV** | Overflow Flag | Set on signed arithmetic overflow. |
| **PSW.1** | **—** | Reserved | User-defined in some 8052 variants. |
| **PSW.0** | **P** | Parity Flag | Set if the accumulator has an odd number of 1s. |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The bank-selection logic is deterministic and can be summarized in a single mapping:

$$
\text{Active Bank Base Address} = (\text{RS1} \times 2 + \text{RS0}) \times 8
$$

Each bank occupies **8 consecutive bytes**, so multiplying the bank index (0–3) by 8 yields the starting address. The complete truth table (an essential **3-mark KTU short answer**) is:

| RS1 | RS0 | Bank Selected | Address Range | Bank Index $k$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 0 | **Bank 0** | 00H – 07H | 0 |
| 0 | 1 | **Bank 1** | 08H – 0FH | 1 |
| 1 | 0 | **Bank 2** | 10H – 17H | 2 |
| 1 | 1 | **Bank 3** | 18H – 1FH | 3 |

## 2.4 Why Register Banks Matter in Real Engineering

In interrupt-driven embedded systems, register banks serve a **brilliant engineering purpose**: an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) can be assigned its own private bank of R0–R7 by simply setting RS1/RS0 in the ISR prologue. The main program keeps using its own bank, and **no registers need to be saved onto the stack**. This:

1. **Reduces interrupt latency** (no PUSH instructions needed).
2. **Guarantees data integrity** — the ISR cannot accidentally corrupt main's R0–R7.
3. **Enables nested interrupts** — each ISR can use a different bank.
4. **Saves code space** — eliminates bulky register-saving boilerplate.

This is a heavily tested KTU concept, especially in questions involving real-time systems and automotive/industrial control applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Tip:**
> In 8051 C programming, the `using` keyword (e.g., `void ISR() using 1`) instructs the C51 compiler to select a specific register bank for the ISR — a direct mapping to the RS1/RS0 hardware bits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Bank Base Address

We shall rigorously derive the formula
$$
A_{\text{base}} = 8k
$$
where $k$ is the bank index determined by RS1 and RS0.

### Step 1 — Define the variables
Let $k \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ be the bank number. Let $R_{S1}$ and $R_{S0}$ be the two PSW bits, each taking values in $\{0, 1\}$.

### Step 2 — Express the bank index in terms of RS bits
Because RS1 is the high-order bit and RS0 is the low-order bit, the bank index forms a 2-bit binary number:
$$
k = (R_{S1} \times 2^{1}) + (R_{S0} \times 2^{0}) = 2 R_{S1} + R_{S0}
$$

### Step 3 — Each bank occupies 8 bytes
The registers R0–R7 within a single bank are stored contiguously, and each register is 1 byte wide. Therefore, the total width of one bank is:
$$
W = 8 \text{ bytes}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the base address
Bank 0 starts at address 00H. Each subsequent bank begins exactly $W = 8$ bytes later. Thus the base address of bank $k$ is:
$$
A_{\text{base}}(k) = k \times W = 8k = 8(2 R_{S1} + R_{S0})
$$

### Step 5 — Verify against the truth table

For RS1 = 0, RS0 = 0:
$$
A_{\text{base}} = 8(2 \cdot 0 + 0) = 0 \text{ (i.e., 00H)} \quad \checkmark
$$

For RS1 = 0, RS0 = 1:
$$
A_{\text{base}} = 8(2 \cdot 0 + 1) = 8 \text{ (i.e., 08H)} \quad \checkmark
$$

For RS1 = 1, RS0 = 0:
$$
A_{\text{base}} = 8(2 \cdot 1 + 0) = 16 \text{ (i.e., 10H)} \quad \checkmark
$$

For RS1 = 1, RS0 = 1:
$$
A_{\text{base}} = 8(2 \cdot 1 + 1) = 24 \text{ (i.e., 18H)} \quad \checkmark
$$

The derivation is mathematically consistent with the architectural specification.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — Address of Register R5 in Bank 2

**Problem:** Determine the physical internal-RAM address accessed when the CPU executes an instruction that references **R5**, given that RS1 = 1 and RS0 = 0.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify the bank.
With RS1 = 1 and RS0 = 0, the active bank is **Bank 2**.

Step 2 — Identify the base address.
$$
A_{\text{base}}(\text{Bank } 2) = 8 \times 2 = 16 \text{ (decimal)} = \text{10H}
$$

Step 3 — Identify the register's offset within the bank.
R5 is the **6th register** (counting from R0 = 0th to R7 = 7th), so its offset is:
$$
\text{Offset}(R_5) = 5
$$

Step 4 — Compute the final address.
$$
A_{R_5} = A_{\text{base}} + \text{Offset} = 16 + 5 = 21 \text{ (decimal)} = \text{15H}
$$

Therefore, **R5 in Bank 2 is physically located at internal RAM address 15H**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Bank-Indexed Address Table

Below is the **complete mapping** from a logical register name to its physical address, for all four banks. This matrix is essential for KTU 14-mark register-bank questions.

| Register | Bank 0 (00H) | Bank 1 (08H) | Bank 2 (10H) | Bank 3 (18H) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **R0** | 00H | 08H | 10H | 18H |
| **R1** | 01H | 09H | 11H | 19H |
| **R2** | 02H | 0AH | 12H | 1AH |
| **R3** | 03H | 0BH | 13H | 1BH |
| **R4** | 04H | 0CH | 14H | 1CH |
| **R5** | 05H | 0DH | 15H | 1DH |
| **R6** | 06H | 0EH | 16H | 1EH |
| **R7** | 07H | 0FH | 17H | 1FH |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The address of register $R_n$ in bank $k$ is given by the unified formula
> $$A(R_n, k) = 8k + n$$
> This compact expression captures the entire address-decoding logic of the 8051 register bank subsystem.

## 3.4 Stack-Pointer vs Register-Bank Interaction

The 8051's **Stack Pointer (SP, SFR at 81H)** initialises to **07H** upon reset. This places the first pushed byte at internal RAM address **08H** — which is precisely the starting address of **Bank 1**. Therefore, if the user's program is also using Bank 1, the first PUSH will silently overwrite R0 of Bank 1, leading to a **subtle and catastrophic data corruption bug**.

**Best Practice (KTU-Examiner Approved):**
Always initialise the stack pointer explicitly at the beginning of every 8051 assembly program, typically to a value safely above the last used register bank (e.g., `MOV SP, #2FH` or higher) to prevent stack–bank collisions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Lower Internal RAM

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the **32 bytes of lower internal RAM (00H–1FH)** are organised into the **four register banks**, and how the **PSW register** acts as the master selector that activates exactly one bank at a time for the **ALU and instruction decoder** of the CPU.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cpu["8051 CPU ALU and Instruction Decoder"]
    psw["PSW Program Status Word SFR at D0H"]
    rs1["RS1 bit PSW dot 4"]
    rs0["RS0 bit PSW dot 3"]
    mux["2 to 4 Bank Selector Multiplexer"]
    bank0["BANK 0 R0 to R7 at 00H to 07H DEFAULT after reset"]
    bank1["BANK 1 R0 to R7 at 08H to 0FH"]
    bank2["BANK 2 R0 to R7 at 10H to 17H"]
    bank3["BANK 3 R0 to R7 at 18H to 1FH"]
    bitregion["Bit Addressable Region 20H to 2FH 128 bits"]
    scratch["General Purpose Scratchpad 30H to 7FH 80 bytes"]

    cpu --> psw
    psw --> rs1
    psw --> rs0
    rs1 --> mux
    rs0 --> mux
    mux --> bank0
    mux --> bank1
    mux --> bank2
    mux --> bank3
    bank0 --> cpu
    bank1 --> cpu
    bank2 --> cpu
    bank3 --> cpu
    bank0 --> bitregion
    bitregion --> scratch
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The solid arrows from each `BANK` block back to `cpu` are intentionally drawn to emphasise that **only one** of the four banks is electrically active at a time. The thick arrow from `mux` to the active bank represents the decode-enable signal.

## 4.2 PSW Bit-Level Structure Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bit7["BIT 7 CY Carry Flag"]
    bit6["BIT 6 AC Auxiliary Carry"]
    bit5["BIT 5 F0 User Flag 0"]
    bit4["BIT 4 RS1 Bank Select High"]
    bit3["BIT 3 RS0 Bank Select Low"]
    bit2["BIT 2 OV Overflow Flag"]
    bit1["BIT 1 Reserved"]
    bit0["BIT 0 P Parity Flag"]

    bit7 --> bit6 --> bit5 --> bit4 --> bit3 --> bit2 --> bit1 --> bit0
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **PSW** is a single-byte (8-bit) Special Function Register located at SFR address **D0H**. Bits **RS1 and RS0** (bits 4 and 3) together form a 2-bit code that selects one of the four register banks.

## 4.3 Bank-Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["RS1 RS0 value on PSW?"]
    case00["00 selects BANK 0 at 00H to 07H"]
    case01["01 selects BANK 1 at 08H to 0FH"]
    case10["10 selects BANK 2 at 10H to 17H"]
    case11["11 selects BANK 3 at 18H to 1FH"]
    act0["Active registers R0 to R7 of Bank 0"]
    act1["Active registers R0 to R7 of Bank 1"]
    act2["Active registers R0 to R7 of Bank 2"]
    act3["Active registers R0 to R7 of Bank 3"]

    start --> case00 --> act0
    start --> case01 --> act1
    start --> case10 --> act2
    start --> case11 --> act3
```

## 4.4 Context-Switching Flow via Register Banks

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    mainprog["Main Program uses Bank 0 RS1 RS0 equals 0 0"]
    intreq["Interrupt Request Received"]
    isr["Interrupt Service Routine begins"]
    setrs["Set RS1 RS0 equals 0 1 to activate Bank 1"]
    switchdone["Bank 1 R0 to R7 now active for ISR"]
    work["ISR executes using fresh registers"]
    restore["ISR exit and PSW restored by RETI"]
    resume["Main Program resumes on Bank 0 seamlessly"]

    mainprog --> intreq
    intreq --> isr
    isr --> setrs
    setrs --> switchdone
    switchdone --> work
    work --> restore
    restore --> resume
    resume --> mainprog
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The hardware automatically saves the **current PC** onto the stack on interrupt, but **PSW must be saved manually** by the ISR if the bank is to be changed. Upon `RETI`, the stack-popped PC plus the manually-restored PSW ensures seamless return to the main context.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Explain the register banks of the 8051 microcontroller. How is the active register bank selected?**

**Model Answer:**

The 8051 has **four register banks** — Bank 0, Bank 1, Bank 2, and Bank 3 — each consisting of **8 general-purpose registers** (R0–R7). All 32 registers together occupy internal RAM addresses **00H to 1FH**, with each bank spanning 8 bytes (00H–07H, 08H–0FH, 10H–17H, 18H–1FH).

The **active register bank** is selected by the two bits **RS1 and RS0** located in the **Program Status Word (PSW)** SFR at address **D0H**. RS1 is bit 4 and RS0 is bit 3. The combination (RS1, RS0) = (0, 0) selects Bank 0 (default after reset); (0, 1) selects Bank 1; (1, 0) selects Bank 2; and (1, 1) selects Bank 3. Only the registers of the active bank are directly addressable as R0–R7 at any given moment.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of the PSW register in the 8051? Mention the bits used for register bank selection.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Program Status Word (PSW)** is a bit-addressable Special Function Register located at SFR address **D0H**. It contains the status flags resulting from arithmetic and logical operations performed by the ALU. Its bits are:

- **PSW.7 — CY**: Carry Flag
- **PSW.6 — AC**: Auxiliary Carry Flag
- **PSW.5 — F0**: User-defined Flag 0
- **PSW.4 — RS1**: Register Bank Select bit 1
- **PSW.3 — RS0**: Register Bank Select bit 0
- **PSW.2 — OV**: Overflow Flag
- **PSW.1 — Reserved**
- **PSW.0 — P**: Parity Flag

The bits **RS1 and RS0** are jointly used to select one of the four register banks of the 8051. After power-on reset, both are cleared to 0, making **Bank 0** the default active bank.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1 Internal Choice]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the internal RAM structure of the 8051 and explain the four register banks in detail with their address ranges.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the PSW register with a neat bit-format diagram. Explain how the RS1 and RS0 bits are used to select the active register bank with examples.

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

The 8051 has **128 bytes of on-chip internal RAM** (addresses 00H to 7FH), which is divided into three regions:

**Region 1 — Register Banks (addresses 00H to 1FH, 32 bytes):**
This region holds the **four register banks**, each containing 8 general-purpose 8-bit registers named R0 through R7. The bank-wise address allocation is:

- **Bank 0** : 00H to 07H
- **Bank 1** : 08H to 0FH
- **Bank 2** : 10H to 17H
- **Bank 3** : 18H to 1FH
