Only one bank is "active" at a time. After reset, **Bank 0 is active** by default.

**Region 2 — Bit-Addressable Area (addresses 20H to 2FH, 16 bytes):**
This 16-byte region provides **128 individually addressable bits** (bit addresses 00H to 7FH), used for single-bit Boolean operations — a unique feature of the 8051 family.

**Region 3 — General-Purpose Scratchpad (addresses 30H to 7FH, 80 bytes):**
This is freely available for user variables, stack, and intermediate computation results.

**Memory Map Diagram (textual):**

| 00H | Bank 0 (R0–R7) |
| 08H | Bank 1 (R0–R7) |
| 10H | Bank 2 (R0–R7) |
| 18H | Bank 3 (R0–R7) |
| 20H | Bit-Addressable (16 bytes) |
| 30H | General-Purpose RAM (80 bytes) |
| 7FH | (end of on-chip RAM) |

The four register banks are physically real registers in the silicon; switching banks simply changes which 8-byte window the CPU's R0–R7 aliases point to.

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

The **PSW (Program Status Word)** is a bit-addressable SFR at address **D0H**. Its bit format is shown below:

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Symbol | **CY** | **AC** | **F0** | **RS1** | **RS0** | **OV** | **—** | **P** |

**Bank-Selection Examples:**

- **Example 1:** To select **Bank 1**, RS1 = 0 and RS0 = 1. This is achieved in assembly by:
  `SETB RS0`  (or equivalently `ORL PSW, #08H`)

- **Example 2:** To select **Bank 2**, RS1 = 1 and RS0 = 0. This is done by:
  `CLR RS0` followed by `SETB RS1`  (or `ORL PSW, #10H`)

- **Example 3:** To return to **Bank 0** (default), RS1 = 0 and RS0 = 0:
  `CLR RS0` followed by `CLR RS1`  (or `ANL PSW, #0E7H`)

- **Example 4:** To select **Bank 3** for an ISR:
  `SETB RS0` followed by `SETB RS1`  (or `ORL PSW, #18H`)

The two bits RS1 and RS0 form a 2-bit code:
(0,0) → Bank 0, (0,1) → Bank 1, (1,0) → Bank 2, (1,1) → Bank 3.

After power-on reset, PSW = 00H, so both RS1 and RS0 are 0, and **Bank 0** is automatically the active bank.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> A common mistake is to write "RS0 and RS1 are bits 3 and 4 of PSW" in the **wrong order**. The correct order is **RS1 = bit 4, RS0 = bit 3** (RS1 is the higher-order bit). Confusing this order will lead to wrong bank selection in your assembly code and cost you **2 full marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1 Internal Choice — Alternative]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, explain the use of register banks in 8051 interrupt handling. Why is this considered efficient?

**(b) [7 Marks]** An 8051 system uses Bank 2 for the main program and Bank 1 for a Timer-0 interrupt service routine.
- (i) What are the values of RS1 and RS0 during the main program?
- (ii) What are the values of RS1 and RS0 inside the ISR?
- (iii) If a variable is stored in R3 of Bank 1 by the main program, what happens to it when an interrupt occurs? Justify.
- (iv) Suggest a method to preserve main's R3 value if Bank 1 must be used by the ISR.

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

In 8051 interrupt-driven systems, **register banks provide a hardware-level context-switching mechanism**. Each interrupt can be assigned its own private set of R0–R7 by setting RS1/RS0 at the start of the ISR.

**Flow of Interrupt Handling:**

1. **Normal Execution:** The main program runs with PSW's RS1/RS0 pointing to its assigned bank (say, Bank 0).
2. **Interrupt Triggered:** Hardware accepts the request and pushes the current **Program Counter (PC)** onto the stack.
3. **ISR Entry:** The CPU jumps to the ISR vector. The ISR prologue executes:
   - Save PSW (PUSH PSW)
   - Set RS1/RS0 to point to a different bank (e.g., Bank 1) by manipulating PSW bits.
4. **ISR Execution:** The ISR uses R0–R7 of the newly activated bank without affecting the main program's registers.
5. **ISR Exit:** Restore PSW (POP PSW) so RS1/RS0 revert to the main's bank setting. The RETI instruction pops the saved PC and resumes the main program seamlessly.

**Why is it efficient?**

- **Speed:** Pushing/popping 8 registers onto the stack consumes 16 machine cycles (8 PUSH + 8 POP) plus 16 bytes of stack space. A single `SETB RS0` instruction consumes only **1 machine cycle and 1 byte** — a **16× speedup** and zero stack usage.
- **Determinism:** ISR latency becomes predictable because no time is wasted saving registers.
- **Safety:** Main's data in R0–R7 is never overwritten by the ISR.

This is why register banks are central to real-time 8051 designs in automotive ECUs, industrial controllers, and consumer electronics.

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**(i) Main program uses Bank 2:**
$$
\text{Bank 2} \implies (RS1, RS0) = (1, 0)
$$

**(ii) ISR uses Bank 1:**
$$
\text{Bank 1} \implies (RS1, RS0) = (0, 1)
$$

**(iii) Effect of interrupt on main's R3 in Bank 1:**

Main's R3 lives at internal RAM address:
$$
A(R_3, \text{Bank 1}) = 8 \times 1 + 3 = 11 \text{ (decimal)} = \text{0BH}
$$

When the interrupt occurs, the ISR activates Bank 1. The R3 of the ISR is **physically the same memory location 0BH**. Therefore, any data the ISR writes to its R3 will **overwrite** main's R3, causing **data corruption** in the main program's context.

**(iv) Suggested remedy:**

There are three standard approaches, all KTU-exam-acceptable:

1. **Reserve Bank 1 exclusively for the ISR** — do not use Bank 1 in the main program at all. This is the cleanest method.
2. **Use a different bank for main** — choose Bank 0 or Bank 2 (already used here), avoiding Bank 1 entirely for main.
3. **Save R3 manually** — execute `PUSH 0BH` at the start of the ISR and `POP 0BH` at the end, preserving the byte at address 0BH on the stack. This costs 2 machine cycles + stack space but is portable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> A very common student error in part (iii) is to answer *"R3 in Bank 1 is automatically saved by the hardware"*. This is **false** — the 8051 hardware saves only the **Program Counter** on interrupt, not the working registers. The PSW (and hence the active bank setting) is also not automatically saved. If the student writes this wrong assumption, they will lose **up to 3 marks** out of 7. The correct answer must explicitly state that **the main's R3 in Bank 1 will be overwritten by the ISR's R3** because they share the same physical memory address.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] The 8051 has **4 register banks**, each with **8 registers (R0–R7)**, occupying a total of **32 bytes** in lower internal RAM (addresses **00H–1FH**).
- [x] The four banks are located at **00H–07H, 08H–0FH, 10H–17H, 18H–1FH**.
- [x] The **PSW (Program Status Word)** at SFR address **D0H** controls the active bank.
- [x] The bank-select bits are **RS1 (PSW.4)** and **RS0 (PSW.3)**.
- [x] The bank-selection code is: **00 → Bank 0, 01 → Bank 1, 10 → Bank 2, 11 → Bank 3**.
- [x] After **power-on reset**, PSW = 00H, so **Bank 0 is the default active bank**.
- [x] The base address of any register $R_n$ in bank $k$ is given by the formula $A = 8k + n$.
- [x] **Stack pointer (SP) initialises to 07H** on reset, so the first PUSH will land at **08H** (start of Bank 1) — always reinitialise SP to avoid bank–stack collisions.
- [x] Register banks enable **fast interrupt context switching** without saving R0–R7 on the stack.
- [x] In 8051 C, the `using n` keyword tells the C51 compiler to switch banks for an ISR.
- [x] Hardware saves only the **PC** (not PSW or registers) on interrupt — the ISR must save/restore PSW manually if it changes the active bank.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stack and Stack Pointer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT404 - MICROCONTROLLER PROGRAMMING & INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stack and Stack Pointer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STACK (Microcontroller Context):** A **stack** is a dedicated section of the microcontroller's internal Random Access Memory (RAM) that operates on the **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** principle, used by the CPU for temporary storage of data, return addresses during subroutine calls, and interrupt handling. The location of the top of this memory region is tracked by a special-purpose register called the **Stack Pointer (SP)**.

In the **8051 microcontroller family**, the Stack Pointer is an **8-bit register** (SFR address `0x81`) that holds the address of the **topmost element** of the stack. The 8051 stack grows **upward** in memory — meaning that each PUSH operation increments SP, and each POP operation decrements SP.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from 8085:** The 8085 microprocessor stack grows **downward** (PUSH decrements SP), whereas the 8051 microcontroller stack grows **upward** (PUSH increments SP). This is one of the most commonly tested distinctions in KTU university exams.

| Parameter | Specification |
|---|---|
| **SP Register Size** | **8 bits** (0x00–0xFF range) |
| **SP SFR Address** | **0x81** (bit-addressable) |
| **Reset Value of SP** | **0x07** |
| **Stack Growth Direction** | **Upward** (PUSH increments SP) |
| **Maximum Stackable RAM** | Internal RAM: **128 bytes** (00H–7FH) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Stack of Plates" Analogy:** Imagine a spring-loaded stack of plates in a cafeteria dispenser. You can only interact with the **topmost plate** — the one most recently placed. To add a plate, you push it down onto the top (PUSH). To remove a plate, you take it from the top (POP). You can never grab the third plate without first removing the top two. The Stack Pointer is like a little arrow or marker indicating "the top plate is right here."

**Why does a microcontroller need a stack?**

1. **Subroutine Calls (`LCALL`/`ACALL`):** The CPU must remember where to return after the subroutine finishes. The return address is automatically pushed onto the stack.
2. **Interrupt Handling:** When an interrupt fires, the CPU automatically pushes the Program Counter (PC) onto the stack so it can resume the main program after the ISR (Interrupt Service Routine) completes.
3. **Temporary Data Storage:** Programmers can manually PUSH/POP registers when registers are scarce (the 8051 has only four register banks: R0–R3 across 4 banks).
4. **Parameter Passing:** Assembly programs use the stack to pass parameters to subroutines.

> [!WARNING]
> **Stack Misconception Alert:** The 8051 stack is **NOT** a hardware LIFO chip — it is a **software convention** implemented using general-purpose internal RAM and tracked via the SP register. The hardware only supports the PUSH/POP instructions; the memory layout is the programmer's responsibility.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — The "Why" and "How"

### Step-by-Step Logic of PUSH Operation

When the CPU executes `PUSH <direct_address>` (a 1-byte, 2-cycle instruction taking **2 machine cycles**):

1. The CPU reads the contents of the specified direct internal RAM location.
2. The **Stack Pointer is incremented by 1** (SP ← SP + 1).
3. The data byte is **written into internal RAM at the new address** pointed to by SP.
4. The original location of the data remains unchanged.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Order:** In the 8051, the PUSH instruction **increments FIRST, then stores**. This is the opposite of the 8085, where the pointer is decremented first.

### Step-by-Step Logic of POP Operation

When the CPU executes `POP <direct_address>`:

1. The CPU reads the data byte from internal RAM at the address **currently pointed to by SP**.
2. The data is loaded into the specified direct internal RAM location.
3. The **Stack Pointer is decremented by 1** (SP ← SP − 1).
4. The RAM location is NOT cleared — it still holds the old data, but is now "logically" free.

## 2.2 Stack Memory Organization (8051)

The 8051 internal RAM structure is critical for understanding where the stack lives:

| Address Range | Region | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **0x00 – 0x1F** | Register Banks (4 banks × 8 bytes) | R0–R7 of selected bank |
| **0x20 – 0x2F** | Bit-Addressable Region (16 bytes) | Boolean flags, bit variables |
| **0x30 – 0x7F** | General Purpose RAM (80 bytes) | User variables, **safe stack zone** |
| **0x80 – 0xFF** | SFR Region | Special Function Registers (NOT stackable) |

> [!NOTE]
> The SFR region (0x80–0xFF) **cannot** be used as stack memory. Pushing data into this region will **overwrite critical registers** like the Accumulator, B register, or Port latches, causing catastrophic system failure.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Effective Stack Address** | $SA_{top} = (SP)$ | Address of the topmost stacked byte |
| **After PUSH** | $SP_{new} = SP_{old} + 1$ | Pointer rises upward |
| **Memory Write on PUSH** | $[SP_{new}] \leftarrow$ data | Data placed at incremented address |
| **After POP** | $SP_{new} = SP_{old} - 1$ | Pointer drops downward |
| **Memory Read on POP** | data $\leftarrow [SP_{old}]$ | Data extracted before decrement |
| **Reset SP Value** | $SP_{reset} = 0x07$ | Default after power-on |
| **Recommended SP Init** | $SP_{init} \geq 0x30$ (typically **0x60** or **0x70**) | Avoids collision with user data |
| **Maximum SP Value** | $SP_{max} = 0x7F$ (8051) or $0xFF$ (8052) | Hard limit before SFR corruption |
| **Stack Depth Available** | $Depth = 0x7F - SP_{init}$ (in bytes) | Number of safe PUSH operations |
| **PUSH/POP Cycle Count** | **2 machine cycles** = **24 oscillator periods** (at 12 MHz → 2 µs) | Execution timing |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Control Systems:** Real-time automotive ECUs use stacks for nested interrupt priority handling.
- **Function Call Chains:** Every C function call in embedded C (compiled to 8051) uses the stack for return addresses and local variables.
- **RTOS Context Switching:** Real-Time Operating Systems like FreeRTOS (8051 port) use the stack to save task context during preemption.
- **Compiler Frame Management:** Keil C51 uses the stack for passing function parameters and storing local (auto) variables in `data` memory.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Analysis of PUSH/POP Mechanics

### Derivation: State Transition of Stack Pointer

Let the current state of the Stack Pointer be $SP_n$ and the new state after a PUSH operation be $SP_{n+1}$.

For a **PUSH** operation on the 8051, the architecture mandates the following order:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & SP_{n+1} = SP_n + 1 \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{RAM}[SP_{n+1}] \leftarrow \text{Source Data} \\
\text{Result:} \quad & \text{Top of stack now holds the new data, pointer has moved up by 1.}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **POP** operation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Destination} \leftarrow \text{RAM}[SP_n] \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & SP_{n+1} = SP_n - 1 \\
\text{Result:} \quad & \text{Data is read from the current top, then pointer moves down by 1.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Note:** The increment-before-write behavior in PUSH is essential because the reset value SP=0x07 falls within the active register bank region. By incrementing first, the very first PUSH writes to address 0x08, **safely avoiding corruption of R7 of Bank 0** during a reset state.

### Worked Example 1: Three Sequential PUSH Operations

Suppose the 8051 is reset (so $SP = 0x07$) and we execute:

```assembly
MOV SP, #0x60      ; Programmer initializes SP to 0x60
MOV A, #0xAA       ; A = 0xAA
PUSH 0x30          ; PUSH the byte at address 0x30 (assume it contains 0x55)
PUSH ACC           ; PUSH Accumulator (0xAA)
PUSH 0x31          ; PUSH the byte at address 0x31 (assume it contains 0x77)
```

**Trace the stack evolution:**

| Step | Instruction | SP (before) | SP (after) | RAM[0x61] | RAM[0x62] | RAM[0x63] |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `MOV SP, #0x60` | 0x07 | 0x60 | undefined | undefined | undefined |
| 2 | `PUSH 0x30` | 0x60 | **0x61** | **0x55** | undefined | undefined |
| 3 | `PUSH ACC` | 0x61 | **0x62** | 0x55 | **0xAA** | undefined |
| 4 | `PUSH 0x31` | 0x62 | **0x63** | 0x55 | 0xAA | **0x77** |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Final SP} &= 0x63 \\
\text{Stack Depth Used} &= 0x63 - 0x60 = 3 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Memory Equation:} \quad & \text{RAM}[0x63 : 0x61] = \{0x77, 0xAA, 0x55\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example 2: LIFO Order Verification

Continuing from above, now execute:

```assembly
POP 0x32          ; POP into RAM[0x32]
POP 0x33          ; POP into RAM[0x33]
POP 0x34          ; POP into RAM[0x34]
```

| Step | Instruction | SP (before) | SP (after) | RAM[0x32] | RAM[0x33] | RAM[0x34] |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `POP 0x32` | 0x63 | **0x62** | **0x77** | undefined | undefined |
| 2 | `POP 0x33` | 0x62 | **0x61** | 0x77 | **0xAA** | undefined |
| 3 | `POP 0x34` | 0x61 | **0x60** | 0x77 | 0xAA | **0x55** |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Verification of LIFO Order:} \quad & \text{Last Pushed (0x77) was First POPped.} \\
& \text{First Pushed (0x55) was Last POPped.} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Stack Pointer States and Edge Conditions

| Scenario | SP Value | Risk Level | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Just after Power-On Reset** | **0x07** | ⚠️ Caution | Points to register bank 0 R7; first PUSH writes to 0x08 |
| **Programmer reinitializes SP to 0x30** | 0x30 | ✅ Safe | Avoids bit-addressable region |
| **Programmer sets SP to 0x60** | 0x60 | ✅ Very Safe | Maximum safe depth: 31 bytes before 0x7F |
| **Programmer sets SP to 0x7F** | 0x7F | ⚠️ Critical | Only **1 byte** of stack headroom |
| **PUSH when SP = 0x7F** | Becomes 0x80 | ❌ **FATAL** | Writes to P0 SFR (port 0 latch) |
| **POP when SP = 0x00** | Becomes 0xFF | ❌ **FATAL** | Wraps to SFR region |
| **Nesting depth of subroutine calls** | $N_{calls}$ | Risk: $N \times 2$ bytes | Each `LCALL` pushes 2 bytes (PC high, PC low) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nested Call Stack Budget:** Each `LCALL` instruction uses **2 bytes** of stack (16-bit return address, pushed high byte first, then low byte). If your program calls 5 subroutines deep, the stack needs at least **10 bytes** of headroom above the current SP.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — 8051 Stack Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU[CPU Core of 8051]
    SPREG[Stack Pointer Register SP Address 0x81 8-bit]
    INTRAM[Internal RAM 128 Bytes 0x00 to 0x7F]
    SFRREG[SFR Region 0x80 to 0xFF]
    
    CPU -->|PUSH instruction| SPREG
    CPU -->|POP instruction| SPREG
    SPREG -->|Holds top address| INTRAM
    CPU -->|Reads or Writes| INTRAM
    INTRAM -.->|Boundary at 0x7F| SFRREG
    
    subgraph REGBANKS[Register Banks 0x00 to 0x1F]
        RB0[Bank 0 R0 to R7]
        RB1[Bank 1 R0 to R7]
        RB2[Bank 2 R0 to R7]
        RB3[Bank 3 R0 to R7]
    end
    
    subgraph BITREG[Bit Addressable 0x20 to 0x2F]
        BITFLG[Bit Variables and Flags]
    end
    
    subgraph GENRAM[General Purpose 0x30 to 0x7F]
        STACKZONE[STACK ZONE Grows Upward]
    end
    
    INTRAM --> REGBANKS
    INTRAM --> BITREG
    INTRAM --> GENRAM
    
    style SPREG fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px
    style STACKZONE fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px
    style SFRREG fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px
    style REGBANKS fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Sequence Flow — PUSH and POP Operation Cycles

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Program
    participant CPU as 8051 CPU
    participant SP as Stack Pointer SP
    participant RAM as Internal RAM
    
    Note over U,RAM: PUSH Operation Sequence
    U->>CPU: Execute PUSH source
    CPU->>SP: Read current SP value
    SP-->>CPU: Return SP equals 0x60
    CPU->>SP: Increment SP equals 0x61
    CPU->>RAM: Write data to address 0x61
    RAM-->>CPU: Acknowledge write complete
    CPU-->>U: PUSH complete in 2 cycles
    
    Note over U,RAM: POP Operation Sequence
    U->>CPU: Execute POP destination
    CPU->>SP: Read current SP value
    SP-->>CPU: Return SP equals 0x61
    CPU->>RAM: Read data from address 0x61
    RAM-->>CPU: Return byte equals 0xAA
    CPU->>SP: Decrement SP equals 0x60
    CPU->>RAM: Store byte in destination address
    CPU-->>U: POP complete in 2 cycles
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Stack Growth Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Address 0x60] --> B[Address 0x61] --> C[Address 0x62] --> D[Address 0x63] --> E[Address 0x64]
    
    A1[Empty] -.-> A
    B1[First Pushed 0x55] -.-> B
    C1[Second Pushed 0xAA] -.-> C
    D1[Third Pushed 0x77 TOP] -.-> D
    E1[Empty Future Growth] -.-> E
    
    SP1[SP ARROW] ===> D
    
    style A1 fill:#E0E0E0
    style B1 fill:#90EE90
    style C1 fill:#90EE90
    style D1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
    style E1 fill:#E0E0E0
    style SP1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** The red box at address `0x63` represents the current top of the stack. The arrow indicates the value SP holds at this moment. The stack will grow rightward (toward higher addresses) on future PUSH operations.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is a Stack Pointer? Why is it important in 8051 microcontroller?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Stack Pointer (SP)** is an 8-bit special function register in the 8051 (SFR address `0x81`) that holds the address of the topmost element of the stack in internal RAM.

**Importance:**
1. It tracks the current top of the stack, enabling PUSH/POP operations.
2. During subroutine calls (`LCALL`/`ACALL`), the CPU uses SP to store the 16-bit return address.
3. During interrupt servicing, SP saves the Program Counter so that normal execution can resume after the ISR.
4. It enables LIFO-based temporary storage when registers are insufficient.

*(Word count: ~65 words — ideal for a 3-mark KTU short answer)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between 8051 stack and 8085 stack.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | 8051 Stack | 8085 Stack |
|---|---|---|
| **Pointer Size** | 8-bit (SP) | 16-bit (SP) |
| **Growth Direction** | Upward (PUSH increments) | Downward (PUSH decrements) |
| **Reset Value** | 0x07 | Undefined (FFFFH typical) |
| **Memory Type** | Internal RAM only | External RAM (64 KB) |
| **Implementation** | SFR-based | Dedicated 16-bit register |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question Choice A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a) Explain the stack structure of 8051 microcontroller with a neat diagram. How does the PUSH and POP instruction work? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Stack Structure:**
The 8051 has 128 bytes of internal RAM (0x00 to 0x7F). The stack is a region of this RAM that operates in **LIFO** fashion. After reset, SP = 0x07. The first PUSH writes to 0x08, so the stack grows **upward** toward 0x7F.

**PUSH Instruction (`PUSH direct`):**
- Opcode: 1 byte, Operand: 1 byte → Total 2 bytes
- Execution time: 2 machine cycles
- Steps:
  1. SP ← SP + 1 (increment first)
  2. RAM[SP] ← data from `direct` address

**POP Instruction (`POP direct`):**
- Opcode: 1 byte, Operand: 1 byte → Total 2 bytes
- Execution time: 2 machine cycles
- Steps:
  1. `direct` ← RAM[SP] (read first)
  2. SP ← SP − 1 (decrement second)

**Diagram:**

```
High Address (0x7F)  →  [    ]
                            [    ]  ← SP after many pushes
                            [TOP ]
                            [    ]
                            [    ]
Low Address (0x30)  →  [    ]  ← Stack base
                    ↖  Grows UPWARD  ↗
```

---

**b) Write an 8051 ALP to initialize the stack pointer at 0x70 and demonstrate three PUSH and three POP operations. Show the stack memory contents after each instruction. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV SP, #70H         ; Initialize SP to 0x70
        MOV A, #11H
        MOV R0, #22H
        MOV R1, #33H
        
        PUSH ACC             ; Push 0x11 onto stack
        PUSH 00H             ; Push R0 = 0x22 onto stack
        PUSH 01H             ; Push R1 = 0x33 onto stack
        
        POP 02H              ; Pop into R2
        POP 03H              ; Pop into R3
        POP 04H              ; Pop into R4
        
        END
```

**Stack Memory Trace:**

| Instruction | SP Before | SP After | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| `MOV SP, #70H` | 0x07 | **0x70** | Initialize |
| `PUSH ACC` | 0x70 | **0x71** | RAM[0x71] = 0x11 |
| `PUSH 00H` | 0x71 | **0x72** | RAM[0x72] = 0x22 |
| `PUSH 01H` | 0x72 | **0x73** | RAM[0x73] = 0x33 |
| `POP 02H` | 0x73 | **0x72** | R2 = 0x33 (Last In, First Out) |
| `POP 03H` | 0x72 | **0x71** | R3 = 0x22 |
| `POP 04H` | 0x71 | **0x70** | R4 = 0x11 (First In, Last Out) |

**Result Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R2 &= 0x33 \quad \text{(was pushed last, popped first — LIFO)} \\
R3 &= 0x22 \quad \text{(middle value)} \\
R4 &= 0x11 \quad \text{(was pushed first, popped last)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Order Error in PUSH:** Students often write "store data, then increment SP" — **WRONG**. The 8051 hardware does **increment FIRST, then write**.
> 2. **Forgetting to initialize SP:** Default 0x07 puts stack in the register bank region. Always set SP explicitly at the start of your program.
> 3. **Pushing into SFR region:** Never let SP exceed 0x7F, or you will overwrite P0, P2, SCON, etc., causing system lockup.
> 4. **Confusing LCALL stack usage:** `LCALL` automatically pushes 2 bytes (PC high, then PC low) — students forget this depth requirement.

---

### **Question Choice B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**a) Explain stack overflow and stack underflow conditions in 8051. What happens when SP exceeds 0x7F? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Stack Overflow:**
A stack overflow occurs when the SP increments past the upper limit of usable stack memory (0x7F for 8051) during repeated PUSH operations without corresponding POPs.

**Consequence when SP = 0x80:**
- The PUSH instruction writes data to internal RAM address 0x80.
- Address 0x80 corresponds to the **P0 SFR (Port 0 Latch)**.
- This **silently corrupts** the Port 0 output state — connected hardware may behave erratically.
- There is **no hardware flag** or interrupt to signal overflow in the original 8051.

**Stack Underflow:**
A stack underflow occurs when POP is executed when SP is already at its lowest meaningful value (e.g., 0x00 or the initial base value).

**Consequence when SP wraps from 0x00 to 0xFF:**
- POP reads from SFR region (0xFF) and stores it in the destination.
- SP becomes 0xFF, and the next PUSH will write to address 0x00 (R0 of Bank 0).
- The program may continue running with corrupted data.

**Prevention Strategies:**
1. Initialize SP to a high address like 0x70 to give maximum depth.
2. Carefully balance PUSH/POP pairs.
3. Track nesting depth in software for critical subroutines.
4. Reserve a "stack canary" byte and check it periodically.

---

**b) An 8051 system is reset and the programmer does NOT reinitialize SP. If a subroutine is called and the ISR also fires, calculate the total stack memory consumed and list the addresses used. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** SP after reset = 0x07, no reinitialization.

**Step 1: `LCALL subroutine`**
- The 8051 automatically pushes the 16-bit return address in two bytes:
  - First: SP ← SP + 1 = 0x08, RAM[0x08] ← PCH (high byte of PC)
  - Then: SP ← SP + 1 = 0x09, RAM[0x09] ← PCL (low byte of PC)
- **Stack used: 2 bytes (0x08, 0x09)**
- **Final SP = 0x09**

**Step 2: Hardware Interrupt fires**
- CPU completes current instruction, then automatically pushes the 16-bit PC:
  - First: SP ← SP + 1 = 0x0A, RAM[0x0A] ← PCH
  - Then: SP ← SP + 1 = 0x0B, RAM[0x0B] ← PCL
- **Stack used: 2 more bytes (0x0A, 0x0B)**
- **Final SP = 0x0B**

**Total Stack Consumption:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Bytes Used} &= 2_{LCALL} + 2_{ISR} = 4 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Addresses Occupied} &= \{0x08, 0x09, 0x0A, 0x0B\} \\
\text{Final SP Value} &= 0x0B = 11_{decimal}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Critical Observation:**
This stack usage intrudes into **Register Bank 1** (addresses 0x08–0x0F correspond to R0–R7 of Bank 1). If the ISR or subroutine uses Bank 1 registers, the return addresses will be **overwritten** when the bank is switched, causing a system crash upon return.

> [!WARNING]
> **This is why reinitializing SP is non-negotiable in professional 8051 code.** The KTU examiner will heavily penalize students who assume the default SP=0x07 is safe.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Stack and Stack Pointer (8051)**

- ✅ SP is an **8-bit SFR at address 0x81** — bit-addressable.
- ✅ Default value after reset is **0x07** — points into Register Bank 0.
- ✅ 8051 stack grows **UPWARD** (PUSH increments, POP decrements) — opposite of 8085.
- ✅ **PUSH order:** Increment SP FIRST, then write data to RAM[SP].
- ✅ **POP order:** Read RAM[SP] FIRST into destination, then decrement SP.
- ✅ PUSH/POP instructions take **2 machine cycles** each (2 µs at 12 MHz).
- ✅ Only **internal RAM (0x00–0x7F)** can be used for stack — never SFR region.
- ✅ Always **reinitialize SP** at the start of your program (recommended: **0x60 or 0x70**).
- ✅ `LCALL`/`ACALL` and interrupts use **2 bytes** of stack each (16-bit PC).
- ✅ Stack overflow at **0x7F → 0x80** silently corrupts **Port 0 (P0) latch**.
- ✅ Stack underflow (SP wraps to 0xFF) reads garbage from **SFR region**.
- ✅ Maximum safe stack depth: **127 bytes** (from 0x01 to 0x7F) in original 8051.
- ✅ The 8052 variant extends internal RAM to 256 bytes, allowing SP up to 0xFF.
- ✅ Stack is purely a **software convention** in 8051 — no dedicated stack hardware exists.
- ✅ **No hardware stack overflow detection** — programmer must ensure safety manually.

---

**End of Module 1 — Stack and Stack Pointer Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Special Function Registers, Addressing Modes of 8051, Instruction Set of 8051 - Assembler Directives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT404 - MICROCONTROLLER PROGRAMMING & INTERFING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Special Function Registers, Addressing Modes of 8051, Instruction Set of 8051 - Assembler Directives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Special Function Registers (SFRs) — Formal Definition

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PBEBT404**, the 8051 microcontroller contains **128 bytes of internal RAM** (addresses 0x00 to 0x7F) and a **Special Function Register (SFR) area** occupying addresses **0x80 to 0xFF**. SFRs are **byte-addressable, dedicated, on-chip registers** that control the internal peripherals, I/O ports, timer/counters, serial port, interrupts, and the arithmetic logic of the CPU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** The standard 8051 has **21 SFRs**, each occupying one byte. SFRs from 0x80 to 0xFF that are not defined are **not implemented** and are treated as **reserved (inaccessible) memory** by the hardware.

#### Conceptual Analogy — The "Dashboard of a Car" 🏎️

Imagine the 8051 chip is a **car dashboard**. The 21 SFRs are the **21 specific gauges and switches** on that dashboard:
- The **Speedometer (TCON)** tells the timers how to behave
- The **Gear Stick (PSW)** selects which "working memory bank" is active
- The **Steering Wheel (P0–P3)** controls the four I/O ports
- The **Ignition Key (PCON)** manages power modes

Just as you cannot read a "speedometer value" from a "headlight switch" position, each SFR has a **fixed address and a fixed purpose**. Writing to a particular SFR controls that specific hardware subsystem.

### 1.2 Addressing Modes of 8051 — Formal Definition

An **addressing mode** specifies *how* (and *where*) the operand(s) of an assembly instruction are located. The 8051 supports **five addressing modes**:

1. **Immediate Addressing** — operand is a constant embedded in the instruction
2. **Register Addressing** — operand is one of the eight internal CPU registers (R0–R7)
3. **Direct Addressing** — operand is the address of an SFR or internal RAM location
4. **Register Indirect Addressing** — address of operand is held in a register (R0, R1, DPTR, or SP)
5. **Indexed Addressing** — effective address = base address + offset (A + PC or A + DPTR)

#### Conceptual Analogy — "Five Ways to Ask for Money" 💰

If a student needs ₹500, he could say:
- **"Here is the exact 500"** → Immediate (the value is right in the instruction)
- **"From my wallet pocket"** → Register (R0–R7 are your "pockets")
- **"From locker #3"** → Direct (you specify the locker number, i.e., address)
- **"From wherever the map says"** → Register Indirect (a register holds the address)
- **"From a list starting at page 10, item 3"** → Indexed (base + offset)

### 1.3 Instruction Set of 8051 — Formal Definition

The **8051 instruction set** is the complete vocabulary of **111 distinct opcodes** (255 opcodes including variants) that the CPU decodes and executes. These are classified into **five functional groups**:

1. **Data Transfer Instructions**
2. **Arithmetic Instructions**
3. **Logical Instructions**
4. **Boolean (Bit) Manipulation Instructions**
5. **Program Branching Instructions**

> [!NOTE]
> The 8051 uses **Harvard architecture** — separate Program (ROM) and Data (RAM) address spaces, allowing simultaneous access via the 16-bit PC bus and 8-bit data bus.

#### Conceptual Analogy — "Verbs of a Language" 🗣️

If the 8051 is a person, instructions are the **verbs** they can perform:
- **MOV** (carry) = Data Transfer
- **ADD/SUBB** (add/subtract) = Arithmetic
- **ANL/ORL/XRL** (compare/filter) = Logical
- **SETB/CLR** (flip switches) = Boolean
- **SJMP/LJMP/JZ** (decide where to go) = Branching

### 1.4 Assembler Directives — Formal Definition

**Assembler Directives** (also called **pseudo-instructions** or **pseudo-ops**) are instructions written in an assembly source file that **direct the assembler** during translation — they are **not** translated into machine code and consume **no memory** in the target.

#### Conceptual Analogy — "Stage Directions in a Play" 🎭

Imagine an actor in a play. The actor performs actions (instructions). But the **stage manager** also issues instructions: "Lights on here," "Scene change at this point," "This actor exits now." These are not the play's actions but guide the production. Assembler directives are the **stage directions** for the assembler (translator).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Assembler directives are **mandatory to know** — they appear frequently in 2-mark and 14-mark questions. Common directives include **ORG, END, DB, DW, EQU, DATA, IDATA, BIT, CODE, USING, CSEG, DSEG, BSEG, ISEG**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Complete SFR Map of the 8051

The SFR area spans internal RAM addresses **0x80–0xFF** (only addressable using **direct addressing**). The detailed map is presented in the high-yield table below.

| SFR | Address (Hex) | Bit-Addressable? | Function / Engineering Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P0** | 0x80 | Yes | Port 0 — 8-bit bidirectional I/O (also serves as multiplexed AD0–AD7) |
| **SP** | 0x81 | No | **Stack Pointer** — points to top of stack (initialized to 0x07) |
| **DPL** | 0x82 | No | Data Pointer **Low** byte |
| **DPH** | 0x83 | No | Data Pointer **High** byte |
| **PCON** | 0x87 | No | Power Control (power-down & idle modes) |
| **TCON** | 0x88 | Yes | Timer/Counter **Control** register |
| **TMOD** | 0x89 | No | Timer/Counter **Mode** select |
| **TL0** | 0x8A | No | Timer 0 **Low** byte |
| **TL1** | 0x8B | No | Timer 1 **Low** byte |
| **TH0** | 0x8C | No | Timer 0 **High** byte |
| **TH1** | 0x8D | No | Timer 1 **High** byte |
| **P1** | 0x90 | Yes | Port 1 — 8-bit bidirectional I/O |
| **SCON** | 0x98 | Yes | Serial Port **Control** register |
| **SBUF** | 0x99 | No | Serial **Data Buffer** (Tx & Rx share one register) |
| **P2** | 0xA0 | Yes | Port 2 — 8-bit bidirectional I/O (also A8–A15) |
| **IE** | 0xA8 | Yes | **Interrupt Enable** register |
| **P3** | 0xB0 | Yes | Port 3 — 8-bit bidirectional I/O (with alt. functions) |
| **IP** | 0xB8 | Yes | **Interrupt Priority** register |
| **PSW** | 0xD0 | Yes | **Program Status Word** (flags + bank select) |
| **ACC** | 0xE0 | Yes | **Accumulator** (A-register, used in most ALU ops) |
| **B** | 0xF0 | Yes | **B-register** (used in MUL/DIV) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The PSW register's **RS1–RS0** bits select one of **four register banks** (Bank 0–3), giving the programmer 32 fast-access registers for rapid context switching in real-time embedded systems.

### 2.2 The 8051 Program Status Word (PSW) — Bit-Level Breakdown

The PSW is one of the most frequently tested SFRs. Its bits govern the ALU flags and the active register bank.

| Bit | Symbol | Address (Bit) | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | **CY** | 0xD7 | Carry Flag — set on unsigned overflow |
| 6 | **AC** | 0xD6 | Auxiliary Carry — used in BCD arithmetic |
| 5 | **F0** | 0xD5 | User-Defined Flag 0 (general purpose) |
| 4 | **RS1** | 0xD4 | Register Bank Select Bit 1 |
| 3 | **RS0** | 0xD3 | Register Bank Select Bit 0 |
| 2 | **OV** | 0xD2 | Overflow Flag — signed arithmetic overflow |
| 1 | **—** | 0xD1 | Reserved (undefined) |
| 0 | **P** | 0xD0 | Parity Flag — set if ACC has odd number of 1's |

#### Register Bank Selection Formula

| RS1 | RS0 | Selected Bank | Address Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | **Bank 0** | 0x00 – 0x07 |
| 0 | 1 | **Bank 1** | 0x08 – 0x0F |
| 1 | 0 | **Bank 2** | 0x10 – 0x17 |
| 1 | 1 | **Bank 3** | 0x18 – 0x1F |

### 2.3 Deep Analysis of the 5 Addressing Modes

#### Mode 1 — Immediate Addressing
- The **data is part of the instruction** itself.
- Used with the **# symbol**.
- Maximum immediate value: **8 bits** (with some 16-bit exceptions like MOV DPTR, #data16).

#### Mode 2 — Register Addressing
- Operand is one of the **8 working registers (R0–R7)** of the currently selected bank.
- The **register bank** is implicitly specified by PSW[3:4].
- **Most efficient** mode (no extra bytes needed).

#### Mode 3 — Direct Addressing
- The operand is the **8-bit address** of a memory location (0x00–0x7F for RAM, 0x80–0xFF for SFRs).
- Requires **2 bytes** of instruction memory.

#### Mode 4 — Register Indirect Addressing
- The **address of the operand** is held in a register.
- Valid pointer registers: **R0, R1, DPTR, SP**.
- Indicated by the **@ symbol**.

#### Mode 5 — Indexed Addressing
- Used exclusively for **MOVC A, @A+DPTR** and **MOVC A, @A+PC**.
- Effective Address = **(A) + (DPTR)** or **(A) + (PC)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Use Case:** Indexed addressing with **MOVC A, @A+DPTR** is the canonical way to implement **lookup tables (LUTs)** in 8051 — widely used in sinusoidal PWM generation, character ROM access, and digital filter coefficient storage.

### 2.4 Instruction Set Classification (KTU Formula Sheet)

| Category | Typical Mnemonics | Bytes | Cycles |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Transfer** | MOV, MOVX, MOVC, PUSH, POP, XCH, XCHD | 1–3 | 1–2 |
| **Arithmetic** | ADD, ADDC, SUBB, INC, DEC, MUL, DIV, DA A | 1–2 | 1–4 |
| **Logical** | ANL, ORL, XRL, CLR, CPL, RL, RLC, RR, RRC, SWAP | 1–2 | 1–2 |
| **Boolean** | SETB, CLR, ANL C, ORL C, MOV C, JC, JNC, JB, JNB, JBC | 2 | 1–2 |
| **Branch** | AJMP, LJMP, SJMP, JMP @A+DPTR, ACALL, LCALL, RET, RETI, JZ, JNZ, CJNE, DJNZ | 2–3 | 1–4 |

### 2.5 Complete Assembler Directives Reference Table

| Directive | Purpose | Example | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ORG** | Set program origin (starting address) | `ORG 0000H` | Places reset vector at 0x0000 |
| **END** | Marks end of source file | `END` | Last line of every assembly file |
| **EQU** | Define a constant symbol | `PORT_A EQU 90H` | Symbolic port naming |
| **DB** | Define Byte(s) | `TABLE: DB 01H, 02H, 03H` | Lookup tables (LUT) |
| **DW** | Define Word(s) — 16-bit | `VECT: DW 1234H, 5678H` | Interrupt vector table |
| **DATA** | Locate in **internal** RAM (0x00–0x7F) | `FLAG BIT 20H` | Fast variable access |
| **IDATA** | Locate in internal RAM (full 256 bytes) | `TEMP IDATA 30H` | Larger scratch variables |
| **CODE** | Locate in **program** ROM | `MSG: DB 'HELLO'` | String constants |
| **BIT** | Define a bit-addressable symbol | `LED BIT P1.0` | Symbolic bit access |
| **USING** | Inform assembler of register bank | `USING 0` | Bank selection in code |
| **CSEG / DSEG / BSEG / ISEG / XSEG** | Segment selection (Keil-style) | `CSEG AT 0` | Memory segment control |
| **DS** | Define Storage (reserve bytes) | `BUFFER: DS 10H` | Buffer reservation in RAM |
| **INCL** | Include another source file | `INCL "REGS.INC"` | Modular code |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Distinction:** `DB` stores **8-bit** values; `DW` stores **16-bit** values (low byte first, then high byte — little-endian format). The `USING` directive only **informs the assembler** — it does NOT alter PSW. You must explicitly set RS0/RS1 to switch banks at runtime.

### 2.6 Engineering Real-World Applications

| Concept | Real Engineering Use |
|---|---|
| **SFRs** | Industrial PLC controllers, washing machines, automotive ECUs all rely on SFR-driven peripheral control |
| **Addressing Modes** | Embedded compilers translate high-level constructs (arrays, pointers) into these addressing modes |
| **Indexed Addressing** | Sinusoidal inverter control, character LCD font generation |
| **Assembler Directives** | Building structured firmware libraries, header files, modular projects |
| **Register Banks** | Real-time OS context switching in RTOS-51, RTX51 |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Tracing an SFR-Based Instruction

**Problem:** Determine the register bank and compute the value in `R3` after the following sequence.

```assembly
MOV PSW, #00H      ; Select Bank 0 (RS1=0, RS0=0)
MOV R3, #25H       ; Load R3 with 25H
MOV PSW, #08H      ; Select Bank 1 (RS1=0, RS0=1)
MOV R3, #40H       ; Load R3 (Bank 1) with 40H
MOV PSW, #00H      ; Switch back to Bank 0
MOV A, R3          ; Move R3 (Bank 0) into A
```

#### Step-by-Step Trace

**Step 1: MOV PSW, #00H**
- Immediate addressing mode.
- PSW ← 0x00 → **CY=0, AC=0, F0=0, RS1=0, RS0=0, OV=0, P=0**.
- **Bank 0** is now active (internal RAM 0x00–0x07).

**Step 2: MOV R3, #25H**
- Register addressing for destination; immediate for source.
- R3 (in Bank 0, address **0x03**) ← 0x25.

**Step 3: MOV PSW, #08H**
- 0x08 = 0000 1000 binary → only bit 3 (RS0) is set.
- **Bank 1** is now active (internal RAM 0x08–0x0F).
- Note: **Bank 0's R3 still holds 0x25** — the data is *not* lost; it is only no longer addressable as R3.

**Step 4: MOV R3, #40H**
- The current R3 is now at address **0x0B** (Bank 1).
- R3 (in Bank 1) ← 0x40.
- **Bank 0's R3 (at 0x03) still = 0x25**.

**Step 5: MOV PSW, #00H**
- Bank 0 is re-activated.

**Step 6: MOV A, R3**
- The current R3 (Bank 0) is 0x25.
- **ACC (A) ← 0x25**.

**Final Result:** `A = 0x25H` (because the final `MOV A, R3` reads Bank 0's R3, not Bank 1's).

### 3.2 Worked Example — Indexed Addressing Lookup Table

**Problem:** Use indexed addressing to retrieve the value corresponding to index `A = 3` from the following table.

```assembly
ORG 0000H
    MOV DPTR, #TABLE     ; DPTR points to start of table
    MOV A, #03H          ; Index = 3
    MOVC A, @A+DPTR      ; A ← ROM[DPTR + A]
    SJMP $               ; Stop here
    ORG 0100H
TABLE:
    DB 10H, 20H, 30H, 40H, 50H   ; Lookup data
    END
```

#### Analytical Derivation

The effective address is computed as:

$$
\text{Effective Address} = (\text{DPTR}) + (\text{A})
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\text{Effective Address} = 0x0100 + 0x0003 = 0x0103
$$

The contents of ROM location **0x0103** are examined. The table `DB 10H, 20H, 30H, 40H, 50H` is stored at addresses 0x0100 through 0x0104:

| Address | Content |
|---|---|
| 0x0100 | 0x10 |
| 0x0101 | 0x20 |
| 0x0102 | 0x30 |
| 0x0103 | **0x40** |
| 0x0104 | 0x50 |

Therefore, after the `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` instruction executes:

$$
\boxed{A = 0x40H}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** `MOVC` (note the **C** for "Code") reads from **program memory (ROM)**. For **external data memory (XRAM)** access, `MOVX` is used. For **internal RAM** access, plain `MOV` is used. This three-way distinction is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Addressing Mode vs Instruction Compatibility

Some addressing modes are **NOT valid** for certain instructions. The KTU examiner frequently tests this.

| Instruction | Immediate | Register | Direct | Register Indirect | Indexed |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **MOV A, src** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| **MOV dest, A** | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| **MOV DPTR, #data16** | ✅ (16-bit) | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **MOVC A, @A+DPTR** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (via DPTR+A) | ✅ |
| **MOVX A, @DPTR** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |
| **ADD A, src** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| **PUSH direct** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **POP direct** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often assume `MOV A, #R0` is valid — it is **NOT**. The `#` symbol requires a *constant*, not a *register reference*. Correct usage: `MOV A, R0` (register addressing) or `MOV A, #0x55` (immediate).

### 3.4 Step-by-Step — Bit-Addressable SFR Access via Assembler Directive

**Problem:** Define a symbolic name for bit P1.0 and use it to blink an LED.

```assembly
LED EQU P1.0          ; Symbolic name (using EQU directive)

ORG 0000H
MAIN:
    SETB LED          ; Make P1.0 HIGH
    ACALL DELAY       ; Call delay subroutine
    CLR LED           ; Make P1.0 LOW
    ACALL DELAY       ; Call delay subroutine
    SJMP MAIN         ; Repeat forever

DELAY:
    MOV R7, #0FFH     ; Load delay counter
LOOP:
    DJNZ R7, LOOP     ; Decrement R7, jump if not zero
    RET               ; Return from subroutine
    END
```

#### Trace of Bit Manipulation

**Step 1: `LED EQU P1.0`**
- Preprocessor substitution: every `LED` becomes the address `0x90.0` (i.e., bit 0 of SFR at 0x90).

**Step 2: `SETB LED`**
- Instruction size: **2 bytes** (opcode + bit address).
- Sets the bit at address 0x90.0 to **1**.
- Cycle count: **1 machine cycle**.

**Step 3: `CLR LED`**
- Sets the bit at 0x90.0 to **0**.
- Voltage on P1.0 pin: **0V (LOW)**.

**Step 4: `DJNZ R7, LOOP`**
- Decrements R7; if R7 ≠ 0, jumps back to LOOP.
- Loops **255 times** (from 0xFF down to 0x01) → a software delay.
- After R7 reaches 0, falls through to `RET`.

**Step 5: `SJMP MAIN`**
- Short jump — **2 bytes**, range –128 to +127.
- Creates an **infinite loop** — LED toggles forever.

### 3.5 Derivation — Stack Pointer (SP) Behavior with PUSH/POP

**Problem:** Show internal RAM state after PUSH and POP operations.

```assembly
MOV SP, #0x40        ; Initialize SP to 0x40
MOV A, #55H          ; A = 0x55
MOV R0, #0x30        ; R0 = 0x30
PUSH ACC             ; Push ACC onto stack
PUSH 00H             ; Push R0 (address 0x00 in Bank 0)
POP ACC              ; Pop into ACC
POP 00H              ; Pop into R0
```

#### Step-by-Step Stack Trace

| Step | Operation | SP Value (after) | RAM[SP] | ACC | R0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `MOV SP, #0x40` | 0x40 | — | — | — |
| 2 | `MOV A, #55H` | 0x40 | — | 0x55 | — |
| 3 | `MOV R0, #0x30` | 0x40 | — | 0x55 | 0x30 |
| 4 | `PUSH ACC` (SP++, RAM[SP]=A) | **0x41** | 0x55 | 0x55 | 0x30 |
| 5 | `PUSH 00H` (SP++, RAM[SP]=R0) | **0x42** | 0x30 | 0x55 | 0x30 |
| 6 | `POP ACC` (A ← RAM[SP], SP--) | 0x41 | 0x30 | **0x30** | 0x30 |
| 7 | `POP 00H` (R0 ← RAM[SP], SP--) | **0x40** | 0x55 | 0x30 | **0x55** |

**Mathematical Representation of PUSH/POP:**

$$
\text{PUSH: } \text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} + 1; \quad \text{RAM[SP]} \leftarrow \text{Source}
$$

$$
\text{POP: } \text{Dest} \leftarrow \text{RAM[SP]}; \quad \text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} - 1
$$

**Key Insight:** A LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) data structure. The stack grows **upward** in the 8051 (toward higher addresses).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The 8051 stack **grows UP**, not down (unlike x86). This trips up many students. Also, `PUSH`/`POP` only support **direct addressing** of the source/destination — you cannot use `PUSH R0` (you must write `PUSH 00H` to push R0 from Bank 0).

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 8051 Internal RAM and SFR Memory Map

The following Mermaid block-diagram depicts the functional layout of the 8051 internal memory, showing how SFRs integrate with the register banks, bit-addressable area, and general-purpose RAM.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef ramBox fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sfrBox fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bitBox fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef unused fill:#FAFAFA,stroke:#9E9E9E,stroke-width:1px,color:#616161

    A["INTERNAL RAM 0x00 to 0x7F (128 bytes)"]:::ramBox
    B["Register Bank 0 (0x00-0x07) R0-R7"]:::bitBox
    C["Register Bank 1 (0x08-0x0F) R0-R7"]:::bitBox
    D["Register Bank 2 (0x10-0x17) R0-R7"]:::bitBox
    E["Register Bank 3 (0x18-0x1F) R0-R7"]:::bitBox
    F["Bit-Addressable Area (0x20-0x2F) 16 bytes = 128 bits"]:::bitBox
    G["General Purpose RAM (0x30-0x7F) 80 bytes"]:::ramBox

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    A --> E
    A --> F
    A --> G

    H["SFR AREA 0x80 to 0xFF (128 addresses, 21 implemented)"]:::sfrBox
    H --> H1["P0 (0x80) Bit-Addr"]
    H --> H2["SP (0x81)"]
    H --> H3["DPL (0x82) DPH (0x83)"]
    H --> H4["PCON (0x87)"]
    H --> H5["TCON (0x88) Bit-Addr"]
    H --> H6["TMOD (0x89) TL0 TH0 TL1 TH1"]
    H --> H7["P1 (0x90) Bit-Addr"]
    H --> H8["SCON (0x98) Bit-Addr SBUF (0x99)"]
    H --> H9["P2 (0xA0) Bit-Addr"]
    H --> H10["IE (0xA8) Bit-Addr"]
    H --> H11["P3 (0xB0) Bit-Addr"]
    H --> H12["IP (0xB8) Bit-Addr"]
    H --> H13["PSW (0xD0) Bit-Addr"]
    H --> H14["ACC (0xE0) Bit-Addr"]
    H --> H15["B (0xF0) Bit-Addr"]
    H --> H16["Unimplemented (reserved) addresses"]:::unused
```

### 4.2 Addressing Modes — Decision Flow Diagram

This flow illustrates how the 8051 CPU decodes the addressing mode field of an instruction during execution.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef modeNode fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef actionNode fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000

    INST["Decode Instruction"]:::modeNode
    Q1{"Operand field\ncontains '#'?"}:::modeNode
    Q2{"Operand is\nR0-R7?"}:::modeNode
    Q3{"Operand uses\n'@' symbol?"}:::modeNode
    Q4{"Contains '@A+\nDPTR or @A+PC'?"}:::modeNode

    A1["IMMEDIATE\nValue embedded in instr\nMOV A, #25H"]:::actionNode
    A2["REGISTER\nValue in CPU register\nMOV A, R0"]:::actionNode
    A3["DIRECT\nOperand = address\nMOV A, 30H"]:::actionNode
    A4["REGISTER INDIRECT\nReg holds address\nMOV A, @R0"]:::actionNode
    A5["INDEXED\nAddr = A + DPTR/PC\nMOVC A, @A+DPTR"]:::actionNode

    INST --> Q1
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1
    Q1 -- No --> Q2
    Q2 -- Yes --> A2
    Q2 -- No --> Q3
    Q3 -- Yes --> A4
    Q3 -- No --> Q4
    Q4 -- Yes --> A5
    Q4 -- No --> A3
```

### 4.3 Assembler Translation Flow (How Directives vs Instructions Differ)

This block diagram contrasts how the assembler treats **executable instructions** (translated to machine code) versus **directives** (used for assembler guidance only).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef source fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px
    classDef action fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:2px

    SRC["Assembly Source File (.asm)"]:::source
    LEX["Lexer/Tokenizer:\nIdentifies line type"]:::action
    Q1{"Starts with\nmnemonic?"}:::action
    Q2{"Starts with\ndirective?"}:::action
    TRANS["Translate to\nmachine code bytes"]:::action
    DIR["Apply directive logic\n(ORG, EQU, DB, etc.)"]:::action
    LIST["Output .LST file\n(machine code + source)"]:::output
    HEX["Output .HEX file\n(Intel HEX format)"]:::output

    SRC --> LEX
    LEX --> Q1
    Q1 -- Yes --> TRANS
    Q1 -- No --> Q2
    Q2 -- Yes --> DIR
    TRANS --> LIST
    TRANS --> HEX
    DIR --> LIST
```

### 4.4 PSW (Program Status Word) Functional Bit Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef flagBox fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef ctrlBox fill:#C5CAE9,stroke:#1A237E,stroke-width:1.5px

    CY["Bit 7: CY (Carry)"]:::flagBox
    AC["Bit 6: AC (Aux Carry)"]:::flagBox
    F0["Bit 5: F0 (User Flag)"]:::flagBox
    RS1["Bit 4: RS1"]:::ctrlBox
    RS0["Bit 3: RS0"]:::ctrlBox
    OV["Bit 2: OV (Overflow)"]:::flagBox
    RES["Bit 1: Reserved"]:::ctrlBox
    P["Bit 0: P (Parity)"]:::flagBox

    CY --- AC --- F0 --- RS1 --- RS0 --- OV --- RES --- P
    RS1 -.controls.-> RS0
    RS1 -.selects.-> BANK["Active Register Bank (0-3)"]:::ctrlBox
    RS0 -.selects.-> BANK
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**List any five Special Function Registers (SFRs) of 8051 along with their functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | SFR | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **ACC (0xE0)** | 8-bit accumulator used as default operand for all arithmetic & logical operations |
| 2 | **B (0xF0)** | Auxiliary register used during `MUL AB` and `DIV AB` instructions |
| 3 | **PSW (0xD0)** | Program Status Word — holds ALU flags (CY, AC, OV, P) and register bank select bits (RS1, RS0) |
| 4 | **SP (0x81)** | Stack Pointer — points to the top of the on-chip stack; initialized to 0x07 on reset |
| 5 | **DPTR (DPL 0x82 + DPH 0x83)** | 16-bit Data Pointer used to access external/XRAM and ROM lookup tables |

*(Any 5 of the 21 SFRs with functions — full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain any two addressing modes of 8051 with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(a) Immediate Addressing:** The operand (data) is a constant embedded in the instruction itself, prefixed by `#`.
Example: `MOV A, #25H` — loads the accumulator with the constant 0x25. The data is part of the opcode and is fetched from program memory along with the instruction.

**(b) Register Addressing:** The operand is one of the eight CPU working registers (R0–R7) of the currently active register bank. This is the most efficient mode (no extra memory access).
Example: `MOV A, R3` — copies the content of register R3 into the accumulator.

*(Any two modes explained with example: 1½ marks each.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the Special Function Registers of 8051 with a neat memory map. Discuss the role of the PSW register in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the five addressing modes of 8051 with suitable examples. State which modes are used for which type of memory access. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### (a) SFRs of 8051 with Memory Map (7 marks)

The 8051 has **21 SFRs** located in internal RAM addresses **0x80 to 0xFF**. They are accessed only through **direct addressing**. SFRs whose addresses end in `0` or `8` (i.e., 0x80, 0x88, 0x90, 0x98, 0xA0, 0xA8, 0xB0, 0xB8, 0xD0, 0xE0, 0xF0) are **bit-addressable** — each of their 8 bits has a unique address from 0x00 to 0x7F in the bit-addressable area.

**Detailed memory map:**

| Address | SFR | Bit-Addressable? | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0x80 | P0 | Yes | Port 0 |
| 0x81 | SP | No | Stack Pointer |
| 0x82-0x83 | DPL/DPH | No | Data Pointer |
| 0x87 | PCON | No | Power Control |
| 0x88 | TCON | Yes | Timer Control |
| 0x89 | TMOD | No | Timer Mode |
| 0x8A-0x8D | TL0,TL1,TH0,TH1 | No | Timer Bytes |
| 0x90 | P1 | Yes | Port 1 |
| 0x98 | SCON | Yes | Serial Control |
| 0x99 | SBUF | No | Serial Buffer |
| 0xA0 | P2 | Yes | Port 2 |
| 0xA8 | IE | Yes | Interrupt Enable |
| 0xB0 | P3 | Yes | Port 3 |
| 0xB8 | IP | Yes | Interrupt Priority |
| 0xD0 | PSW | Yes | Program Status Word |
| 0xE0 | ACC | Yes | Accumulator |
| 0xF0 | B | Yes | B Register |

**Role of PSW (Program Status Word) — 0xD0:**

The PSW holds **CPU status flags** and the **active register bank selector**:

- **CY (bit 7):** Carry flag — set on unsigned overflow in ADD/ADDC/SUBB.
- **AC (bit 6):** Auxiliary carry — set when carry occurs from bit 3 to bit 4 (used in BCD correction via `DA A`).
- **F0 (bit 5):** User-defined general-purpose flag.
- **RS1, RS0 (bits 4, 3):** Select one of the four register banks (00→Bank 0, 01→Bank 1, 10→Bank 2, 11→Bank 3).
- **OV (bit 2):** Overflow flag — set on signed arithmetic overflow.
- **P (bit 0):** Parity flag — automatically set if ACC has an odd number of 1's.

##### (b) Five Addressing Modes of 8051 (7 marks)

1. **Immediate Addressing** — Operand is a constant prefixed with `#`. Used for loading constants.
   - Example: `MOV A, #0x30` → A = 0x30 (2 bytes, 1 cycle).

2. **Register Addressing** — Operand is in R0–R7 of the active bank. Most efficient.
   - Example: `ADD A, R0` → A = A + R0 (1 byte, 1 cycle).

3. **Direct Addressing** — Operand is the 8-bit address of the data.
   - Example: `MOV A, 30H` → A = RAM[0x30] (2 bytes, 1 cycle). Used to access SFRs and lower 128 bytes of RAM.

4. **Register Indirect Addressing** — A register (R0, R1, DPTR) holds the address of the operand. Denoted by `@`.
   - Example: `MOV A, @R0` → A = RAM[R0] (1 byte, 1 cycle). Used for arrays and pointer-based access. Only mode that can access the upper 128 bytes of internal RAM (when pointer is in R0/R1, with MOV using direct+indirect on 8052).

5. **Indexed Addressing** — Effective address = (A) + (DPTR) or (A) + (PC).
   - Example: `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` → A = ROM[DPTR + A] (1 byte, 2 cycles). Used for **lookup tables** in code memory.

**Memory Access Mapping:**

| Memory | Preferred Mode |
|---|---|
| Lower 128 RAM (0x00–0x7F) | Direct, Register, Register Indirect |
| Upper 128 RAM (8052 only) | Register Indirect (only) |
| SFRs (0x80–0xFF) | Direct (only) |
| Program ROM | Indexed (via MOVC) |
| External XRAM | Register Indirect with MOVX (DPTR or R0/R1) |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Classify the instruction set of 8051 with examples for each category. Explain the data transfer instructions in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are assembler directives? Explain the following directives with examples: **ORG, END, EQU, DB, DW, BIT, USING**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### (a) Instruction Set Classification (7 marks)

The 8051 instruction set contains **111 distinct mnemonics** divided into **5 groups**:

1. **Data Transfer Instructions (29 instructions):** Move data between registers, RAM, SFRs, and program memory.
   - Examples: `MOV A, #25H` (immediate), `MOV A, R0` (register), `MOV A, 30H` (direct), `MOVX A, @DPTR` (XRAM read), `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` (ROM read), `PUSH direct`, `POP direct`, `XCH A, Rn` (exchange).

2. **Arithmetic Instructions (24 instructions):** Perform addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, increment, decrement, and BCD correction.
   - Examples: `ADD A, R0`, `ADDC A, #25H`, `SUBB A, 30H`, `INC A`, `DEC @R1`, `MUL AB`, `DIV AB`, `DA A`.

3. **Logical Instructions (25 instructions):** AND, OR, XOR, NOT, rotate, swap, and clear.
   - Examples: `ANL A, #0F0H`, `ORL A, R0`, `XRL A, 30H`, `CPL A`, `RL A`, `RLC A`, `SWAP A`.

4. **Boolean (Bit) Instructions (17 instructions):** Operate on individual bits of bit-addressable SFRs and the bit-addressable RAM area.
   - Examples: `SETB P1.0`, `CLR C`, `ANL C, /bit`, `ORL C, bit`, `MOV C, bit`, `JC label`, `JNC label`, `JB P1.0, label`.

5. **Program Branching Instructions (17 instructions):** Unconditional and conditional jumps, calls, returns.
   - Examples: `LJMP target` (long jump), `AJMP target` (absolute jump), `SJMP target` (short jump), `JMP @A+DPTR` (indirect jump), `ACALL target`, `LCALL target`, `RET`, `RETI`, `JZ label`, `JNZ label`, `CJNE A, direct, label`, `DJNZ Rn, label`.

**Detailed discussion of Data Transfer instructions:**

- **MOV A, src** — 4 modes (immediate, register, direct, indirect).
- **MOV dest, A** — 3 modes (register, direct, indirect).
- **MOV Rn, #imm** — 2 bytes, loads constant to register.
- **MOV direct1, direct2** — copies from one address to another.
- **MOV DPTR, #data16** — the only 16-bit immediate load.
- **MOVC A, @A+DPTR** / **MOVC A, @A+PC** — read from code ROM.
- **MOVX** — read/write from external data memory (XRAM).
- **PUSH direct** — increments SP, writes to RAM[SP].
- **POP direct** — reads from RAM[SP], decrements SP.
- **XCH A, src** — exchanges ACC with source.
- **XCHD A, @Ri** — exchanges low nibble only.

##### (b) Assembler Directives (7 marks)

**Definition:** Assembler directives are instructions to the assembler that guide the translation process. They are **not translated into machine code** and consume **zero bytes of program memory**.

| Directive | Syntax | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ORG** | `ORG <addr>` | Sets the location counter (origin) for the next instruction | `ORG 0000H` (reset vector) |
| **END** | `END` | Marks the end of the source file; assembler ignores anything after this | `END` (last line) |
| **EQU** | `<symbol> EQU <value>` | Assigns a constant value to a symbol (cannot be redefined) | `PORTB EQU P1` |
| **DB** | `[label:] DB <data>` | Stores 8-bit byte(s) in program memory (often used for lookup tables and strings) | `LUT: DB 00H, 55H, AAH` |
| **DW** | `[label:] DW <data>` | Stores 16-bit word(s) in program memory (used for interrupt vector table) | `IVT: DW 0000H, 1000H` |
| **BIT** | `<symbol> BIT <bitaddr>` | Assigns a symbolic name to a bit-addressable location | `LED BIT P1.0` |
| **USING** | `USING <bank>` | Tells the assembler which register bank is in use (for symbolic register resolution) | `USING 0` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial distinction:** `USING` only informs the assembler; it does NOT change PSW. The programmer must set RS1, RS0 manually or via `SETB PSW.3` if a bank change is required at runtime.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing MOV / MOVC / MOVX** — These are three different instructions. `MOV` accesses internal RAM, `MOVC` reads code ROM, `MOVX` reads/writes external XRAM. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the `#` symbol** — `MOV A, 25H` means "load from RAM address 0x25" (direct), NOT "load the constant 25H" (immediate). Missing `#` = -1 mark.
>
> 3. **Stack grows UP** — On 8051, PUSH increments SP. Many students wrongly assume downward growth (x86-style). Marks lost: 1–2.
>
> 4. **`PUSH R0` is INVALID syntax** — You must use `PUSH 00H` (direct). PUSH/POP only support direct addressing.
>
> 5. **Confusing `DB` vs `DW`** — DB = 8-bit, DW = 16-bit. Mixing them = -1 mark.
>
> 6. **SFR addresses are not decimal** — Students sometimes write "ACC = 224" instead of "ACC = 0xE0". Always use **hex** in 8051 contexts.
>
> 7. **Using `USING` and thinking PSW changes automatically** — The USING directive is purely informational for the assembler; you must modify PSW bits manually at runtime.
>
> 8. **Bit-addressable SFRs only** — Writing `SETB ACC.0` is valid (ACC is bit-addressable), but `SETB SP.0` is **NOT** (SP is not bit-addressable).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

- ✅ **SFRs** occupy addresses **0x80–0xFF** in internal RAM. Only **21** are implemented; the rest are reserved.
- ✅ **PSW bits**: CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, —, P — remember the order.
- ✅ **Four register banks** (each 8 bytes), selectable via PSW[4:3]. Default after reset = **Bank 0**.
- ✅ **Five addressing modes**: Immediate, Register, Direct, Register Indirect, Indexed.
- ✅ **Indexed addressing** is used **only** for code ROM access via `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` or `MOVC A, @A+PC`.
- ✅ **`MOV` vs `MOVC` vs `MOVX`**: MOV → internal RAM; MOVC → program ROM; MOVX → external XRAM.
- ✅ **SP** is initialized to **0x07** on reset; the stack grows **upward**.
- ✅ **Bit-addressable SFRs** have addresses ending in **0 or 8** (hex).
- ✅ **Assembler directives** (`ORG`, `END`, `EQU`, `DB`, `DW`, `BIT`, `USING`) do not generate machine code.
- ✅ **DB = 8-bit data, DW = 16-bit data** (little-endian order).
- ✅ **EQU** cannot be redefined; values are constants.
- ✅ **Instruction set** = 5 categories: Data Transfer, Arithmetic, Logical, Boolean, Branching.
- ✅ **PUSH/POP** require direct addressing — `PUSH R0` is invalid; use `PUSH 00H`.
- ✅ **Parity flag (P)** is set automatically based on number of 1's in ACC.
- ✅ **Carry flag (CY)** doubles as the accumulator for the **bit processor** (boolean instructions).
- ✅ **Auxiliary carry (AC)** is critical for **BCD arithmetic** using the `DA A` instruction.
- ✅ **Overflow flag (OV)** is for **signed** arithmetic; CY is for **unsigned**.
- ✅ The **Data Pointer (DPTR)** is the only 16-bit SFR and is the gateway to XRAM and ROM tables.
- ✅ The **8051 has Harvard architecture** — separate code and data address spaces.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Transfer Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT404 - MICROCONTROLLER PROGRAMMING & INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Transfer Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Intel 8051 microcontroller architecture**, *Data Transfer Instructions* constitute the most fundamental and frequently used category of the instruction set. They are responsible for **copying (not moving)** the contents of a *source* operand into a *destination* operand without altering the source. Formally, in KTU 2024 scheme terminology, these are classified under the **MOV (Move), PUSH/POP, XCH (Exchange), and MOVX/MOVC** instruction groups, which collectively enable data movement between the **Accumulator (A)**, **general-purpose registers (R0–R7)**, **internal data memory (IRAM, 00H–7FH)**, **Special Function Registers (SFRs, 80H–FFH)**, **external data memory (XRAM, up to 64 KB)**, and **program code memory (ROM, up to 64 KB)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The exam demands mastery of the **source-to-destination flow**, the **addressing mode used**, and the **opcodes** of all 8051 data transfer instructions. Questions frequently test the difference between *immediate*, *direct*, *register*, and *register-indirect* addressing within the MOV family.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Post Office Parcel Room"

Imagine the **8051 microcontroller** as a large *post office parcel sorting room*:

- The **Accumulator (A)** is the *head clerk's desk* — the most accessible, fastest, and most powerful location. Every major operation must pass through it.
- **Registers R0–R7** are the *eight personal lockers* assigned to the sorting clerks — extremely fast, but limited in number.
- **Internal RAM (00H–7FH)** is the *general storage shelf* with **128 numbered slots**.
- **SFRs (80H–FFH)** are the *specialized equipment rooms* (timers, serial port, I/O ports) accessed only by the head clerk using direct keys.
- **External Data Memory (XRAM)** is the *warehouse outside the building* — large (up to **64 KB**), but slow to access.
- **Code Memory (ROM)** is the *instruction manual archive* — read-only, used to fetch constants and tables.

A *Data Transfer Instruction* is simply a **"parcel handover slip"** that tells the system: *"Take this item from Box X and place a copy of it into Box Y."* The original item in Box X remains untouched. The *addressing mode* on the slip (e.g., "R3", "30H", "@R0", or "#45H") determines *how* the clerk locates the source or destination box.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> The following **bold parameters** must be memorized for KTU examinations:
>
> - **Internal RAM size = 128 bytes (00H–7FH)** for standard 8051 (256 bytes for 8052).
> - **SFR address space = 128 bytes (80H–FFH)**.
> - **External Data Memory capacity = 64 KB** (addresses **0000H–FFFFH**).
> - **Program (Code) Memory capacity = 64 KB**.
> - **Accumulator (A) width = 8 bits**; **DPTR width = 16 bits**.
> - **Number of general-purpose registers = 8 (R0–R7)**; in total **4 register banks** (Bank 0–3) selectable via **PSW.3 (RS0)** and **PSW.4 (RS1)**.
> - **Data Transfer instructions DO NOT affect any Program Status Word (PSW) flags** — a key exam point.

---

## 1.4 Classification of 8051 Data Transfer Instructions

For KTU 2024 evaluation, data transfer instructions are categorized as follows:

1. **MOV instructions** — Generic 8-bit data copy across registers, RAM, SFRs, and immediate values.
2. **MOV DPTR, #data16** — The *only* 16-bit immediate data load instruction.
3. **MOVC (Move Code)** — Reads constant data from **program ROM** using **A + DPTR** or **A + PC** base addressing.
4. **MOVX (Move External)** — Reads/Writes data from/to **external XRAM** (used for memory-mapped peripherals).
5. **PUSH and POP** — Stack-based data transfer (with implicit SP increment/decrement).
6. **XCH and XCHD** — Atomic *swap* between the accumulator and another location.
7. **SWAP** — Nibble-level exchange within the accumulator.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The MOV Instruction Family — Operational Theory

The `MOV` instruction in 8051 follows the **Intel ASM51 syntax**:
```
MOV   <destination>, <source>
```
Semantically:
$$[\text{Destination}] \leftarrow [\text{Source}]$$
$$[\text{Source}] \leftarrow \text{Unchanged (copy semantics)}$$

The instruction occupies **1 byte, 2 bytes, or 3 bytes** of program memory depending on the addressing modes used. The first byte is the *opcode*, and the remaining bytes are the *operand bytes*.

### 2.1.1 The 15 Possible MOV Combinations (KTU High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Out of 256 theoretically possible source-destination combinations, **only 15 are valid** in 8051 MOV. The mnemonic rule: *"A appears in nearly every combo, but two memory locations cannot be moved directly."* KTU exams routinely ask: *"Why can't we use MOV 30H, 40H directly?"* — Answer: It *is* valid, but `MOV Rn, Rn` and `MOV @Ri, @Rj` and `MOV direct, @Ri → direct` (specific combinations) require careful decoding.

| # | Instruction | Bytes | Machine Cycles | Operation |
|---|-------------|-------|----------------|-----------|
| 1 | `MOV A, Rn` | 1 | 1 | A ← Rn |
| 2 | `MOV A, direct` | 2 | 1 | A ← (direct) |
| 3 | `MOV A, @Ri` | 1 | 1 | A ← (Ri) |
| 4 | `MOV A, #data` | 2 | 1 | A ← data |
| 5 | `MOV Rn, A` | 1 | 1 | Rn ← A |
| 6 | `MOV Rn, direct` | 2 | 2 | Rn ← (direct) |
| 7 | `MOV Rn, #data` | 2 | 1 | Rn ← data |
| 8 | `MOV direct, A` | 2 | 1 | (direct) ← A |
| 9 | `MOV direct, Rn` | 2 | 2 | (direct) ← Rn |
| 10 | `MOV direct, direct` | 3 | 2 | (direct) ← (direct) |
| 11 | `MOV direct, @Ri` | 2 | 2 | (direct) ← (Ri) |
| 12 | `MOV direct, #data` | 3 | 2 | (direct) ← data |
| 13 | `MOV @Ri, A` | 1 | 1 | (Ri) ← A |
| 14 | `MOV @Ri, direct` | 2 | 2 | (Ri) ← (direct) |
| 15 | `MOV @Ri, #data` | 2 | 1 | (Ri) ← data |

### 2.1.2 Addressing Modes Used in Data Transfer

- **Immediate (`#data`)** — Operand is a constant in the instruction itself. Source-only.
- **Register (`Rn`)** — Operand is one of R0–R7 in the active register bank.
- **Direct (`direct`)** — Operand is the 8-bit address of an IRAM cell or SFR.
- **Register Indirect (`@Ri`)** — Operand's address is the *content* of R0 or R1. Used for pointer-based access.

## 2.2 The 16-Bit Data Load — MOV DPTR

```
MOV   DPTR, #data16
```
- **Bytes:** 3 | **Cycles:** 2
- Operation: `DPH ← data15–8`, `DPL ← data7–0`
- Significance: DPTR is the **Data Pointer** used to access external memory and code tables. It is the only 16-bit immediate load in the 8051 base instruction set.

## 2.3 MOVC — Code Memory Read

Used to fetch constants and lookup tables from **program ROM**.

| Instruction | Operation |
|-------------|-----------|
| `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | A ← Code[DPTR + A] |
| `MOVC A, @A+PC` | A ← Code[PC + A] (where PC is *post-incremented* PC) |

> [!NOTE]
> The `@A+PC` form uses the **current PC value after the instruction fetch**, i.e., `PC = current_PC + 1`. This is a classic KTU trick question.

## 2.4 MOVX — External Data Memory Access

`MOVX` is the only instruction family that addresses the **64 KB external data memory** space. It uses either an **8-bit** (`@R0` or `@R1`) or **16-bit** (`@DPTR`) address. The external memory access uses dedicated pins: **P0 (A0–A7 + D0–D7 multiplexed)** and **P2 (A8–A15)**.

| Instruction | Bytes | Cycles | Operation |
|-------------|-------|--------|-----------|
| `MOVX A, @Ri` | 1 | 2 | A ← XRAM[Ri] (8-bit addr) |
| `MOVX A, @DPTR` | 1 | 2 | A ← XRAM[DPTR] (16-bit addr) |
| `MOVX @Ri, A` | 1 | 2 | XRAM[Ri] ← A |
| `MOVX @DPTR, A` | 1 | 2 | XRAM[DPTR] ← A |

## 2.5 PUSH and POP — Stack Operations

The 8051 stack resides in **internal RAM**, growing **upward** (toward higher addresses). The **Stack Pointer (SP)** is an 8-bit SFR at address **81H**, initialized to **07H** on reset.

```
PUSH direct   ; SP ← SP+1,  (SP) ← (direct)
POP  direct   ; (direct) ← (SP), SP ← SP-1
```

- **PUSH** is **2 bytes, 2 cycles**.
- **POP** is **2 bytes, 2 cycles**.
- Pushing the accumulator uses `PUSH ACC` (0xE0), **not** `PUSH A`.

## 2.6 Exchange Instructions — XCH, XCHD, SWAP

| Instruction | Operation |
|-------------|-----------|
| `XCH A, Rn` | A ↔ Rn (full byte swap) |
| `XCH A, direct` | A ↔ (direct) |
| `XCH A, @Ri` | A ↔ (Ri) |
| `XCHD A, @Ri` | A[low nibble] ↔ (Ri)[low nibble]; high nibbles unchanged |
| `SWAP A` | A[high nibble] ↔ A[low nibble] |

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Unit / Note |
|---------|-----------------|-------------|
| Internal RAM Range | $00\text{H} - 7\text{F}_{\text{H}}$ | **128 bytes** (8051), 256 bytes (8052) |
| SFR Range | $80\text{H} - \text{FF}_{\text{H}}$ | Direct-addressable only |
| XRAM Address Space | $0000_{\text{H}} - \text{FFFF}_{\text{H}}$ | **64 KB**, accessed via MOVX |
| Code Memory Space | $0000_{\text{H}} - \text{FFFF}_{\text{H}}$ | **64 KB**, accessed via MOVC |
| Program Counter Increment per Instruction | $PC \leftarrow PC + N$ | $N$ = instruction byte count |
| Stack Push (effective) | $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} + 1$ | Always increments first |
| Stack Pop (effective) | $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 1$ | Decrement after read |
| MOVC @A+PC effective address | $\text{Addr} = (PC_{\text{current}} + 1) + A$ | $PC_{\text{current}}$ is address of MOVC opcode |
| MOVX 8-bit paging | Address $\in [00_{\text{H}}, \text{FF}_{\text{H}}]$ | Uses P2 as page latch |
| DPTR load time | 2 machine cycles | Slowest MOV variant |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Data transfer instructions form the **backbone of every embedded system**: transferring sensor readings into RAM for processing, copying constants from code ROM for LCD/LED display, buffering data into external SRAM for data loggers, saving the program context onto the stack during interrupts, and reading/writing external peripheral registers. Mastery of MOV, MOVC, and MOVX is non-negotiable for **embedded firmware engineers, IoT device programmers, and automotive ECU developers**.
