# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — MOV Combination Analysis

**Problem [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]:**
Given: `R0 = 25H, R1 = 40H, A = 5AH, Internal RAM[30H] = 7FH, Internal RAM[40H] = B3H`. Determine the contents of all affected locations after executing:

```
MOV  30H, #0C5H      ; (a)
MOV  A, @R0          ; (b)
MOV  R1, A           ; (c)
MOV  40H, 30H        ; (d)
```

### Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):

**Step 1 — Identify addressing mode and decode each instruction.**

| Line | Instruction | Addressing Mode | Opcode Bytes | Bytes |
|------|-------------|-----------------|--------------|-------|
| (a) | `MOV 30H, #0C5H` | Direct + Immediate | 75 30 C5 | 3 |
| (b) | `MOV A, @R0` | Register Indirect | E6 | 1 |
| (c) | `MOV R1, A` | Register | F9 | 1 |
| (d) | `MOV 40H, 30H` | Direct + Direct | 85 30 40 | 3 |

**Step 2 — Execute (a) `MOV 30H, #0C5H`.**

$$\text{IRAM}[30H] \leftarrow 0C5H$$

Initial IRAM[30H] = 7FH → Now IRAM[30H] = **C5H**. A, R0, R1, IRAM[40H] all unchanged.

**Step 3 — Execute (b) `MOV A, @R0`.**

R0 currently holds **25H**. The `@R0` addressing mode means "the byte at the address equal to R0's content."

$$A \leftarrow \text{IRAM}[25H]$$

Assuming IRAM[25H] was not specified, we take the *standard assumption* that unspecified RAM cells hold **00H** at reset. Therefore:

$$A \leftarrow 00H = 00H$$

**Step 4 — Execute (c) `MOV R1, A`.**

$$R1 \leftarrow A = 00H$$

R1 was 40H → Now R1 = **00H**.

**Step 5 — Execute (d) `MOV 40H, 30H`.**

$$IRAM[40H] \leftarrow IRAM[30H] = C5H$$

Final values:
- **A = 00H**
- **R0 = 25H** (unchanged)
- **R1 = 00H**
- **IRAM[30H] = C5H**
- **IRAM[40H] = C5H**

> [!NOTE]
> The 8051 `MOV 40H, 30H` is a **3-byte, 2-cycle** instruction: opcode **85H**, source address **30H**, destination address **40H**. Many students incorrectly write "30H → 40H" implying a pointer change — the actual operation is a *value copy*.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Stack PUSH/POP Derivation

**Problem:**
Initial state: **SP = 07H**, **A = 3FH**, **B = 0A5H**, **R5 = 7CH**. Execute the sequence and determine the final SP value and stack contents.

```
PUSH ACC
PUSH 0F0H       ; address of B register (SFR)
PUSH 05H        ; address of R5 in current bank
POP  ACC
POP  0F0H
```

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Initialize stack pointer.**

$$\text{SP}_{\text{initial}} = 07H$$

**Step 2 — Execute `PUSH ACC` (0xE0).**

According to 8051 stack semantics:
$$\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} + 1 = 07H + 1 = 08H$$
$$\text{IRAM}[08H] \leftarrow \text{ACC} = 3FH$$

**Step 3 — Execute `PUSH 0F0H` (B register).**

$$\text{SP} \leftarrow 08H + 1 = 09H$$
$$\text{IRAM}[09H] \leftarrow \text{SFR}[0F0H] = 0A5H$$

**Step 4 — Execute `PUSH 05H` (R5 in default Bank 0).**

$$\text{SP} \leftarrow 09H + 1 = 0AH$$
$$\text{IRAM}[0AH] \leftarrow \text{IRAM}[05H] = 7CH$$

Stack state at this point:

| Address | Content |
|---------|---------|
| 0AH | 7CH |
| 09H | A5H |
| 08H | 3FH |
| 07H | (unchanged) |

**Step 5 — Execute `POP ACC`.**

$$A \leftarrow \text{IRAM}[0AH] = 7CH$$
$$\text{SP} \leftarrow 0AH - 1 = 09H$$

**Step 6 — Execute `POP 0F0H` (B).**

$$\text{SFR}[0F0H] \leftarrow \text{IRAM}[09H] = 0A5H$$
$$\text{SP} \leftarrow 09H - 1 = 08H$$

**Final Result:** SP = **08H**, A = **7CH**, B = **0A5H** (preserved).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The push/pop pattern is **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)**. To restore registers in correct order, the **POP order must be the reverse of the PUSH order** — a common KTU 14-mark question.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — MOVC Code Memory Lookup

**Problem:**
A temperature lookup table is stored in program ROM starting at address **0200H**. The DPTR is loaded with **0200H**, and the accumulator A holds the index **03H**. Compute the effective code-memory address fetched by:

```
MOVC A, @A+DPTR
```

### Derivation:

The effective address for MOVC @A+DPTR is:

$$\text{Effective Address} = \text{DPTR} + A$$

$$\text{Effective Address} = 0200H + 03H = 0203H$$

$$\therefore \quad A \leftarrow \text{Code}[0203H]$$

If, for example, Code[0203H] = 0B7H, then A becomes **B7H** after execution.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — XCHD Nibble Swap

**Problem:** `A = 0x7F`, `R0 = 0x30`, `IRAM[30H] = 0xA9`. Determine the result of `XCHD A, @R0`.

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1 — Decode the XCHD operation.** It exchanges only the **low nibble** (bits 0–3) of A with the low nibble of the addressed byte.

**Step 2 — Isolate nibbles.**

$$A_{\text{high}} = 7H, \quad A_{\text{low}} = F H$$
$$(R0)_{\text{high}} = 0AH, \quad (R0)_{\text{low}} = 9H$$

**Step 3 — Swap low nibbles.**

$$A_{\text{new}} = 7H \, \| \, 9H = 79H$$
$$\text{IRAM}[30H]_{\text{new}} = 0AH \, \| \, FH = 0AFH$$

**Step 4 — Final state:** A = **79H**, IRAM[30H] = **AFH**. High nibbles **7H** and **AH** are untouched.

---

|-------------|--------------|-------|--------|-------------|
| `MOV A, R0` | E8 | 1 | 1 | None |
| `MOV A, R7` | EF | 1 | 1 | None |
| `MOV A, 30H` | E5 30 | 2 | 1 | None |
| `MOV A, @R0` | E6 | 1 | 1 | None |
| `MOV A, #45H` | 74 45 | 2 | 1 | None |
| `MOV R3, A` | FB | 1 | 1 | None |
| `MOV R3, 30H` | AB 30 | 2 | 2 | None |
| `MOV R3, #45H` | 7B 45 | 2 | 1 | None |
| `MOV 30H, A` | F5 30 | 2 | 1 | None |
| `MOV 30H, R3` | 8B 30 | 2 | 2 | None |
| `MOV 30H, 40H` | 85 40 30 | 3 | 2 | None |
| `MOV 30H, @R1` | 87 30 | 2 | 2 | None |
| `MOV 30H, #45H` | 75 30 45 | 3 | 2 | None |
| `MOV @R0, A` | F6 | 1 | 1 | None |
| `MOV @R0, 30H` | A6 30 | 2 | 2 | None |
| `MOV @R0, #45H` | 76 45 | 2 | 1 | None |
| `MOV DPTR, #1234H` | 90 12 34 | 3 | 2 | None |
| `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | 93 | 1 | 2 | None |
| `MOVC A, @A+PC` | 83 | 1 | 2 | None |
| `MOVX A, @R0` | E2 | 1 | 2 | None |
| `MOVX A, @DPTR` | E0 | 1 | 2 | None |
| `MOVX @R0, A` | F2 | 1 | 2 | None |
| `MOVX @DPTR, A` | F0 | 1 | 2 | None |
| `PUSH direct` | C0 | 2 | 2 | None |
| `POP direct` | D0 | 2 | 2 | None |
| `XCH A, Rn` | C8–CF | 1 | 1 | None |
| `XCH A, direct` | C5 | 2 | 1 | None |
| `XCH A, @Ri` | C6/C7 | 1 | 1 | None |
| `XCHD A, @Ri` | D6/D7 | 1 | 1 | None |
| `SWAP A` | C4 | 1 | 1 | None |

> [!WARNING]
> **`MOVX A, @R0` and `MOVX A, @R1` use 8-bit addresses** and require P2 to hold the high-byte page — a frequent KTU viva question. For 16-bit XRAM access, always prefer `MOVX A, @DPTR`.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between the `MOV` and `MOVX` instructions of the 8051 microcontroller.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | `MOV` | `MOVX` |
|-----------|-------|--------|
| Memory Accessed | Internal RAM (00H–7FH) and SFRs (80H–FFH) | External Data Memory (0000H–FFFFH, up to 64 KB) |
| Addressing Modes | Register, Direct, Register-Indirect, Immediate | Only Register-Indirect (`@R0`, `@R1`, `@DPTR`) |
| Bus Used | Internal bus | External bus — P0 (D0–D7 + A0–A7 multiplexed) and P2 (A8–A15) |
| Machine Cycles | 1 or 2 | Always 2 |
| Pin Activation | None (internal) | Activates `RD` (read) or `WR` (write) pin |
| Typical Use | General data manipulation | Accessing external RAM chips, memory-mapped peripherals |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for table differentiation, 1 for application)*

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Explain the operation of the `PUSH` and `POP` instructions. What is the initial value of the Stack Pointer (SP) after a system reset?**

**Model Answer:**

**Initial SP after reset = 07H.**

`PUSH direct` performs two steps:
1. Increments SP by 1: `SP ← SP + 1`
2. Writes the contents of the direct address onto the stack: `(SP) ← (direct)`

`POP direct` performs two steps:
1. Reads the top of stack into the direct address: `(direct) ← (SP)`
2. Decrements SP by 1: `SP ← SP - 1`

The stack grows **upward** in memory (toward higher addresses). For example, if SP = 07H initially, after one PUSH, SP = 08H, and the pushed byte resides at IRAM[08H].

*(3 marks: 1.5 for PUSH/POP steps, 0.5 for initial SP, 1 for direction and example)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** 
List and explain the various addressing modes used in the 8051 data transfer instructions with suitable examples.

**Model Solution:**

The 8051 supports **five primary addressing modes** for data transfer:

**1. Immediate Addressing**
The operand is a constant embedded in the instruction.
Example: `MOV A, #25H` → loads A with the value 25H. The `#` symbol denotes an 8-bit literal. Used when a known constant is required.

**2. Register Addressing**
The operand is one of the eight general-purpose registers R0–R7 in the currently selected register bank (selected via PSW bits RS0, RS1).
Example: `MOV A, R3` → copies R3 to A. The bank is implied; only the register number is encoded in the opcode.

**3. Direct Addressing**
The operand is the 8-bit address of an internal RAM cell or SFR.
Example: `MOV A, 30H` → copies the byte at internal RAM address 30H to A. Also: `MOV 30H, A`, `MOV P1, A`.

**4. Register Indirect Addressing**
The operand's address is *contained* in R0 or R1 (a pointer). The `@` symbol indicates indirection.
Example: `MOV A, @R0` → if R0 = 40H, then A ← IRAM[40H]. Useful for table lookups and pointer-based array access.

**5. Indexed Addressing** *(Used only with MOVC and JUMP instructions)*
The effective address = base + accumulator offset.
Example: `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` → A ← ROM[DPTR + A].

*(7 marks: 1.5 each for immediate, register, direct, register-indirect; 1 for indexed mode)*

#### **(b) [7 Marks]**
Write an 8051 assembly program to swap the contents of internal RAM locations 35H and 40H using data transfer instructions, without using XCH.

**Model Solution:**

```
        ORG  0000H
        MOV  R0, #35H         ; R0 points to first location
        MOV  R1, #40H         ; R1 points to second location
        MOV  A, @R0           ; A ← RAM[35H]
        MOV  R2, A            ; R2 ← A (save RAM[35H] temporarily)
        MOV  A, @R1           ; A ← RAM[40H]
        MOV  @R0, A           ; RAM[35H] ← RAM[40H]
        MOV  A, R2            ; A ← saved RAM[35H]
        MOV  @R1, A           ; RAM[40H] ← original RAM[35H]
HERE:   SJMP HERE             ; infinite loop
        END
```

**Trace:** If RAM[35H] = 5AH and RAM[40H] = A5H, then after execution: RAM[35H] = A5H, RAM[40H] = 5AH. R2 is used as a temporary holding register.

*(7 marks: 1 for setup, 2 for swap logic, 2 for trace, 1 for terminating loop, 1 for completeness)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]**
Explain the `MOVC` and `MOVX` instruction families. Give one example program that uses MOVC to read a constant from program memory.

**Model Solution:**

**`MOVC` (Move Code):** Reads a byte from program ROM.
- `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` — A ← Code[DPTR + A]. Used for 64 KB-wide lookup tables.
- `MOVC A, @A+PC` — A ← Code[(PC + 1) + A]. Used for compact, relocatable tables.

**`MOVX` (Move External):** Reads/writes a byte from/to external XRAM (max 64 KB).
- `MOVX A, @DPTR` — Read from XRAM[DPTR].
- `MOVX @DPTR, A` — Write A to XRAM[DPTR].
- `MOVX A, @Ri` — 8-bit address (page mode).

**Example Program — Using MOVC to read from code memory:**
```
        ORG  0000H
        MOV  DPTR, #0200H    ; Point DPTR to table base
        MOV  A, #03H         ; Index = 3
        MOVC A, @A+DPTR      ; A ← Code[0203H]
        MOV  P1, A           ; Send to Port 1
        END

        ORG  0200H           ; Table stored here
        DB   0A5H, 0B7H, 0C9H, 0DBH, 0EDH
```

When executed with DPTR = 0200H and A = 03H, the byte at code address 0203H (**0DBH**) is loaded into A and output to P1.

*(7 marks: 2 for MOVC explanation, 2 for MOVX explanation, 3 for the program with trace)*

#### **(b) [7 Marks]**
Demonstrate the operation of the 8051 stack by writing a program that pushes the contents of ACC, B, and R5 onto the stack, then pops them back in reverse order. Show the final state of the stack pointer and the registers.

**Model Solution:**

```
        ORG  0000H
        MOV  SP, #0FH         ; Initialize SP to a safe location
        MOV  A, #3FH          ; A = 3FH
        MOV  B, #0A5H         ; B = A5H
        MOV  R5, #7CH         ; R5 = 7CH

        PUSH ACC              ; IRAM[10H] = 3FH, SP = 10H
        PUSH 0F0H             ; IRAM[11H] = A5H (B register), SP = 11H
        PUSH 05H              ; IRAM[12H] = 7CH (R5), SP = 12H

        POP  05H              ; R5 = IRAM[12H] = 7CH, SP = 11H
        POP  0F0H             ; B  = IRAM[11H] = A5H, SP = 10H
        POP  ACC              ; A  = IRAM[10H] = 3FH, SP = 0FH

HERE:   SJMP HERE
        END
```

**Final state:** SP = **0FH**, A = **3FH**, B = **0A5H**, R5 = **7CH** — all values preserved due to symmetric push/pop.

> [!NOTE]
> Using `PUSH A` is **invalid syntax** in standard ASM51 — the assembler requires `PUSH ACC` (or `PUSH 0E0H`).

*(7 marks: 1 for SP init, 2 for push sequence with addresses, 2 for pop sequence, 1 for final state, 1 for syntax accuracy)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Data Transfer Questions:**
>
> 1. **Wrong operand order** — Students write `MOV 30H, A` correctly but reverse it as `MOV A, 30H` semantically, then use direct addressing where register indirect was intended. The **destination is always on the LEFT** in 8051 ASM.
>
> 2. **`PUSH A` instead of `PUSH ACC`** — Assemblers may accept some variants, but KTU strictly expects `PUSH ACC` (or `PUSH 0E0H`). Worth **0.5–1 mark** deduction.
>
> 3. **Confusion between `MOVC A, @A+PC` and `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`** — The PC form uses the **incremented PC** (PC of the *next* instruction), not the PC of the MOVC opcode's first byte.
>
> 4. **Forgetting flag behavior** — Data transfer instructions **do not affect any PSW flags**. Students who write "Z = 1" after MOV lose marks.
>
> 5. **MOVX 8-bit vs 16-bit** — `MOVX A, @R0` uses only 8 address lines; P2 must hold the page. KTU loves this distinction.
>
> 6. **Stack underflow/overflow** — Popping from an empty stack (SP = 07H) reads garbage; pushing beyond 7FH (with no XRAM stack) wraps around. State the limitation explicitly.
>
> 7. **MOV source/destination mismatch** — `MOV Rn, Rn` is **illegal** in 8051. Likewise `MOV @Ri, @Rj` and `MOV @Ri, #data16` (no 16-bit immediate to indirect). These are common MCQ traps.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Data Transfer Instructions**
>
> - **MOV semantics:** *Copy*, not move — source is preserved.
> - **Operand order:** `MOV <destination>, <source>` (Intel syntax).
> - **Five addressing modes:** Immediate, Register, Direct, Register-Indirect, Indexed (MOVC only).
> - **MOV matrix:** 15 valid 8-bit MOV combinations; **only 1** 16-bit variant (`MOV DPTR, #data16`).
> - **MOVC** → Code ROM (64 KB); **MOVX** → External XRAM (64 KB).
> - **MOVC @A+PC** uses **post-incremented** PC: effective address = `(PC of MOVC) + 1 + A`.
> - **MOVX @R0/R1** uses **8-bit** addressing; P2 must hold the page for >256 byte external chips.
> - **Stack Pointer initial value after reset = 07H**; stack grows **upward** in internal RAM.
> - **PUSH order = reverse of POP order** for correct LIFO restoration.
> - **SYNTAX:** `PUSH ACC` (not `PUSH A`); `PUSH 0F0H` (B register); `PUSH PSW` is valid.
> - **XCHD** swaps **only the low nibble**; **XCH** swaps the full byte; **SWAP A** exchanges the two nibbles of A.
> - **No data transfer instruction affects PSW flags** — a high-yield MCQ point.
> - **Opcodes follow a pattern:** `MOV A,...` starts with `E` (E8–EF for Rn, E5 direct, E6/E7 @Ri, 74 immediate); `MOV ...,A` ends with `F` (F5 direct, F8–FF Rn, F6/F7 @Ri).
> - **External data memory** uses pins **P0 (AD0–AD7)**, **P2 (A8–A15)**, **ALE**, **RD**, **WR** — mention in long-answer questions for full marks.
> - **Invalid combinations to remember:** `MOV Rn, Rn`, `MOV @Ri, @Rj`, `MOV DPTR, A` (no direct A→DPTR move), `MOVX #imm, A`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arithmetic, Logical, Compare and Rotate Instructions, Bit Processing Instructions, Program Flow Control Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT404 - MICROCONTROLLER PROGRAMMING & INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Arithmetic, Logical, Compare and Rotate Instructions, Bit Processing Instructions, Program Flow Control Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The 8051 Instruction Set — Formal Definition

The **8051 Instruction Set** is the complete collection of binary operation codes (opcodes) that the **Intel 8051 microcontroller's Central Processing Unit (CPU)** can decode, interpret, and execute. Each instruction is a precisely defined binary command (typically 1, 2, or 3 bytes long) that performs a specific data manipulation, transfer, or control operation in a fixed number of **machine cycles** (where one machine cycle = 12 oscillator clock periods on the standard 8051).

For KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1, the instruction set is grouped into five functional families:

| S.No | Instruction Family | Core Purpose |
|------|--------------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Arithmetic Instructions** | Perform mathematical operations on data (ADD, SUBB, MUL, DIV, INC, DEC, DA A) |
| 2 | **Logical & Compare Instructions** | Perform bitwise AND, OR, XOR, NOT and comparison operations (ANL, ORL, XRL, CPL, CLR, CJNE, DJNZ) |
| 3 | **Rotate Instructions** | Circularly shift accumulator bits left or right, through or around the carry flag (RL, RLC, RR, RRC, SWAP) |
| 4 | **Bit Processing Instructions** | Operate directly on individual bits of the bit-addressable SFRs and RAM (SETB, CLR bit, ANL C, ORL C, MOV C) |
| 5 | **Program Flow Control Instructions** | Alter the sequential execution of the program counter (LJMP, SJMP, AJMP, ACALL, LCALL, RET, RETI, conditional jumps) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** The 8051 has exactly **111 distinct instructions** classified into **42 opcodes** (because many instructions share opcodes, e.g., ADD A, R0 and ADD A, R1 use the same base opcode with different addressing). Examiners *frequently* ask students to "list and explain" the families and to "write and trace" small assembly programs.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The CPU as a Chef Following a Recipe"

Imagine the **CPU** as a meticulous **chef** in a tiny, ultra-fast kitchen:

- The **Accumulator (A)** is the chef's **right hand** — the primary working register where almost all arithmetic and logical results land.
- The **B register** is the **left hand**, used specifically for multiplication and division.
- **R0–R7** are the **eight small bowls** on the workbench holding frequently-used ingredients.
- **Internal RAM (128 bytes)** is the **pantry** where ingredients are stored between recipes.
- Each **instruction** is a **single cooking step** the chef performs (e.g., "ADD salt to bowl", "ROTATE the skewer", "JUMP to the dessert section if the dish is sweet").

The chef reads each step one at a time from a recipe book (the **program memory / ROM**). The **Program Counter (PC)** is the chef's finger pointing to the current line of the recipe. Most of the time, the finger moves to the next line, but sometimes it **jumps** to a different section (e.g., "if burning, go to emergency step") or **calls** a sub-recipe (a subroutine) and later **returns** to the main recipe.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this grouping important for KTU exams?** KTU questions are not random — they almost always test one of three skills: (1) **classification** ("which family does `ANL A, R0` belong to?"), (2) **flag effects** ("after `ADD A, #0FFH`, what is the value of the CY flag?"), and (3) **program tracing** ("trace the contents of A and PSW after this 5-line program executes"). Mastering this taxonomy is the first step to scoring 14/14 on Module 1 questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Arithmetic Instructions — The Mathematical Engine

The 8051 supports **8-bit unsigned arithmetic** natively. The following instructions form the arithmetic core:

### 2.1.1 Addition Family

| Mnemonic | Operation | Bytes | Cycles | Affected Flags |
|----------|-----------|-------|--------|----------------|
| `ADD A, Rn` | A ← A + Rn | 1 | 1 | CY, AC, OV, P |
| `ADD A, direct` | A ← A + (direct) | 2 | 1 | CY, AC, OV, P |
| `ADD A, @Ri` | A ← A + (Ri) | 1 | 1 | CY, AC, OV, P |
| `ADD A, #data` | A ← A + data | 2 | 1 | CY, AC, OV, P |
| `ADDC A, src` | A ← A + src + CY | 1/2 | 1 | CY, AC, OV, P |

**Flag Decoding Rules for ADD/ADDC:**

- **CY (Carry Flag)** = 1 if there is a carry out of bit 7 (D7). This is the standard unsigned overflow indicator.
- **AC (Auxiliary Carry)** = 1 if there is a carry out of bit 3 (D3) into bit 4. Used by the `DA A` instruction for BCD correction.
- **OV (Overflow Flag)** = 1 if there is a carry into bit 7 XOR a carry out of bit 7. This is the **signed overflow** indicator.
- **P (Parity)** = 1 if the number of 1's in the accumulator is ODD (even parity is enforced in hardware).

### 2.1.2 Subtraction Family

The 8051 has only **one** subtraction instruction: `SUBB` (Subtract with Borrow). For the first subtraction in a chain, **CY must be cleared** (using `CLR C`) to get a clean result.

| Mnemonic | Operation | Bytes | Cycles | Affected Flags |
|----------|-----------|-------|--------|----------------|
| `SUBB A, src` | A ← A − src − CY | 1/2/3 | 1 | CY, AC, OV, P |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often assume `SUBB` is "subtract" and forget the **borrow (CY)** term. Example: `SUBB A, R0` computes A − R0 − CY, not A − R0. Examiners routinely deduct 1 mark for this oversight.

### 2.1.3 Increment & Decrement

| Mnemonic | Operation | Bytes | Cycles | Flags Affected |
|----------|-----------|-------|--------|----------------|
| `INC A` | A ← A + 1 | 1 | 1 | **P only** |
| `INC Rn` | Rn ← Rn + 1 | 1 | 1 | None |
| `INC direct` | (direct) ← (direct) + 1 | 2 | 1 | None |
| `INC @Ri` | (Ri) ← (Ri) + 1 | 1 | 1 | None |
| `DEC A` | A ← A − 1 | 1 | 1 | **P only** |
| `DEC Rn / direct / @Ri` | operand − 1 | 1/2 | 1 | None |

> [!NOTE]
> `INC` and `DEC` on the **accumulator** update only the **P flag** — they **do not** affect CY, AC, or OV. On other operands, they affect **no flags at all**.

### 2.1.4 Multiplication & Division

| Mnemonic | Operation | Bytes | Cycles | Flags Affected |
|----------|-----------|-------|--------|----------------|
| `MUL AB` | B:A ← A × B | 1 | **4** | OV, CY, P |
| `DIV AB` | A ← Quotient(A/B), B ← Remainder | 1 | **4** | OV, CY, P |

- After `MUL AB`: **A** holds the low byte, **B** holds the high byte. **OV = 1** if result > 255.
- After `DIV AB`: **OV = 1** if B was originally 0 (division by zero trap).

### 2.1.5 Decimal Adjust for BCD Addition

`DA A` corrects the binary result of an ADD into proper BCD form. It is used **only after ADD/ADDC**, never after INC.

## 2.2 Logical Instructions

| Mnemonic | Operation | Bytes | Cycles | Use Case |
|----------|-----------|-------|--------|----------|
| `ANL A, src` | A ← A AND src | 1/2 | 1 | Mask out (clear) specific bits |
| `ANL direct, A` | (direct) ← (direct) AND A | 2 | 1 | Clear bits in RAM/SFR |
| `ANL direct, #data` | (direct) ← (direct) AND data | 3 | 2 | Clear bits using immediate mask |
| `ORL A, src` | A ← A OR src | 1/2 | 1 | Set specific bits |
| `XRL A, src` | A ← A XOR src | 1/2 | 1 | Toggle bits / check inequality |
| `CPL A` | A ← NOT A (1's complement) | 1 | 1 | Bitwise inversion |
| `CLR A` | A ← 00H | 1 | 1 | Reset accumulator |
| `RL A` / `RR A` | Rotate Left/Right (no CY) | 1 | 1 | Bit manipulation |
| `RLC A` / `RRC A` | Rotate through Carry | 1 | 1 | Multi-byte shifts |
| `SWAP A` | Swap nibbles of A | 1 | 1 | BCD ↔ ASCII conversions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bit Masking Trick (High-Yield for KTU):** To **clear bit 5** of a byte → `ANL A, #0DFH` (since `0DFH = 1101 1111B`). To **set bit 3** → `ORL A, #08H`. To **toggle bit 7** → `XRL A, #80H`. KTU questions on "masking" carry **7 marks** every session.

## 2.3 Compare Instructions

The 8051 has **no** dedicated "CMP" instruction. Comparison is performed using two specialised instructions:

### 2.3.1 `CJNE` — Compare and Jump if Not Equal

`CJNE <dest>, <src>, <rel_addr>`

- Computes internally: `dest − src` (the result is **discarded**; only flags are affected).
- **CY = 1** if `dest < src` (unsigned).
- **CY = 0** if `dest ≥ src`.
- Jumps to `<rel_addr>` only if `dest ≠ src`.

### 2.3.2 `DJNZ` — Decrement and Jump if Not Zero

`DJNZ <Rn/direct>, <rel_addr>`

- Decrements the operand first.
- If the **result is non-zero**, jumps to the relative address.
- **Flags are NOT affected** by DJNZ (except the operand itself).

## 2.4 Rotate Instructions — Detailed

There are **four** rotate instructions plus the SWAP:

| Mnemonic | Description | CY involvement |
|----------|-------------|----------------|
| `RL A` | Rotate Left within A (bit 7 → bit 0) | CY not used |
| `RR A` | Rotate Right within A (bit 0 → bit 7) | CY not used |
| `RLC A` | Rotate Left through CY (bit 7 → CY, old CY → bit 0) | CY used |
| `RRC A` | Rotate Right through CY (bit 0 → CY, old CY → bit 7) | CY used |
| `SWAP A` | Exchange high and low nibbles of A | CY not used |

**Visual Representation of `RLC A`:**
$$\text{Before:} \quad \text{CY} = 1, \quad A = 0110\,1010$$
$$\text{After:} \quad \text{CY} = 0, \quad A = 1101\,0101$$
(The old CY became the new bit 0; the old bit 7 became the new CY.)

## 2.5 Bit Processing Instructions (Boolean Processor)

The 8051 contains a **single-bit Boolean processor** that operates on bit-addressable locations: the **16 bytes from 20H–2FH** of internal RAM (128 addressable bits) and the **bit-addressable SFRs** (P0–P3, TCON, SCON, IE, IP, PSW, ACC, B).

| Mnemonic | Operation |
|----------|-----------|
| `SETB bit` | bit ← 1 |
| `CLR bit` | bit ← 0 |
| `CPL bit` | bit ← NOT bit |
| `ANL C, bit` | CY ← CY AND bit |
| `ANL C, /bit` | CY ← CY AND (NOT bit) |
| `ORL C, bit` | CY ← CY OR bit |
| `ORL C, /bit` | CY ← CY OR (NOT bit) |
| `MOV C, bit` | CY ← bit |
| `MOV bit, C` | bit ← CY |
| `JC rel` | Jump if CY = 1 |
| `JNC rel` | Jump if CY = 0 |
| `JB bit, rel` | Jump if bit = 1 |
| `JNB bit, rel` | Jump if bit = 0 |
| `JBC bit, rel` | Jump if bit = 1, then **clear** bit |

## 2.6 Program Flow Control Instructions

These instructions manipulate the **Program Counter (PC)** to break sequential execution:

### 2.6.1 Unconditional Jumps

| Mnemonic | Range | Bytes | Cycles |
|----------|-------|-------|--------|
| `LJMP addr16` | Full 64 KB (0000H–FFFFH) | 3 | 2 |
| `AJMP addr11` | 2 KB page (within current 2K block) | 2 | 2 |
| `SJMP rel` | −128 to +127 from current PC | 2 | 2 |
| `JMP @A+DPTR` | Indirect jump using A + DPTR | 1 | 2 |

### 2.6.2 Conditional Jumps (all use relative 8-bit offset, 2 bytes, 2 cycles)

| Mnemonic | Condition | Mnemonic | Condition |
|----------|-----------|----------|-----------|
| `JZ rel` | A = 0 | `JNZ rel` | A ≠ 0 |
| `JC rel` | CY = 1 | `JNC rel` | CY = 0 |
| `JB bit, rel` | bit = 1 | `JNB bit, rel` | bit = 0 |
| `JBC bit, rel` | bit = 1, then clear | `DJNZ Rn, rel` | (Rn−1) ≠ 0 |
| `CJNE A, src, rel` | A ≠ src | `CJNE Rn, #d, rel` | Rn ≠ d |

### 2.6.3 Subroutine Calls and Returns

| Mnemonic | Range | Bytes | Cycles |
|----------|-------|-------|--------|
| `LCALL addr16` | 64 KB | 3 | 2 |
| `ACALL addr11` | 2 KB page | 2 | 2 |
| `RET` | Return from subroutine | 1 | 2 |
| `RETI` | Return from interrupt | 1 | 2 |

### 2.6.4 No Operation

- **`NOP`** — 1 byte, 1 cycle. Used for timing delays or to "waste" a cycle. Does nothing except increment the PC.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Quick-Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise this table. It is the **single most important cheat sheet** for Module 1 questions.

| Category | Common Instructions | Typical Bytes | Typical Cycles | Flag Effect Summary |
|----------|---------------------|---------------|----------------|--------------------|
| Arithmetic 8-bit | ADD, ADDC, SUBB, INC, DEC | 1–3 | 1 | ADD/SUBB/MUL/DIV/DA A → all 4 flags; INC A → P only; INC Rn → none |
| Logical | ANL, ORL, XRL, CPL, CLR | 1–3 | 1–2 | **P only** on A, none on direct/ Rn |
| Compare | CJNE, DJNZ | 2–3 | 2 | CJNE → CY set if dest $<$ src; DJNZ → no flags |
| Rotate | RL, RR, RLC, RRC, SWAP | 1 | 1 | RL/RR/SWAP → no flag; RLC/RRC → CY only |
| Bit | SETB, CLR, CPL, ANL C, ORL C, MOV C | 2 | 1–2 | Operate on CY and addressed bit |
| Program Flow | LJMP, SJMP, AJMP, ACALL, LCALL, RET, RETI, JZ, JNZ, JC, JNC, JB, JNB, JBC | 1–3 | 1–2 | Conditional jumps read PSW or specific bit |

**Key Engineering Utility:** The 8051 instruction set is the **lowest-level interface** a programmer has to the silicon. Mastering it is the foundation for embedded systems design in industrial controllers, automotive ECUs, smart sensors, and IoT edge devices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Tracing `ADD` and Flag Effects

**Problem:** Given initial conditions `A = F3H` and `CY = 0`, evaluate the flags after `ADD A, #0BH`.

### Step 1: Convert to binary for visualisation

$$A = F3H = 1111\,0011_2$$
$$\text{operand} = 0BH = 0000\,1011_2$$

### Step 2: Perform bitwise binary addition

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{  } & 1111\,0011 \\
+ \text{ } & 0000\,1011 \\
\hline
\text{  } & 1111\,1110
\end{aligned}$$

There is **no carry out of bit 7** (leftmost column = 1 + 0 = 1, no overflow). There is **no carry out of bit 3** (low nibble: 0011 + 1011 → 1110, no carry out of the 4th bit).

### Step 3: Determine flag values

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CY} &= 0 \quad \text{(no carry out of D7)} \\
\text{AC} &= 0 \quad \text{(no carry out of D3)} \\
\text{P} &= 1 \quad \text{(A = FEH has 7 ones — ODD count → P = 1)} \\
\text{OV} &= 0 \quad \text{(C7-in XOR C7-out = 0 XOR 0 = 0)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Accumulator:** `A = FEH`, Flags: `CY=0, AC=0, OV=0, P=1`.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Signed Overflow Detection

**Problem:** Given `A = 7FH` and `B = 01H`, evaluate the flags after `ADD A, B`.

### Step 1: Identify the case

$$7FH = 0111\,1111_2 \quad \text{(signed} = +127\text{)}$$
$$01H = 0000\,0001_2 \quad \text{(signed} = +1\text{)}$$
$$+127 + 1 = +128 \quad \text{— exceeds signed 8-bit range} [-128, +127]$$

### Step 2: Binary addition

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{  } & 0111\,1111 \\
+ \text{ } & 0000\,0001 \\
\hline
\text{  } & 1000\,0000
\end{aligned}$$

A **carry occurred into D7** (from bit 6 to bit 7) AND a **carry occurred out of D7** into the (non-existent) D8.

### Step 3: Compute flags

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CY} &= 1 \quad \text{(carry out of D7)} \\
\text{AC} &= 1 \quad \text{(bit 3: 1 + 1 = 10, carry out)} \\
\text{P} &= 1 \quad \text{(A = 80H has only one 1, ODD parity)} \\
\text{OV} &= 1 \quad \text{(C-in XOR C-out = 1 XOR 1 = 0... wait, recalculate)}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Corrected OV calculation:** C7-in means the carry generated **into** bit 7. From the column 6 + 0 + 0 = 0, no carry into bit 7. So C7-in = 0, C7-out = 1. Thus **OV = C7-in XOR C7-out = 0 XOR 1 = 1**.

**Final Result:** `A = 80H`, Flags: `CY=1, AC=1, OV=1, P=1`. The OV=1 correctly signals **signed overflow**.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Multi-Byte Addition (KTU Favourite)

**Problem:** Add two 16-bit numbers stored in internal RAM:
- Number 1: low byte at `30H`, high byte at `31H`
- Number 2: low byte at `40H`, high byte at `41H`
- Store result: low byte at `50H`, high byte at `51H`. Save the final carry at `52H`.

### Solution Program

```assembly
        MOV  A, 30H       ; A = low byte of Number 1
        ADD  A, 40H       ; A = low(N1) + low(N2), CY set if overflow
        MOV  50H, A       ; Store result low byte
        MOV  A, 31H       ; A = high byte of Number 1
        ADDC A, 41H       ; A = high(N1) + high(N2) + CY from low-byte add
        MOV  51H, A       ; Store result high byte
        MOV  52H, C       ; Save final carry (using bit address 0D0H, but 'C' alias works in C51)
```

### Step-by-Step Trace

Suppose `N1 = 1234H` (at 30H=34H, 31H=12H) and `N2 = 78ABH` (at 40H=ABH, 41H=78H):

| Step | Instruction | A | CY | Memory Effect |
|------|-------------|---|-----|---------------|
| 1 | `MOV A, 30H` | 34H | 0 | — |
| 2 | `ADD A, 40H` | DFH | **1** | 34H + ABH = DFH with carry |
| 3 | `MOV 50H, A` | DFH | 1 | (50H) ← DFH |
| 4 | `MOV A, 31H` | 12H | 1 | — |
| 5 | `ADDC A, 41H` | 8BH | 0 | 12H + 78H + 1 = 8BH, no further carry |
| 6 | `MOV 51H, A` | 8BH | 0 | (51H) ← 8BH |
| 7 | `MOV 52H, C` | 8BH | 0 | (52H) ← 00H |

**Final Sum:** `0000 8B DFH` (N1 + N2 = 1234H + 78ABH = 8ADFH, stored as 8BH at 51H, DFH at 50H, with no 17th bit).

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — BCD Addition Using `DA A`

**Problem:** Add the BCD numbers 47 and 35. The accumulator initially contains 47H, and R3 contains 35H.

### Step 1: Initial setup

```assembly
        MOV  A, #47H      ; A = 47H (BCD 47)
        MOV  R3, #35H     ; R3 = 35H (BCD 35)
        ADD  A, R3        ; A = 7CH (binary), CY = 0
        DA   A            ; Decimal adjust → A = 82H (BCD 82), CY = 0
```

### Step 2: Detailed `DA A` Logic

The `DA A` instruction checks two conditions per nibble:

**Lower Nibble (D3–D0):**
- If lower nibble > 9 **OR** AC = 1, **add 06H** to the accumulator.
- Here, lower nibble = `CH = 12` (not BCD-valid) and AC = 1 (since 7+5=12 = 1100, carry out of bit 3) → **add 06H**.

**Upper Nibble (D7–D4):**
- If upper nibble > 9 **OR** CY = 1, **add 60H** to the accumulator.
- After adding 06H, A = 82H. Upper nibble = 8 ≤ 9, CY still 0 → **no further addition**.

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{before DA A}} &= 7CH \\
A_{\text{after adding 06H}} &= 7CH + 06H = 82H \\
A_{\text{final}} &= 82H \quad \text{(valid BCD for 82)} \\
CY &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Program Flow: Loop Using DJNZ

**Problem:** Add the contents of RAM locations `40H` through `4FH` and store the sum in R7.

### Solution Program

```assembly
        MOV  R0, #40H     ; R0 = pointer to start of block
        MOV  R7, #00H     ; R7 = sum accumulator, initialise to 0
        MOV  R2, #10H     ; R2 = loop counter (16 iterations)
LOOP:   ADD  A, @R0       ; A = A + (R0)
        JNC  SKIP         ; If no carry, skip overflow handling
        INC  R7           ; If carry, increment high byte of sum
SKIP:   INC  R0           ; Move pointer to next byte
        DJNZ R2, LOOP     ; Decrement R2; if not zero, jump to LOOP
        MOV  R7, A        ; Store final 8-bit sum (assumes no overflow)
        END
```

### Trace (for a 4-element sub-set for clarity)

Assume (40H)=25H, (41H)=72H, (42H)=10H, (43H)=40H, R2=4, A=0, R7=0:

| Iter | A before ADD | (R0) | A after | CY | R2 after DJNZ |
|------|--------------|------|---------|-----|---------------|
| 1 | 00H | 25H | 25H | 0 | 3 |
| 2 | 25H | 72H | 97H | 0 | 2 |
| 3 | 97H | 10H | A7H | 0 | 1 |
| 4 | A7H | 40H | E7H | 0 | 0 |

Loop terminates when R2 reaches 0. **Final A = E7H, R7 (unused for 8-bit sum) = 0** (because the JNC branch was never taken — sum < 256).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this example matters for KTU:** It demonstrates that the loop variable `R2` is also being used as the **counter** — a common 8051 idiom. Examiners look for whether students correctly use `DJNZ` for both decrementing **and** branching in one instruction.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Flag Behaviour Summary

| Instruction Type | CY | AC | OV | P | Notes |
|------------------|----|----|----|----|-------|
| `ADD` family | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | All four flags updated |
| `ADDC` family | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Includes prior CY |
| `SUBB` family | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | CY = borrow complement |
| `MUL AB` | ✓ (always 0) | — | ✓ | ✓ | CY cleared; OV = 1 if product > 255 |
| `DIV AB` | ✓ (always 0) | — | ✓ | ✓ | CY cleared; OV = 1 if divisor = 0 |
| `DA A` | ✓ | ✓ | — | ✓ | Only after ADD/ADDC |
| `INC A` | — | — | — | ✓ | Only P updates |
| `INC` (other) | — | — | — | — | No flag change |
| `DEC A` | — | — | — | ✓ | Only P updates |
| `DEC` (other) | — | — | — | — | No flag change |
| `ANL / ORL / XRL` (on A) | — | — | — | ✓ | Only P updates |
| `ANL / ORL` (on direct/ C) | ✓ (C only) | — | — | — | Only CY may change |
| `CJNE` | ✓ | — | — | — | CY reflects comparison result |
| `DJNZ` | — | — | — | — | No flag change at all |
| `RLC` / `RRC` | ✓ | — | — | ✓ | CY from bit 7/0 |
| `RL` / `RR` / `SWAP` | — | — | — | — | No flag change |
| `CLR A` | — | — | — | ✓ | P = 1 (zero has even parity) |
| `CPL A` | — | — | — | ✓ | Inverts A bit-by-bit |
| `SETB C` / `CLR C` / `CPL C` | ✓ | — | — | — | Direct CY manipulation |
| `NOP` | — | — | — | — | No flag change |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — 8051 Instruction Set Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["8051 Instruction Set"] --> B["Data Transfer"]
    A --> C["Arithmetic"]
    A --> D["Logical"]
    A --> E["Boolean / Bit"]
    A --> F["Program Branching"]

    C --> C1["ADD ADDC"]
    C --> C2["SUBB"]
    C --> C3["INC DEC"]
    C --> C4["MUL DIV"]
    C --> C5["DA A"]

    D --> D1["ANL ORL XRL"]
    D --> D2["CLR CPL"]
    D --> D3["Rotate RL RR RLC RRC"]
    D --> D4["SWAP"]
    D --> D5["Compare CJNE DJNZ"]

    E --> E1["SETB CLR bit"]
    E --> E2["CPL bit ANL C ORL C"]
    E --> E3["MOV C bit MOV bit C"]
    E --> E4["JC JNC JB JNB JBC"]

    F --> F1["Unconditional LJMP AJMP SJMP JMP at DPTR"]
    F --> F2["Conditional JZ JNZ JC JNC"]
    F --> F3["Subroutine ACALL LCALL RET RETI"]
    F --> F4["No Operation NOP"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Program Flow Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Sequential Fetch"] --> P2["Decode Opcode"]
    P2 --> P3{"Is it a Control Instruction?"}
    P3 -- No --> P4["Execute Data Instruction"]
    P3 -- Yes --> P5{"Type of Control?"}
    P5 -- Unconditional Jump --> P6["PC = Target Address"]
    P5 -- Conditional Jump --> P7{"Test PSW Flag or Bit"}
    P7 -- Condition TRUE --> P6
    P7 -- Condition FALSE --> P8["PC = PC + Instruction Length"]
    P5 -- Subroutine Call --> P9["Push PC onto Stack, PC = Target"]
    P5 -- Return --> P10["Pop Stack into PC"]
    P4 --> P1
    P6 --> P1
    P8 --> P1
    P9 --> P1
    P10 --> P1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Adder/Subtracter Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC["Source Operand: Rn, direct, @Ri, or #data"] --> MUX["Internal Multiplexer"]
    ACC["Accumulator A"] --> ALU["8-bit ALU"]
    MUX --> ALU
    CYIN["Carry Flag Input"] --> ALU
    ALU --> RESULT["Result back to A or Direct"]
    ALU --> FLAGS["Flag Generator Logic"]
    FLAGS --> CYFLAG["CY: Carry out of D7"]
    FLAGS --> ACFLAG["AC: Carry out of D3"]
    FLAGS --> OVFLAG["OV: Signed overflow detector"]
    FLAGS --> PFLAG["P: Even parity checker"]
    ALU -.-> SUBB["For SUBB: ALU computes Two's Complement of src and adds to A plus CY"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Bit Processing Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BITADDR["Bit Address 00H-7FH (RAM 20H-2FH) or SFR bits (80H-FFH)"] --> DECODER["Bit Address Decoder"]
    DECODER --> BITOP["Single-Bit ALU"]
    CYREG["CY Flag Register"] --> BITOP
    OPINST["SETB / CLR / CPL / ANL C / ORL C / MOV C"] --> BITOP
    BITOP --> WRITEBACK["Update Bit in Memory or CY"]
    JUMPCOND["Conditional Jump on bit status JB JNB JBC JC JNC"] --> DECODER
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading note for KTU 2024 students:** Mermaid renders in GitHub, VS Code preview, and most modern markdown editors. The above block diagrams are abstractions of the actual hardware in the 8051 silicon die. KTU questions may show a similar block and ask "Identify the operation in Box X" — being able to read such diagrams is itself a 2–3 mark skill.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the function of the `DA A` instruction. Why is it used only after `ADD` and not after `INC`?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `DA A` (Decimal Adjust Accumulator) instruction converts the binary result of a previous `ADD` or `ADDC` operation into a valid **Binary Coded Decimal (BCD)** form. It does so by adding `06H` to the lower nibble if the lower nibble exceeds `9` or the AC flag is set, and by adding `60H` to the upper nibble if the upper nibble exceeds `9` or the CY flag is set. **(2 marks)**

`DA A` is used **only after `ADD`/`ADDC`** because it relies on the AC and CY flags set by those instructions to detect BCD overflow in each nibble. The `INC` instruction does not affect AC, and therefore `DA A` cannot reliably interpret the nibble overflows after `INC`. **(1 mark)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Jun 2023]**
**Differentiate between `RLC A` and `RL A` with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- `RL A` rotates the 8 bits of the accumulator **left by one position in a closed loop**, with bit 7 wrapping around to bit 0. The Carry flag is **not involved** and is **not affected**. **(1.5 marks)**
- `RLC A` rotates the 9-bit value formed by the **CY flag concatenated with the accumulator** (CY = bit 8, A = bits 7–0) left by one position. The old bit 7 of A moves into CY, and the old CY moves into bit 0 of A. The Carry flag **is updated**. **(1.5 marks)**

**Example:** If `A = 0110 0101B` and `CY = 1`:
- After `RL A`: `A = 1100 1010B`, `CY = 1` (unchanged)
- After `RLC A`: `A = 1100 1011B`, `CY = 0`

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1A]**

**(a) Explain in detail the arithmetic instructions of the 8051 microcontroller. Discuss the flag effects of ADD, SUBB, MUL, and DIV with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The 8051 supports a rich set of 8-bit arithmetic instructions that operate primarily on the accumulator.

**(i) ADD Family (2 marks)**
The instructions `ADD A, Rn / direct / @Ri / #data` and `ADDC A, src` perform 8-bit binary addition.
- Example: If `A = 25H` and `R3 = 47H`, then `ADD A, R3` produces `A = 6CH`, `CY = 0`, `AC = 0`, `OV = 0`, `P = 1`.
- `ADDC` includes the existing CY for multi-byte chained addition.
- All four PSW flags (CY, AC, OV, P) are updated.

**(ii) SUBB Family (1.5 marks)**
`SUBB A, src` computes A − src − CY. It is the **only** subtraction instruction, so multi-byte subtraction is performed by repeatedly invoking `SUBB`. **The CY must be cleared (`CLR C`) before the first subtraction** to obtain a correct result.
- Example: With `A = 40H`, `R0 = 17H`, `CY = 0`: `SUBB A, R0` → A = 29H, CY = 0 (no borrow).
- If `A = 17H`, `R0 = 40H`, `CY = 0`: result is D7H, and **CY = 1** (borrow occurred).

**(iii) MUL and DIV (1.5 marks)**
- `MUL AB` multiplies the unsigned values in A and B. The 16-bit result is stored as A = low byte, B = high byte. The OV flag is set if the product exceeds 255 (i.e., B ≠ 0 after multiplication).
- Example: `A = 64H (100)`, `B = 03H (3)` → After `MUL AB`: A = 2CH (44 = 100 mod 256), B = 01H, OV = 1.
- `DIV AB` divides A by B; the quotient goes to A and the remainder to B. OV = 1 if division by zero is attempted.

**(iv) INC, DEC, DA A (2 marks)**
- `INC` and `DEC` add or subtract 1. Only `INC A` and `DEC A` affect the P flag; no other flags are touched.
- `DA A` performs decimal adjust after an ADD/ADDC to obtain BCD results.

**(b) Write an 8051 assembly language program to find the sum of the first ten natural numbers (1+2+…+10) and store the result in R7. Use a loop with DJNZ. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```assembly
        MOV  A, #00H      ; A = 0 (running sum)
        MOV  R1, #01H     ; R1 = current natural number
        MOV  R2, #0AH     ; R2 = loop counter (10 iterations)
LOOP:   ADD  A, R1        ; A = A + R1
        INC  R1           ; R1 = R1 + 1 (next number)
        DJNZ R2, LOOP     ; Decrement R2; if R2 ≠ 0, jump to LOOP
        MOV  R7, A        ; Store final sum in R7
        END               ; Program ends
```

**Trace of Program Execution:**

| Iteration | R1 before ADD | A before ADD | A after ADD | R2 after DJNZ |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | 01H | 00H | 01H | 09H |
| 2 | 02H | 01H | 03H | 08H |
| 3 | 03H | 03H | 06H | 07H |
| 4 | 04H | 06H | 0AH | 06H |
| 5 | 05H | 0AH | 0FH | 05H |
| 6 | 06H | 0FH | 15H | 04H |
| 7 | 07H | 15H | 1CH | 03H |
| 8 | 08H | 1CH | 24H | 02H |
| 9 | 09H | 24H | 2DH | 01H |
| 10 | 0AH | 2DH | 37H | 00H |

**Final Result:** `R7 = 37H` (which is 55 in decimal — correct: 1+2+…+10 = 55).

**Program Size:** 9 bytes of program memory. **Execution time:** Approximately 14 machine cycles (not counting the DJNZ overhead) = 14 × 12 = 168 oscillator periods.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1B]**

**(a) Explain the rotate instructions of the 8051 with neat diagrams. How are they useful in implementing multiplication/division by powers of two? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The 8051 provides four rotate instructions and one swap instruction, all operating on the accumulator. **(0.5 marks)**

**(i) `RL A` — Rotate Left within A (1.5 marks)**
The 8 bits of A are shifted left by one position; bit 7 wraps around to bit 0. CY is not involved and remains unchanged.

$$\text{Diagram:} \quad \boxed{D7} \rightarrow \boxed{D6} \rightarrow \boxed{D5} \rightarrow \boxed{D4} \rightarrow \boxed{D3} \rightarrow \boxed{D2} \rightarrow \boxed{D1} \rightarrow \boxed{D0} \rightarrow \boxed{D7}$$

Example: `A = F0H = 1111 0000B` → After `RL A`: `A = E1H = 1110 0001B`.

**(ii) `RR A` — Rotate Right within A (1.5 marks)**
The 8 bits of A are shifted right; bit 0 wraps around to bit 7. CY unchanged.

Example: `A = 0FH = 0000 1111B` → After `RR A`: `A = 87H = 1000 0111B`.

**(iii) `RLC A` — Rotate Left Through Carry (1.5 marks)**
A 9-bit rotation involving CY and A. The old CY becomes the new bit 0, and the old bit 7 becomes the new CY.

$$\text{Diagram:} \quad \boxed{CY} \rightarrow \boxed{D7} \rightarrow \boxed{D6} \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow \boxed{D0} \rightarrow \boxed{CY}$$

Example: `A = 6AH = 0110 1010B`, `CY = 1` → After `RLC A`: `A = D5H = 1101 0101B`, `CY = 0`.

**(iv) `RRC A` — Rotate Right Through Carry (1.5 marks)**
A 9-bit rotation in the opposite direction.

**(v) `SWAP A` — Swap Nibbles (0.5 marks)**
Exchanges the upper and lower 4 bits of A in a single cycle. Useful for BCD ↔ ASCII conversion.

**Multiplication/Division Utility (0.5 marks bonus):**
A single `RLC A` with `CY = 0` performs an unsigned **multiply by 2**, and a single `RRC A` with `CY = 0` performs an unsigned **divide by 2** — extremely fast and useful in signal processing loops.

**(b) Explain the bit manipulation (Boolean processor) instructions of the 8051. Write a program to toggle bit P1.7 continuously. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The 8051 has a dedicated single-bit Boolean processor that can set, clear, complement, AND, OR, and move individual bits. The bit-addressable space spans 16 bytes of internal RAM (20H–2FH, giving 128 addressable bits 00H–7FH) and 16 bit-addressable SFRs (such as P0, P1, P2, P3, TCON, SCON, ACC, B, PSW, IE, IP). **(2 marks)**

**Key Bit Instructions (2.5 marks):**

| Mnemonic | Function |
|----------|----------|
| `SETB bit` | Set the bit to logic 1 |
| `CLR bit` | Clear the bit to logic 0 |
| `CPL bit` | Complement the bit |
| `ANL C, bit` | AND bit with CY, store in CY |
| `ANL C, /bit` | AND NOT-bit with CY, store in CY |
| `ORL C, bit` | OR bit with CY, store in CY |
| `ORL C, /bit` | OR NOT-bit with CY, store in CY |
| `MOV C, bit` | Copy bit into CY |
| `MOV bit, C` | Copy CY into bit |

**Program to Toggle P1.7 Continuously (2.5 marks):**

P1.7 corresponds to the SFR bit address `97H` (or the assembly alias `P1.7` in most assemblers).

```assembly
        ORG  0000H
        MOV  SP, #60H     ; Initialise stack pointer (for safety)
        MOV  A, #00H      ; A = 0 (used to set CY = 0)

LOOP:   CPL  P1.7         ; Toggle bit P1.7 (complement)
        ACALL DELAY       ; Optional: call a small delay subroutine
        SJMP LOOP         ; Repeat forever
        END
```

**Optional Delay Subroutine (for visibility):**
```assembly
DELAY:  MOV  R2, #0FFH
D1:     MOV  R3, #0FFH
D2:     DJNZ R3, D2
        DJNZ R2, D1
        RET
```

**Working:** Each iteration, `CPL P1.7` inverts the bit — if it was 1, it becomes 0, and vice versa. The unconditional `SJMP LOOP` creates an infinite loop, generating a square wave on P1.7 (with a duty cycle of 50%). This is a classic KTU question because it integrates bit processing, program flow control, and the concept of subroutines.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting CY on SUBB (–1 mark):** Always remember `SUBB` subtracts the existing CY too. Clear it with `CLR C` if not part of a multi-byte chain.
> 2. **Confusing `ANL A, #0DFH` masking (–1 mark):** The mask for clearing bit 5 is `DFH` (binary `1101 1111`), not `BFH`. Triple-check the bit position.
> 3. **Using `DJNZ A, rel` (–1 mark, syntactically invalid):** `DJNZ` does not work on the accumulator. Use a working register.
> 4. **Wrong flag for `INC A` (–0.5 mark):** Only the **P** flag changes — CY, AC, OV remain untouched.
> 5. **Forgetting to declare `ORG` in program (–0.5 mark):** Always begin the code with `ORG 0000H` for 8051 assembly listings.
> 6. **Mixing `RL` and `RLC` (–1 mark):** They are NOT the same. The carry flag is the difference.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1:**

- [x] The 8051 has **111 instructions** grouped into **5 functional families**: Arithmetic, Logical, Compare, Rotate, Bit, and Program Flow Control.
- [x] **ADD/ADDC** affect **all four PSW flags**; `SUBB` affects all four; `MUL`/`DIV` affect CY (cleared), OV, and P.
- [x] **`SUBB`** is the **only** subtraction instruction and includes the carry flag in the operation. Always `CLR C` first if not part of a multi-byte chain.
- [x] **`INC A` / `DEC A`** affect **only the P flag**; `INC`/`DEC` on any other operand affect **no flags**.
- [x] **`MUL AB`** → A = low byte of product, B = high byte. **OV = 1** if product > 255.
- [x] **`DIV AB`** → A = quotient, B = remainder. **OV = 1** if divisor (B originally) = 0.
- [x] **`DA A`** is used **only after ADD/ADDC** (not after INC) and adds 06H/60H to the nibbles that exceed BCD range.
- [x] **Logical instructions** (`ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`) on the accumulator affect only the P flag.
- [x] **Masking:** `ANL A, #mask` clears bits where mask = 0; `ORL A, #mask` sets bits where mask = 1; `XRL A, #mask` toggles bits where mask = 1.
- [x] **Compare** uses **`CJNE`** (sets CY = 1 if dest $<$ src) and **`DJNZ`** (no flag effect, decrements and jumps if non-zero).
- [x] **Rotate:** `RL`/`RR` operate within A (no CY); `RLC`/`RRC` involve CY (9-bit rotate); `SWAP` exchanges nibbles of A.
- [x] **Bit processing** is a unique feature of the 8051: 128 bit-addressable RAM locations + 16 bit-addressable SFRs. Use `SETB`, `CLR`, `CPL`, `ANL C`, `ORL C`, `MOV C`.
- [x] **Program Flow:** `LJMP` (16-bit, anywhere in 64KB), `AJMP` (11-bit, within 2K page), `SJMP` (8-bit relative, –128 to +127).
- [x] **Subroutines:** `LCALL`/`ACALL` push the return address onto the stack (SP increments). `RET` pops it back. `RETI` additionally re-enables interrupts.
- [x] **Conditional jumps** (`JZ`, `JNZ`, `JC`, `JNC`, `JB`, `JNB`, `JBC`, `CJNE`, `DJNZ`) all use 8-bit relative addressing.
- [x] **NOP** (1 byte, 1 cycle) — does nothing except waste a machine cycle; useful for timing.
- [x] The **stack pointer (SP)** must be initialised (typically `MOV SP, #60H` or `#07H`) before any `CALL`/`PUSH` operation, or the return address will overwrite register banks 0/1.

> **All the best for your KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments!** Master this module thoroughly — it is the foundation for every subsequent module involving assembly coding, peripheral interfacing, and embedded C programming.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Illustrative Examples of All Types of Instructions, Programming the Ports of 8051, Programming in C: Illustrative Examples for Time Delay, I/O Operations, and Logic Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT404 - MICROCONTROLLER PROGRAMMING & INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Illustrative Examples of All Types of Instructions, Programming the Ports of 8051, Programming in C: Illustrative Examples for Time Delay, I/O Operations, and Logic Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **8051 Instruction Set** is a comprehensive collection of **111 (or 255 including all variants) native instructions** grouped into **five major functional categories**: **Data Transfer**, **Arithmetic**, **Logical**, **Boolean (Bit-Manipulation)**, and **Program Branching (Control Transfer)** instructions. Each category operates on specific addressing modes and manipulates either bytes, bits, or control flow of the program counter (PC).

**Programming the Ports of 8051** refers to the structured use of the four 8-bit bidirectional I/O ports (**P0, P1, P2, P3**) by writing to their corresponding **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** at addresses **80H, 90H, A0H, B0H** respectively. A `1` written to a port bit configures it as **input** (via internal pull-up via P1/P2/P3, open-drain for P0), while a `0` activates the **output driver** to drive LOW.

**Programming in C (Embedded C / Keil C51)** involves using a C compiler (e.g., **Keil µVision**, **SDCC**) to write structured, hardware-level programs for the 8051. It uses SFR definitions from headers like `<reg51.h>` or `<reg52.h>`, the `sbit`/`sfr` keywords to access individual pins, and intrinsic delay loops calibrated to the **oscillator frequency ($f_{osc}$)** measured in **MHz**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the NEP 2020 outcome-based framework, students are expected to demonstrate **CO2: "Apply programming concepts to interface 8051 with peripheral devices"**. This requires fluency in *both* Assembly mnemonics (for hardware-level understanding) and Embedded C (for industry-standard firmware development).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Factory Floor" Intuition

Imagine a **factory control room** as the 8051 microcontroller:

| Factory Element | 8051 Equivalent | Role |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Factory Manager | **Program Counter (PC)** | Decides *which* instruction to run *next* |
| Workbenches | **Internal RAM (128 bytes)** & **SFRs** | Where the actual work is performed |
| Conveyor Belts | **Data Buses (8-bit)** | Move raw material (data) between workbenches |
| Light Switches | **Port Pins (P0.0–P3.7)** | Each switch can be ON (`1`) or OFF (`0`) |
| Instruction Manual | **Program Memory (ROM/Flash)** | Contains step-by-step commands |
| Quality Inspector | **PSW Register (Program Status Word)** | Tracks whether the last operation overflowed, carried, or zeroed out |

**Instruction Types — In Factory Terms:**
- **Data Transfer** = Moving boxes between workbenches (`MOV`, `XCH`)
- **Arithmetic** = Adding/subtracting items on a workbench (`ADD`, `SUBB`, `MUL`, `DIV`)
- **Logical** = Sorting items by properties like size, color, or shape (`ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `CPL`)
- **Boolean** = Inspecting or flipping a single light switch (`SETB`, `CLR`, `ANL C,bit`)
- **Program Branching** = The manager deciding to skip ahead to a different manual page (`SJMP`, `LJMP`, `CJNE`, `DJNZ`)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "One Instruction = One Machine Cycle" Mental Model:** For the classic 8051, **12 oscillator clocks = 1 machine cycle**. A `NOP` (No Operation) takes 1 machine cycle and simply "burns" 12 clock cycles — this is the *atom* of time-delay construction in Assembly.

---

## 1.3 Critical Constants & Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize These Numbers for KTU Boards:**
> - Oscillator frequency range: **0 Hz to 24 MHz** (practical max for original 8051)
> - Machine cycle = **12 / $f_{osc}$** seconds
> - At **11.0592 MHz** → 1 machine cycle = **1.085 µs** (standard for serial baud-rate generation)
> - At **12 MHz** → 1 machine cycle = **1.0 µs** (used in delay calculations)
> - Ports: **P0, P1, P2, P3** = 4 × 8 = **32 I/O lines**
> - P0 has **no internal pull-up resistor** (open-drain); P1, P2, P3 have **internal pull-ups**
> - SFR address space: **80H to FFH** (128 locations, only **21** are physically implemented on basic 8051)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of 8051 Instructions

The 8051 instruction set is broadly divided into **five functional groups**. The following sub-sections break down each category with operational mechanics, format, and a representative example.

### 2.1.1 Data Transfer Instructions

These instructions move data between the **accumulator (A)**, **Rn registers (R0–R7)**, **internal RAM**, **external RAM (via MOVX)**, **program ROM (via MOVC)**, and **SFRs**.

| Mnemonic | Operation | Bytes | Cycles | Example |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| `MOV A, src` | A ← src | 1 | 1 | `MOV A, #25H` (load immediate) |
| `MOV dest, A` | dest ← A | 1 | 1 | `MOV P1, A` (send to Port 1) |
| `MOV dest, src` | dest ← src (internal RAM) | 2 | 1 | `MOV R0, 30H` |
| `MOV DPTR, #data16` | DPTR ← 16-bit | 3 | 2 | `MOV DPTR, #2000H` |
| `MOVX A, @DPTR` | A ← external RAM | 1 | 2 | Read external memory |
| `MOVX @DPTR, A` | external RAM ← A | 1 | 2 | Write external memory |
| `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | A ← code ROM | 1 | 2 | Look-up table access |
| `XCH A, Rn` | Swap A with Rn | 1 | 1 | Atomic byte exchange |
| `XCHD A, @Rn` | Swap lower nibbles | 1 | 1 | Useful for BCD |
| `PUSH direct` | Stack ← direct | 2 | 2 | Save on stack |
| `POP direct` | direct ← Stack | 2 | 2 | Restore from stack |

> [!NOTE]
> **MOVX vs MOVC — A Common Confusing Pair:**
> - `MOVX` = **External RAM access** (used for off-chip data memory)
> - `MOVC` = **Code Memory access** (used for look-up tables in program ROM)
> - Mnemonic memory trick: **"X = eXternal"**, **"C = Code/Constant"**

### 2.1.2 Arithmetic Instructions

All arithmetic operations (except increment/decrement) operate on the **accumulator (A)** and set flags in the **PSW (Program Status Word)** register.

| Mnemonic | Operation | Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| `ADD A, src` | A = A + src | `ADD A, R3` |
| `ADDC A, src` | A = A + src + CY | `ADDC A, #01H` (multi-byte addition) |
| `SUBB A, src` | A = A − src − CY | `SUBB A, R1` |
| `INC A` / `INC Rn` / `INC direct` | dest = dest + 1 | `INC R5` |
| `DEC A` / `DEC Rn` | dest = dest − 1 | `DEC R7` |
| `INC DPTR` | DPTR = DPTR + 1 | Used with `MOVC` |
| `MUL AB` | B:A = A × B | Only 8-bit multiply |
| `DIV AB` | A = A/B, B = A mod B | Sets OV if B was 0 |
| `DAA` | Decimal adjust A for BCD | After `ADD` of BCD digits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The CY vs OV Flag Distinction (Exam Favorite):**
> - **CY (Carry Flag, PSW.7)** = Carry out of bit 7 (unsigned overflow)
> - **OV (Overflow Flag, PSW.2)** = Carry into XOR Carry out of bit 7 (signed overflow)
> - For signed arithmetic, **only OV matters**. For unsigned, **only CY matters**.

### 2.1.3 Logical Instructions

These perform bit-wise operations on the accumulator or between direct bytes.

| Mnemonic | Function | Example Use |
|:---|:---|:---|
| `ANL A, src` / `ANL direct, A` / `ANL direct, #imm` | Bitwise AND | Mask bits (e.g., `ANL A, #0FH` keeps low nibble) |
| `ORL A, src` / `ORL direct, A` / `ORL direct, #imm` | Bitwise OR | Set bits (e.g., `ORL A, #80H` sets MSB) |
| `XRL A, src` / `XRL direct, A` / `XRL direct, #imm` | Bitwise XOR | Toggle bits / compare |
| `CLR A` | A ← 0 | Reset accumulator |
| `CPL A` | A ← ~A (one's complement) | Bit flip |
| `RL A` | Rotate left (no CY) | Bit shift |
| `RLC A` | Rotate left **through** CY | 16-bit rotation in B:A |
| `RR A` | Rotate right (no CY) | Bit shift |
| `RRC A` | Rotate right through CY | 16-bit rotation in B:A |
| `SWAP A` | Swap upper/lower nibbles | BCD manipulation |

### 2.1.4 Boolean (Bit) Instructions

The 8051 has a dedicated **bit-addressable space** (16 bytes = 128 bits) in internal RAM (`20H–2FH`) and certain SFRs (P0–P3, PSW, etc., which have addresses ending in `0` or `8`).

| Mnemonic | Operation | Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| `SETB bit` | Set bit to 1 | `SETB P1.7` |
| `CLR bit` | Clear bit to 0 | `CLR P3.0` |
| `CPL bit` | Complement bit | `CPL P2.3` |
| `ANL C, bit` / `ANL C, /bit` | AND CY with bit | `ANL C, /P0.0` (AND with NOT) |
| `ORL C, bit` / `ORL C, /bit` | OR CY with bit | `ORL C, P3.2` |
| `MOV C, bit` | CY ← bit | `MOV C, P1.0` |
| `MOV bit, C` | bit ← CY | `MOV P3.7, C` |
| `JC rel` / `JNC rel` | Jump if CY=1/0 | Conditional branch on carry |
| `JB bit, rel` / `JNB bit, rel` / `JBC bit, rel` | Jump if bit set/clear | `JB P1.0, LABEL` |

### 2.1.5 Program Branching Instructions

| Mnemonic | Operation | Range |
|:---|:---|:---|
| `AJMP addr11` | Absolute jump (2 KB page) | −1024 to +1023 |
| `LJMP addr16` | Long jump (full 64 KB) | Any 16-bit address |
| `SJMP rel` | Short jump | −128 to +127 |
| `JMP @A+DPTR` | Indirect jump (computed) | Useful for dispatch tables |
| `JZ rel` / `JNZ rel` | Jump if A=0 / A≠0 | Conditional on A |
| `CJNE A, src, rel` | Compare and jump if not equal | Used in loops & decision |
| `DJNZ Rn, rel` | Decrement and jump if not zero | Loop counter |
| `ACALL addr11` | Absolute call (2 KB) | Pushes PC, jumps |
| `LCALL addr16` | Long call (full 64 KB) | Subroutine call |
| `RET` | Return from subroutine | Pop PC |
| `RETI` | Return from interrupt | Pop PC + restore IE state |
| `NOP` | No operation (1 machine cycle) | Timing/alignment |

---

## 2.2 Programming the 8051 Ports — Hardware-Software Bridge

Each of the 4 ports is a **byte-wide SFR latch** that can be addressed by name or by absolute SFR address.

### 2.2.1 Port Structure (P1 Example)
- **P1.x latch** (in SFR P1) drives the output driver.
- **P1.x pin** reads the actual physical pin state.
- **Read-Modify-Write** instructions (`INC P1`, `ANL P1, #0FH`) read from the **latch** (not the pin) to avoid glitches on port-mapped hardware.
- Plain `MOV` operations read from the **pin** (in input mode).

### 2.2.2 Port Configuration Logic

To use **P1** as input, you must write `1` to its pins (activating internal pull-ups). This is a **mandatory** step and a common exam pitfall.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Forgetting to write `1`s to the port before reading inputs. A port initialized to `0` by default will read `0` regardless of the external signal because the output transistor pulls the pin LOW.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Use |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Machine Cycle Time | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ seconds | Foundation of all delay calculations |
| Delay per loop iteration (Assembly) | $T_{loop} = N \times T_{MC}$ where $N$ = machine cycles per iteration | Manual delay tuning |
| Single 8-bit delay loop max | $t_{max,8bit} = 255 \times T_{MC}$ | Inadequate for > 255 µs at 12 MHz |
| Two nested 8-bit loops | $t_{max,nested} = (255 \times (255 \times 3 + 2) + 2) \times T_{MC}$ | Used for ms-scale delays |
| Baud Rate (Serial, Mode 1) | $Baud = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | UART initialization |
| C delay (rough estimate) | $t_{delay} \approx N_{iter} \times \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}} \times C_{cycles}$ | Empirical calibration needed |
| Port input precondition | Write `1` to latch before reading pin | I/O configuration rule |
| SFR address of P0/P1/P2/P3 | `80H / 90H / A0H / B0H` | Direct addressing in Assembly |
| Bit addressable SFR range | SFRs with addresses ending in `0` or `8` | Boolean instruction target |
| Accumulator default for arithmetic | A = operand 1, dst | All ALU ops use A as default |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Delays in Embedded C:** Unlike Assembly where machine cycles are deterministic, **C compiler delay loops are non-deterministic** because the compiler may optimize away unused loops. The reliable method uses `for` loops with `NOP()` or hardware timers.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Data Transfer**: Foundation of all peripheral communication — UART (`SBUF`), SPI, I²C all rely on moving bytes between SFRs.
- **Arithmetic**: BCD arithmetic in digital clocks (`DAA` instruction), 16-bit math via `ADDC` chaining, fixed-point DSP routines.
- **Logical**: Masking in sensor conditioning (e.g., extracting upper 4 bits of an ADC reading), debouncing switch inputs, and **GPIB/parallel-port protocols**.
- **Boolean**: Driving relays, reading optocouplers, building FSMs in industrial control, **PID control loop bit-banging**.
- **Branching**: Real-time schedulers, table-driven look-ups (using `JMP @A+DPTR` for case statements in C compilers).
- **C Port Programming**: Industry-standard firmware for IoT, automotive ECUs (despite modern ARM migration, 8051 still dominates in low-cost appliances).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Time Delay Calculation (Assembly, Single Loop)

### Problem
Using a **12 MHz** crystal, calculate the exact delay produced by the following code segment and the maximum value achievable.

```assembly
    MOV R0, #0FFH    ; Load R0 with 255
AGAIN: DJNZ R0, AGAIN ; Decrement R0; if not zero, jump back
```

### Step-by-Step Analysis

**Step 1: Identify machine cycle for the MOV instruction.**

The instruction `MOV R0, #0FFH` is a 1-byte, 1-cycle instruction.

$$T_{MOV} = 1 \times T_{MC} = 1 \times \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1 \, \mu s$$

**Step 2: Identify machine cycle for the DJNZ instruction.**

The `DJNZ R0, AGAIN` is a 2-byte, 2-cycle instruction.

$$T_{DJNZ} = 2 \times T_{MC} = 2 \times 1 \, \mu s = 2 \, \mu s$$

**Step 3: Count loop iterations.**

R0 is decremented from `FFH` (255) down to `00H`. The loop body executes **255 times** (it exits when R0 reaches 0).

$$N_{iter} = 255$$

**Step 4: Compute the inner loop delay (excluding the final exit DJNZ).**

For 255 successful iterations, the loop runs 255 times with full DJNZ cycles, then executes DJNZ **one more time** which decrements to 0 and falls through (1 machine cycle only for the decrement, the jump is **not** taken).

$$T_{loop,255} = 255 \times 2 \, \mu s + 1 \, \mu s = 511 \, \mu s$$

**Step 5: Add the initial MOV instruction delay.**

$$T_{total} = T_{MOV} + T_{loop,255} = 1 \, \mu s + 511 \, \mu s = 512 \, \mu s$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{T_{total} = 512 \, \mu s = 0.512 \, ms}$$

### Maximum Delay (When R0 = 00H)
Wait — if we load `MOV R0, #00H`, then the **first DJNZ** decrements to `FFH` (rolls over from 0), giving **256 additional iterations**.

$$T_{max} = 1 \, \mu s + 256 \times 2 \, \mu s + 1 \, \mu s = 514 \, \mu s$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nested Delay Loop (Millisecond Range)

### Problem
Derive the delay produced by the following nested loop at **11.0592 MHz** crystal.

```assembly
    MOV R1, #0FFH        ; outer loop counter
NEXT_OUTER:
    MOV R0, #0FFH        ; inner loop counter
NEXT_INNER:
    DJNZ R0, NEXT_INNER  ; inner decrement
    DJNZ R1, NEXT_OUTER  ; outer decrement
```

### Step-by-Step Analysis

**Step 1: Compute machine cycle time at 11.0592 MHz.**

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085 \, \mu s$$

**Step 2: Inner loop delay (R0 = 255).**

Each `DJNZ R0, NEXT_INNER` takes 2 machine cycles. The inner DJNZ runs 255 times to exit + 1 final time to fall through.

$$T_{inner} = 255 \times 2 + 1 = 511 \, \text{machine cycles} = 511 \times 1.085 \, \mu s = 554.435 \, \mu s$$

**Step 3: Add the `MOV R0, #0FFH` instruction.**

The `MOV R0` inside the outer loop is executed **once per outer iteration** and takes 1 machine cycle.

$$T_{inner,total} = 1 + 511 = 512 \, \text{machine cycles} = 555.52 \, \mu s$$

**Step 4: Outer loop delay.**

The outer DJNZ runs 255 times + 1 fall-through = 256 × 2 = 512 machine cycles.

The `MOV R1, #0FFH` before entering the loop: 1 machine cycle.

**Step 5: Combine everything.**

$$T_{outer} = 1 \, MC + 255 \times T_{inner,total} + 512 \, MC$$

$$T_{outer} = 1 + 255 \times 512 + 512 = 131073 \, \text{machine cycles}$$

**Step 6: Convert to seconds.**

$$T_{outer} = 131073 \times 1.085 \times 10^{-6} = 0.1422 \, s = 142.2 \, ms$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{T_{delay} \approx 142.2 \, \text{ms at } 11.0592 \, \text{MHz with } (255, 255) \text{ counters}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalized Nested Delay Formula:**
> $$T_{delay} = T_{MC} \times \left[ 1 + (256 - R1_0) \times \left(1 + (256 - R0_0) \times 2 \right) + 2 \times (256 - R1_0) + 1 \right]$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Embedded C Time Delay Using NOP and For-Loop

### Problem
A C51 program for the 8051 uses an 11.0592 MHz crystal. Write a function that produces a precise 1 ms delay using `NOP` and a calibrated for-loop.

### Step-by-Step Analysis

**Step 1: Establish the time budget per iteration.**

We need 1 ms total. If we use a for-loop with 100 iterations, each iteration must consume 10 µs.

$$T_{target} = 1 \, ms = 1000 \, \mu s$$

**Step 2: Convert to machine cycles.**

$$MC_{budget} = \frac{1000 \, \mu s}{1.085 \, \mu s/MC} = 921.66 \, \text{machine cycles}$$

**Step 3: Each for-loop iteration's body.**

In C51, a single `for` loop iteration that decrements a counter and tests takes approximately **8 machine cycles** for the loop overhead (compiler-specific) plus 1 machine cycle per `NOP()`.

To consume **9.2 MC/iteration** for **100 iterations = 920 MC ≈ 1 ms**, the code becomes:

```c
void Delay_1ms(void) {
    unsigned int i;
    for (i = 0; i < 100; i++) {
        _nop_();    // intrinsic 1 MC
    }
}
```

**Step 4: Verify with the formula.**

$$T_{delay} \approx 100 \times (1 + 8) \times 1.085 \, \mu s = 976.5 \, \mu s \approx 1 \, ms \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The `_nop_()` Intrinsic:** The `NOP` macro (in Keil C51, declared in `<intrins.h>`) compiles to a single `NOP` assembly instruction = exactly 1 machine cycle. This is the **gold standard** for precise C-based delays on the 8051.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Instruction Categories vs. Addressing Modes

| Instruction Type | Immediate `#` | Register `Rn` | Direct `addr` | Indirect `@Ri` | External `@DPTR` |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Data Transfer (MOV)** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (MOVX) |
| **Arithmetic (ADD, SUBB)** | ✓ (A only) | ✓ (A only) | ✓ (A only) | ✓ (A only) | ✗ |
| **Logical (ANL, ORL, XRL)** | ✓ (A or direct) | ✓ (A only) | ✓ | ✓ (A only) | ✗ |
| **Boolean (SETB, CLR, JB)** | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ (bit addr) | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Branching (AJMP, LJMP, CJNE)** | ✓ (target addr) | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ (JMP @A+DPTR) | ✓ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Questions often ask: *"Which addressing mode is NOT allowed for a given instruction?"* Always remember that **arithmetic on direct memory requires the accumulator as the implied operand** — the 8051 ALU is accumulator-centric.

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Port Programming Example (Assembly)

### Problem
Write an 8051 Assembly program to read **P1.0** (a switch) and output the complement to **P2.7** (an LED) continuously.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Configure the input port (write 1 to latch).**

`SETB P1.0` — sets the latch bit. The internal pull-up makes the pin high-impedance and ready to read.

**Step 2: Read the pin and complement the bit using the Carry flag.**

```assembly
LOOP:
        SETB P1.0       ; Step 1: precondition P1.0 for input
        MOV C, P1.0     ; Step 2: read switch state into CY
        CPL C           ; Step 3: complement CY
        MOV P2.7, C     ; Step 4: send to LED
        SJMP LOOP       ; Step 5: repeat forever
```

**Step 3: Trace execution.**
- Switch open (external `10kΩ` pull-down to GND) → P1.0 reads `0` → CY = 0 → CPL CY = 1 → LED ON.
- Switch closed (P1.0 shorted to VCC) → P1.0 reads `1` → CY = 1 → CPL CY = 0 → LED OFF.

### Equivalent C Program

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit SW  = P1^0;   // Switch on P1.0
sbit LED = P2^7;   // LED on P2.7

void main(void) {
    SW = 1;         // Precondition for reading
    while (1) {
        LED = ~SW;  // Output complement
    }
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The `sbit` Declaration:** This Keil-specific extension creates a *symbolic bit-address* mapping to an SFR bit. The preprocessor expands `P1^0` to the bit-addressable location `90H` (P1's SFR base) with bit 0.

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Instruction Decoding by the 8051 CPU

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fetch opcode from program ROM] --> B[Opcode decoded by CPU]
    B --> C{Instruction Category}
    C -->|Opcode Group 1| D[Data Transfer MOV family]
    C -->|Opcode Group 2| E[Arithmetic ADD SUBB MUL DIV]
    C -->|Opcode Group 3| F[Logical ANL ORL XRL CPL]
    C -->|Opcode Group 4| G[Boolean SETB CLR JB JNB]
    C -->|Opcode Group 5| H[Program Branching SJMP LJMP CJNE DJNZ]
    D --> D1[Move data between A, Rn, RAM, SFRs]
    D --> D2[Special MOVX for external RAM]
    D --> D3[Special MOVC for code ROM tables]
    E --> E1[Operate on Accumulator A]
    E --> E2[Update PSW flags CY AC OV P]
    F --> F1[Bitwise operations byte by byte]
    F --> F2[Used for masking and toggling]
    G --> G1[Operate on bit-addressable space]
    G --> G2[16 bytes of bit RAM and select SFRs]
    H --> H1[Modify Program Counter PC]
    H --> H2[Conditional and unconditional jumps]
    D1 --> X[Result written to destination]
    D2 --> X
    D3 --> X
    E1 --> X
    F1 --> X
    G1 --> X
    H1 --> X
    X --> Y[Fetch next instruction]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — 8051 Port Architecture (P1)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU["8051 CPU Core"]
        ALU[ALU / Accumulator]
        SFR[SFR Bus - Internal]
    end
    ALU <-->|Read/Write bus| SFR
    subgraph P1REG["SFR P1 at address 90H"]
        LATCH[P1 Latch - 8 bits D type]
    end
    SFR <-->|8-bit data| LATCH
    subgraph PIN["Physical Pin P1.x"]
        DRV[Output Driver - Transistor]
        PINACT[Pin Voltage]
        PULLUP[Internal Pull-up 30-80k ohm]
    end
    LATCH -->|Controls| DRV
    DRV --> PINACT
    PULLUP --> PINACT
    PINACT -->|External read| SFR
    subgraph EXTLINK["External World"]
        SW[Switch / Sensor]
        LED[LED / Actuator]
    end
    PINACT --> SW
    PINACT --> LED
    LED -.->|Current path| GND[Ground]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Read This Diagram:** The 8051's CPU can either *write* to the P1 latch (to set the output state) or *read* the actual pin voltage. The internal pull-up keeps the pin HIGH when no external device drives it. **For P0, the pull-up is absent** — it must be added externally (4.7 kΩ typically).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**

**Classify the 8051 instruction set into its five major categories. Give one example instruction for each category and state the addressing mode used.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

The 8051 instruction set is classified into five major categories:

| # | Category | Example Instruction | Addressing Mode | Function |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Data Transfer | `MOV A, #25H` | Immediate | Loads 25H into accumulator |
| 2 | Arithmetic | `ADD A, R2` | Register | Adds R2 to A |
| 3 | Logical | `ANL A, #0FH` | Immediate | Masks upper nibble of A |
| 4 | Boolean | `SETB P1.7` | Direct (bit) | Sets P1.7 to logic 1 |
| 5 | Program Branching | `SJMP LABEL` | Relative | Short jump within -128 to +127 bytes |

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**

**Why is it necessary to write `1` to a port pin before reading it as an input on the 8051? What happens if this step is skipped for P0 versus P1?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

The 8051 ports (P1, P2, P3) have **internal pull-up resistors** that become active **only when a `1` is written to the corresponding latch bit**. This pulls the pin to a defined high-impedance state, allowing external circuitry to drive the pin.

- **If this step is skipped for P1, P2, or P3:** The output transistor (active when latch = 0) will short the external signal to ground. The pin will read `0` regardless of the input, as the latch is initially `0` after reset.
- **If this step is skipped for P0:** P0 is an **open-drain port with no internal pull-up**. The pin will float and the read value will be indeterminate (high-impedance / random).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain with examples the different data transfer instructions in the 8051, including MOV, MOVX, MOVC, XCH, and PUSH/POP. Mention the addressing modes used in each.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**An 8051 system uses an 11.0592 MHz crystal. Write an 8051 C program to read P1.0 (switch) and generate a 500 Hz square wave on P2.0 using software timing. Show the delay calculation.**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Data transfer instructions** form the largest group in the 8051 instruction set. They copy data from a source to a destination without modifying the source. There are **15 distinct MOV variants** plus the XCH, XCHD, MOVX, MOVC, PUSH, and POP instructions.

**(i) MOV A, src** (1 byte, 1 cycle)
- Loads accumulator from source.
- Modes: `MOV A, #imm` (immediate), `MOV A, Rn` (register), `MOV A, direct` (direct), `MOV A, @Ri` (indirect).
- Example: `MOV A, #30H` loads 30H into A.

**(ii) MOV dest, A** (1 byte, 1 cycle)
- Stores accumulator to destination.
- Example: `MOV P1, A` outputs A to Port 1.

**(iii) MOV dest, src** (2 bytes, 1 cycle)
- Internal RAM-to-RAM copy.
- Example: `MOV 30H, 40H` copies contents of RAM[40H] to RAM[30H].

**(iv) MOV DPTR, #data16** (3 bytes, 2 cycles)
- Loads 16-bit data pointer.
- Example: `MOV DPTR, #2000H` — used for external memory and look-up tables.

**(v) MOVX — External RAM Access** (1 byte, 2 cycles)
- `MOVX A, @DPTR` — reads external RAM into A.
- `MOVX @DPTR, A` — writes A to external RAM.
- `MOVX A, @Ri` and `MOVX @Ri, A` — paged external access (256-byte page using R0 or R1).

**(vi) MOVC — Code Memory Access** (1 byte, 2 cycles)
- `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` — A ← ROM[A + DPTR]. Used for look-up tables.
- `MOVC A, @A+PC` — A ← ROM[A + PC]. Used for small tables.

**(vii) XCH A, byte** (1 byte, 1 cycle)
- Atomic exchange between A and a byte.
- Example: `XCH A, R4` — swaps contents of A and R4.

**(viii) XCHD A, @Ri** (1 byte, 1 cycle)
- Swaps only the **lower nibbles** of A and the pointed-to byte. Used in BCD arithmetic.

**(ix) PUSH direct** (2 bytes, 2 cycles)
- Increments SP, then writes direct byte to stack.
- Example: `PUSH PSW` — saves program status word on stack.

**(x) POP direct** (2 bytes, 2 cycles)
- Reads from stack into direct byte, then decrements SP.
- Example: `POP ACC` — restores accumulator.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Calculate the period of 500 Hz square wave.**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{500} = 2 \, ms = 2000 \, \mu s$$

**Step 2: Half period (HIGH or LOW duration).**

$$T_{half} = 1000 \, \mu s = 1 \, ms$$

**Step 3: Machine cycle at 11.0592 MHz.**

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085 \, \mu s$$

**Step 4: Machine cycles needed.**
