$$MC_{needed} = \frac{1000 \, \mu s}{1.085 \, \mu s} = 921.66 \approx 922 \, \text{cycles}$$

**Step 5: C51 program with calibrated delay.**

```c
#include <reg51.h>
#include <intrins.h>

sbit WAVE = P2^0;       // Square wave output
sbit SW   = P1^0;       // Input switch

void delay_1ms(void) {
    unsigned char i;
    for (i = 0; i < 100; i++) {
        _nop_();        // Each NOP is exactly 1 machine cycle
    }
}

void main(void) {
    SW = 1;             // Precondition for reading
    while (1) {
        if (SW == 0) {
            WAVE = 1;
            delay_1ms();
            WAVE = 0;
            delay_1ms();
        } else {
            WAVE = 0;
        }
    }
}
```

**Calculation of compiled delay:** The Keil C51 `for` loop with NOP produces approximately 9–10 machine cycles per iteration at this optimization level. With 100 iterations:

$$T \approx 100 \times 10 \times 1.085 \, \mu s = 1085 \, \mu s \approx 1 \, ms \quad \checkmark$$

**Loop structure for continuous square wave:**
- Set P2.0 HIGH → wait 1 ms → set P2.0 LOW → wait 1 ms → repeat.

---

### Question B — [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the following 8051 instructions with one example each: (i) `CJNE A, direct, rel` (ii) `DJNZ Rn, rel` (iii) `JMP @A+DPTR` (iv) `XRL direct, #imm`. For each, state a practical application.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Write an 8051 C program using a for-loop and the `_nop_()` intrinsic to generate a 5 ms delay. The crystal is 12 MHz. Show the derivation of the loop counter value.**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**(i) `CJNE A, direct, rel` — Compare and Jump if Not Equal**
- **Operation:** Performs A − direct internally. If A ≠ direct, jumps to `rel`; otherwise, falls through. Sets CY = 1 if A < direct.
- **Bytes/Cycles:** 3 bytes, 2 cycles.
- **Example:** `CJNE A, #0AH, NOT_TEN` — branches to `NOT_TEN` if A ≠ 10.
- **Application:** Multi-way decision structures, A/D converter threshold detection.

**(ii) `DJNZ Rn, rel` — Decrement and Jump if Not Zero**
- **Operation:** Decrements Rn by 1. If result ≠ 0, jumps to `rel`; otherwise falls through.
- **Bytes/Cycles:** 2 bytes (Rn) or 3 bytes (direct), 2 cycles.
- **Example:**
  ```assembly
  MOV R2, #10
  LOOP: DJNZ R2, LOOP    ; repeats 10 times
  ```
- **Application:** Loop counter for delay generation, table traversal, repeated I/O operations.

**(iii) `JMP @A+DPTR` — Computed Jump**
- **Operation:** PC ← A + DPTR. An unconditional jump to an address computed at runtime.
- **Bytes/Cycles:** 1 byte, 2 cycles.
- **Example:**
  ```assembly
  MOV DPTR, #JUMP_TABLE
  MOV A, R5        ; R5 contains 0, 1, 2, or 3
  RL A             ; Multiply by 2 (for AJMP entries)
  JMP @A+DPTR
  JUMP_TABLE: AJMP CASE0
              AJMP CASE1
              AJMP CASE2
              AJMP CASE3
  ```
- **Application:** Implements a `switch`/`case` construct in C; useful in state machines.

**(iv) `XRL direct, #imm` — Logical XOR with Immediate**
- **Operation:** Performs bitwise XOR between a direct byte and an 8-bit immediate, stores result back in direct byte.
- **Bytes/Cycles:** 3 bytes, 2 cycles.
- **Example:** `XRL P1, #0FFH` — toggles all bits of P1.
- **Application:** LED toggling, bit inversion in protocols, generating pseudo-random sequences.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Required delay.**

$$T_{delay} = 5 \, ms = 5000 \, \mu s$$

**Step 2: Machine cycle at 12 MHz.**

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1.0 \, \mu s$$

**Step 3: Total machine cycles needed.**

$$MC_{total} = \frac{5000 \, \mu s}{1.0 \, \mu s} = 5000 \, \text{machine cycles}$$

**Step 4: Loop overhead in Keil C51.**

For a `for` loop decrementing an `unsigned int` counter, the loop prologue and counter check consume approximately **8 machine cycles per iteration** at the default optimization level. Each `_nop_()` adds **1 machine cycle**.

To consume 5000 MC over N iterations:

$$N \times (8 + 1) \approx 5000 \implies N = \frac{5000}{9} \approx 555.5$$

**Step 5: Adjusted code.**

```c
#include <reg51.h>
#include <intrins.h>

void delay_5ms(void) {
    unsigned int i;
    for (i = 0; i < 555; i++) {
        _nop_();
        _nop_();  // Two NOPs to fine-tune to ~5000 cycles
    }
}
```

**Verification:** $555 \times (8 + 2) = 5550$ MC = $5550 \, \mu s \approx 5.5 \, ms$.

**Alternative — Outer Nested Loop Approach:**

```c
void delay_5ms(void) {
    unsigned char i, j;
    for (i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
        for (j = 0; j < 110; j++) {
            _nop_();
        }
    }
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not forget `#include <intrins.h>`** — without it, `_nop_()` will not compile. **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Delay loops in C are NOT portable across compilers.** Always verify with your specific toolchain's cycle simulator. The Keil simulator shows exact cycle counts under "Performance Analyzer".
> 3. **Crystal frequency MUST be specified in the answer.** A wrong $f_{osc}$ changes the entire delay. **−1 mark per missed frequency**.
> 4. **CJNE writes only after the branch decision** — do not assume it executes after the jump target.
> 5. **Port 0 (P0) requires external pull-up resistors** — common confusion with P1–P3 in board exams.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Five instruction categories**: Data Transfer, Arithmetic, Logical, Boolean, Branching.
- [ ] **SFR addresses for ports**: P0=80H, P1=90H, P2=A0H, P3=B0H.
- [ ] **Bit-addressable SFRs** must have addresses ending in `0` or `8` (e.g., P0=80H ✓, TCON=88H ✓, but SP=81H ✗).
- [ ] **P0 has no internal pull-up** — P1, P2, P3 do (≈30–80 kΩ).
- [ ] **Machine cycle formula**: $T_{MC} = 12 / f_{osc}$.
- [ ] **Standard frequencies**: 11.0592 MHz (UART-friendly), 12 MHz (clean µs timing), 24 MHz (max for classic 8051).
- [ ] **`MOVX` = eXternal RAM; `MOVC` = Code/constant ROM.**
- [ ] **ALU is accumulator-centric** — most arithmetic uses A as both source and destination.
- [ ] **CY = unsigned overflow; OV = signed overflow** (distinguish in exams!).
- [ ] **Embedded C requires `reg51.h`** for SFR definitions and `intrins.h` for `NOP`.
- [ ] **`sbit` keyword** creates bit-level symbolic names (e.g., `sbit LED = P2^7;`).
- [ ] **Precondition ports before reading**: write `1` to the latch bit first.
- [ ] **Nested delays formula**: Use the "outer × inner" structure for ms-scale timings.
- [ ] **C delays are compiler-dependent** — always verify via simulator or hardware.
- [ ] **Read-Modify-Write instructions** (INC, ANL, ORL on ports) operate on the **latch**, not the pin.
- [ ] **Stack grows upward** in memory; PUSH increments SP first, then writes.
- [ ] **Interrupts use `RETI`** (not `RET`) to re-enable the interrupt system.
- [ ] **DPTR (16-bit) is the workhorse** for look-up tables and external memory addressing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** In 14-mark Part B questions, always structure your answer as **Definition → Example → Application → Diagram/Code**. Examiners award partial marks for each section, so even incomplete answers can earn 7–9 marks. For 3-mark Part A, write **3 distinct points** — never combine them.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: All Modes of Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT404 - MICROCONTROLLER PROGRAMMING & INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** All Modes of Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Intel 8051 microcontroller family**, the **Timer/Counter subsystem** is a critical on-chip peripheral that can be configured into **four distinct Modes of Operation** (Mode 0, Mode 1, Mode 2, and Mode 3). These modes are programmed using the **Timer Mode Register (TMOD)** and controlled by the **Timer Control Register (TCON)**. The mode selection governs the bit-width of the timer, the reload mechanism, and the behaviour of the overflow flag.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "The 8051 timers can operate in four software-selectable modes. The mode determines the size of the timer register, whether it functions as a timer or counter, and how the overflow is handled."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine four different **kitchen measuring cups** lined up in front of you:

- **Mode 0 (13-bit cup):** A weird, antique cup that holds 8192 drops before spilling — rarely used today.
- **Mode 1 (16-bit cup):** A standard, large cup holding 65536 drops. You must **manually refill** it after every spill.
- **Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload cup):** A smart, small cup holding only 256 drops, but the moment it spills, an **invisible hand instantly refills** it with a preset value. Perfect for repetitive, high-speed timing.
- **Mode 3 (Split-Timer cup):** The cup is **physically cut into two halves**. Timer 0 becomes two independent 8-bit timers, while Timer 1 is essentially "frozen" (only its oscillator keeps running, used as a baud-rate generator).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Mode 1 is the most commonly used for **event delays** (e.g., blinking an LED every 50 ms). Mode 2 is critical for **Serial Port Baud-Rate Generation** and **high-speed repetitive interrupts**. Mode 3 is used in **multi-task embedded applications** needing three independent timers.

## 1.3 The Two Control Registers (Mandatory Foundation)

### TMOD Register (Timer Mode Register) — Not Bit-Addressable

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | **GATE** | **C/T̄** | **M1** | **M0** | **GATE** | **C/T̄** | **M1** | **M0** |
| Timer | \multicolumn{4}{c}{TIMER 1} | \multicolumn{4}{c}{TIMER 0} |

- **GATE:** 1 = Hardware control via INTx pin; 0 = Software control only.
- **C/T̄:** 1 = **Counter mode** (counts external pulses on T0/T1 pin); 0 = **Timer mode** (counts internal oscillator cycles).
- **M1, M0:** Mode select bits (00=Mode 0, 01=Mode 1, 10=Mode 2, 11=Mode 3).

### TCON Register (Timer Control Register) — Bit-Addressable

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | **TF1** | **TR1** | **TF0** | **TR0** | **IE1** | **IT1** | **IE0** | **IT0** |

- **TRx:** 1 = Run timer; 0 = Stop timer.
- **TFx:** 1 = Timer overflow flag (set by hardware, must be cleared by software).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Mode-Wise Operational Analysis

### 🔹 Mode 0 — 13-Bit Timer Mode (Compatible with 8048)

- The timer register acts as a **13-bit register**. The upper 8 bits of `THx` and the lower 5 bits of `TLx` are used.
- The remaining 3 bits of `TLx` are ignored.
- Maximum count: $2^{13} = 8192$ states.
- When the count rolls over from $8191 \to 0$ (all 1s to all 0s), the **TFx flag is set**.
- **Why 13 bits?** It is a legacy compatibility mode with the Intel 8048 microcontroller. KTU rarely asks design questions on this mode but loves asking **"Why is Mode 0 used?"** (Answer: Backward compatibility with 8048).

### 🔹 Mode 1 — 16-Bit Timer Mode (Most Common)

- Uses **all 16 bits** of `THx` (upper 8) and `TLx` (lower 8).
- Maximum count: $2^{16} = 65536$ states.
- On overflow, `TFx` is set to 1, and the timer resets to `0x0000`.
- **Reload is manual** — the programmer must re-load `THx` and `TLx` with the initial value inside the ISR (Interrupt Service Routine).

### 🔹 Mode 2 — 8-Bit Auto-Reload Mode (Critical for UART)

- `TLx` acts as the **8-bit timer/counter**.
- `THx` holds the **reload value** (a constant, never modified by the timer).
- When `TLx` overflows (255 → 0), `TFx` is set, AND `THx` is **automatically copied** into `TLx`.
- Maximum count: $2^8 = 256$ states. Minimum useful delay: 1 machine cycle.
- **Why important?** This mode is used to generate the **baud rate clock for the Serial Port (UART)** in the 8051. The auto-reload makes the timing **drift-free**.

### 🔹 Mode 3 — Split Timer Mode (Timer 0 Only)

- **Timer 0 splits into two independent 8-bit timers:** `TL0` and `TH0`.
- `TL0` uses the **Timer 0 control bits** (`TR0`, `TF0`).
- `TH0` is **trapped** into a timer mode; it uses `TR1` for run control and `TF1` for overflow.
- **Timer 1 in Mode 3 simply halts** (it holds its count). It is, however, commonly used as a baud-rate generator for the serial port when Timer 0 is in Mode 3.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Machine Cycle Frequency | $f_{MC} = \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{12}$ | Hz (Crystal = **11.0592 MHz** typically) |
| 2 | Machine Cycle Period | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{1}{f_{MC}} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | Seconds per count |
| 3 | Max Count (Mode 0) | $2^{13} = 8192$ | States |
| 4 | Max Count (Mode 1) | $2^{16} = 65536$ | States |
| 5 | Max Count (Mode 2/3) | $2^{8} = 256$ | States |
| 6 | Timer Initial Value (16-bit) | $V_{initial} = 65536 - N$ | Decimal (N = desired count) |
| 7 | Timer Initial Value (8-bit) | $V_{initial} = 256 - N$ | Decimal (Auto-reload / Mode 2) |
| 8 | Delay Time | $T_{delay} = N \times T_{MC}$ | Seconds |
| 9 | Reload Value (Mode 2) | $THx = 256 - N$ | Hex value placed in THx |
| 10 | Baud Rate from Timer 1 Overflow | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \cdot f_{OSC}}{32 \cdot 12 \cdot (256 - TH1)}$ | bps (SMOD = 0 or 1) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the Crystal:** KTU questions **almost always** use **$f_{OSC} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$** for serial port problems because it produces exact standard baud rates (9600, 4800, 19200). For delay problems, they often use **$f_{OSC} = 12 \text{ MHz}$** for clean math.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Industrial Process Control:** Mode 1 timers trigger solenoid valves and conveyor belts at exact millisecond intervals.
2. **Automotive ECU:** Mode 2 auto-reload timers are used in **Anti-lock Braking Systems (ABS)** to sample wheel speed sensors at fixed, drift-free intervals.
3. **Smart Energy Meters:** Mode 3 split timers allow one timer to count pulses from the energy meter (via T0 pin) while the other measures display refresh intervals.
4. **Consumer Electronics (Washing Machines):** Mode 1 timers coordinate wash, rinse, and spin cycles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Calculating Timer Delay (Mode 1, 16-bit)

### Given (Typical KTU Problem)
- $f_{OSC} = 12 \text{ MHz}$
- Required Delay = **50 ms**
- Use Timer 0, Mode 1

### Step 1: Compute the Machine Cycle Period
The 8051 divides the crystal frequency by 12 internally to get the machine cycle frequency.

$$f_{MC} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{12} = \frac{12 \times 10^6}{12} = 1 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ MHz}$$

$$T_{MC} = \frac{1}{f_{MC}} = \frac{1}{1 \times 10^6} = 1 \mu s$$

*(One count of the timer register represents 1 µs in this crystal setup.)*

### Step 2: Compute the Number of Counts (N)
We need a 50 ms delay. Since each count takes 1 µs:

$$N = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{MC}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}}{1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}} = 50000 \text{ counts}$$

### Step 3: Verify N fits within Mode 1 Capacity
Mode 1 max count is 65536. Since $50000 \leq 65536$, Mode 1 is feasible. ✅

### Step 4: Compute the 16-bit Initial Value
The timer counts UP from the initial value to 65536 (which is 0x10000, causing an overflow).

$$V_{initial} = 65536 - N = 65536 - 50000 = 15536$$

### Step 5: Convert to Hex (for TH0 and TL0)

$$15536 \div 16 = 971 \text{ R } 0 \quad (LSB = 0)$$
$$971 \div 16 = 60 \text{ R } 11 \quad (B)$$
$$60 \div 16 = 3 \text{ R } 12 \quad (C)$$
$$3 \div 16 = 0 \text{ R } 3 \quad (3)$$

So $15536_{10} = 0x3CB0_{16}$.

$$\text{TH0} = 0x3C \quad ; \quad \text{TL0} = 0xB0$$

### Step 6: TMOD Configuration
For Timer 0 in **Mode 1** (M1=0, M0=1), **Software Control** (GATE=0), **Timer Mode** (C/T̄=0):
- Upper nibble (Timer 1) is left as 0000.
- Lower nibble = `0001` (Mode 1).

$$\text{TMOD} = 0x01$$

### Step 7: Final Assembly Snippet
```
    MOV TMOD, #01H   ; Timer 0, Mode 1, Software control
    MOV TH0, #3CH    ; Load high byte
    MOV TL0, #B0H    ; Load low byte
    SETB TR0         ; Start Timer 0
WAIT:
    JNB TF0, WAIT    ; Loop until overflow flag is set
    CLR TR0          ; Stop timer
    CLR TF0          ; Clear overflow flag (for next use)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Multiplication by integer N:** If the delay exceeds Mode 1's range, use a software loop counter. For example, for 1 second delay, loop the 50 ms delay **20 times** using a register like R7.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mode 2 Auto-Reload (Baud Rate Generation)

### Given
- $f_{OSC} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$
- Desired Baud Rate = **9600 bps**
- Serial Mode 1 (8-bit UART), SMOD = 0
- Use Timer 1, Mode 2

### Step 1: Apply the Standard 8051 Baud Rate Equation

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \cdot f_{OSC}}{32 \cdot 12 \cdot (256 - TH1)}$$

### Step 2: Substitute Known Values
With SMOD = 0, $2^{\text{SMOD}} = 1$:

$$9600 = \frac{1 \cdot 11.0592 \times 10^6}{32 \cdot 12 \cdot (256 - TH1)}$$

### Step 3: Solve the Denominator

$$9600 = \frac{11059200}{384 \cdot (256 - TH1)}$$

$$384 \cdot (256 - TH1) = \frac{11059200}{9600} = 1152$$

$$256 - TH1 = \frac{1152}{384} = 3$$

$$TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253_{10} = 0xFD_{16}$$

### Step 4: Verify
- $11059200 \div (384 \cdot 3) = 11059200 \div 1152 = 9600$ bps ✅
- This is **exactly** why 11.0592 MHz crystals are chosen — they produce **zero fractional error**.

### Step 5: TMOD Configuration
Timer 1, Mode 2, Timer mode, Software control: Lower nibble (Timer 0) = 0000; Upper nibble = `0010`.

$$\text{TMOD} = 0x20$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mode Comparison

| Parameter | Mode 0 | Mode 1 | Mode 2 | Mode 3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bit Width** | 13 | 16 | 8 | 8 (×2 split) |
| **Registers Used** | THx (8) + TLx (5) | THx (8) + TLx (8) | TLx (counter), THx (reload) | TL0 + TH0 |
| **Max Count** | 8192 | 65536 | 256 | 256 each |
| **Auto-Reload** | No | No | **Yes (from THx)** | No |
| **Overflow Flag** | TFx | TFx | TFx | TF0 (TL0) & TF1 (TH0) |
| **Overflow When** | 8192 → 0 | 65536 → 0 | 256 → 0 | 256 → 0 each |
| **Initial Value Formula** | $8192 - N$ | $65536 - N$ | $256 - N$ | $256 - N$ each |
| **Primary Use** | Legacy (8048) | General delays | **Baud Rate Gen** | Extra timers |
| **Available To** | T0, T1 | T0, T1 | T0, T1 | **T0 only** (T1 halts) |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Maximum Possible Delay in Mode 1

This is a classic KTU 2-mark short question.

### Given
- $f_{OSC} = 12 \text{ MHz}$
- Timer in Mode 1

### Step 1
$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1 \mu s$$

### Step 2
For maximum delay, set $N = 65536$ (timer counts from 0 to 0):

$$T_{max} = 65536 \times 1 \mu s = 65.536 \text{ ms}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **General Formula:** $T_{max} = \dfrac{12 \times 2^{bits}}{f_{OSC}}$, where "bits" = 13, 16, or 8 depending on mode.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 8051 Timer/Counter Internal Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OSC["OSCILLATOR (f_OSC = 11.0592 or 12 MHz)"]
        OSC1["Crystal Oscillator Pin"]
    end

    subgraph DIV12["÷ 12 Prescaler"]
        PRE["Internal Frequency Divider"]
    end

    subgraph T0CNTRL["TIMER 0 CONTROL BLOCK"]
        T0GATE["GATE Bit (TMOD.3)"]
        T0CT["C/T-bar Bit (TMOD.2)"]
        T0M1M0["M1 M0 Bits (TMOD.1, TMOD.0)"]
        T0TR["TR0 Bit (TCON.4)"]
    end

    subgraph T0REGS["TIMER 0 REGISTERS"]
        TL0["TL0 Register (Lower 8 bits)"]
        TH0["TH0 Register (Upper 8 bits)"]
    end

    subgraph T0OV["OVERFLOW"]
        TF0["TF0 Flag (TCON.5)"]
    end

    subgraph T1CNTRL["TIMER 1 CONTROL BLOCK"]
        T1GATE["GATE Bit (TMOD.7)"]
        T1CT["C/T-bar Bit (TMOD.6)"]
        T1M1M0["M1 M0 Bits (TMOD.5, TMOD.4)"]
        T1TR["TR1 Bit (TCON.6)"]
    end

    subgraph T1REGS["TIMER 1 REGISTERS"]
        TL1["TL1 Register (Lower 8 bits)"]
        TH1["TH1 Register (Upper 8 bits)"]
    end

    subgraph T1OV["OVERFLOW"]
        TF1["TF1 Flag (TCON.7)"]
    end

    EXTPIN["External Pin T0 (P3.4) / T1 (P3.5)"]

    OSC1 --> PRE
    PRE -- Internal Clock Pulses --> T0CT
    EXTPIN -- External Pulses --> T0CT
    T0CT --> TL0
    T0M1M0 -- "Configures Width" --> TL0
    T0M1M0 -- "Configures Width" --> TH0
    TL0 -- "Carry to TH0" --> TH0
    T0GATE -- "AND with INT0 pin" --> TL0
    T0TR -- "Run / Stop control" --> TL0
    TH0 --> TF0

    PRE -- Internal Clock Pulses --> T1CT
    EXTPIN -- External Pulses --> T1CT
    T1CT --> TL1
    T1M1M0 -- "Configures Width" --> TL1
    T1M1M0 -- "Configures Width" --> TH1
    TL1 -- "Carry to TH1" --> TH1
    T1GATE -- "AND with INT1 pin" --> TL1
    T1TR -- "Run / Stop control" --> TL1
    TH1 --> TF1
```

## 4.2 Mode Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["START: Choose Timer Mode"] --> B{"Need exact, drift-free\nrepetitive intervals?"}
    B -- Yes --> C["MODE 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload)"]
    B -- No --> D{"Need maximum\n16-bit range?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["MODE 1 (16-bit)"]
    D -- No --> F{"Need legacy\n8048 compatibility?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["MODE 0 (13-bit)"]
    F -- No --> H{"Need THREE\nindependent timers?"}
    H -- Yes --> I["MODE 3 (Split Timer 0)"]
    H -- No --> J["Default: MODE 1"]

    C -. "Used for UART Baud Rate" .-> K["APPLICATION"]
    E -. "Used for general delays" .-> K
    G -. "Rarely used today" .-> K
    I -. "Used for multi-task apps" .-> K
    J -. "Most common choice" .-> K
```

## 4.3 Mode 2 Auto-Reload Operational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["THx loaded with\nRELOAD VALUE (constant)"] --> B["TLx counts UP\nfrom reload value"]
    B --> C{"TLx overflow?\n(255 to 0)"}
    C -- No --> B
    C -- Yes --> D["Set TFx = 1"]
    D --> E["AUTOMATIC:\nTHx copied to TLx"]
    E --> F["TLx continues\ncounting from reload"]
    F --> B
    D --> G["Interrupt CPU\n(If Enabled)"]
```

## 4.4 Mode 3 Split Timer Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph T0["TIMER 0 in MODE 3"]
        TL0["TL0: 8-bit timer\n(uses TR0, TF0)\nFull C/T-bar and GATE control"]
        TH0["TH0: 8-bit timer (trapped)\n(uses TR1, TF1)\nNO C/T-bar, NO GATE\n(Timer mode ONLY)"]
    end

    subgraph T1HALT["TIMER 1 STATUS"]
        T1H["Timer 1 is HALTED\n(count stops incrementing)\nCan still be used as Baud-Rate Generator"]
    end

    TL0 -- "Independent overflow" --> TF0["TF0 Flag"]
    TH0 -- "Independent overflow" --> TF1["TF1 Flag (borrowed)"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
List the four modes of operation of the 8051 Timer. Which mode is used to generate the baud rate for the serial port, and why?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four modes are:
1. **Mode 0:** 13-bit timer (8048 compatibility)
2. **Mode 1:** 16-bit timer
3. **Mode 2:** 8-bit auto-reload
4. **Mode 3:** Split timer (Timer 0 only)

**Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload)** is used to generate the baud rate. The reason is that Mode 2 automatically reloads `TLx` with the value stored in `THx` upon overflow, providing a **continuous, drift-free, repetitive time base** that is essential for accurate UART communication. Mode 1 would require software reload inside an ISR, which would introduce jitter and timing errors in the baud rate. (1 mark for listing, 1 mark for identifying Mode 2, 1 mark for the "why")

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
Differentiate between the **Timer** and **Counter** functions of the 8051. How is the selection made at the register level?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Timer Mode:** The 8051 counts **internal oscillator pulses** (after a ÷12 prescaler). Used for generating time delays.
- **Counter Mode:** The 8051 counts **external events/pulses** applied to the T0 (P3.4) or T1 (P3.5) pin. Used for event counting (e.g., counting rotations of a shaft).
- **Register Level Selection:** The bit **C/T̄** in the **TMOD register** performs this selection. If `C/T̄ = 0`, the timer operates as a Timer; if `C/T̄ = 1`, it operates as a Counter.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In timer problems, students frequently (a) forget to clear the `TFx` flag before re-entering the wait loop, (b) forget to convert the count to hex correctly (showing decimal in TH/TL), and (c) confuse the Mode 1 formula (`65536 - N`) with Mode 2 (`256 - N`). **Always** show the hex conversion step explicitly. KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** for wrong TMOD value alone.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the four modes of operation of the 8051 Timer/Counter with the help of suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an 8051 assembly language program to generate a delay of **30 ms** using Timer 0 in Mode 1, given that the crystal frequency is **$f_{OSC} = 12 \text{ MHz}$**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Mode | Width | Operation | Diagram Concept |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mode 0** | 13-bit | Uses 8 bits of `THx` and lower 5 bits of `TLx`. Max count = $2^{13} = 8192$. Legacy 8048 compatibility. | `THx [8] : TLx [5] : xxx` |
| **Mode 1** | 16-bit | Uses full 8 bits of both `THx` and `TLx`. Max count = $2^{16} = 65536$. Most commonly used for delays. | `THx [8] : TLx [8]` |
| **Mode 2** | 8-bit Auto-Reload | `TLx` counts; `THx` stores the reload value. On overflow, `THx` → `TLx` automatically. Max count = 256. | `THx` (reload) → auto → `TLx` (counter) |
| **Mode 3** | Split Timer (T0 only) | `TL0` acts as an 8-bit timer (using TR0/TF0). `TH0` is a trapped 8-bit timer (using TR1/TF1). Timer 1 halts. | Two independent 8-bit counters from one timer |

**Key points for full marks:**
- Mention overflow flag behaviour for each mode.
- Mention the unique auto-reload property of Mode 2.
- Mention the "Timer 1 halts" behaviour in Mode 3.
- Sketch the register widths.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Machine Cycle Period**
$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1 \mu s$$

**Step 2: Number of Counts Required**
$$N = \frac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 30000 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 3: Verify capacity for Mode 1**
$30000 \leq 65536$ ✅ (Mode 1 is sufficient)

**Step 4: Initial Value (16-bit)**
$$V_{initial} = 65536 - 30000 = 35536_{10}$$

**Step 5: Hex Conversion**
$35536 \div 16 = 2221$ R 0
$2221 \div 16 = 138$ R 13 (D)
$138 \div 16 = 8$ R 10 (A)
$8 \div 16 = 0$ R 8

So $35536_{10} = 0x8AD0_{16}$

$$\text{TH0} = 0x8A, \quad \text{TL0} = 0xD0$$

**Step 6: Assembly Program**
```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   MOV TMOD, #01H     ; Timer 0, Mode 1, Software control
AGAIN:  ACALL DELAY_30MS   ; Call 30 ms delay subroutine
        ; (Insert your application code here, e.g., toggle P1.0)
        SJMP AGAIN

;---------- DELAY 30 ms SUBROUTINE ----------
DELAY_30MS:
        MOV TH0, #8AH      ; Load high byte
        MOV TL0, #D0H      ; Load low byte
        SETB TR0           ; Start Timer 0
HERE:   JNB TF0, HERE      ; Wait for overflow flag
        CLR TR0            ; Stop Timer 0
        CLR TF0            ; Clear overflow flag
        RET
        END
```

**Mark Distribution (KTU Style):**
- Correct formula & machine cycle: 2 marks
- Correct N and initial value: 2 marks
- Correct hex conversion: 1 mark
- TMOD value: 1 mark
- Correct assembly code: 1 mark

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the **TMOD** and **TCON** registers of the 8051 microcontroller. List the four timer modes and explain **Mode 2 (Auto-Reload)** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the value to be loaded into **TH1** to generate a baud rate of **4800 bps** using Timer 1 in Mode 2, given $f_{OSC} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$ and SMOD = 0. Write the initialization code. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**TMOD Register (89H, Not Bit-Addressable):**
| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| | GATE | C/T̄ | M1 | M0 | GATE | C/T̄ | M1 | M0 |
| Timer | \multicolumn{4}{c}{TIMER 1} | \multicolumn{4}{c}{TIMER 0} |

- **GATE:** 1 = Hardware gating via INTx pin; 0 = Software start/stop.
- **C/T̄:** 1 = Counter; 0 = Timer.
- **M1, M0:** Mode select (00=Mode 0, 01=Mode 1, 10=Mode 2, 11=Mode 3).

**TCON Register (88H, Bit-Addressable):**
| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| | TF1 | TR1 | TF0 | TR0 | IE1 | IT1 | IE0 | IT0 |

- **TR0, TR1:** Run control bits (1 = Run, 0 = Stop).
- **TF0, TF1:** Timer overflow flags (set by hardware, cleared by software).

**Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload) — Detailed Explanation:**
In Mode 2, `TLx` operates as an **8-bit timer/counter**, while `THx` holds an **8-bit constant reload value** that is pre-loaded by the programmer. The timer counts up in `TLx`. When `TLx` rolls over from $0xFF$ to $0x00$, two things happen **simultaneously and automatically in hardware**:
1. The overflow flag `TFx` is set to 1 (which can trigger an interrupt if enabled).
2. The constant value stored in `THx` is **automatically copied** into `TLx`, so the count immediately restarts from the loaded value without any software intervention.

This makes Mode 2 the **preferred choice for serial port baud rate generation**, as the timing is **drift-free** (no software reload jitter) and **continuous**.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Apply the Baud Rate Equation**
$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \cdot f_{OSC}}{32 \cdot 12 \cdot (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 2: Substitute Values**
With SMOD = 0 → $2^{\text{SMOD}} = 1$, $f_{OSC} = 11059200$, Baud = 4800:

$$4800 = \frac{1 \cdot 11059200}{384 \cdot (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 3: Solve for TH1**
$$384 \cdot (256 - TH1) = \frac{11059200}{4800} = 2304$$

$$256 - TH1 = \frac{2304}{384} = 6$$

$$TH1 = 256 - 6 = 250_{10} = 0xFA_{16}$$

**Verification:** $11059200 \div (384 \cdot 6) = 11059200 \div 2304 = 4800$ bps ✅

**Step 4: Initialization Code**
```assembly
        MOV TMOD, #20H     ; Timer 1, Mode 2, Software control
        MOV TH1, #0FAH     ; Load reload value (250 decimal)
        MOV SCON, #50H     ; Serial Mode 1 (8-bit UART), REN enabled
        SETB TR1           ; Start Timer 1 (begins baud clock)
```

**Step 5: TMOD Value Explained**
- Timer 1 → Mode 2 → M1=1, M0=0 → Upper nibble = `0010`
- Timer 0 → Unused → Lower nibble = `0000`
- Therefore, **TMOD = `0010 0000` = 0x20**

**Mark Distribution (KTU Style):**
- TMOD/TCON diagram and explanation: 3 marks
- Mode 2 explanation: 4 marks
- Baud rate equation: 2 marks
- TH1 calculation: 1 mark
- Assembly code: 2 marks
- Verification: 1 mark (bonus-style)

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] The 8051 has **two timers** (T0, T1), each with **4 modes** of operation.
- [x] **TMOD** is **not bit-addressable**; **TCON** is **bit-addressable**.
- [x] **Mode 0 = 13-bit**, **Mode 1 = 16-bit**, **Mode 2 = 8-bit Auto-Reload**, **Mode 3 = Split Timer (T0 only)**.
- [x] Machine cycle = $f_{OSC} \div 12$. With $f_{OSC} = 12 \text{ MHz}$, $T_{MC} = 1 \mu s$.
- [x] With $f_{OSC} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$, $T_{MC} \approx 1.085 \mu s$.
- [x] **Initial Value Formulas:**
  - Mode 0: $V_{init} = 8192 - N$
  - Mode 1: $V_{init} = 65536 - N$
  - Mode 2: $V_{init} = 256 - N$ (also the value to be placed in `THx`)
- [x] **Mode 2 is mandatory for UART Baud-Rate Generation** — it is drift-free and continuous.
- [x] **Mode 3 traps Timer 0's TH0** as a second timer; **Timer 1 halts** (but can still serve as baud generator).
- [x] **Baud Rate Equation:** $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \cdot f_{OSC}}{32 \cdot 12 \cdot (256 - TH1)}$
- [x] **$f_{OSC} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$** is preferred for serial communication as it gives integer divisions for standard baud rates (1200, 2400, 4800, 9600, 19200).
- [x] Always **clear the TFx flag** in software before/after use; it is **not** auto-cleared in all modes (though it auto-clears on ISR entry in some interrupt configurations).
- [x] The **TRx bit** is the **run/stop switch** — `SETB TRx` starts, `CLR TRx` stops.
- [x] Maximum delay in Mode 1 with 12 MHz crystal = **65.536 ms**. For longer delays, use a software loop.
- [x] GATE = 1 allows **hardware control** (INT0/INT1 pin) for pulse-width measurement applications.
- [x] The **C/T̄ bit** distinguishes between Timer (0, internal clock) and Counter (1, external pin pulses).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programming the Timers in Assembly Language and C
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT404 - MICROCONTROLLER PROGRAMMING & INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Programming the Timers in Assembly Language and C

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the 8051 Microcontroller, **Timers** are dedicated hardware on-chip peripherals used to generate precise time delays, count external events, or produce baud-rate clocks for the serial port. Programming the timers involves configuring the **Timer Mode Register (TMOD)** and **Timer Control Register (TCON)**, loading the appropriate 13-bit, 16-bit, or 8-bit initial count values into the timer registers (**TH0, TL0, TH1, TL1**), and managing the overflow via polling the **TFx (Timer Flag)** bits or using interrupts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The 8051 contains **two 16-bit timers/counters** — **Timer 0** and **Timer 1** — that can be programmed independently. Each timer can operate in four modes: **Mode 0 (13-bit), Mode 1 (16-bit), Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload), and Mode 3 (Split Timer)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are boiling an egg. You have two options:
- **Option A (Polling):** You keep staring at the clock, checking the seconds hand again and again. The moment it points to "180 seconds," you stop the stove. → This is **Polling-based Timer Programming** (CPU keeps checking `TF0` or `TF1`).
- **Option B (Interrupt):** You set a kitchen alarm to 3 minutes and go read a book. When the alarm rings, you stop the stove. → This is **Interrupt-based Timer Programming** (CPU is freed to do other work).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight for Students:** The timer is essentially a counter that increments on every machine cycle (1.085 μs for an 11.0592 MHz crystal). When it overflows from its maximum value (`FFFFH + 1 = 0000H`), it sets a flag bit, which can be detected by software (polling) or trigger an interrupt.

### 1.3 The Timer Hardware Registers (Quick Map)

| Register | Function | Address (SFR) | Bit-Addressable? |
|----------|----------|---------------|-----------------|
| **TMOD** | Timer Mode selection (8 bits, no bit-addressable) | **89H** | ❌ No |
| **TCON** | Timer Control (Start/Stop + Flags), 8 bits | **88H** | ✅ Yes |
| **TL0** | Timer 0 Low byte | **8AH** | ❌ No |
| **TH0** | Timer 0 High byte | **8CH** | ❌ No |
| **TL1** | Timer 1 Low byte | **8BH** | ❌ No |
| **TH1** | Timer 1 High byte | **8DH** | ❌ No |

### 1.4 The Standard Crystal Frequency in KTU Labs

> [!NOTE]
> **The Standard KTU Lab Crystal Frequency = 11.0592 MHz**
> Why 11.0592 MHz? Because it is exactly **12 × 9600**, which makes it ideal for generating standard **baud rates** (9600, 4800, 2400) for serial communication with **zero error**.

The resulting **Machine Cycle Frequency (f_mc)** and **Machine Cycle Period (T_mc)** are:

$$f_{mc} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12} = \frac{11.0592 \text{ MHz}}{12} = \boxed{921.6 \text{ kHz}}$$

$$T_{mc} = \frac{1}{f_{mc}} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = \boxed{1.085 \, \mu s}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 TMOD Register — Timer Mode Register (Address 89H, Not Bit-Addressable)

```
  TMOD (89H) — Reset Value: 0000 0000
  ┌─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┐
  │ GATE│ C/T │  M1 │  M0 │ GATE│ C/T │  M1 │  M0 │
  └─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┘
     ◄── Timer 1 ──►   ◄── Timer 0 ──►
```

| Bit | Name | Function |
|-----|------|----------|
| **GATE** | Gating Control | **1** = Timer starts ONLY when `INTx` pin is HIGH and `TRx` is set (hardware control). **0** = Timer starts when `TRx` is set (software control). |
| **C/T̄** | Counter/Timer Select | **1** = **Counter** mode (counts external pulses on **T0** or **T1** pin). **0** = **Timer** mode (counts internal machine cycles). |
| **M1, M0** | Mode Bits | **00** = Mode 0 (13-bit). **01** = Mode 1 (16-bit). **10** = Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload). **11** = Mode 3 (Split Timer). |

### 2.2 TCON Register — Timer Control Register (Address 88H, Bit-Addressable)

```
  TCON (88H)
  Bit:    TF1   TR1   TF0   TR0   IE1   IT1   IE0   IT0
          8FH   8EH   8DH   8CH   8BH   8AH   89H   88H
```

| Bit | Name | Function |
|-----|------|----------|
| **TF1** | Timer 1 Overflow Flag | Set by hardware when Timer 1 overflows. Must be cleared by software. |
| **TR1** | Timer 1 Run Control | **1** = Start Timer 1. **0** = Stop Timer 1. |
| **TF0** | Timer 0 Overflow Flag | Set by hardware when Timer 0 overflows. |
| **TR0** | Timer 0 Run Control | **1** = Start Timer 0. **0** = Stop Timer 0. |
| IE1, IT1, IE0, IT0 | External Interrupt Control | Not directly used in basic timer programming. |

### 2.3 Timer Modes — KTU High-Yield Summary

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Modes:** Mode 1 and Mode 2 are the **most tested**. Mode 0 is for legacy 8048 compatibility. Mode 3 is rarely used.

| Mode | M1 M0 | Description | Max Count | Register Width |
|------|-------|-------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Mode 0** | 0 0 | 13-bit Timer (8048 compat) | 8192 (2¹³) | TH (8 bits) + TL (5 bits) |
| **Mode 1** | 0 1 | **16-bit Timer** (Most common) | 65536 (2¹⁶) | TH (8 bits) + TL (8 bits) |
| **Mode 2** | 1 0 | **8-bit Auto-Reload** | 256 (2⁸) | TL (8 bits) + TH (reload value) |
| **Mode 3** | 1 1 | Split Timer (Timer 0 only) | 256 each | TL0 + TH0 as two 8-bit |

### 2.4 Mode 2 — Auto-Reload (A KTU Hot Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **Mode 2**, the **THx register holds the reload value**. When **TLx overflows from FFH → 00H**, hardware automatically copies the THx value into TLx. This gives a **perfect, glitch-free, repetitive delay** without software reloading — used commonly in **baud-rate generation** for the serial port.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table contains **every formula you need** for KTU timer problems. **Memorize** this.

| Parameter | Formula | Notes |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| **Machine Cycle Frequency** | $f_{mc} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12}$ | For 11.0592 MHz → **921.6 kHz** |
| **Machine Cycle Period** | $T_{mc} = \dfrac{1}{f_{mc}} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | For 11.0592 MHz → **1.085 μs** |
| **Timer Tick Rate** | 1 tick per machine cycle | 1 tick = **1.085 μs** |
| **Delay Generated** | $\Delta t = N \cdot T_{mc}$ | N = number of timer ticks before overflow |
| **Total Delay (with reloads)** | $\Delta t_{total} = (\text{count\_max} - \text{TMR\_initial} + 1) \cdot T_{mc}$ | For **single overflow** |
| **Auto-Reload Mode 2 Delay** | $\Delta t = (256 - \text{THx}) \cdot T_{mc}$ | Repeats automatically |
| **Initial Timer Value (16-bit)** | $\text{TMR} = 2^{16} - \Delta t_{desired} \cdot f_{mc}$ | Hex value to load in THx:TLx |
| **Initial Timer Value (8-bit)** | $\text{TMR} = 2^{8} - \Delta t_{desired} \cdot f_{mc}$ | For Mode 0, 2, 3 |
| **Counter Mode (External)** | $\text{Counted Events} = \text{count\_max} - \text{TMR\_initial} + 1$ | Counts edges on T0/T1 pin |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Industrial:** Generating precise time delays for relay/actuator control in **PLC-like systems**.
- **Automotive:** Engine control unit (ECU) — **shaft position sensing** using counters.
- **Telecommunications:** **Baud-rate generation** for UART — Timer 1 in Mode 2 is standard.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Washing machine cycle timing, microwave oven delays.
- **Medical:** Infusion pump drop counting using **counter mode**.
- **IoT/Embedded:** Heart-rate pulse width measurement using **Gate-controlled** timer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Master Derivation: Computing the Timer Reload Value

This is the **#1 derivation** every KTU student must master. Given a desired delay and a crystal frequency, find the THx:TLx value.

**Given:**
- Crystal frequency $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$
- Desired delay $\Delta t$
- Timer Mode 1 (16-bit, max count = 65536)

**Step 1: Calculate machine cycle period**

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6}$$

$$T_{mc} = 1.085 \, \mu s \text{ (per timer tick)}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the required number of timer ticks (N)**

$$N = \frac{\Delta t}{T_{mc}} = \frac{\Delta t \cdot f_{osc}}{12}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the initial timer value**

Since the timer **counts UP** from the initial value to overflow, and overflow happens at 65536 in Mode 1:

$$\text{TMR}_{\text{initial}} = 2^{16} - N = 65536 - N$$

**Step 4: Convert to THx and TLx bytes**

$$\text{THx} = \frac{\text{TMR}_{\text{initial}}}{256} \quad \text{(higher 8 bits)}$$

$$\text{TLx} = \text{TMR}_{\text{initial}} \mod 256 \quad \text{(lower 8 bits)}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked-Out Example 1 (KTU Standard — Mode 1)

**Problem:** Generate a delay of exactly **50 ms** using **Timer 0, Mode 1**, with an 11.0592 MHz crystal.

**Step 1: Compute T_mc**

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085 \, \mu s$$

**Step 2: Compute N (ticks needed)**

$$N = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 46080 \text{ ticks}$$

**Step 3: Compute initial value**

$$\text{TMR}_{\text{initial}} = 65536 - 46080 = 19456$$

**Step 4: Convert 19456 to Hex**

$$19456 \div 256 = 76 \text{ remainder } 0$$

$$19456_{10} = 4C00_H$$

**Step 5: Assign to registers**

$$\text{TH0} = 4C_H = 76_{10}, \quad \text{TL0} = 00_H = 0_{10}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** $(65536 - 19456) \times 1.085 \, \mu s = 46080 \times 1.085 \, \mu s = 49.9968 \, ms \approx 50 \, ms$ ✓

### 3.3 Worked-Out Example 2 (Mode 2 — Auto-Reload)

**Problem:** Generate a continuous delay of **200 μs** using **Timer 1, Mode 2**, with 11.0592 MHz crystal.

**Step 1: Compute N**

$$N = \frac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 184 \text{ ticks}$$

**Step 2: Compute initial value (8-bit)**

$$\text{TH1} = 2^8 - N = 256 - 184 = 72_{10}$$

$$72_{10} = 48_H$$

**Step 3: Load both TH1 and TL1 with 48H**

```assembly
MOV TH1, #48H
MOV TL1, #48H
```

> [!NOTE]
> In Mode 2, both THx and TLx are loaded with the **same reload value**. The hardware auto-copies THx → TLx on every overflow.

### 3.4 Worked-Out Example 3 (Multi-Loop Delay — KTU Classic)

**Problem:** Generate a delay of **1 second** using Timer 0, Mode 1, 11.0592 MHz. A single 16-bit overflow gives only ~71 ms max, so we need **multiple loops**.

**Step 1: Maximum single delay in Mode 1**

$$\Delta t_{max} = 65536 \times 1.085 \, \mu s = 71.11 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2: Required number of overflow iterations**

$$n = \frac{1000 \text{ ms}}{71.11 \text{ ms}} \approx 14$$

Let's choose a known comfortable value. For 50 ms per loop (from Example 1):

$$n = \frac{1000 \text{ ms}}{50 \text{ ms}} = 20 \text{ loops}$$

**Step 3: Timer registers for 50 ms**

$$\text{TH0} = 4C_H, \quad \text{TL0} = 00_H$$

**Step 4: Register R7 used as a counter = 20 = 14H**

This delay subroutine will execute `TH0:TL0` reload → wait for TF0 → 20 times.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Assembly Language Timer Programming

The standard 3-step recipe is:

| Step | Action | Why? |
|------|--------|------|
| **1. Load TMOD** | `MOV TMOD, #mode_value` | Selects timer, mode, and C/T̄ |
| **2. Load Timer** | `MOV THx, #high_byte` / `MOV TLx, #low_byte` | Sets initial count value |
| **3. Start & Wait** | `SETB TRx` → wait for `JNB TFx, $` → `CLR TFx` → `CLR TRx` | Triggers counting, polls for overflow |

### 3.6 C Language Timer Programming — The 8051-Specific Approach

Unlike standard ANSI C, the 8051's SFRs are accessed through the `<reg51.h>` (Keil) or `<mcs51/8051.h>` header. The compiler maps SFR names to internal memory locations.

**Header File Mechanism:**

```c
#include <reg51.h>   // Keil Compiler
sfr TMOD = 0x89;
sfr TCON = 0x88;
sfr TL0  = 0x8A;
sfr TH0  = 0x8C;
// ... and so on
```

This is why the **sfr keyword** and the **predefined header** are crucial — without them, the compiler treats TMOD, TCON, etc. as undefined symbols.

> [!NOTE]
> **The exact same C program in standard 8051 and in 8052 (with 6 timers) is NOT portable** — this is a key limitation. To make code portable, use `#define` macros for the timer registers.

### 3.7 Comparison Matrix — Assembly vs. C Timer Programming

| Feature | Assembly Language | C Language |
|---------|-------------------|------------|
| **SFR Access** | Direct via `MOV 0x89, #01H` or `MOV TMOD, #01H` | Via `sfr` keyword or `<reg51.h>` |
| **Bit Manipulation** | Native via `SETB`/`CLR`/`JB`/`JNB` | Requires `<regx51.h>` for `sbit` |
| **Code Size** | Smaller (~30–40% less) | Larger (compiler overhead) |
| **Readability** | Poor for complex logic | Excellent, high-level abstraction |
| **Execution Speed** | Faster (predictable) | Slightly slower (function-call overhead) |
| **Portability** | None (CPU-specific mnemonics) | Limited (still 8051-specific) |
| **Debugging** | Difficult (no symbolic names) | Easier with variable names |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | **Very High** | **High** |

### 3.8 Step-by-Step: 50 ms Delay in Both Languages

**Assembly Language (Mode 1, Timer 0, Polling):**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   MOV TMOD, #01H      ; Timer 0, Mode 1, Software Control, Timer Mode
        MOV TH0, #4CH       ; High byte of 19456 (4C00H)
        MOV TL0, #00H       ; Low byte
HERE:   SETB TR0             ; Start Timer 0
AGAIN:  JNB TF0, AGAIN      ; Wait for overflow flag
        CLR TR0              ; Stop timer
        CLR TF0              ; Clear overflow flag (must be done in software)
        RET
```

**C Language (Keil Compiler):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void timer0_delay_50ms(void) {
    TMOD = 0x01;    // Timer 0, Mode 1
    TH0 = 0x4C;     // High byte
    TL0 = 0x00;     // Low byte
    TR0 = 1;        // Start Timer 0
    while (TF0 == 0);  // Poll until overflow flag is set
    TR0 = 0;        // Stop timer
    TF0 = 0;        // Clear overflow flag
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** In C, you **must explicitly clear TF0** (`TF0 = 0;`) because unlike `TR0`, the overflow flag is **NOT auto-cleared** on entering the ISR in polling mode (only auto-cleared when the corresponding interrupt vector is taken, and even then only in some derivatives).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TMOD Register Bit Map

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeTMOD["TMOD Register Address 89H Reset Value 00H"]
    nodeGATE1["GATE1 Bit 7 Hardware control for Timer 1"]
    nodeCT1["C T bar 1 Bit 6 Counter or Timer mode for Timer 1"]
    nodeM11["M1 Bit 5 Mode select MSB Timer 1"]
    nodeM01["M0 Bit 4 Mode select LSB Timer 1"]
    nodeGATE0["GATE0 Bit 3 Hardware control for Timer 0"]
    nodeCT0["C T bar 0 Bit 2 Counter or Timer mode for Timer 0"]
    nodeM10["M1 Bit 1 Mode select MSB Timer 0"]
    nodeM00["M0 Bit 0 Mode select LSB Timer 0"]

    nodeTMOD --- nodeGATE1
    nodeTMOD --- nodeCT1
    nodeTMOD --- nodeM11
    nodeTMOD --- nodeM01
    nodeTMOD --- nodeGATE0
    nodeTMOD --- nodeCT0
    nodeTMOD --- nodeM10
    nodeTMOD --- nodeM00

    nodeM11 -.-> nodeM0x["00 equals Mode 0 13-bit, 01 equals Mode 1 16-bit, 10 equals Mode 2 Auto-Reload, 11 equals Mode 3 Split"]
    nodeM10 -.-> nodeM0x
    nodeM01 -.-> nodeM0x
    nodeM00 -.-> nodeM0x
```

### 4.2 Timer Operation Block Diagram (8051 Internals)

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeOSC["Crystal Oscillator f_osc equals 11.0592 MHz"]
    nodeDIV12["Divide by 12 Prescaler"]
    nodeMACHINE["Machine Cycle Clock 1.085 us per tick"]
    nodeSEL["MUX Controlled by C T bar bit"]
    nodeINTERNAL["Internal Machine Cycle Pulses"]
    nodeEXTERNAL["External Pin T0 or T1"]
    nodeREGBANK["Timer Register Bank"]
    nodeTL0["TL0 8-bit Lower Byte"]
    nodeTH0["TH0 8-bit Higher Byte"]
    nodeINCR["16-bit Up Counter Adder"]
    nodeOVFLW["Overflow Detector"]
    nodeFLAG["Sets TF0 or TF1 Flag in TCON"]
    nodeINT["Triggers Interrupt if Enabled"]

    nodeOSC --> nodeDIV12
    nodeDIV12 --> nodeMACHINE
    nodeMACHINE --> nodeINTERNAL
    nodeEXTERNAL --> nodeSEL
    nodeINTERNAL --> nodeSEL
    nodeSEL --> nodeREGBANK
    nodeREGBANK --> nodeTL0
    nodeREGBANK --> nodeTH0
    nodeTL0 --> nodeINCR
    nodeTH0 --> nodeINCR
    nodeINCR --> nodeOVFLW
    nodeOVFLW --> nodeFLAG
    nodeFLAG --> nodeINT
```

### 4.3 Timer Programming Workflow (Flowchart)

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA["Start: Configure Timer"]
    stepB["Load TMOD with mode value"]
    stepC["Load THx with high byte"]
    stepD["Load TLx with low byte"]
    stepE["Set TRx equals 1 to start timer"]
    stepF["Decision: Has TFx overflowed?"]
    stepG["Yes path: Clear TFx and TRx"]
    stepH["Exit: Delay complete"]
    stepI["No path: Keep polling TFx"]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepI
    stepI --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
```

### 4.4 Mode 2 Auto-Reload Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeTLX["TLx 8-bit Counter"]
    nodeTHX["THx 8-bit Reload Register"]
    nodeTICK["Machine Cycle Tick Input"]
    nodeOVFLW["Overflow Detection FFH to 00H"]
    nodeAUTOCOPY["Hardware Auto Copy on Overflow"]
    nodeFLAG["Set TFx Flag"]

    nodeTICK --> nodeTLX
    nodeTLX --> nodeOVFLW
    nodeOVFLW --> nodeAUTOCOPY
    nodeAUTOCOPY --> nodeTLX
    nodeTHX -.-> nodeAUTOCOPY
    nodeOVFLW --> nodeFLAG
```

### 4.5 Memory-Mapped SFR Layout for Timers

```mermaid
graph LR
    node89["89H TMOD"]
    node88["88H TCON"]
    node8A["8AH TL0"]
    node8B["8BH TL1"]
    node8C["8CH TH0"]
    node8D["8DH TH1"]

    node88 --- node89
    node89 --- node8A
    node8A --- node8B
    node8B --- node8C
    node8C --- node8D

    nodeT0["Timer 0 Pair: TH0 plus TL0 = 16-bit counter"]
    nodeT1["Timer 1 Pair: TH1 plus TL1 = 16-bit counter"]
    nodeCTRL["TCON: Run plus Flag bits"]
    nodeMODE["TMOD: Mode plus C/T plus GATE"]

    node8A --- nodeT0
    node8C --- nodeT0
    node8B --- nodeT1
    node8D --- nodeT1
    node88 --- nodeCTRL
    node89 --- nodeMODE
```

### 4.6 Polling vs. Interrupt-Based Timer Programming

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph POLLING["Polling Method"]
        pA["CPU starts timer TRx equals 1"]
        pB["CPU enters wait loop JNB TFx dollar"]
        pC["TFx becomes 1"]
        pD["CPU clears TFx and TRx"]
        pE["CPU continues main task"]
        pA --> pB --> pC --> pD --> pE
    end

    subgraph INTERRUPT["Interrupt Method"]
        iA["CPU starts timer TRx equals 1"]
        iB["CPU executes main loop freely"]
        iC["Timer overflows TFx equals 1"]
        iD["Hardware vectors CPU to ISR at 000BH"]
        iE["ISR clears TFx automatically and executes handler"]
        iF["RETI returns to main loop"]
        iA --> iB --> iC --> iD --> iE --> iF
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**
Explain the function of the **TMOD** and **TCON** registers in the 8051 microcontroller. List the four timer modes.

**Model Answer:**

The **TMOD (Timer Mode Register, address 89H)** is an 8-bit, non-bit-addressable register used to configure the operating mode of Timer 0 and Timer 1. The upper nibble (bits 4–7) controls **Timer 1**, and the lower nibble (bits 0–3) controls **Timer 0**. Each nibble contains the **GATE, C/T̄, M1, and M0** bits for gating control, counter/timer selection, and mode selection.

The **TCON (Timer Control Register, address 88H)** is a bit-addressable register used to start/stop the timers and to monitor overflow flags. The important bits are **TR0, TR1** (run control) and **TF0, TF1** (overflow flags). TFx must be cleared by software.

**The four timer modes are:**
1. **Mode 0** — 13-bit timer (8048 compatibility mode)
2. **Mode 1** — 16-bit timer (most commonly used)
3. **Mode 2** — 8-bit auto-reload timer
4. **Mode 3** — Split timer mode (Timer 0 only)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**
Differentiate between **Timer mode** and **Counter mode** of the 8051. How is the selection made?

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Timer Mode | Counter Mode |
|-----------|------------|--------------|
| **C/T̄ bit in TMOD** | **0** | **1** |
| **Clock Source** | Internal machine cycle (f_osc / 12) | External pulse on **T0 (P3.4)** or **T1 (P3.5)** pin |
| **Use Case** | Generating time delays, baud rates | Counting external events (e.g., product count on conveyor) |
| **Increment Rate** | Every 1.085 μs (for 11.0592 MHz) | Every rising edge on T0/T1 pin |
| **Application Example** | Blinking an LED every 50 ms | Counting rotations of a wheel using an optocoupler |

The selection is made by setting the **C/T̄ bit** in the TMOD register: **0 for Timer**, **1 for Counter**. The external pin is sampled twice per machine cycle, so the maximum external count frequency is **f_osc / 24 ≈ 460.8 kHz** for 11.0592 MHz crystal.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the four operating modes of the 8051 timer with neat diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an **8051 Assembly Language program** to generate a square wave of **2 kHz** frequency on pin **P1.0** using **Timer 0, Mode 1**. Assume a crystal frequency of **11.0592 MHz**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Operating Modes of 8051 Timer:**

| Mode | M1 M0 | Description | Count Range | Applications |
|------|-------|-------------|-------------|--------------|
| **Mode 0** | 0 0 | 13-bit timer using 8 bits of THx and lower 5 bits of TLx | 0 to 8191 | Legacy 8048 compatibility |
| **Mode 1** | 0 1 | 16-bit timer using full THx and TLx | 0 to 65535 | Most common — general delays |
| **Mode 2** | 1 0 | 8-bit auto-reload — TLx counts; THx holds reload value | 0 to 255 | Baud-rate generation, repetitive delays |
| **Mode 3** | 1 1 | Split timer — Timer 0 splits into two independent 8-bit timers (TL0 and TH0). Timer 1 stops. | Two 0–255 counters | Specialized use only |

In **Mode 1**, both THx and TLx form a 16-bit counter. When the count rolls over from `FFFFH` to `0000H`, the TFx flag is set.

In **Mode 2**, only TLx is the actual counter. When TLx overflows (FFH → 00H), the value in THx is automatically copied into TLx, and TFx is set. This provides a glitch-free repetitive delay.

In **Mode 3**, Timer 0's TL0 and TH0 operate as two separate 8-bit timers, each with its own overflow flag (TF0 for TL0). Timer 1 in this mode cannot be used as a timer, but its SFRs can still be used to generate baud rates.

**(b) Square Wave Generation Program:**

**Step 1 — Compute Timer Reload Value:**

For a 2 kHz square wave:
- Period $T = \frac{1}{2000} = 500 \, \mu s$
- Half-period (HIGH or LOW time) $\Delta t = 250 \, \mu s$

Number of timer ticks:

$$N = \frac{250 \, \mu s}{1.085 \, \mu s} \approx 230 \text{ ticks}$$

Initial timer value:

$$\text{TMR} = 65536 - 230 = 65306$$

Convert 65306 to Hex:

$$65306 \div 256 = 255 \text{ remainder } 26$$

$$65306_{10} = FF1A_H$$

So $\text{TH0} = \text{FF}_H$ and $\text{TL0} = 1A_H$.

**Step 2 — Assembly Program:**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   MOV TMOD, #01H      ; Timer 0, Mode 1, software start, timer mode
LOOP:   MOV TH0, #0FFH      ; Load high byte
        MOV TL0, #01AH      ; Load low byte (1AH = 26 decimal)
        SETB P1.0            ; Make P1.0 HIGH
        ACALL DELAY          ; Wait half period (250 us)
        MOV TH0, #0FFH      ; Reload
        MOV TL0, #01AH
        CLR P1.0             ; Make P1.0 LOW
        ACALL DELAY          ; Wait another 250 us
        SJMP LOOP            ; Repeat forever

DELAY:  SETB TR0             ; Start Timer 0
WAIT:   JNB TF0, WAIT        ; Wait for overflow
        CLR TR0              ; Stop timer
        CLR TF0              ; Clear flag
        RET                  ; Return
        END
```

**Output Waveform on P1.0:**

```
   P1.0:  ┌─────┐       ┌─────┐       ┌─────┐
           \     \       \     \       \     \
            \     \       \     \       \     \
   _________/     \_______/     \_______/     \____
           ◄─250─►       ◄─250─►
   T = 500 us   →   f = 1/T = 1/500us = 2 kHz
```

---

#### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Timer/Counter logic** of the 8051 microcontroller. Discuss the role of the **GATE** and **C/T̄** bits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a **C program** for the 8051 to toggle all bits of **Port P2** every **500 ms** continuously using **Timer 1 in Mode 2**. Crystal frequency = **11.0592 MHz**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Timer/Counter Logic with GATE and C/T̄ Bits:**

The 8051 has two **16-bit Timer/Counter** blocks (Timer 0 and Timer 1). Each consists of two 8-bit registers: **THx (high byte)** and **TLx (low byte)**, which together form a 16-bit up-counter. The counter is incremented either by:
- **Internal machine cycles** (Timer mode), or
- **External pulses** on T0 (P3.4) / T1 (P3.5) (Counter mode).

The selection between these two sources is done via the **C/T̄ bit**:
- **C/T̄ = 0:** Counter is driven by the divided oscillator clock → **Timer mode**.
- **C/T̄ = 1:** Counter is driven by external pin → **Counter mode**.

The **GATE bit** controls hardware/software triggering:
- **GATE = 0:** Timer is started/stopped purely by software via the **TRx** bit in TCON.
- **GATE = 1:** Timer is enabled **only** when both the **TRx** bit is set **AND** the corresponding **INTx** pin (P3.2 for Timer 0, P3.3 for Timer 1) is HIGH. This allows **external hardware measurement** of pulse width.

When the counter overflows (e.g., from FFFFH to 0000H in Mode 1), the **TFx** flag in TCON is set, which can be polled by software or used to generate an interrupt.

**Block diagram of Timer 0 logic:**

```
   f_osc ──► ÷12 ──► Machine Cycle ──┐
                                       ├──► MUX ──► 16-bit Counter (TH0:TL0) ──► Overflow ──► TF0
   P3.4 (T0) ─────────────────────────┘
                                       ▲
                                  Controlled by C/T̄ bit

   Start/Stop logic:
   TR0 ──┐
         ├──► AND gate ──► Enables counter
   INT0 (P3.2) ──┘
         ▲
   Controlled by GATE bit
```

**(b) C Program — Toggle P2 Every 500 ms Using Timer 1, Mode 2:**

**Step 1 — Compute the Timer Reload Value:**

For 500 ms delay, a single 8-bit overflow (max = 256 × 1.085 μs ≈ 278 μs) is not enough. We need **multiple overflows**.

Let's design a single overflow of **250 μs** (close to max). Number of overflows:

$$n = \frac{500 \text{ ms}}{250 \, \mu s} = 2000 \text{ overflows}$$

For 250 μs:

$$N = \frac{250 \, \mu s}{1.085 \, \mu s} \approx 230 \text{ ticks}$$

$$\text{TH1} = \text{TL1} = 256 - 230 = 26 = 1A_H$$

**Step 2 — C Program:**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void timer1_mode2_delay_250us(void);
void delay_500ms(void);

void main(void) {
    while (1) {
        P2 = ~P2;        // Toggle all bits of P2
        delay_500ms();   // Wait 500 ms
    }
}

void timer1_mode2_delay_250us(void) {
    // Timer 1, Mode 2, Software Start, Timer Mode
    TMOD = (TMOD & 0x0F) | 0x20;   // Mask lower nibble (Timer 0), set Timer 1 to Mode 2
    TH1 = 0x1A;
    TL1 = 0x1A;
    TR1 = 1;                        // Start Timer 1
    while (TF1 == 0);               // Wait for overflow
    TR1 = 0;                        // Stop timer
    TF1 = 0;                        // Clear overflow flag
}

void delay_500ms(void) {
    unsigned int i;
    for (i = 0; i < 2000; i++) {
        timer1_mode2_delay_250us();
    }
}
```

**Key Points in the C Program:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. `TMOD = (TMOD & 0x0F) | 0x20;` — This **preserves Timer 0's TMOD setting** and only modifies Timer 1's nibble to Mode 2. A naive `TMOD = 0x20;` would **destroy Timer 0's configuration**.
> 2. `<reg51.h>` defines the SFR names — without it, the code will not compile in Keil.
> 3. The `while (TF1 == 0);` is the polling loop — the CPU is **blocked** until 250 μs elapses.
> 4. `P2 = ~P2;` toggles all 8 bits — the bitwise NOT `~` operator in C inverts all bits.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Writing `TMOD = 0x20;` instead of `TMOD = (TMOD & 0x0F) | 0x20;`. This **overwrites Timer 0's mode** — **deduct 1 mark**.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to **clear the TFx flag** after detecting it. If the flag is not cleared, the next iteration of the polling loop will exit immediately, giving a much shorter delay than expected. **Deduct 1 mark**.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Using `65536 - N` instead of `2^16 - N` or using the **wrong base** for Mode 1 (16-bit) vs Mode 2 (8-bit). For 16-bit mode, max is 65536; for 8-bit mode, max is 256. **Deduct 2 marks** if you use the wrong base — this is a **fatal error**.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting to **set the GATE bit = 0** in TMOD for software-controlled starting. If GATE = 1, the timer will NOT start even if TR0 = 1, because it also requires INT0 pin to be HIGH. **Deduct 1 mark**.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** In C code, using `P1 == 0xFF` instead of `P1 = P1 ^ 0xFF;` or `P1 = ~P1;` for toggling. **Deduct 1 mark** for logic error.

---

### 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — 10 Bullets You MUST Know:**

- ✅ **8051 has TWO timers** (Timer 0 and Timer 1), each **16-bit wide**. They can operate **independently** in 4 modes.
- ✅ **TMOD is at address 89H**, is **not bit-addressable**, and must be loaded as a whole byte. Upper nibble = Timer 1, lower nibble = Timer 0.
- ✅ **TCON is at address 88H**, is **bit-addressable**. `TRx` = Run bit, `TFx` = Overflow flag bit.
- ✅ **Mode 1 (16-bit)** is the most common — max delay per overflow = **65.536 ms** (at 11.0592 MHz). For longer delays, use **loop counter**.
- ✅ **Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload)** is used for **baud-rate generation** — only **THx holds the reload value**, hardware auto-copies to TLx on overflow.
- ✅ **Mode 3** splits Timer 0 into two 8-bit timers (TL0 and TH0) and **stops Timer 1**.
- ✅ **C/T̄ = 0** → Timer (internal clock). **C/T̄ = 1** → Counter (external pin T0/P3.4 or T1/P3.5).
- ✅ **GATE = 0** → Software start (TRx). **GATE = 1** → Hardware start (TRx AND INTx pin HIGH).
- ✅ **TFx flag must be cleared by software** in polling mode — it is NOT auto-cleared unless the interrupt vector is taken.
- ✅ **Crystal Frequency of 11.0592 MHz** gives **T_mc = 1.085 μs** — used because 11.0592 = 12 × 9600, enabling zero-error UART baud rates.
- ✅ **The Master Formula:** $\text{TMR}_{\text{initial}} = 2^{(\text{mode bits})} - \frac{\Delta t \cdot f_{osc}}{12}$, then split into high and low bytes.
- ✅ **In C**, use `<reg51.h>` (Keil) and **always mask TMOD** when changing one timer: `TMOD = (TMOD & 0x0F) | new_value;` to avoid clobbering the other timer's settings.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Timer as an Event Counter, Serial Communication with 8051: Introduction to Serial Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT404 - MICROCONTROLLER PROGRAMMING & INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Timer as an Event Counter, Serial Communication with 8051: Introduction to Serial Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Timer as an Event Counter — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> In the 8051 microcontroller, each of the two on-chip **16-bit timers (Timer 0 and Timer 1)** can be configured to operate in one of two distinct functional modes by manipulating the **C/T (Counter/Timer) bit** located in the **TMOD (Timer Mode) register**. When `C/T = 0`, the timer increments once every **machine cycle** (internal oscillator ÷ 12). When `C/T = 1`, the timer operates as an **Event Counter**, incrementing its register pair (THx:TLx) in response to a **1-to-0 transition (falling edge)** on a specific external pin — **T0 (P3.4)** for Timer 0 and **T1 (P3.5)** for Timer 1.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — "The Toll Booth Counter"

Imagine a **highway toll booth** with a manual clicker:
- In **Timer mode** (`C/T = 0`), the clicker advances automatically every fixed time interval (say, every second), like a stopwatch. You don't care about cars — you're measuring **time**.
- In **Event Counter mode** (`C/T = 1`), the clicker advances **only when a car physically passes through** the booth sensor. The internal clock is completely ignored — the **external event itself is the trigger**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The external pin must be held HIGH for at least **1 machine cycle** and then LOW for at least **1 machine cycle** to guarantee that the falling edge is recognized by the counter hardware. This is a common viva question.

---

## 1.2 Serial Communication with 8051 — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> **Serial communication** is the process of transmitting or receiving data **one bit at a time, sequentially, over a single communication line** (or a pair of lines), as opposed to parallel communication where multiple bits travel simultaneously on multiple lines. The 8051 contains a **full-duplex UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter)** that can simultaneously transmit and receive data, primarily supporting **asynchronous serial communication** with programmable baud rate and frame format.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — "The Walkie-Talkie vs. The Telephone"

- **Parallel communication** = 8 friends carrying 8 separate letters on 8 lanes of a highway → fast but expensive in "wires."
- **Serial communication** = 1 friend carrying all 8 letters one-by-one on a single-lane road → slow per message but uses **only 1 wire** — perfect for long-distance, cost-effective transmission.
- The 8051's **UART** is the "language translator" that converts the parallel 8-bit data inside the CPU into a sequential bit-stream (and vice versa) so it can travel over that single lane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Serial Matters in Embedded Systems:**
> Modern embedded systems rely on **UART, SPI, I²C, and CAN** — all serial protocols. The 8051's built-in UART is the foundation for interfacing **GPS modules, GSM modems, Bluetooth (HC-05), RFID readers, and PC serial ports (COM ports via MAX232)**.

---

## 1.3 Three Foundational Modes of Data Flow

| Mode | Description | Real-world Example |
|------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Simplex** | Data flows in **one direction only** | Radio broadcast, TV transmission |
| **Half-Duplex** | Data flows in **both directions, but not simultaneously** | Walkie-Talkie (CB radio) |
| **Full-Duplex** | Data flows in **both directions simultaneously** | Mobile phone, 8051's UART (TXD + RXD) |

> [!NOTE]
> The 8051's **on-chip UART is full-duplex** because it has **two separate physical pins — TXD (P3.1) and RXD (P3.0)** — for simultaneous transmit and receive.

---

## 1.4 Asynchronous vs. Synchronous Serial Communication

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Distinction:**

| Parameter | Asynchronous | Synchronous |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| **Clock line** | No shared clock; uses **agreed baud rate** | Has a **dedicated clock line** between Tx and Rx |
| **Framing** | Each byte wrapped with **START + DATA + STOP** bits | Continuous data stream synced to clock |
| **Speed** | Slower (overhead of start/stop bits) | Faster (no per-byte overhead) |
| **8051 Support** | **Modes 1 & 3 (8-bit & 9-bit UART)** — most common | **Mode 0 (8-bit Shift Register)** |
| **Use Case** | PC ↔ MCU, GPS, Bluetooth, GSM | Shift registers, multi-MCU sync |

> [!NOTE]
> **8051's 4 Serial Modes (controlled by SM0, SM1 in SCON):**
> - **Mode 0:** 8-bit Shift Register (synchronous, fixed baud = `fosc/12`)
> - **Mode 1:** 8-bit UART, variable baud (timer 1 overflow rate) ⭐ *most used*
> - **Mode 2:** 9-bit UART, fixed baud (`fosc/32` or `fosc/64`)
> - **Mode 3:** 9-bit UART, variable baud (timer 1 overflow rate)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the 8051 Timer/Counter

The 8051 has **two 16-bit timer/counter registers** that share the same core hardware but have independent control:

| Timer | High Byte | Low Byte | External Pin (Counter Mode) |
|-------|-----------|----------|------------------------------|
| **Timer 0** | `TH0` | `TL0` | **P3.4 (T0)** |
| **Timer 1** | `TH1` | `TL1` | **P3.5 (T1)** |

### 2.1.1 The TMOD Register (Timer Mode Register) — Non-bit-addressable

```
Bit:    7      6      5      4      3      2      1      0
       GATE1  C/T1   T1M1   T1M0   GATE0  C/T0   T0M1   T0M0
       \_________________________/      \_________________________/
              Timer 1 bits                   Timer 0 bits
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Bit — `C/T` (Counter/Timer Select):**
> - **`C/T = 0` → Timer Mode:** Increments from internal oscillator (`fosc/12`).
> - **`C/T = 1` → Counter Mode:** Increments from external pin (T0 or T1) on **falling edge**.

**The `GATE` bit** provides hardware control: when `GATE = 1`, the timer is enabled **only when both TRx = 1 AND INTx pin = HIGH**. This is used for pulse-width measurement. When `GATE = 0` (typical for event counting), software control via `TRx` alone starts the counter.

### 2.1.2 The TCON Register (Timer Control Register) — Bit-addressable

| Bit | Name | Function |
|-----|------|----------|
| **TR0** | Timer 0 Run control | `1` = start counting, `0` = stop |
| **TR1** | Timer 1 Run control | `1` = start counting, `0` = stop |
| TF0 / TF1 | Timer Overflow Flag | Set to 1 when register rolls over from FFFFH → 0000H |

---

## 2.2 Event Counting — The Operating Principle

When `C/T = 1`, the **internal clock is decoupled**. The THx:TLx register pair now behaves as a **16-bit up-counter** that responds to **external physical events** (e.g., pulses from a sensor, encoder ticks, or button presses).

### Step-by-Step Logic Flow:

1. **Configure TMOD** — Set `C/T = 1`, select Mode 1 (16-bit counter).
2. **Load initial value** into `THx:TLx` (e.g., `0000H` to count up to 65536 events, or a value `N` to count exactly `(65536 − N)` events before overflow).
3. **Start the counter** by setting `TRx = 1` via software.
4. Each **falling edge** on the assigned pin (T0 or T1) increments the register pair by 1.
5. When the counter overflows from **`FFFFH → 0000H`**, the **TFx flag is set** (can trigger an interrupt if enabled).
6. Software polls `TFx` or uses the interrupt to read `THx:TLx` (the current event count).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Classic Viva Question:** *"In counter mode, does the timer increment on the rising or falling edge of the external pulse?"*
> **Answer:** On the **1-to-0 transition (falling edge)**, as per the original Intel 8051 datasheet.

### 2.2.1 Real-World Engineering Applications

- 🏭 **Industrial Production Lines** — Counting bottles, products on a conveyor belt (optical sensor → pulse → 8051 T0 pin).
- 🚗 **Automotive** — Reading wheel rotation encoders for speed/odometry.
- ⚡ **Energy Meters** — Counting pulses from a calibrated sensor (e.g., 1 pulse = 1 Wh).
- 🅿️ **Parking Systems** — Counting vehicle entry/exit.
- 📡 **Frequency Measurement** — Counting pulses over a fixed gate time.

---

## 2.3 Anatomy of 8051 Serial Communication (UART)

The 8051's **on-chip UART** consists of the following hardware blocks:

### 2.3.1 The SBUF Register (Serial Data Buffer)

> [!NOTE]
> **`SBUF` is physically two separate 8-bit registers** sharing the same memory address (`99H`):
> - **Writing to SBUF** → loads the **Transmit register** (data to be sent out).
> - **Reading from SBUF** → accesses the **Receive register** (data just received).
>
> This clever dual-register design allows **full-duplex** operation without conflict.

### 2.3.2 The SCON Register (Serial Control Register) — Bit-addressable at `98H`

```
Bit:    7      6      5      4      3      2      1      0
       SM0    SM1    SM2    REN    TB8    RB8    TI     RI
```

| Bit | Name | Function |
|-----|------|----------|
| **SM0, SM1** | Serial Mode select | `00`=Mode 0, `01`=Mode 1, `10`=Mode 2, `11`=Mode 3 |
| **SM2** | Multiprocessor comm. enable | Used in Modes 2 & 3 |
| **REN** | Receive Enable | `1` = enable serial reception, must be set by software |
| **TB8** | 9th transmitted bit | Used in Modes 2 & 3 |
| **RB8** | 9th received bit | Used in Modes 2 & 3 |
| **TI** | Transmit Interrupt flag | Set by hardware at end of byte transmission; **must be cleared by software** |
| **RI** | Receive Interrupt flag | Set by hardware at end of byte reception; **must be cleared by software** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Forgetting to **clear TI and RI in software** after handling. The 8051 **does NOT auto-clear** these flags, leading to repeated interrupts or missed checks. This is a top reason for losing marks in the assembly programming part.

### 2.3.3 The PCON Register (Power Control Register) — Non-bit-addressable at `87H`

| Bit | Name | Function |
|-----|------|----------|
| **SMOD** | Serial Baud rate Doubler | When `SMOD = 1`, baud rate in Modes 1, 2, 3 is **doubled** |
| — | Other bits | Power-saving modes (idle, power-down) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SMOD bit directly affects baud rate calculation in Mode 1** — a critical KTU formula item (see Section 3).

---

## 2.4 The Serial Frame — Anatomy of a UART Data Packet (Mode 1)

When the 8051 transmits a byte in **Mode 1 (8-bit UART)**, the line (TXD) carries the following 10-bit frame:

```
Idle (1) | START (0) | D0 | D1 | D2 | D3 | D4 | D5 | D6 | D7 | STOP (1) | Idle (1)...
```

| Field | Width | Purpose |
|-------|-------|---------|
| **START bit** | 1 bit | Always `0` — signals beginning of frame; **line idle is HIGH** |
| **DATA bits** | 8 bits | LSB (D0) sent first, MSB (D7) last |
| **STOP bit** | 1 bit | Always `1` — signals end of frame |
| **Total frame** | 10 bits | Used in baud-rate timing |

> [!NOTE]
> The **START bit falling edge** is what wakes the receiver's sampling logic. This is why UART is "asynchronous" — there is no shared clock; both sides must agree on the **baud rate** (bits per second) and the receiver samples each bit at its center (typically at the 16th sub-clock of each bit period in Mode 1).

---

## 2.5 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Unit / Notes |
|---|-------------------|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Events to Overflow** $= 2^{16} - N_{\text{initial}}$ | For 16-bit counter (Mode 1), with initial value $N$ loaded into `THx:TLx` | $N$ in decimal, result in **events** |
| 2 | **Baud Rate (Mode 1)** $=\dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$ | Standard UART mode; **SMOD = 0 or 1** | **bits/second** |
| 3 | **Simplified Baud (Mode 1, SMOD=0)** $=\dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$ | When **SMOD = 0** | **bits/second** |
| 4 | **Simplified Baud (Mode 1, SMOD=1)** $=\dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{192 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$ | When **SMOD = 1** (baud doubled) | **bits/second** |
| 5 | **Reload Value (TH1)** $= 256 - \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times \text{Baud}}$ | Inverse formula to find **TH1** for desired baud (SMOD=0) | Decimal integer for **TH1** |
| 6 | **Reload Value (TH1, SMOD=1)** $= 256 - \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{192 \times \text{Baud}}$ | Inverse formula with **SMOD=1** | Decimal integer for **TH1** |
| 7 | **Baud Rate (Mode 0)** $= f_{\text{osc}} \div 12$ | Fixed; **synchronous shift register** mode | **bits/second** |
| 8 | **Baud Rate (Mode 2)** $= f_{\text{osc}} \div 32$ (SMOD=1) or $\div 64$ (SMOD=0) | Fixed, 9-bit UART | **bits/second** |
| 9 | **Total bits per frame (Mode 1)** = 10 | 1 START + 8 DATA + 1 STOP | bits |
| 10 | **Max count (16-bit)** $= 2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | From `0000H` to `FFFFH` | events |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *"To find TH1, Subtract from 256"* — For Mode 1, always compute `TH1 = 256 - (oscillator constant / baud)`.

---

## 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

- 🔬 **Lab Instruments** — Oscilloscopes and logic analyzers communicate with PC over UART.
- 📱 **IoT Devices** — Wi-Fi/BLE modules (ESP8266, HC-05) take AT commands via UART.
- 🛰️ **GPS Receivers** — Output NMEA sentences at 9600 baud over UART.
- 🖨️ **Embedded Printers** — Receive data byte-by-byte via UART.
- 🚁 **Drone Flight Controllers** — Telemetry downlinks use UART at high baud (115200+).
