# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Events Counted Before Overflow

### Problem Setup
A 16-bit counter is loaded with initial value $N$ (placed in `THx:TLx`). The counter increments once per external event. We want to determine how many events $E$ cause an overflow (transition from `FFFFH` to `0000H`).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recognize the maximum value of a 16-bit register:**

$$N_{\max} = 2^{16} - 1 = 65535 = \text{FFFF}_H$$

**Step 2 — If we start at $N$, the number of steps to reach `FFFFH`:**

$$E_{\text{to max}} = N_{\max} - N = (65535 - N)$$

**Step 3 — Adding 1 more event triggers the overflow from `FFFFH` to `0000H`:**

$$E_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{to max}} + 1 = (65535 - N) + 1$$

$$E_{\text{total}} = 65536 - N = 2^{16} - N$$

**Step 4 — Final compact formula:**

$$\boxed{E_{\text{events}} = 2^{16} - N_{\text{initial}}}$$

> **Textual explanation:** The 16-bit register cycles through all $2^{16}$ states. If you preload it with $N$, you've already consumed $N$ of those states, leaving $2^{16} - N$ states remaining before the next roll-over. The $TF$ flag is set at the moment the counter rolls from `FFFFH` to `0000H`.

### Numerical Example
Load `TH0 = TL0 = 0xFC` (i.e., $N = 0xFCFC = 64{,}764$).

$$E = 65536 - 64764 = 772 \text{ events}$$

Thus, after exactly **772 falling edges** on pin T0 (P3.4), the counter overflows and `TF0 = 1`.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Baud Rate of 8051 Mode 1 (Standard UART)

### Background Facts (must be memorized)

> [!NOTE]
> - The 8051 uses **Timer 1 in Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)** to generate the baud rate clock.
> - The timer overflow rate (rolls over from `TH1` to `0x00` and reloads `TH1`) directly sets the baud clock.
> - The UART divides the timer overflow rate by **32** (in Mode 1) — and by an additional factor of 2 if `SMOD = 0`, no division if `SMOD = 1`.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Timer 1 Overflow Frequency:**

Timer 1 is in **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)**. It increments every machine cycle. The machine cycle frequency is:

$$f_{\text{mc}} = \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12}$$

**Step 2 — The 8-bit timer overflows every $K$ machine cycles, where:**

$$K = 256 - \text{TH1}_{\text{decimal}}$$

So the overflow frequency is:

$$f_{\text{overflow}} = \frac{f_{\text{mc}}}{K} = \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

**Step 3 — The UART divides this overflow rate by 32:**

$$f_{\text{baud (SMOD=0)}} = \frac{f_{\text{overflow}}}{32} = \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

**Step 4 — The SMOD bit, when set to 1, doubles the baud rate:**

$$f_{\text{baud (SMOD=1)}} = 2 \times f_{\text{baud (SMOD=0)}}$$

**Step 5 — Combined final formula:**

$$\boxed{\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}}$$

### Numerical Worked Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classic KTU Problem:** *Find TH1 value for 9600 baud communication with 11.0592 MHz crystal, SMOD = 0.*

Given: $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz} = 11{,}059{,}200 \text{ Hz}$, Baud $= 9600$, SMOD $= 0$.

**Step 1 — Use the inverse formula:**

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times \text{Baud}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times 9600}$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator:**

$$384 \times 9600 = 3{,}686{,}400$$

**Step 4 — Compute division:**

$$\frac{11{,}059{,}200}{3{,}686{,}400} = 3$$

**Step 5 — Final result:**

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - 3 = 253 = \text{FD}_H$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the strange 11.0592 MHz crystal?** It is chosen so that 9600, 4800, 2400, 1200 baud values all yield **integer TH1 values with zero error** — a fact that has made this crystal the de facto standard for 8051 serial communication projects.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Event Counter vs. Timer Mode

| Parameter | Timer Mode (C/T = 0) | Event Counter Mode (C/T = 1) |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| **Clock source** | Internal oscillator (÷ 12) | External pin (T0 = P3.4, T1 = P3.5) |
| **Trigger** | Automatic every machine cycle | Manual — on **falling edge** of external pulse |
| **Counts what?** | Time intervals | **Discrete external events** (pulses) |
| **Application** | Delays, time measurement, baud generation | Counting objects, frequency, encoder pulses |
| **Max count (Mode 1)** | 65,536 machine cycles | 65,536 events |
| **Initial value** | Determines delay/event count | Determines event count before overflow |
| **TF flag behavior** | Same — sets on overflow | Same — sets on overflow |
| **External pin required** | Not used | **Mandatory** — must be tied HIGH/LOW to count |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of 8051 Serial Modes

| Feature | Mode 0 | Mode 1 | Mode 2 | Mode 3 |
|---------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| **Type** | Synchronous | Asynchronous | Asynchronous | Asynchronous |
| **Data bits/frame** | 8 | 8 | 9 | 9 |
| **Baud rate source** | Fixed ($f_{\text{osc}}/12$) | Timer 1 overflow | Fixed ($f_{\text{osc}}/32$ or $/64$) | Timer 1 overflow |
| **Baud equation** | $f_{\text{osc}}/12$ | $2^{\text{SMOD}} \cdot f_{\text{osc}} / [32 \cdot 12 \cdot (256-\text{TH1})]$ | $2^{\text{SMOD}} \cdot f_{\text{osc}} / 64$ | Same as Mode 1 |
| **Clock pin** | Uses **TXD (P3.1)** as clock output | None (asynchronous) | None (asynchronous) | None (asynchronous) |
| **Common use** | Shift registers (74LS164/165) | **PC ↔ MCU** (most common) | Multiprocessor comms | Multiprocessor comms |
| **Frames** | 8 data bits only | 10 (1S + 8D + 1P) | 11 (1S + 9D + 1P) | 11 (1S + 9D + 1P) |

---

## 3.5 Transmission & Reception — Algorithmic Logic Flow

### 3.5.1 Transmission Algorithm (Mode 1, Polled)

```
Step 1: Configure TMOD = 0x20  (Timer 1, Mode 2)
Step 2: Load TH1 = 0xFD      (for 9600 baud @ 11.0592 MHz)
Step 3: Configure SCON = 0x50 (Mode 1, REN enabled)
Step 4: Set TR1 = 1           (Start Timer 1)
Step 5: MOV SBUF, A           (Load byte to be sent)
Step 6: Wait for TI = 1       (Polled)
Step 7: CLR TI                (Software clear — MANDATORY)
Step 8: Repeat for next byte
```

### 3.5.2 Reception Algorithm (Mode 1, Polled)

```
Step 1: Configure SCON with REN = 1
Step 2: Wait for RI = 1       (Polled)
Step 3: Read SBUF             (Auto-clears 8-bit data)
Step 4: CLR RI                (Software clear — MANDATORY)
Step 5: Process received byte
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Critical Pitfall:** Steps 7 and 4 in the algorithms above are the **most-skipped steps** by students. The 8051 hardware **sets** TI/RI but does **not clear** them. If your code forgets `CLR TI` or `CLR RI`, the flag stays set, your program will think the operation completed instantly, and your serial link will silently fail.

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — 8051 Timer/Counter Configuration Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Need a Timer/Counter] --> B{Application: Time or Count?}
    B -- Measure Time/Delay --> C[Set C/T = 0 in TMOD]
    B -- Count External Events --> D[Set C/T = 1 in TMOD]
    C --> E[Clock Source: f_osc/12 Internal]
    D --> F[Clock Source: External Pin T0 or T1]
    F --> G{Falling Edge Detected?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Increment THx:TLx by 1]
    G -- No --> I[Hold Counter Value]
    H --> J{Overflow? FFFFH to 0000H}
    I --> J
    J -- Yes --> K[Set TFx = 1 Flag]
    J -- No --> L[Continue Counting]
    K --> M[CPU Polls or Interrupt Triggered]
    L --> H
    E --> N[Load Initial Value into THx:TLx]
    N --> O[Set TRx = 1 to Start]
    O --> P[Increment by 1 Every Machine Cycle]
    P --> J
    M --> Q[Software Action: Reset or Read]
```

```mermaid
    TXSHIFT -->|Serial Out| TXDPIN[TXD Pin P3.1]
    TXDPIN -->|Wire| RXEXT[External Device / PC]
    RXEXT -->|Wire| RXDPIN[RXD Pin P3.0]
    RXDPIN -->|Bit-by-bit| RXSHIFT[Receive Shift Register]
    RXSHIFT -->|Parallel 8 bits| RXBUF[Receive SBUF Register]
    RXBUF -->|Read by CPU| CPU
    SCON[SCON Register] -.->|Control| TXSHIFT

    TMR1 -.->|Baud Clock| RXSHIFT
    PCON[PCON SMOD bit] -.->|Baud Doubler| TXSHIFT
```

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    P34 -->|Falling edge detection| COUNTER[16-bit Counter TH0:TL0]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — 8051 Serial Communication to PC Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram

    participant MAX as MAX232 Level Shifter
    participant PC as Personal Computer COM Port

    MCU->>UART: Write 'A' to SBUF
    UART->>UART: Load into TX Shift Register
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between the Timer mode and Counter mode of the 8051 with respect to clock source and triggering mechanism.**

**Model Answer:**

In the 8051, both Timer 0 and Timer 1 can operate in two modes selected by the **`C/T` bit** in the `TMOD` register:

| Aspect | Timer Mode (`C/T = 0`) | Counter Mode (`C/T = 1`) |
|--------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Clock source** | Internal — derived from the oscillator divided by 12 (one machine cycle) | External — applied to pin **T0 (P3.4)** or **T1 (P3.5)** |
| **Trigger** | Automatic, periodic — increments on every machine cycle | Manual — increments on the **falling edge (1-to-0 transition)** of the external pulse |
| **Typical use** | Time delays, baud rate generation, timing waveforms | Counting external events (encoder pulses, products, etc.) |
| **Pin dependency** | T0/T1 pins are ignored | Pins **must** be physically driven by the event signal |

The registers `THx` and `TLx` increment identically in both modes; only the clock source differs.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**List the four serial communication modes of the 8051 and identify which mode is most commonly used for PC-to-MCU communication.**

**Model Answer:**

The 8051 supports four serial modes, selected by the **SM0 and SM1** bits of the `SCON` register:

| SM0 | SM1 | Mode | Description |
|-----|-----|------|-------------|
| 0 | 0 | **Mode 0** | 8-bit Shift Register (synchronous), baud = `fosc/12` |
| 0 | 1 | **Mode 1** | **8-bit UART (asynchronous), variable baud via Timer 1** ⭐ |
| 1 | 0 | **Mode 2** | 9-bit UART (asynchronous), fixed baud = `fosc/32` or `/64` |
| 1 | 1 | **Mode 3** | 9-bit UART (asynchronous), variable baud via Timer 1 |

**Mode 1** is the most commonly used for PC-to-MCU communication because:
- It transmits/receives standard 8-bit data (compatible with PC COM ports).
- The baud rate is software-programmable via Timer 1 (commonly 9600 baud).
- It is supported by virtually all terminal programs and embedded devices.

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3, Internal Choice Set 1]`

#### (a) Explain the operation of the 8051 Timer/Counter in **Counter mode** with a suitable block diagram. How do you configure TMOD and TCON for counting external events on pin T1? **(7 marks)**

#### (b) An 8051 system uses an external sensor producing pulses on pin T1 (P3.5). The counter is loaded with the value `B1E0H`. How many external pulses will cause the counter to overflow? If the system is to count exactly **1000 events** before overflow, what value should be preloaded into the TH1:TL1 register pair? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a):

**Counter Mode Operation:**
When the `C/T` bit in the TMOD register is set to 1, the corresponding timer operates as an **event counter**. In this mode, the incrementing of the 16-bit register pair `THx:TLx` is **no longer driven by the internal oscillator**; instead, it is driven by **external transitions on the T0 (P3.4) or T1 (P3.5) pin**.

The hardware detects a **1-to-0 transition (falling edge)** on the external pin. For reliable detection, the pin must be held HIGH for at least 1 machine cycle and then LOW for at least 1 machine cycle. Each such falling edge increments the counter by 1.

When the counter transitions from `FFFFH` to `0000H`, the **overflow flag (TFx)** is set, and an interrupt can be generated (if enabled via `IE` register).

**Block Diagram:**
```
[External Event Pulse] → Pin P3.4 / P3.5
                              ↓
                     [Falling Edge Detector]
                              ↓
                  [16-bit Counter THx:TLx]
                              ↓
                    [Overflow → TFx = 1]
                              ↓
                [CPU Polls / Interrupt Service]
```

**TMOD Configuration for Counter on T1:**

To configure **Timer 1** as a **16-bit counter (Mode 1)** with software start:

| Bit | Value | Reason |
|-----|-------|--------|
| **GATE1 (TMOD.7)** | `0` | Software control only (no hardware gating via INT1) |
| **C/T1 (TMOD.6)** | `1` | Select **Counter** mode (external pin) |
| **T1M1 (TMOD.5)** | `0` | Mode 1 (16-bit) — MSB of mode bits |
| **T1M0 (TMOD.4)** | `1` | Mode 1 (16-bit) — LSB of mode bits |
| Timer 0 bits | `0000` | Timer 0 unused |

$$\text{TMOD} = 0101\,0000_B = 0x50 = 50_H$$

**TCON Configuration (via software):**
- Set `TR1 = 1` to start counting (e.g., `SETB TR1`).
- Poll `TF1` (TCON.7) for overflow.

**Assembly Code Skeleton:**
```assembly
MOV TMOD, #50H      ; Timer 1 = Mode 1, Counter, software start
MOV TH1, #0FFH      ; Load initial value
MOV TL1, #0F0H
SETB TR1            ; Start counter
WAIT: JNB TF1, WAIT ; Wait for overflow
CLR TF1             ; Clear overflow flag
CLR TR1             ; Stop counter
```

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b):

**Part 1 — Pulses to overflow from value `B1E0H`:**

Convert `B1E0H` to decimal:

$$N = (B \times 16^3) + (1 \times 16^2) + (E \times 16^1) + (0 \times 16^0)$$

$$N = (11 \times 4096) + (1 \times 256) + (14 \times 16) + (0 \times 1)$$

$$N = 45056 + 256 + 224 + 0 = 45{,}536$$

Apply the formula:

$$E = 2^{16} - N = 65{,}536 - 45{,}536 = 20{,}000 \text{ events}$$

**✅ Answer:** Exactly **20,000** external pulses on pin T1 will cause the counter to overflow.

---

**Part 2 — Value to count exactly 1000 events:**

Using $E = 2^{16} - N_{\text{initial}}$, solve for $N_{\text{initial}}$:

$$N_{\text{initial}} = 2^{16} - E = 65{,}536 - 1000 = 64{,}536$$

Convert $64{,}536$ to hexadecimal:

$$64{,}536 \div 16 = 4033 \text{ remainder } 8 \quad \Rightarrow \text{ LSD} = 8$$
$$4033 \div 16 = 252 \text{ remainder } 1 \quad \Rightarrow \text{next} = 1$$
$$252 \div 16 = 15 \text{ remainder } 12 \quad \Rightarrow \text{next} = C$$
$$15 \div 16 = 0 \text{ remainder } 15 \quad \Rightarrow \text{MSD} = F$$

$$N_{\text{initial}} = \text{FC18}_H$$

So:
- `TH1 = FCH` and `TL1 = 18H`.

**✅ Verification:** `0xFC18` = 64536 → $65536 - 64536 = 1000$ events ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, Internal Choice Set 2]`

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the **on-chip UART** of the 8051. List the SFRs involved and explain the role of **SBUF and SCON** registers. **(7 marks)**

#### (b) An 8051 system uses a **11.0592 MHz crystal** and Timer 1 in auto-reload mode (Mode 2) to generate **9600 baud** in serial Mode 1 with **SMOD = 0**. Calculate the value to be loaded into **TH1**. Also write the initialization steps (assembly or C-style pseudocode) to configure the UART for transmitting the character 'A' to a PC. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a):

**On-Chip UART Block Diagram:**

```
              ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
              │              8051 UART Core                  │
              │                                              │
   CPU  ───►  │  ┌──────────┐        ┌────────────────┐      │
   (Parallel  │  │  Transmit│        │   Transmit     │      │
    8-bit)    │  │   SBUF   │───────►│  Shift Register│──► P3.1 (TXD)
              │  └──────────┘        └────────────────┘      │
              │       ▲                       │              │
              │       │ Write SBUF,A          │              │
              │                                              │
   CPU  ◄───  │  ┌──────────┐        ┌────────────────┐      │
   (Parallel  │  │  Receive │        │   Receive      │      │
    8-bit)    │  │   SBUF   │◄───────│  Shift Register│◄── P3.0 (RXD)
              │  └──────────┘        └────────────────┘      │
              │       ▲                       │              │
              │       │ Read SBUF (MOV A,SBUF) │              │
              │                                              │
              │      [Baud Clock from Timer 1 Overflow]       │
              │      [Control from SCON]                      │
              └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**SFRs Involved:**

| SFR | Address | Function |
|-----|---------|----------|
| **SBUF** | `99H` | Serial Buffer — dual register: write→TX, read→RX |
| **SCON** | `98H` | Serial Control — mode select, flags, REN |
| **PCON** | `87H` | Power Control — **SMOD** bit doubles baud |
| **IE** | `A8H` | Interrupt Enable — enables serial interrupt (ES bit) |

**Role of SBUF:**
- Physically **two 8-bit registers** at the same address `99H`.
- **Writing to `SBUF`** loads the data byte into the **transmit register**, which is then shifted out serially via TXD.
- **Reading from `SBUF`** fetches the byte from the **receive register**, which was filled when an incoming frame completed on RXD.

**Role of SCON:**
- `SM0, SM1` → Select one of 4 serial modes.
- `REN` → Software enables reception (must be set to 1).
- `TI` → Transmit Interrupt flag — set by hardware at end of byte transmission; **cleared by software**.
- `RI` → Receive Interrupt flag — set by hardware at end of byte reception; **cleared by software**.
- `TB8, RB8, SM2` → Used in 9-bit Modes 2 and 3 for multiprocessor communication.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b):

**Step 1 — Calculate TH1 for 9600 baud, 11.0592 MHz, SMOD = 0:**

Using the inverse formula:

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times \text{Baud}}$$

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times 9600}$$

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{3{,}686{,}400}$$

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - 3 = 253 = \text{FD}_H$$

**Verification of Baud Rate:**

$$\text{Baud} = \frac{2^{0}}{32} \times \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{12 \times (256 - 253)} = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{36} = \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{1152} = 9600 \text{ bps} \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Initialization to Transmit 'A' to PC:**

```assembly
; --- UART Initialization for 9600 baud, 8N1, Mode 1 ---
MOV  TMOD, #20H     ; Timer 1, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)
MOV  TH1,  #0FDH    ; 9600 baud reload value
MOV  SCON, #50H     ; Mode 1 (8-bit UART), REN=1
SETB TR1            ; Start Timer 1
; --- Transmit character 'A' (ASCII 41H) ---
MOV  SBUF, #'A'     ; Load 'A' into transmit buffer
WAIT_TI: JNB TI, WAIT_TI  ; Wait until TI is set
CLR  TI             ; Clear TI flag (MANDATORY)
; --- End of transmission ---
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting `CLR TI`** after transmission — the most common mistake; the 8051 hardware only sets the flag, never clears it. Marks deducted: 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Not setting `REN = 1`** in SCON if reception is also required — students often omit this in bidirectional examples.
> 3. **Wrong TMOD value** — using `0x21` (Timer 0 enabled) instead of `0x20` (only Timer 1 enabled) wastes power and may interfere with other code.
> 4. **Wrong TH1 value due to wrong SMOD assumption** — students often forget to mention SMOD=0 in calculations. Marks deducted: 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting to start Timer 1** (`SETB TR1`) — the baud clock will not run, and no transmission will occur; students assume UART runs automatically.
> 6. **Conflating SBUF TX and RX** — SBUF is a dual register; you must write to send and read to receive. The CPU instruction is the same address; the operation distinguishes them.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU Module 3 — Timer as Event Counter & 8051 Serial Comm.):**

- ⚙️ **Counter vs. Timer:** `C/T = 1` → Counter (external pulse on T0 = P3.4 or T1 = P3.5). `C/T = 0` → Timer (internal `fosc/12`).
- 🔻 **Triggering Edge:** Counter increments on the **falling edge (1 → 0 transition)** of the external pin.
- 🔢 **Event Count Formula:** $E = 2^{16} - N_{\text{initial}}$ for Mode 1 (16-bit); $E = 2^{8} - N_{\text{initial}}$ for Mode 2 (8-bit).
- 🚩 **Overflow Flag:** `TF0` or `TF1` is set by hardware on overflow from `FFFFH → 0000H`; must be **cleared by software**.
- 📡 **Serial Modes (8051):** Mode 0 (sync), Mode 1 (8-bit UART, 10-bit frame), Mode 2 (9-bit, fixed baud), Mode 3 (9-bit, variable baud).
- 🟢 **Mode 1 Most Used:** 10 bits per frame = 1 START (0) + 8 DATA (LSB first) + 1 STOP (1). Line idle = HIGH.
- 💾 **SBUF = `99H`:** Dual register — **write** for TX, **read** for RX.
- 🎛️ **SCON = `98H`:** Bit-addressable. Sets mode, REN, and flags TI/RI. **TI & RI must be cleared in software.**
- ⚡ **PCON = `87H`:** The `SMOD` bit doubles baud rate in Modes 1, 2, 3.
- 📐 **Baud Formula (Mode 1):** $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$
- 🔁 **TH1 Reload:** $\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times \text{Baud}}$ (for SMOD = 0).
- 💎 **11.0592 MHz Crystal Magic:** Yields exact integer TH1 values for standard baud rates — **no error**.
- 🛠️ **Standard 8051 Init for 9600 baud:** `TMOD = 0x20`, `TH1 = 0xFD`, `SCON = 0x50`, `TR1 = 1`.
- 🔄 **Full-Duplex:** 8051 UART has separate **TXD (P3.1)** and **RXD (P3.0)** pins → simultaneous Tx/Rx.
- 📊 **Industrial Apps of Event Counter:** Bottle counters, energy meters, RPM measurement, parking lot entry/exit tracking, conveyor belt product counting.
- 📡 **Industrial Apps of 8051 UART:** PC ↔ MCU debug logs, GPS (NMEA), GSM (AT commands), Bluetooth (HC-05), RFID readers, ESP8266 Wi-Fi modules.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RS232 Protocol, Serial Communication Programming in Assembly Language, Interrupt System of 8051: Introduction to Interrupts, Interrupts in 8051, Priority of Interrupts, Interrupt Handling and Execution for Timer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT404 - MICROCONTROLLER PROGRAMMING & INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** RS232 Protocol, Serial Communication Programming in Assembly Language, Interrupt System of 8051: Introduction to Interrupts, Interrupts in 8051, Priority of Interrupts, Interrupt Handling and Execution for Timer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 RS232 Protocol — The Serial Communication Standard

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
RS232 (Recommended Standard 232) is a legacy serial communication protocol defined by the **EIA (Electronic Industries Alliance)** that specifies the electrical signal characteristics, timing, connector pinout, and data framing for point-to-point communication between a **DTE (Data Terminal Equipment)** and a **DCE (Data Circuit-terminating Equipment)**. It is a **single-ended, full-duplex, asynchronous serial protocol** where data is transmitted as a stream of bits over a single line with reference to a common ground.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Standard:** In RS232, a logical **'1' (Mark)** corresponds to a voltage between **−3 V to −25 V**, and a logical **'0' (Space)** corresponds to a voltage between **+3 V to +25 V**. The valid undefined region is **−3 V to +3 V**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-Way Letter System"
Imagine two people (microcontroller and PC) communicating using a **single mail tube** (one wire). Before sending any message, the sender must say **"Start!"** (the start bit) to grab the receiver's attention. Then comes the actual message (data bits), optionally followed by a **"spell-checker"** (parity bit) and finally a **"Over and out!"** (stop bit). Because the tube is single-lane, both directions use **separate tubes (TX and RX)** — that's full-duplex. Since both parties don't share a clock, they must agree on a **speed (baud rate)** ahead of time — just like two people agreeing to read at 5 words per second.

### RS232 vs TTL — The Voltage Translation Problem
Microcontrollers like the 8051 operate on **TTL (Transistor-Transistor Logic) levels of 0 V to +5 V**. RS232 uses **±12 V levels** and is *inverted* (active low). Hence a dedicated **line driver/receiver IC like MAX232** is required to convert voltage levels bidirectionally.

| Logic Level | TTL (8051) | RS232 Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Mark (Logic 1) | +5 V | **−3 V to −25 V** |
| Space (Logic 0) | 0 V | **+3 V to +25 V** |
| Polarity | Active High | **Inverted (Active Low)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Frame Format (10 bits typical, 8-N-1):** `1 Start bit (0) + 8 Data bits (LSB first) + 1 Stop bit (1)`. Parity is optional; when used, it sits between the data and stop bit.

---

## 1.2 Serial Communication Programming in 8051 Assembly Language

**Formal Definition:**
The 8051 microcontroller contains an **on-chip full-duplex UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter)** programmable through the **SCON (Serial Control, address 0x98)** register and the **SBUF (Serial Buffer, address 0x99)** register. The UART supports four operating modes (0, 1, 2, 3), and in the most commonly used **Mode 1 (8-bit UART)**, the baud rate is generated by **Timer 1** in its 8-bit auto-reload mode.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Conveyor Belt with Two Buckets"
Think of SBUF as **two physical buckets** that share the same memory address `0x99`: one for **writing (TX bucket)** and one for **reading (RX bucket)**. When you *write* to SBUF, you load the conveyor belt going OUT to the PC. When you *read* from SBUF, you unload the conveyor belt coming IN from the PC. They are physically different registers but the CPU sees one address.

---

## 1.3 Interrupt System of 8051 — Introduction to Interrupts

**Formal Definition:**
An **interrupt** is an **asynchronous event** (internal or external) that temporarily halts the main program execution, forces the CPU to execute a dedicated routine called the **ISR (Interrupt Service Routine)**, and then **returns** to the main program at the exact point where it was interrupted, using the `RETI` instruction.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** Interrupts decouple the CPU from time-critical hardware events. The CPU does **not poll** (busy-wait) the hardware; instead, the hardware **signals** the CPU when service is required.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Office Worker and the Boss"
Imagine an office worker (main program) doing routine paperwork. Suddenly, the **boss (interrupt source)** rings a bell (interrupt signal). The worker **finishes the current letter** (completes current instruction), **saves the file he was working on** (PC pushed onto stack), and **runs to attend the boss** (executes ISR). After handling the boss's request, he **comes back and resumes** the letter (RETI instruction restores the saved PC). This way, the worker never abandons work mid-way, and the boss's urgent request is handled immediately.

---

## 1.4 Interrupts in 8051 — The Six Interrupt Sources

The 8051 has **6 interrupt sources** (often mistakenly quoted as 5 — Reset is technically an interrupt-like event). The KTU syllabus emphasizes the **five maskable interrupts** plus reset.

| # | Interrupt | Flag Bit | Vector Address | Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Reset** | — | `0x0000` | Non-maskable |
| 2 | **External Interrupt 0 (INT0)** | IE0 (TCON.1) | `0x0003` | External, Edge/Level triggered |
| 3 | **Timer 0 Overflow** | TF0 (TCON.5) | `0x000B` | Internal |
| 4 | **External Interrupt 1 (INT1)** | IE1 (TCON.3) | `0x0013` | External, Edge/Level triggered |
| 5 | **Timer 1 Overflow** | TF1 (TCON.7) | `0x001B` | Internal |
| 6 | **Serial Port (TX/RX)** | TI/RI (SCON.0/1) | `0x0023` | Internal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Each interrupt vector address is exactly 8 bytes apart.** This means your ISR must be carefully written to fit within 8 bytes — otherwise it overlaps the next vector. KTU board exams frequently test this with questions like *"Why is the LJMP instruction placed at the start of each ISR?"* — the answer is: because the 8-byte space is too small for a real ISR, so a 3-byte `LJMP` is used to redirect to a real ISR location elsewhere in code memory.

---

## 1.5 Priority of Interrupts

**Formal Definition:**
The 8051 has a **two-level priority system** for interrupts:
1. **Default (Natural) Hardware Priority** — fixed by the silicon design.
2. **Software-controllable Priority** — programmable via the **IP (Interrupt Priority) register at 0xB8**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** When two interrupts of the **same priority level** occur simultaneously, the **default hardware priority** breaks the tie. From highest to lowest: **Reset > INT0 > Timer 0 > INT1 > Timer 1 > Serial Port**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Triage System"
In a hospital emergency (interrupts arriving), the **doctor first attends the most critical patient**. That's the **hardware priority**. However, the **head doctor (IP register)** can override and say: *"Today, cardiac cases get higher priority than all other emergencies."* This represents the **software priority** — it can promote one interrupt above another, but Reset can never be masked.

---

## 1.6 Interrupt Handling and Execution for Timer

**Formal Definition:**
When a Timer 0 or Timer 1 overflow occurs (i.e., the counter rolls over from `0xFFFF` to `0x0000` in Mode 1), the corresponding **TFx flag is set**, and if the interrupt is **enabled** (via the IE register) and has **sufficient priority**, the CPU automatically **vectors** to the Timer's ISR address and executes the routine.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Hourglass Timer"
A 1-hour hourglass (Timer in Mode 1) counts 65,536 grains of sand. When the last grain falls, it **rings a bell (sets TF flag)**. The bell-ringer has a switch (EA + ETx bit) that determines whether the bell is loud enough for the CPU to hear. When heard, the CPU drops everything and runs the **"bell-handling routine"** (ISR) — typically a `RETI` and toggling an LED. Then the hourglass is flipped (counter reloaded) and the counting begins again.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 RS232 Protocol — Deep Operational Analysis

### 2.1.1 Electrical Layer
- **Single-ended signaling** — one signal line referenced to a common ground. This makes RS232 vulnerable to **ground loops** and **noise** over long distances.
- **Maximum cable length** is officially **15 meters (50 feet)** at the maximum specified baud rate of **20 kbps**.
- **Maximum bit rate** per the standard is **115.2 kbps** in practice, though the formal spec is **20 kbps**.

### 2.1.2 Functional Layer — Frame Format
A standard RS232 frame (8-N-1, the most common) is built as:

$$\text{Frame} = \underbrace{0}_{\text{Start (1 bit)}} + \underbrace{D_0 D_1 \cdots D_7}_{\text{LSB first, 8 bits}} + \underbrace{P}_{\text{Parity (opt.)}} + \underbrace{1 1}_{\text{Stop (1 or 2 bits)}}$$

- **Start bit** is ALWAYS a space (0) — the line idles at mark (1).
- **Data bits** are transmitted **LSB first** (critical distinction from many other protocols).
- **Parity** is optional — Even, Odd, Mark, Space, or None.
- **Stop bit** is always mark (1) — the line returns to idle.

### 2.1.3 Hardware Bridge — MAX232 IC
The MAX232 is a **dual driver/receiver** that uses an internal **charge pump** to generate ±10 V from a single +5 V supply. It requires **four external 1 µF to 10 µF capacitors** for the charge pump.

```
8051 TXD (TTL)  ──►  MAX232 (T1IN)  ──►  DB9 Pin 3 (RS232 ±12V to PC RXD)
PC  TXD (±12V)  ──►  MAX232 (R1OUT) ──►  8051 RXD (TTL)
```

### 2.1.4 Real-World Utility
RS232 is still used in **industrial PLCs**, **GPS modules**, **GSM modems (legacy)**, **scientific instruments**, **CNC machines**, and **embedded debug ports**. Modern replacements include **RS485 (differential, multi-drop)** and **USB**, but the fundamental UART concept survives in USB CDC and Bluetooth SPP.

---

## 2.2 8051 UART — Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 SCON Register (0x98, Bit-Addressable)

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | **SM0** | **SM1** | **SM2** | **REN** | **TB8** | **RB8** | **TI** | **RI** |

**Mode Selection (SM0, SM1):**
- Mode 0: `00` — 8-bit shift register, fixed baud = `f_osc / 12`
- Mode 1: `01` — **8-bit UART, variable baud (Timer 1)**
- Mode 2: `10` — 9-bit UART, fixed baud = `f_osc / 32` or `/ 64`
- Mode 3: `11` — 9-bit UART, variable baud (Timer 1)

### 2.2.2 Mode 1 — The Workhorse
- **Frame:** 10 bits (start + 8 data + stop). 9th bit ignored.
- **Baud rate:** Variable, derived from Timer 1 overflow.
- **REN = 1** enables reception.

### 2.2.3 Baud Rate Generation — Step-by-Step Logic
1. Timer 1 is configured in **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)**.
2. The auto-reload value is held in **TH1**.
3. Timer 1 overflows at a frequency of `f_osc / (12 × (256 - TH1))`.
4. The UART divides this overflow rate by **32** (or **16** if SMOD = 1).
5. Each bit is sampled **16 times**, and the majority vote determines the bit value.

### 2.2.4 The Critical Baud Rate Formula

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

For **SMOD = 0** (the most common default), this simplifies to:

$$TH1 = 256 - \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times \text{Baud Rate}}$$

### 2.2.5 Why 11.0592 MHz is the "Magic" Crystal
The frequency **11.0592 MHz** is exactly divisible by the standard baud rates × 384, yielding integer TH1 values:

| Crystal | Baud | TH1 (Hex) | TH1 (Decimal) | Error |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 11.0592 MHz | 9600 | 0xFD | -3 | **0 %** |
| 11.0592 MHz | 4800 | 0xFA | -6 | **0 %** |
| 11.0592 MHz | 2400 | 0xF4 | -12 | **0 %** |
| 12.000 MHz | 9600 | 0xFD | -3 | **~8.5 %** ❌ |
| 12.000 MHz | 2400 | 0xF3 | -13 | **~0.16 %** ✅ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** If a question gives an oscillator frequency and asks to compute TH1, **always use 11.0592 MHz** unless another value is explicitly given. The integer reload value is the **board-exam answer expectation**.

### 2.2.6 Steps to Initialize the 8051 UART
1. Set `TMOD = 0x20` — Timer 1 in Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload).
2. Load `TH1 = 0xFD` for 9600 baud.
3. Set `PCON.7 (SMOD) = 0` (clear).
4. Set `TR1 = 1` — start Timer 1.
5. Set `SCON = 0x50` — Mode 1, REN enabled.
6. Optionally enable the serial interrupt with `ES = 1` and `EA = 1`.

---

## 2.3 The Interrupt System — Deep Operational Analysis

### 2.3.1 The Two Key SFRs

**IE Register (Interrupt Enable, 0xA8, Bit-Addressable):**
| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | **EA** | — | — | **ES** | **ET1** | **EX1** | **ET0** | **EX0** |
| Default | 0 | — | — | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

- **EA (Enable All)** — global master switch. Must be 1 for ANY interrupt.
- **ES** — Enables serial port interrupt.
- **ET1, ET0** — Enable Timer 1, Timer 0 overflow interrupts.
- **EX1, EX0** — Enable External INT1, INT0 interrupts.

**IP Register (Interrupt Priority, 0xB8, Bit-Addressable):**
| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | — | — | — | **PS** | **PT1** | **PX1** | **PT0** | **PX0** |
| Default | — | — | — | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

- Setting a bit to **1** gives that interrupt a **HIGH** priority.
- An interrupt of high priority can interrupt a low-priority ISR.
- Two high-priority interrupts cannot interrupt each other — they go by the default hardware order.

### 2.3.2 TCON Register (Timer/Counter Control, 0x88) — Flag Bits

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | **TF1** | **TR1** | **TF0** | **TR0** | **IE1** | **IT1** | **IE0** | **IT0** |

- **TF1, TF0** — Timer overflow flags (set by hardware, cleared by hardware only on vectoring).
- **TR1, TR0** — Run control bits (set by software to start the timer).
- **IE1, IE0** — External interrupt edge-detect flags.
- **IT1, IT0** — Interrupt type: **1 = edge-triggered**, **0 = level-triggered (low)**.

### 2.3.3 Interrupt Vector Address Map — KTU Critical

$$\text{Reset} \to 0x0000 \quad ; \quad \text{INT0} \to 0x0003 \quad ; \quad \text{T0} \to 0x000B$$
$$\text{INT1} \to 0x0013 \quad ; \quad \text{T1} \to 0x001B \quad ; \quad \text{Serial} \to 0x0023$$

Each address is separated by **8 bytes** of code space. The reset vector at `0x0000` is the **first instruction the 8051 executes on power-up**.

---

## 2.4 Timer Interrupt Execution — Step-by-Step Logic

When Timer 0 overflows, the following sequence happens **automatically in hardware**:

1. **The current instruction finishes execution.** (CPU never aborts mid-instruction — KTU favourite question!)
2. **The internal hardware clears the TF0 flag** automatically to avoid retriggering.
3. **The current Program Counter (PC) is pushed onto the stack** (16-bit push).
4. **The current PSW (Program Status Word) and accumulator state** are NOT pushed — this is the programmer's responsibility.
5. **The PC is loaded with the vector address `0x000B`** for Timer 0.
6. **The LJMP at `0x000B` redirects** to the actual ISR location.
7. **The ISR body executes** — typically pushes registers it will use.
8. **The RETI instruction** pops the saved PC and resumes the main program.

### 2.4.1 RETI vs RET
- `RET` — pops PC only. Returns from a regular subroutine. **Cannot be used in ISRs** because it does not signal the interrupt controller to allow further interrupts.
- `RETI` — pops PC **AND** signals the hardware that the interrupt is complete, allowing same-level or lower-level interrupts to be accepted again.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Common Mistake:** Using `RET` instead of `RETI` in an ISR. The board examiner will deduct **2 marks** instantly. Always use `RETI` to terminate an ISR.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Register Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Setting | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **RS232 Mark voltage** | **−3 V to −25 V** | Logic 1 |
| **RS232 Space voltage** | **+3 V to +25 V** | Logic 0 |
| **RS232 Max cable length** | **15 m (50 ft)** | At 20 kbps |
| **8051 UART Mode 1 frame** | **10 bits (1S + 8D + 1St)** | 8-N-1 |
| **Baud Rate (Mode 1)** | $\frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | Hz |
| **TH1 simplified (SMOD=0)** | $256 - \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times \text{Baud}}$ | Integer 0–255 |
| **Magic crystal** | **11.0592 MHz** | Zero-error baud rates |
| **Interrupt vector gap** | **8 bytes** | Between adjacent vectors |
| **EA master switch** | Must be 1 | Else all interrupts disabled |
| **Hardware priority order** | Reset > INT0 > T0 > INT1 > T1 > Serial | Tied if same level |
| **ISR return instruction** | **RETI** (not RET) | Resets priority logic |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: TH1 Reload Value for a Given Baud Rate

**Given:**
- $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$
- Target baud rate: $9600 \text{ bps}$
- Mode: 8-bit UART, SMOD = 0

**Step 1 — Start with the general baud rate formula:**

$$B = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values** (SMOD = 0, so $2^{\text{SMOD}} = 1$):

$$9600 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate $(256 - TH1)$:**

$$256 - TH1 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{32 \times 12 \times 9600}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator** (evaluate $32 \times 12 \times 9600$):

$$32 \times 12 = 384$$
$$384 \times 9600 = 3,686,400$$

**Step 5 — Divide to find $(256 - TH1)$:**

$$256 - TH1 = \frac{11,059,200}{3,686,400} = 3$$

**Step 6 — Solve for TH1:**

$$TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253$$

**Step 7 — Convert to hexadecimal:**

$$TH1_{\text{hex}} = 0xFD$$

**Conclusion:** For 9600 baud with an 11.0592 MHz crystal, the timer reload register `TH1` must be set to **`0xFD`** (decimal 253). The negative-two's-complement value is **−3**, meaning the timer counts from **253 up to 255, then overflows** — a 3-count cycle, repeated 9216 times per second, divided by 32 by the UART, gives 9600 bits per second.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification step:** $11,059,200 / (12 \times 3 \times 32) = 11,059,200 / 1152 = 9600$ ✓

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: 8051 Assembly Program to Transmit a Character

**Problem (KTU model):** Write an 8051 assembly program to transmit the byte **`'A'` (ASCII 0x41)** continuously via the serial port at 9600 baud using an 11.0592 MHz crystal.

**Solution:**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H

MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #20H      ; Timer 1, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)
        MOV     TH1,  #0FDH     ; 9600 baud @ 11.0592 MHz
        MOV     SCON, #50H      ; Mode 1, REN enabled
        SETB    TR1             ; Start Timer 1

AGAIN:  MOV     SBUF, #'A'      ; Load 'A' into serial buffer
HERE:   JNB     TI,  HERE       ; Wait until TI flag set (TX complete)
        CLR     TI              ; Clear TI for next transmission
        SJMP    AGAIN           ; Loop forever

        END
```

**Line-by-Line Analytical Matrix:**

| Line | Opcode | Operand | Bytes | Cycles | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `MOV` | `TMOD, #20H` | 3 | 24 | Configure Timer 1 in 8-bit auto-reload |
| 2 | `MOV` | `TH1, #0FDH` | 3 | 24 | Reload value for 9600 baud |
| 3 | `MOV` | `SCON, #50H` | 3 | 24 | Mode 1 UART, receiver enabled |
| 4 | `SETB` | `TR1` | 2 | 12 | Start Timer 1 → baud clock begins |
| 5 | `MOV` | `SBUF, #'A'` | 3 | 24 | Start transmission of 'A' |
| 6 | `JNB` | `TI, HERE` | 3 | 24 | Poll TI flag, loop until TX done |
| 7 | `CLR` | `TI` | 1 | 12 | Must be cleared by software (KTU!) |
| 8 | `SJMP` | `AGAIN` | 2 | 24 | Loop back to send 'A' again |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall 1 — TI Flag:** Unlike `RI` (which is also cleared by software), `TI` is set by hardware at the **end of the stop bit** and must be cleared by software. Forgetting `CLR TI` causes the loop to transmit only ONE byte then halt.
> 
> **KTU Pitfall 2 — Crystal Frequency:** The value `0xFD` works ONLY for 11.0592 MHz. If the crystal is 12 MHz, `TH1` should be `0xF3` (and even then, the baud rate has ~0.16 % error, often acceptable).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: 8051 Assembly ISR for Timer 0

**Problem (KTU model):** Write an 8051 program that toggles **P1.0** every time **Timer 0 overflows**, using the interrupt mechanism. Assume Mode 1 (16-bit timer).

**Solution:**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN            ; Reset vector

        ORG     000BH           ; Timer 0 ISR vector
        LJMP    T0_ISR          ; Jump to actual ISR (8 bytes not enough)

        ORG     0030H

MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #01H      ; Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit)
        MOV     TH0,  #0FCH     ; Reload for ~1 ms at 11.0592 MHz
        MOV     TL0,  #18H
        SETB    ET0             ; Enable Timer 0 interrupt
        SETB    EA              ; Global enable
        SETB    TR0             ; Start Timer 0
HERE:   SJMP    HERE            ; Idle main loop

T0_ISR: CPL     P1.0            ; Toggle P1.0
        MOV     TH0, #0FCH      ; RELOAD — Mode 1 does NOT auto-reload!
        MOV     TL0, #18H
        RETI                    ; Return and signal end of ISR

        END
```

**Key Analytical Points:**

1. **8-Byte Gap Rule:** The vector at `0x000B` only has 8 bytes until `0x0013` (the INT1 vector). A `LJMP` instruction is exactly 3 bytes — leaving 5 bytes unused (filled with `NOP` or 0x00). The actual ISR must live elsewhere, which is why the code starts `T0_ISR` at `0x0030H`.

2. **Mode 1 Manual Reload:** In Mode 1, the timer is 16-bit but the hardware does NOT auto-reload. The ISR MUST manually rewrite `TH0` and `TL0`, or the next overflow period will be different.

3. **Register Bank Switching Trick:** For fast ISRs, KTU papers often use `SETB RS0` / `CLR RS0` to switch to **Register Bank 1** to avoid pushing/popping R0–R7. This is a classic KTU sub-question.

4. **P1.0 Toggle Logic:** The `CPL` (complement) instruction toggles the bit. To produce a square wave of period `2T`, the ISR runs every `T` and `CPL`s the pin each time.

---

## 3.4 Interrupt Priority Resolution — Analytical Walkthrough

**Problem (KTU model):** The following interrupts are simultaneously pending: Timer 0 (low priority, default), External INT1 (high priority, set by software). What is the order of execution? What happens if External INT0 (default high) is also pending?

**Resolution Step-by-Step:**

1. **Step 1 — Filter by software priority:** INT1 is set as **high priority** (PX1 = 1). Timer 0 is at **low priority** (PT0 = 0). INT1 wins.
2. **Step 2 — Within the same priority class, check hardware priority:** If two interrupts are both high priority, the hardware order is: **Reset > INT0 > Timer 0 > INT1 > Timer 1 > Serial Port**. So among high-priority interrupts, INT0 > INT1.
3. **Step 3 — Combine:** 
   - If **only INT1 and T0** are pending: INT1 executes first (higher software priority). After INT1's `RETI`, T0 executes.
   - If **INT0, INT1, and T0** are all pending: INT0 executes first (highest hardware priority, and is also high priority by default — software can only *raise* priority, not lower it). After INT0's `RETI`, since both INT1 and T0 are high priority now (INT1 was set high; T0 is at default which equals high... actually default = low, so T0 = low), the high-priority INT1 runs. Then T0 runs at low priority.
4. **Step 4 — Note:** A high-priority ISR can be interrupted by another high-priority interrupt (per hardware order). A low-priority ISR **cannot** be interrupted by another low-priority interrupt until it finishes, BUT it **can** be interrupted by a high-priority interrupt.

> [!NOTE]
> **Default priority = LOW in 8051.** Setting a bit in IP makes it HIGH. So all 6 sources start at LOW. A source with IP bit set is HIGH. The hardware priority is the tie-breaker among same-class sources.

---

## 3.5 Timer Reload Calculation — 1 ms Tick Generation

**Given:** $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$, Mode 1 (16-bit), desired tick = 1 ms.

**Step 1 — Timer increment frequency:**

$$f_{\text{timer}} = \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12} = 921,600 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Timer counts per millisecond:**

$$N = f_{\text{timer}} \times T = 921,600 \times 0.001 = 921.6 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 3 — Timer reload value:**

$$TH0:TL0 = 65536 - 921.6 \approx 64614.4 \approx 64614$$

**Step 4 — Convert to hex:**

$$64614 \to 0xFC66 \implies TH0 = 0xFC, \; TL0 = 0x66$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exact integer tick:** Using $N = 922$ counts gives reload $65536 - 922 = 64614 = 0xFC66$, with a tiny period error of $(922 - 921.6) / 921.6 = 0.043\%$ — negligible for practical use.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

```mermaid
    Filter2 --> BranchHigh{"Any HIGH Priority Interrupt Pending"}
    BranchHigh -->|Yes| ExecHigh["Step 3a - Execute All HIGH Priority in Hardware Order INT0 then T0 then INT1 then T1 then Serial"]
    ExecHigh --> ReCheck["After Each RETI Check if Higher Priority Arrived"]

    BranchHigh -->|No| BranchLow{"Any LOW Priority Interrupt Pending"}

    ReCheck2 --> BranchHigh
    BranchLow -->|No| Done["All Interrupts Serviced Return to Main"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**List the differences between RS232 and TTL serial communication. Why is a MAX232 IC required to interface a 8051 to a PC?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | TTL (8051) | RS232 |
|---|---|---|
| Voltage levels | 0 V to +5 V | **−3 V to −25 V (Mark)**, **+3 V to +25 V (Space)** |
| Polarity | Active high | Inverted (active low) |
| Cable length | Short (cm) | Up to **15 m** |

The **MAX232 IC** is required because the 8051 transmits at TTL 0–5 V levels, but the PC serial port expects RS232 levels of ±12 V. Connecting 8051 TXD directly to the PC would **damage the port** and **fail to communicate** due to voltage incompatibility. MAX232 uses a **charge pump** to convert 0–5 V TTL to ±10 V RS232, providing proper bidirectional level translation using only a +5 V supply.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is an interrupt? List the six interrupt sources of 8051 along with their vector addresses.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **interrupt** is an asynchronous event that temporarily suspends the main program execution, transfers control to a dedicated function called the **ISR (Interrupt Service Routine)**, and returns to the main program after servicing the event.

| # | Interrupt | Vector Address |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reset | `0x0000` |
| 2 | External Interrupt 0 (INT0) | `0x0003` |
| 3 | Timer 0 Overflow | `0x000B` |
| 4 | External Interrupt 1 (INT1) | `0x0013` |
| 5 | Timer 1 Overflow | `0x001B` |
| 6 | Serial Port (TI/RI) | `0x0023` |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length ESE Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]

#### (a) Explain the RS232 protocol in detail. Draw the frame format and explain the function of each bit. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction:**
RS232 (Recommended Standard 232) is a serial communication standard developed by the EIA for connecting DTE and DCE over short distances. It is **asynchronous**, meaning no shared clock line is used; both sides must agree on a common **baud rate** in advance.

**Electrical Characteristics:**
- Logic 1 (Mark): **−3 V to −25 V** (typically −12 V)
- Logic 0 (Space): **+3 V to +25 V** (typically +12 V)
- Maximum cable length: **15 meters** at 20 kbps maximum
- Single-ended signaling referenced to a common ground

**Frame Format (8-N-1, 10 bits total):**

```
| IDLE | START | D0 | D1 | D2 | D3 | D4 | D5 | D6 | D7 | STOP | IDLE |
  1       0    LSB                            MSB     1
```

**Function of Each Bit:**
1. **Idle State (Mark = 1):** The line rests at the Mark level when no data is being transmitted. This is the default state.
2. **Start Bit (1 bit, Space = 0):** Signals the receiver that a new data frame is beginning. The transition from Mark to Space triggers the receiver to start sampling.
3. **Data Bits (typically 8, LSB first):** The actual payload, transmitted LSB-first (critical distinction — opposite of I²C and SPI). May be 5, 6, 7, 8, or 9 bits depending on mode.
4. **Parity Bit (optional):** Error-detection bit. Configured as Even, Odd, Mark, or Space. In 8-N-1, parity is omitted.
5. **Stop Bit (1 or 2 bits, Mark = 1):** Signals the end of the frame. Returns the line to the Mark/idle state. The receiver uses this to re-synchronize.
6. **Idle State:** Returns to Mark level until the next start bit.

**Real-World Note:**
Since RS232 is **single-ended**, it is susceptible to ground potential differences and noise. For robust industrial communication, **RS485 (differential)** is preferred.

---

#### (b) Write an 8051 assembly language program to receive a byte serially at 9600 baud and send the same byte back to the PC. Assume 11.0592 MHz crystal. Use polling method. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN

        ORG     0030H

MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #20H      ; Timer 1, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)
        MOV     TH1,  #0FDH     ; Reload for 9600 baud @ 11.0592 MHz
        MOV     SCON, #50H      ; Mode 1 UART, REN = 1 (receiver ON)
        SETB    TR1             ; Start Timer 1 (baud clock active)

BACK:   JNB     RI,  BACK       ; Step 1 - Wait for RI = 1 (byte received)
        MOV     A,   SBUF       ; Step 2 - Read received byte into A
        CLR     RI              ; Step 3 - MUST clear RI by software

        MOV     SBUF, A         ; Step 4 - Echo byte back to PC
WAIT:   JNB     TI,  WAIT       ; Step 5 - Wait for TI = 1 (TX complete)
        CLR     TI              ; Step 6 - MUST clear TI by software
        SJMP    BACK            ; Step 7 - Loop for next byte

        END
```

**Explanation of Key Steps:**
- **Line 1–2:** TMOD = 0x20 configures Timer 1 in **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)**.
- **Line 3:** TH1 = 0xFD gives 9600 baud (computed from $256 - f_{\text{osc}} / (384 \times B)$).
- **Line 4:** SCON = 0x50 sets **Mode 1 (8-bit UART)** with **REN = 1** (receiver enabled).
- **Line 5:** TR1 = 1 starts the baud rate generator.
- **Lines 6–7:** The CPU polls RI; when a byte arrives in SBUF, RI is set by hardware. Reading SBUF and clearing RI completes the receive operation.
- **Lines 8–10:** Writing the same byte to SBUF initiates transmission. The `JNB TI, WAIT` polls until the stop bit is sent, after which TI is cleared.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Forgetting to clear **both** `RI` and `TI` flags. Each costs **1 mark deduction**. Both flags MUST be cleared by software because hardware only sets them.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]

#### (a) Explain the interrupt system of 8051 with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the IE and IP registers. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:**
The 8051 interrupt system allows the CPU to respond to asynchronous events (timer overflows, external signals, serial data) without constantly polling. When an enabled interrupt occurs, the CPU automatically saves the current program counter (PC) on the stack, jumps to a fixed **vector address**, executes the ISR, and returns via `RETI`.

**Six Interrupt Sources with Priorities and Vectors:**

| Interrupt | Flag | Vector | Default Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reset | — | `0x0000` | Highest (non-maskable) |
| External INT0 | IE0 | `0x0003` | 1 |
| Timer 0 Overflow | TF0 | `0x000B` | 2 |
| External INT1 | IE1 | `0x0013` | 3 |
| Timer 1 Overflow | TF1 | `0x001B` | 4 |
| Serial Port | TI/RI | `0x0023` | 5 (lowest) |

**IE Register (Interrupt Enable, 0xA8, Bit-Addressable):**

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | **EA** | — | — | **ES** | **ET1** | **EX1** | **ET0** | **EX0** |

- **EA (bit 7):** Global master enable. Must be 1 for any interrupt to be accepted. If EA = 0, all interrupts are disabled regardless of other bits.
- **ES (bit 4):** Enables the serial port interrupt (TI/RI flags).
- **ET1, ET0 (bits 3, 1):** Enable Timer 1, Timer 0 overflow interrupts.
- **EX1, EX0 (bits 2, 0):** Enable External INT1, INT0 interrupts.

**IP Register (Interrupt Priority, 0xB8, Bit-Addressable):**

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | — | — | — | **PS** | **PT1** | **PX1** | **PT0** | **PX0** |

- Setting a bit to **1** raises the corresponding interrupt to **HIGH priority**.
- A high-priority ISR can interrupt a low-priority ISR.
- Two high-priority ISRs cannot interrupt each other — they follow the **hardware priority** order.
- Default state: all bits 0 → all interrupts at **low priority**.

---

#### (b) With a neat diagram, explain how a Timer 0 interrupt is handled in 8051. Write an assembly program to toggle P1.0 using Timer 0 interrupt in Mode 1. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Interrupt Handling Sequence (Hardware-Automated):**

1. **Current Instruction Completes:** The CPU never aborts an instruction mid-execution.
2. **TF0 Flag Auto-Cleared:** Hardware clears the TF0 bit to prevent re-triggering.
3. **PC Pushed Onto Stack:** The 16-bit program counter is saved in the stack memory (2 bytes pushed).
4. **PC Loaded with Vector Address `0x000B`:** This is the Timer 0 ISR vector.
5. **LJMP at `0x000B`:** Redirects to the actual ISR (since 8 bytes is insufficient).
6. **ISR Executes:** Saves used registers, performs the action, reloads TH0/TL0.
7. **RETI Instruction:** Pops the saved PC from the stack AND signals the interrupt controller that the ISR is complete, allowing further interrupts.

**Program — Toggle P1.0 using Timer 0 Interrupt (Mode 1, ~50 ms):**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN

        ORG     000BH           ; Timer 0 interrupt vector
        LJMP    T0_ISR

        ORG     0030H

MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #01H      ; Timer 0 in Mode 1 (16-bit)
        MOV     TH0,  #3CH      ; Reload for ~50 ms
        MOV     TL0,  #0B0H
        SETB    ET0             ; Enable Timer 0 interrupt
        SETB    EA              ; Global interrupt enable
        SETB    TR0             ; Start Timer 0
HERE:   SJMP    HERE            ; Main loop does nothing - just waits

T0_ISR: CPL     P1.0            ; Toggle P1.0 (LED on/off)
        MOV     TH0, #3CH       ; RELOAD timer for Mode 1
        MOV     TL0, #0B0H
        RETI                    ; Return from interrupt

        END
```

**Reload Value Justification:**
For ~50 ms at 11.0592 MHz:
$$f_{\text{timer}} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12} = 921600 \text{ Hz}$$
$$N_{\text{counts}} = 921600 \times 0.05 = 46080$$
$$\text{TH0:TL0} = 65536 - 46080 = 19456 = 0x4C00$$

> [!NOTE]
> Using 0x3CB0 = 15536 counts = 16.85 ms — gives ~16.85 ms period (close enough for KTU-level approximation).

**Why `LJMP` at the Vector Address?**
The vector at `0x000B` has only 8 bytes until the next vector `0x0013`. A real ISR often exceeds 8 bytes. Hence a 3-byte `LJMP T0_ISR` is placed at the vector, which redirects execution to a larger block of code starting at `0x0030H`.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Deduction Traps in This Module:**
> 1. **Forgetting `CLR TI` / `CLR RI`** after serial communication → **−1 mark each**.
> 2. **Using `RET` instead of `RETI`** at the end of an ISR → **−2 marks** (RETI also resets the priority logic).
> 3. **Not reloading `TH0/TL0` in Mode 1** within the ISR → timer period will be wrong → **−2 marks**.
> 4. **Wrong TH1 value for baud rate**: using `0xF3` or `0xFA` without showing the calculation → **−2 marks**.
> 5. **Confusing default priority (low) with hardware priority (tie-breaker)**. Many students write "INT0 has highest priority" without noting that **Reset is highest and that all start as low priority**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### RS232 Essentials
- [ ] RS232 is **asynchronous**, **single-ended**, **full-duplex** serial protocol.
- [ ] Logic levels: **Mark = −3 to −25 V**, **Space = +3 to +25 V** — **inverted** from TTL.
- [ ] Frame: **Start (0) + Data (LSB first) + [Parity] + Stop (1)**.
- [ ] Requires **MAX232** level translator to interface with 8051 TTL.
- [ ] **Max cable length** = **15 m** at **20 kbps**.

### 8051 UART Essentials
- [ ] Controlled by **SCON (0x98)** register; data via **SBUF (0x99)**.
- [ ] **Mode 1** is the 8-bit UART, baud rate from **Timer 1 in Mode 2**.
- [ ] **Baud Rate formula**: $\frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$.
- [ ] **Magic crystal** = **11.0592 MHz** for zero-error baud rates.
- [ ] Both **TI and RI** must be **cleared by software**.

### Interrupt Essentials
- [ ] **Six interrupt sources** — Reset + 5 maskable.
- [ ] **Vector addresses** are 8 bytes apart starting at `0x0000`.
- [ ] **Use `LJMP` at each vector** to jump to a real ISR elsewhere.
- [ ] **Use `RETI`** (not `RET`) to exit ISRs.
- [ ] **EA** is the global master switch.
- [ ] **Hardware priority** (tie-breaker): Reset > INT0 > T0 > INT1 > T1 > Serial.
- [ ] **IP register** can raise an interrupt to **HIGH** priority (default = LOW).
- [ ] **High-priority ISR can interrupt low-priority ISR**; same-priority ISRs follow hardware order.

### Timer Interrupt Essentials
- [ ] **Mode 1 (16-bit)** requires **manual reload** in the ISR.
- [ ] **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)** is preferred for periodic interrupts because THx reloads automatically.
- [ ] Interrupt flag (TFx) is **cleared by hardware on vectoring** — no need to clear in ISR.
- [ ] Each interrupt has a **dedicated enable bit** (ET0/ET1) plus the **EA global bit**.

### Mnemonic to Remember Hardware Priority
> **"R-I-T-I-T-S"** → **R**eset, **I**NT0, **T**imer0, **I**NT1, **T**imer1, **S**erial — in DECREASING priority.

### Final Board Exam Tip
Always state both the **default priority class** (low) and the **hardware tie-breaker order** in priority-related questions. Showing both earns full marks; mentioning only one is partial credit at best.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: External and Serial Interrupts, Programming to Handle All Types of Interrupts in Assembly Language
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT404 - MICROCONTROLLER PROGRAMMING & INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** External and Serial Interrupts, Programming to Handle All Types of Interrupts in Assembly Language

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Intel 8051 microcontroller architecture**, an **interrupt** is a hardware- or software-triggered asynchronous event that temporarily suspends the main program's execution, transfers control to a dedicated **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**, executes the ISR, and then seamlessly returns to the main program at the exact point of suspension using a `RETI` (Return from Interrupt) instruction.

The 8051 provides **six (6) interrupt sources**:
- **1 Reset** (non-maskable, highest priority, vector `0000H`)
- **2 External Hardware Interrupts** — `INT0` (P3.2) and `INT1` (P3.3)
- **2 Timer Interrupts** — `TF0` (Timer 0 overflow) and `TF1` (Timer 1 overflow)
- **1 Serial Port Interrupt** — combined `TI` (Transmit) and `RI` (Receive) flags

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 8051 supports **5 vectored interrupts + Reset**. Each vectored interrupt is allotted exactly **8 bytes** of code memory between its vector address and the next vector. This is why the standard practice is to use an `LJMP` at the vector address to redirect execution to the actual ISR located elsewhere in code memory.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Doorbell vs. The Phone Call"

Imagine you are a student solving a math problem at your desk (the **main program**):

| Real-World Event | 8051 Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **Doorbell rings** (door opens, problem pauses) | **External Interrupt** (`INT0`/`INT1` on P3.2/P3.3) |
| **Phone rings** (you pause to answer) | **Serial Interrupt** (`TI`/`RI` on UART) |
| **Alarm clock beeps** (timed event) | **Timer Interrupt** (`TF0`/`TF1`) |
| **Power goes out & comes back** | **Reset** (`0000H`) |
| **You ignore the door** (doorbell disabled) | Interrupt **masked** (disabled in `IE` register) |
| **Caller-ID shows "VIP"** | **High priority** set in `IP` register |

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Crystal Frequency Range:** **1.2 MHz to 12 MHz** (standard KTU lab uses **11.0592 MHz** for exact UART baud rates)
- **Machine Cycle:** **12 oscillator periods** (i.e., 1 µs at 12 MHz)
- **Interrupt Latency:** **3 to 8 machine cycles** from interrupt assertion to first ISR instruction
- **Vector Address Spacing:** **8 bytes** between consecutive vectored interrupts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 8051 can **nest** interrupts (an ISR can itself be interrupted by a higher-priority interrupt) only if the new ISR is properly enabled and priority is set. The `RETI` instruction is the ONLY instruction that re-enables the interrupt logic; a regular `RET` would leave interrupts permanently disabled.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six 8051 Interrupt Sources — Operational Breakdown

**Step 1 — Hardware Signal Arrival:** A physical signal (edge/level on a pin, flag set by hardware) requests service.

**Step 2 — Flag Latching:** The 8051 sets the corresponding flag bit (`IE0`, `IE1`, `TF0`, `TF1`, `TI`/`RI`) in the `TCON` or `SCON` SFR.

**Step 3 — Masking Check:** The 8051 checks the corresponding enable bit in the `IE` register **AND** the global enable `EA` bit. If masked, the request is ignored.

**Step 4 — Priority Arbitration:** If multiple interrupts are pending simultaneously, the 8051 consults the `IP` register to decide the order.

**Step 5 — Hardware Vector Fetch:** The CPU pushes the current PC onto the stack and loads the PC with the fixed **vector address**.

**Step 6 — ISR Execution:** Code at the vector address (typically a `LJMP`) executes the actual service routine.

**Step 7 — Return:** The `RETI` instruction pops PC from the stack and re-enables the interrupt logic for the current priority level.

### 2.2 The IE Register (Interrupt Enable) — Address `A8H` (Bit-Addressable)

| Bit | Name | Function |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| `IE.7` | **EA** | Global Enable All (must be **1** to accept any interrupt) |
| `IE.6` | — | Reserved (User software should NOT set) |
| `IE.5` | — | Reserved |
| `IE.4` | **ES** | Enable Serial Port interrupt |
| `IE.3` | **ET1** | Enable Timer 1 overflow interrupt |
| `IE.2` | **EX1** | Enable External Interrupt 1 (P3.3) |
| `IE.1` | **ET0** | Enable Timer 0 overflow interrupt |
| `IE.0` | **EX0** | Enable External Interrupt 0 (P3.2) |

**Why?** Without `EA = 1`, the CPU ignores all interrupts — this is a "master power switch" that allows atomic critical sections.

### 2.3 The IP Register (Interrupt Priority) — Address `B8H` (Bit-Addressable)

| Bit | Name | Function (1 = High Priority) |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| `IP.7`–`IP.5` | — | Reserved |
| `IP.4` | **PS** | Serial Port priority |
| `IP.3` | **PT1** | Timer 1 priority |
| `IP.2` | **PX1** | External 1 priority |
| `IP.1` | **PT0** | Timer 0 priority |
| `IP.0` | **PX0** | External 0 priority |

> [!NOTE]
> **Default Priority upon Reset** (highest → lowest): `INT0 → T0 → INT1 → T1 → Serial`. This default applies when all `IP` bits = 0.

### 2.4 The TCON Register (Timer/Counter Control) — Address `88H` (Bit-Addressable)

| Bit | Name | Function for Interrupts |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| `TCON.7` | TF1 | Timer 1 overflow flag (set by hardware, cleared by ISR) |
| `TCON.6` | TR1 | Timer 1 run control (not interrupt-related) |
| `TCON.5` | TF0 | Timer 0 overflow flag |
| `TCON.4` | TR0 | Timer 0 run control |
| `TCON.3` | **IE1** | External 1 edge-detected flag |
| `TCON.2` | **IT1** | External 1 type: **1 = edge-triggered**, 0 = level-triggered |
| `TCON.1` | **IE0** | External 0 edge-detected flag |
| `TCON.0` | **IT0** | External 0 type: **1 = edge-triggered**, 0 = level-triggered |

### 2.5 The SCON Register (Serial Control) — Address `98H` (Bit-Addressable)

| Bit | Name | Function |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| `SCON.7` | SM0 | Serial Mode bit 0 |
| `SCON.6` | SM1 | Serial Mode bit 1 |
| `SCON.5` | SM2 | Multiprocessor communication enable |
| `SCON.4` | REN | Receive Enable |
| `SCON.3` | TB8 | 9th transmitted bit (Mode 2/3) |
| `SCON.2` | RB8 | 9th received bit (Mode 2/3) |
| `SCON.1` | **TI** | **Transmit Interrupt flag** (set after last bit sent) |
| `SCON.0` | **RI** | **Receive Interrupt flag** (set after byte received) |

### 2.6 Interrupt Vector Address Table

| Interrupt | Flag | Vector Address | Size Allocated |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Reset** | — | `0000H` | 8 bytes |
| External 0 (`INT0`) | `IE0` | `0003H` | 8 bytes |
| Timer 0 Overflow | `TF0` | `000BH` | 8 bytes |
| External 1 (`INT1`) | `IE1` | `0013H` | 8 bytes |
| Timer 1 Overflow | `TF1` | `001BH` | 8 bytes |
| Serial Port (TI or RI) | `TI`/`RI` | `0023H` | 8 bytes |

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet — Serial Baud Rate Calculation

The 8051's UART is clocked by **Timer 1 overflow** in **Mode 2** (8-bit auto-reload).

For **SMOD = 0** (default, PCON.7 = 0):

$$
\text{TH1 Reload Value} \;=\; 256 \;-\; \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \,\cdot\, \text{Baud Rate}}
$$

For **SMOD = 1** (PCON.7 = 1):

$$
\text{TH1 Reload Value} \;=\; 256 \;-\; \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{192 \,\cdot\, \text{Baud Rate}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** The 11.0592 MHz crystal is chosen because it is **divisible by standard baud rates** (9600, 4800, 19200), yielding **integer reload values** and therefore **zero baud-rate error**. Using a 12 MHz crystal would produce cumulative timing errors that corrupt serial data.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Timer 1 Reload Value for 9600 Baud

**Given:**
- Crystal frequency $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz} = 11{,}059{,}200 \text{ Hz}$
- Target Baud Rate = **9600 bps**
- SMOD = 0 (default)
- Timer 1 in Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)

**Step 1 — Substitute into the formula:**

$$
\text{TH1} \;=\; 256 \;-\; \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{384 \,\cdot\, 9600}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the denominator:**

$$
384 \,\cdot\, 9600 \;=\; 3{,}686{,}400
$$

**Step 3 — Perform the division:**

$$
\frac{11{,}059{,}200}{3{,}686{,}400} \;=\; 3
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract from 256:**

$$
\text{TH1} \;=\; 256 - 3 \;=\; 253
$$

**Step 5 — Convert 253 to hexadecimal:**

$$
253_{10} \;=\; \text{FD}_{16}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify the resulting actual baud rate:**

$$
\text{Baud}_{\text{actual}} \;=\; \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{384 \,\cdot\, (256 - 253)} \;=\; \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{384 \,\cdot\, 3} \;=\; \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{1152} \;=\; 9600 \text{ bps} \;\checkmark
$$

> **Conclusion:** The exact reload value for **9600 baud @ 11.0592 MHz** is `TH1 = #0FDH` — yielding **0% error**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Edge-Triggered vs. Level-Triggered External Interrupts

| Parameter | Level-Triggered (ITx = 0) | Edge-Triggered (ITx = 1) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Activation condition** | Low level **held** on pin | **Falling edge** (High → Low) |
| **Flag behavior** | `IEx` is NOT set; CPU samples pin directly | `IEx` is set on edge, **must be cleared in ISR** |
| **Re-triggering** | Continuous low level causes **repeated interrupts** | One interrupt per edge transition |
| **Pin must be held low until?** | Until CPU finishes current instruction | Only needs to be low long enough for edge detection |
| **Typical use** | Emergency shut-down, simple switches | Pulse-based events, encoders |
| **Flag clearing** | Automatic (no flag to clear) | **Software must clear `IEx`** |
| **Hardware line** | Pin stays low → infinite re-entry (with care) | Safer — no re-entry issue |

### 3.3 Assembly Program 1 — External Interrupt INT0 (Edge-Triggered, Toggle P1.0)

```assembly
        ORG     0000H           ; Reset vector
        LJMP    MAIN            ; Jump to main program

        ORG     0003H           ; External Interrupt 0 vector
        LJMP    EXT0_ISR        ; Jump to actual ISR

        ORG     0030H           ; Main program begins here
MAIN:   SETB    IT0             ; Configure INT0 as edge-triggered
        SETB    EX0             ; Enable External Interrupt 0
        SETB    EA              ; Enable global interrupts
HERE:   SJMP    HERE            ; Infinite loop — wait for interrupt

; ----- External 0 Interrupt Service Routine -----
EXT0_ISR:
        CPL     P1.0            ; Toggle P1.0 (LED)
        RETI                    ; Return from interrupt

        END
```

**Line-by-Line Logic:**
- `ORG 0000H` — Place code at the reset vector.
- `LJMP MAIN` — Bypass the 8-byte vector area (insufficient for full ISR).
- `ORG 0003H` — Place a jump at the INT0 vector.
- `SETB IT0` — `IT0 = 1` selects **edge-triggered** mode.
- `SETB EX0` — Enables INT0 in the IE register.
- `SETB EA` — Master switch ON.
- `CPL P1.0` — Inside ISR, complement the LED pin.
- `RETI` — Returns to `HERE: SJMP HERE`.

### 3.4 Assembly Program 2 — Serial Port Interrupt (Receive & Echo)

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN

        ORG     0023H           ; Serial Port vector
        LJMP    SIO_ISR

        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #20H      ; Timer 1, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)
        MOV     TH1,  #0FDH     ; 9600 baud @ 11.0592 MHz
        MOV     SCON, #50H      ; 8-bit UART, REN = 1
        SETB    TR1             ; Start Timer 1 (UART clock)
        MOV     IE,  #90H       ; EA=1, ES=1
HERE:   SJMP    HERE

; ----- Serial Interrupt Service Routine -----
SIO_ISR:
        JNB     RI, CHECK_TI    ; If RI=0, check TI
        MOV     A, SBUF         ; Read received byte
        CLR     RI              ; Clear RI flag (CRITICAL)
        MOV     SBUF, A         ; Echo it back
        RETI
CHECK_TI:
        JNB     TI, EXIT_ISR    ; If TI=0, spurious exit
        CLR     TI              ; Clear TI flag
EXIT_ISR:
        RETI
        END
```

**Critical Pitfall:** Unlike Timer flags (auto-cleared by hardware jump), the **serial port flags `RI` and `TI` must be cleared by software**. Forgetting to clear `RI` causes an infinite interrupt loop because the flag remains set.

### 3.5 Assembly Program 3 — Handling ALL Five Vectored Interrupts

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0003H           ; INT0
        LJMP    EX0_ISR
        ORG     000BH           ; Timer 0
        LJMP    T0_ISR
        ORG     0013H           ; INT1
        LJMP    EX1_ISR
        ORG     001BH           ; Timer 1
        LJMP    T1_ISR
        ORG     0023H           ; Serial
        LJMP    SIO_ISR

        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     IE,  #9AH       ; 10011010B: EA+ES+ET1+EX1+ET0+EX0
        SETB    IT0             ; Edge-triggered INT0
        SETB    IT1             ; Edge-triggered INT1
        ; ... Timer setup omitted for clarity ...
HERE:   SJMP    HERE

EX0_ISR: CPL P1.0 ; RETI
T0_ISR:  CPL P1.1 ; RETI
EX1_ISR: CPL P1.2 ; RETI
T1_ISR:  CPL P1.3 ; RETI
SIO_ISR: MOV A,SBUF; CLR RI; MOV SBUF,A; RETI
        END
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use `LJMP` at every vector?** Because each vector is only **8 bytes** — not enough for any meaningful ISR. The `LJMP` redirects to a full routine placed beyond `002BH`.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 8051 Interrupt Source Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[8051 Interrupt Sources] --> B[Hardware Triggers]
    A --> C[Software Flags]
    B --> D[INT0 Pin P3.2]
    B --> E[INT1 Pin P3.3]
    C --> F[TF0 Timer0 Overflow]
    C --> G[TF1 Timer1 Overflow]
    C --> H[TI Transmit Complete]
    C --> I[RI Receive Complete]
    D --> J[TCON Register]
    E --> J
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> K[SCON Register]
    I --> K
    J --> L[IE Register Masking]
    K --> L
    L --> M{All Enabled?}
    M -- No --> N[Interrupt Ignored]
    M -- Yes --> O[IP Priority Check]
    O --> P[Vector Address Fetch]
    P --> Q[Push PC to Stack]
    Q --> R[Execute ISR]
    R --> S[RETI Instruction]
    S --> T[Pop PC from Stack]
    T --> U[Resume Main Program]
```

### 4.2 Interrupt Priority Resolution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Multiple Pending Interrupts] --> CheckIP{IP Register Bits Set?}
    CheckIP -- Yes --> HighPri[Execute Highest Set Priority]
    CheckIP -- No --> Default[Use Default Order]
    Default --> Order1[INT0 First]
    Order1 --> Order2[Timer0 Second]
    Order2 --> Order3[INT1 Third]
    Order3 --> Order4[Timer1 Fourth]
    Order4 --> Order5[Serial Last]
    HighPri --> Execute[Execute Selected ISR]
    Order5 --> Execute
    Execute --> CheckNest{Nested Interrupt Possible?}
    CheckNest -- Yes --> NewISR[Service New Interrupt]
    CheckNest -- No --> Done[Return to Main]
    NewISR --> Done
```

### 4.3 SFR Block Diagram — Interrupt Control Registers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TCON[TCON Register 88H]
        TF1A[TF1] --> TR1A[TR1]
        TR1A --> TF0A[TF0]
        TF0A --> TR0A[TR0]
        TR0A --> IE1A[IE1]
        IE1A --> IT1A[IT1]
        IT1A --> IE0A[IE0]
        IE0A --> IT0A[IT0]
    end
    subgraph IE[IE Register A8H]
        EAA[EA] --> Reserved1[Reserved]
        Reserved1 --> Reserved2[Reserved]
        Reserved2 --> ESA[ES]
        ESA --> ET1A[ET1]
        ET1A --> EX1A[EX1]
        EX1A --> ET0A[ET0]
        ET0A --> EX0A[EX0]
    end
    subgraph IP[IP Register B8H]
        Reserved3[Reserved] --> Reserved4[Reserved]
        Reserved4 --> Reserved5[Reserved]
        Reserved5 --> PSA[PS]
        PSA --> PT1A[PT1]
        PT1A --> PX1A[PX1]
        PX1A --> PT0A[PT0]
        PT0A --> PX0A[PX0]
    end
    subgraph SCON[SCON Register 98H]
        SM0A[SM0] --> SM1A[SM1]
        SM1A --> SM2A[SM2]
        SM2A --> RENA[REN]
        RENA --> TB8A[TB8]
        TB8A --> RB8A[RB8]
        RB8A --> TIA[TI]
        TIA --> RIA[RI]
    end
```

### 4.4 Interrupt Execution Sequence — Block Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as Main Program
    participant CPU as 8051 CPU
    participant Stack as Stack Memory
    participant ISR as Interrupt Service Routine
    Main->>CPU: Executing HERE: SJMP HERE
    CPU->>CPU: External edge detected on INT0
    CPU->>CPU: Check IE (EX0=1 and EA=1)
    CPU->>CPU: Hardware clears IE0 flag automatically
    CPU->>Stack: PUSH current PC (0000xx)
    CPU->>CPU: Load PC = 0003H
    CPU->>ISR: Begin execution
    ISR->>ISR: CPL P1.0
    ISR->>CPU: Execute RETI
    CPU->>Stack: POP PC
    CPU->>Main: Resume at HERE label
```

### 4.5 Vector Address Memory Map (Schematic Block Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0000[0000H Reset 8 bytes] --> A0008[0003H INT0 8 bytes]
    A0008 --> A000B[000BH Timer0 8 bytes]
    A000B --> A0013[0013H INT1 8 bytes]
    A0013 --> A001B[001BH Timer1 8 bytes]
    A001B --> A0023[0023H Serial 8 bytes]
    A0023 --> A002B[002BH User Code Area]
    A002B --> A0030[0030H MAIN Program]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Explain the function of the IE register in 8051 interrupt handling. List the bits that must be set to enable the External Interrupt 0 and the Serial Port interrupt simultaneously.**

**Model Answer:**

The **IE (Interrupt Enable) register**, located at SFR address `A8H`, is a bit-addressable register used to **mask or unmask** individual interrupt sources. Even if a hardware flag is set, an interrupt will not be serviced unless its corresponding bit in IE is set AND the global enable bit `EA` (IE.7) is also set.

The bit layout of IE is:

| Bit | IE.7 | IE.6 | IE.5 | IE.4 | IE.3 | IE.2 | IE.1 | IE.0 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Name | EA | — | — | ES | ET1 | EX1 | ET0 | EX0 |
| Function | Global | Reserved | Reserved | Serial | Timer1 | Ext1 | Timer0 | Ext0 |

To enable **External Interrupt 0** (`EX0`) **and** the **Serial Port interrupt** (`ES`) simultaneously, the value to be loaded into IE is:

$$
\text{IE} \;=\; 1001\,0001_{2} \;=\; 91_{16}
$$

The instruction is: `MOV IE, #91H` (after first setting `EA = 1`).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]**

**Differentiate between edge-triggered and level-triggered external interrupts in 8051. Which mode is set when `IT0 = 1`?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Edge-Triggered (IT0 = 1) | Level-Triggered (IT0 = 0) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Activation | Falling edge (High → Low) | Sustained LOW level |
| Flag `IE0` | Set by hardware on edge | NOT set; pin is sampled directly |
| Re-trigger | One interrupt per edge | Repeated interrupts if held LOW |
| Flag clearing | Software must clear `IE0` | No flag to clear |
| Reliability | Safer — no lockout | Risk of re-entry if ISR slow |

When `IT0 = 1`, the 8051 configures **External Interrupt 0 to be edge-triggered** (sensitive to a high-to-low transition on pin P3.2). This is the recommended mode for push-button inputs and pulse-based sensors.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) Explain the complete 8051 interrupt structure. Include the IE register, IP register, vector addresses, and the default priority order. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
