For ATmega32, it is **14 bits wide** (can address 16K words = 32 KB of Flash). - **Stack Pointer (SP):** 16-bit, points to the SRAM stack. In ATmega32, it is implemented as two 8-bit I/O registers (**SPH** and **SPL**). - **Status Register (SREG):** 8-bit register holding critical flags – **I (Global Interrupt Enable), T (Bit Copy), H (Half Carry), S (Sign), V (Two's Complement Overflow), N (Negative), Z (Zero), C (Carry)**. - **Instruction Register & Decoder:** Decodes fetched opcodes and generates control signals. - **Instruction Execution Unit:** Coordinates micro-operations. ## 2.2 Memory Architecture – Harvard with Unified Addressing The ATmega32 uses a **modified Harvard architecture** with three distinct physical memories: 1. **Flash (Program Memory): 32 KB** - Non-volatile, byte-addressable for read, page-erasable for write. - Organized as **16K × 16 bits** (since AVR instructions are 16-bit). - Endurance: **10,000 write/erase cycles**. 2. **SRAM (Data Memory): 2 KB** - Volatile, used for runtime data, stack, and heap. - Address range: **0x0060 to 0x085F** (after register and I/O mapping). 3. **EEPROM: 1 KB** - Non-volatile, byte-addressable, used for storing permanent parameters. - Endurance: **100,000 write/erase cycles**. - Accessed via three I/O registers: **EEAR (Address), EEDR (Data), EECR (Control)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory Map Hierarchy (Linear Address Space):** > - `0x0000 – 0x001F` → 32 General Purpose Registers (R0–R31) > - `0x0020 – 0x005F` → 64 I/O Registers > - `0x0060 – 0x085F` → Internal SRAM (2 KB) > - `0x0000 – 0x7FFF` → Flash Program Memory (32 KB, separate bus) ## 2.3 I/O Ports and Pin Multiplexing The ATmega32 has **four 8-bit I/O ports** – **PORTA, PORTB, PORTC, PORTD** – giving **32 I/O lines** in total. Each port is controlled by **three registers**: - **DDRx** (Data Direction Register): `1` = Output, `0` = Input. - **PORTx** (Port Output / Pull-up Register): When DDRx = 1 → output value; when DDRx = 0 → enables internal pull-up. - **PINx** (Port Input Pin Address): Used to **read** the physical logic level on the pin. **Multiplexing Highlights:** - **PORTA (PA0–PA7):** Shared with the **8 ADC channels (ADC0–ADC7)**. - **PORTB (PB0–PB7):** Shared with **SPI (MOSI, MISO, SCK, SS)** and programming interface. - **PORTC (PC0–PC7):** Shared with **JTAG (TDI, TDO, TMS, TCK, JTAG-RST)** and **TWI (I²C) SDA, SCL**. - **PORTD (PD0–PD7):** Shared with **USART (RXD, TXD)**, external interrupts (INT0, INT1), and timers (OC1A, OC1B, OC2, ICP). ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Flash Size | $N_{flash} = 2^{PC_{bits}} \times W$ | Bytes | $N_{flash} = 2^{14} \times 2 = 32$ KB | | 2 | SRAM Direct Addressing | $A_{SRAM} = 0x0060 + \text{offset}$ | Hex | Maximum offset = **0x085F − 0x0060 + 1 = 0x0800 = 2048 = 2 KB** | | 3 | Instruction Throughput | $MIPS = f_{CLK}$ in MHz | MIPS | One instruction per clock cycle | | 4 | ADC Conversion Time | $T_{ADC} = (Prescaler \times (Resolution+1))/f_{CLK}$ | seconds | For 10-bit resolution, minimum **13 ADC clock cycles** | | 5 | Timer Resolution | $T_{tick} = Prescaler / f_{CLK}$ | seconds | e.g., prescaler=64, $f_{CLK}=16$ MHz → $T_{tick} = 4 \mu s$ | | 6 | UART Baud Rate | $UBRR = \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{16 \times BAUD} - 1$ | integer | For asynchronous normal mode | | 7 | Flash Endurance | $N_{W/E} \geq 10{,}000$ | cycles | Per page | | 8 | EEPROM Endurance | $N_{W/E} \geq 100{,}000$ | cycles | Per byte | | 9 | Pull-up Resistance | $R_{PU} = 20{-}50$ | kΩ | Typical internal value | | 10 | Active Current @ 1 MHz | $I_{CC} \approx 1.1$ | mA | At $V_{CC} = 3$ V | > [!NOTE] > Always remember that the **PC width in ATmega32 = 14 bits**, which limits the maximum Flash to $2^{14} \times 2 = 32$ KB. This is why ATmega32 (and not ATmega128) is the textbook choice in KTU 2024. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The ATmega32's architecture makes it ideal for: - **Industrial automation** (PLCs, sensor hubs, motor drivers). - **Automotive ECUs** (body control, instrument clusters). - **Consumer electronics** (washing machines, microwave ovens, remote controls). - **IoT edge nodes** (low-power sensor aggregation). - **Educational platforms** (Arduino-class prototyping boards, university labs). Its deterministic single-cycle execution and rich I/O make it a strong choice where **real-time response** is required. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section develops the **core numerical relationships** that govern the ATmega32 architecture – derivations a KTU 2024 examiner expects a student to master. ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Program Memory Addressable The ATmega32 Program Counter (PC) is **14 bits wide**. We derive the maximum Flash memory it can address. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{PC width} &= 14 \text{ bits} \\ \text{Maximum word-addressable locations} &= 2^{14} \\ &= 16{,}384 \text{ locations} \\ \text{Each AVR instruction is 16 bits (2 bytes) wide} \\ \text{Total Flash memory} &= 16{,}384 \times 2 \text{ bytes} \\ &= 32{,}768 \text{ bytes} \\ &= 32 \text{ KB} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ **Engineering Insight:** This is exactly why ATmega32 has **32 KB Flash** and not more. If a design needs >32 KB code, one must migrate to ATmega64/128 (16-bit PC) or external memory. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: SRAM Address Range Calculation The ATmega32 SRAM starts at data address `0x0060` (after registers and I/O) and ends at the top of internal SRAM. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SRAM start address} &= 0x0060 \\ \text{SRAM size} &= 2 \text{ KB} = 2 \times 1024 = 2048 \text{ bytes} \\ \text{SRAM end address} &= 0x0060 + 2048 - 1 \\ &= 0x0060 + 0x07FF \\ &= 0x085F \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > Addresses `0x0000–0x001F` map to the 32 registers. `0x0020–0x005F` map to the 64 I/O registers. SRAM data space begins immediately after, at `0x0060`. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Throughput vs Clock Frequency The AVR executes **one instruction per clock cycle** in the standard pipelined mode. $$ \begin{aligned} f_{CLK} &= 16 \text{ MHz} = 16 \times 10^6 \text{ cycles/sec} \\ \text{Instruction Execution Rate (IER)} &= \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{1} \\ &= 16 \times 10^6 \text{ instructions/sec} \\ &= 16 \text{ MIPS} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4: ADC Conversion Time for Single Conversion The ATmega32 ADC requires **13 ADC clock cycles** for a 10-bit single-ended conversion (first conversion takes 25 cycles due to initialization, subsequent take 13). $$ \begin{aligned} f_{ADC} &= \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{Prescaler} \\ \text{Conversion time } T_{ADC} &= \dfrac{13}{f_{ADC}} \\ &= \dfrac{13 \times Prescaler}{f_{CLK}} \\ \text{For } f_{CLK} = 16 \text{ MHz}, Prescaler = 128 \quad &:\quad f_{ADC} = 125 \text{ kHz} \\ T_{ADC} &= \dfrac{13}{125 \times 10^3} \\ &= 104 \times 10^{-6} \text{ seconds} \\ &= 104 \mu s \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ Maximum ADC sampling rate = $\dfrac{1}{T_{ADC}} \approx 9.6$ kSamples/sec. ## 3.5 Derivation 5: UART Baud Rate Register Value The KTU 2024 syllabus often asks to compute the **UBRR** value for a desired baud rate. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Asynchronous Normal Mode (U2X = 0):} \quad UBRR &= \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{16 \times BAUD} - 1 \\ \text{Asynchronous Double Speed (U2X = 1):} \quad UBRR &= \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{8 \times BAUD} - 1 \\ \\ \text{For } f_{CLK} = 16 \text{ MHz}, \text{BAUD} &= 9600, \text{U2X} = 0: \\ UBRR &= \dfrac{16 \times 10^6}{16 \times 9600} - 1 \\ &= \dfrac{16 \times 10^6}{153{,}600} - 1 \\ &= 104.166 - 1 \\ &= 103.166 \\ \text{Rounded integer: } UBRR &= 103 \\ \text{Actual baud rate} &= \dfrac{16 \times 10^6}{16 \times (103+1)} = 9615.38 \\ \text{Error} &= \dfrac{9615.38 - 9600}{9600} \times 100\% = 0.16\% \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix – Architecture Quick Reference | Subsystem | Registers / Pins | Address / Pin | Function | |---|---|---|---| | PORTA | DDRA, PORTA, PINA | `0x1A, 0x1B, 0x19` | ADC inputs / digital I/O | | PORTB | DDRB, PORTB, PINB | `0x17, 0x18, 0x16` | SPI / digital I/O | | PORTC | DDRC, PORTC, PINC | `0x14, 0x15, 0x13` | JTAG / TWI / I/O | | PORTD | DDRD, PORTD, PIND | `0x11, 0x12, 0x10` | USART / Timers / INT | | Timer/Counter0 | TCCR0, TCNT0, OCR0 | `0x53, 0x46, 0x5C` | 8-bit timer | | Timer/Counter1 | TCCR1A/B, TCNT1, OCR1A/B | `0x4F, 0x4E, 0x4C, ...` | 16-bit timer | | Timer/Counter2 | TCCR2, TCNT2, OCR2 | `0x45, 0x44, 0x43` | 8-bit timer, RTC | | ADC | ADMUX, ADCSRA, ADC | `0x07, 0x06, 0x04-0x05` | 10-bit, 8-channel | | USART | UDR, UCSRA/B/C, UBRR | `0x0C, 0x0B, 0x0A, 0x20` | Serial comm | | SPI | SPCR, SPSR, SPDR | `0x0D, 0x0E, 0x0F` | High-speed serial | | TWI (I²C) | TWBR, TWSR, TWAR, TWDR, TWCR | `0x20, 0x21, 0x22, 0x23, 0x36` | 2-wire bus | | EEPROM | EEARH, EEARL, EEDR, EECR | `0x1F, 0x1E, 0x1D, 0x1C` | Non-volatile storage | | Stack Pointer | SPH, SPL | `0x3E, 0x3D` | 16-bit stack | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 ATmega32 Top-Level Functional Block Diagram The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete internal architecture** of the ATmega32. All node IDs follow the alphanumeric + letter-prefix rule to avoid Mermaid reserved-keyword conflicts. ```mermaid flowchart TB pwrclk[POWER AND CLOCK SUBSYSTEM] --> cpucore pwrclk[AVCC VCC AREF XTAL1 XTAL2 RESET]:::pwr cpucore[AVR 8 BIT CPU CORE]:::cpu cpucore --> alu[ALU 8 BIT]:::cpu cpucore --> regfile[REGISTER FILE R0 TO R31]:::cpu cpucore --> sreg[SREG STATUS REGISTER]:::cpu cpucore --> pc[PROGRAM COUNTER 14 BIT]:::cpu cpucore --> sp[STACK POINTER 16 BIT]:::cpu flash[FLASH PROGRAM MEMORY 32 KB]:::mem flash --> cpucore sram[SRAM DATA MEMORY 2 KB]:::mem sram --> cpucore eeprom[EEPROM 1 KB]:::mem eeprom --> cpucore porta[PORTA 8 BIT ADC0 TO ADC7]:::io portb[PORTB 8 BIT SPI]:::io portc[PORTC 8 BIT JTAG TWI]:::io portd[PORTD 8 BIT USART TIMERS]:::io cpucore --> porta cpucore --> portb cpucore --> portc cpucore --> portd timer0[TIMER COUNTER 0 8 BIT]:::per timer1[TIMER COUNTER 1 16 BIT]:::per timer2[TIMER COUNTER 2 8 BIT]:::per watchdog[WATCHDOG TIMER]:::per cpucore --> timer0 cpucore --> timer1 cpucore --> timer2 cpucore --> watchdog adc[ADC 10 BIT 8 CH]:::per cpucore --> adc adc --> porta usart[USART]:::per spi[SPI]:::per twi[TWI I2C]:::per cpucore --> usart cpucore --> spi cpucore --> twi intctrl[INTERRUPT UNIT]:::per cpucore --> intctrl intctrl --> timer0 intctrl --> timer1 intctrl --> timer2 intctrl --> adc intctrl --> usart classDef pwr fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,color:#000 classDef cpu fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#06c,color:#000 classDef mem fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#b88,color:#000 classDef io fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#2a7,color:#000 classDef per fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#639,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows indicate **data/control flow paths**. The CPU Core is the hub; everything either feeds instructions/data in (Flash, SRAM, EEPROM) or extends I/O outward (ports, timers, communication blocks). ## 4.2 Memory Map Visualization ```mermaid flowchart LR addr00[0x0000 to 0x001F
32 GENERAL REGISTERS]:::r1 addr01[0x0020 to 0x005F
64 IO REGISTERS]:::r2 addr02[0x0060 to 0x085F
INTERNAL SRAM 2 KB]:::r3 addr03[0x0860 to 0xFFFF
NOT IMPLEMENTED]:::r4 addr00 --> addr01 --> addr02 --> addr03 pflash[0x0000 to 0x7FFF
FLASH 32 KB
SEPARATE BUS]:::r5 peep[0x000 to 0x3FF
EEPROM 1 KB
SEPARATE BUS]:::r6 classDef r1 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#06c,color:#000 classDef r2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#b88,color:#000 classDef r3 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#2a7,color:#000 classDef r4 fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#639,color:#000 classDef r5 fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,color:#000 classDef r6 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#a55,color:#000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > Notice that **Flash and EEPROM are NOT in the data memory address space** – they are accessed via dedicated I/O registers and special instructions (`LPM`, `SPM` for Flash, `EEAR/EEDR/EECR` for EEPROM). Only SRAM is in the unified data space. ## 4.3 Power Supply & Reset Circuit Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB vcc5[VCC 5V REGULATED] --> avcc[AVCC 5V ANALOG] vcc5 --> dvcc[DVCC 5V DIGITAL] avcc --> aref[AREF PIN ADC REFERENCE] avcc --> adcunit[ADC MODULE] dvcc --> cpudig[CPU AND DIGITAL PERIPHERALS] gnd[GND COMMON] --> avcc gnd --> dvcc xtal1[XTAL1] --> osc[INTERNAL OSCILLATOR] xtal2[XTAL2] --> osc osc --> cpudig resetpin[RESET PIN] --> rstctrl[RESET CIRCUIT] brownout[BROWN OUT DETECTOR] --> rstctrl watchdog[WATCHDOG TIMER] --> rstctrl rstctrl --> cpudig classDef pwr fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,color:#000 classDef dig fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#06c,color:#000 classDef clk fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#b88,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] – Q1** > **Q: List any six key features of the ATmega32 microcontroller.** **Model Answer (Key Points):** 1. **8-bit AVR RISC architecture** with 131 instructions, most single-cycle. 2. **32 KB In-System Self-Programmable Flash**, 2 KB SRAM, 1 KB EEPROM. 3. **32 × 8-bit general purpose registers** directly connected to ALU. 4. **32 programmable I/O lines** across 4 ports (PORTA–PORTD). 5. **8-channel, 10-bit ADC** with optional differential input mode. 6. **Three timers/counters** (one 16-bit, two 8-bit) with separate prescalers. 7. **USART, SPI, and TWI (I²C)** serial communication interfaces. 8. **Operating voltage: 4.5 V – 5.5 V**, clock up to **16 MHz** → **16 MIPS throughput**. 9. **Six sleep modes** for low-power applications (Idle, Power-down, Power-save, etc.). --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024] – Q2** > **Q: Differentiate between Harvard and Von Neumann architectures with reference to ATmega32.** **Model Answer (Comparison Table in Text):** | Aspect | Harvard (ATmega32) | Von Neumann (e.g., 8051 classic) | |---|---|---| | **Bus structure** | Separate buses for Program and Data memory | Single shared bus for both | | **Simultaneous access** | Yes – instruction fetch and data read can occur in the same cycle | No – bus contention | | **Speed** | Higher throughput (single-cycle execution) | Slower (fetch + execute sequentially) | | **Complexity** | More pins / complex internal routing | Simpler hardware | | **Application** | Used in **ATmega32** for high-performance embedded control | Used in 8051 for cost-sensitive designs | The ATmega32 uses a **modified Harvard architecture** – separate physical memories for program and data, but with the ability to read program memory as data using the `LPM` instruction. --- ## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] – Model Question Paper Q4 (Module 4)]** ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) – **Option 1** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of the ATmega32 microcontroller**. List its key subsystems. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The ATmega32 internal architecture is organized around the **AVR enhanced RISC CPU core** with the following key subsystems: **1. CPU Core:** - **ALU** – performs 8-bit arithmetic, logic, and bit operations in a single clock cycle. - **32 General Purpose Registers (R0–R31)** – directly connected to ALU to avoid accumulator bottlenecks. - **Status Register (SREG)** – holds arithmetic flags (C, Z, N, V, S, H, T, I). - **Program Counter (PC, 14-bit)** – addresses up to 32 KB Flash. - **Stack Pointer (SP, 16-bit)** – implemented as SPH:SPL for SRAM stack management. **2. Memory Subsystem (Modified Harvard):** - **Flash (32 KB):** Non-volatile program storage, accessed by 14-bit PC; instruction length is 16 bits. - **SRAM (2 KB):** Volatile data memory at addresses `0x0060–0x085F`. - **EEPROM (1 KB):** Non-volatile data, accessed via EEAR/EEDR/EECR registers. **3. I/O Subsystem:** - **Four 8-bit I/O ports (PORTA, PORTB, PORTC, PORTD)** controlled by DDRx, PORTx, PINx. - Each pin can be independently configured as input/output with optional internal pull-up. **4. Peripheral Subsystem:** - **Three Timer/Counters:** 8-bit (T0, T2), 16-bit (T1) with PWM, capture, compare modes. - **ADC:** 8-channel, 10-bit successive approximation, with multiplexer. - **Communication:** USART (asynchronous/synchronous), SPI (master/slave), TWI (I²C-compatible). - **Analog Comparator** on dedicated pins. - **Watchdog Timer** with on-chip oscillator for system reset on hang. **5. Interrupt Subsystem:** - 21 interrupt sources; vectored interrupt controller; global enable via SREG I-bit. **6. Power Management:** - Six sleep modes (Idle, ADC Noise Reduction, Power-save, Power-down, Standby, Extended Standby) for power-sensitive designs. [Refer to **Section 4.1 Block Diagram** for the supporting schematic.] --- **(b)** Explain the **memory organization of ATmega32** with a clear memory map. Compute the **end address of internal SRAM**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The ATmega32 has three physically separate memories, but the data memory is mapped in a unified linear address space: **Memory Organization Table:** | Memory Type | Size | Address Range | Address Bus Used | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | Flash (Program) | 32 KB | `0x0000 – 0x7FFF` | 14-bit PC | 16-bit wide words | | 32 GPRs | 32 B | `0x0000 – 0x001F` | Data bus | Mapped to data space | | 64 I/O Registers | 64 B | `0x0020 – 0x005F` | Data bus | Accessed via IN/OUT | | Internal SRAM | 2 KB | `0x0060 – 0x085F` | Data bus | Volatile workspace | | EEPROM | 1 KB | `0x000 – 0x3FF` | Separate registers | Non-volatile storage | **SRAM End Address Calculation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SRAM start address} &= 0x0060 \\ \text{SRAM size} &= 2 \text{ KB} = 2 \times 1024 = 2048 \text{ bytes} = 0x0800 \\ \text{SRAM end address} &= 0x0060 + 0x0800 - 1 \\ &= 0x085F \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ **Flash Memory End Address Calculation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Flash size} &= 32 \text{ KB} = 0x8000 \text{ bytes} = 16{,}384 \text{ words} \\ \text{Flash start (word address)} &= 0x0000 \\ \text{Flash end (word address)} &= 0x3FFF \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse the **word address** and **byte address** of Flash. The PC holds a **word address** (0x0000–0x3FFF) for Flash, but the assembler accepts a **byte address** (0x0000–0x7FFF) when using word-oriented data. Mixing them up is the #1 reason students lose marks on memory questions. --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) – **Option 2 (Internal Choice)** **(a)** Explain the **role of the Status Register (SREG) in ATmega32**. List and briefly describe all eight status flags. Also explain the function of **three pointer registers X, Y, Z** formed from R26–R31. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **SREG (Status Register) – Address `0x005F`:** The SREG is an 8-bit register whose bits reflect the result of the most recently executed arithmetic/logic operation. It is critical for decision-making (branch/jump) instructions. | Bit | Name | Description | |---|---|---| | **I (bit 7)** | Global Interrupt Enable | Must be set (`1`) for any maskable interrupt to be serviced. | | **T (bit 6)** | Bit Copy Storage | Used by `BLD` and `BST` instructions to copy/store individual bits. | | **H (bit 5)** | Half Carry | Set when carry from bit 3 to bit 4 occurs (BCD arithmetic). | | **S (bit 4)** | Sign Bit | `S = N ⊕ V` (exclusive OR of Negative and Two's complement overflow). | | **V (bit 3)** | Two's Complement Overflow | Set when signed arithmetic overflow occurs. | | **N (bit 2)** | Negative | Copy of MSB of result (1 = negative). | | **Z (bit 1)** | Zero | Set when result is zero; cleared otherwise. | | **C (bit 0)** | Carry | Set when carry/borrow occurs in arithmetic or shift operations. | **Pointer Registers X, Y, Z:** The upper six GPRs (R26–R31) can be paired into three **16-bit pointer registers**, used for indirect addressing of data memory (SRAM). - **X-register:** R26 (XL) and R27 (XH) → used by `LD`/`ST` instructions. - **Y-register:** R28 (YL) and R29 (YH) → used by `LDD`/`STD` with displacement. - **Z-register:** R30 (ZL) and R31 (ZH) → used by `LPM` (program memory read), `IJMP`, `ICALL`. For example: `LD R16, X+` loads R16 from address X, then auto-increments X. --- **(b)** With a suitable circuit and equations, derive the **UBRR value for a USART communication at 9600 baud with a 16 MHz crystal** in ATmega32. Compute the **percentage error**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **USART Configuration:** - Mode: Asynchronous Normal (`U2X = 0`). - Frame: 8 data bits, no parity, 1 stop bit (8-N-1). - Crystal frequency: $f_{CLK} = 16 \text{ MHz}$. **Formula:** $$ UBRR = \frac{f_{CLK}}{16 \times BAUD} - 1 $$ **Substitution:** $$ \begin{aligned} UBRR &= \frac{16 \times 10^6}{16 \times 9600} - 1 \\ &= \frac{16 \times 10^6}{153{,}600} - 1 \\ &= 104.1667 - 1 \\ &= 103.1667 \end{aligned} $$ UBRR must be an integer (12-bit register, range 0–4095). Round to nearest integer: **UBRR = 103**. **Actual Baud Rate Achieved:** $$ BAUD_{actual} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{16 \times (103 + 1)} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{1664} \approx 9615.38 $$ **Percentage Error:** $$ \begin{aligned} \% \text{Error} &= \left| \frac{BAUD_{actual} - BAUD_{desired}}{BAUD_{desired}} \right| \times 100 \\ &= \left| \frac{9615.38 - 9600}{9600} \right| \times 100 \\ &= \left| \frac{15.38}{9600} \right| \times 100 \\ &\approx 0.16\% \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ Since the error is well within the **±2% tolerance** for reliable UART communication, **UBRR = 103 is acceptable**. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Don't forget the `-1`** in the UBRR formula. Many students write `UBRR = f_CLK / (16 × BAUD)`. > 2. **UBRR is a 12-bit register** in ATmega32 (split into UBRRH and UBRRL), not 16-bit. Reading/writing order matters: **always write UBRRH first, then UBRRL**. > 3. If error exceeds 2%, switch to **U2X = 1 (double speed mode)** or use a different crystal (e.g., 14.7456 MHz, which gives zero error at 9600 baud). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Typically Lose Marks in Module 4 (ATmega32 Architecture):** > 1. **Confusing "Harvard" vs "Modified Harvard":** The ATmega32 has a *modified* Harvard architecture – program and data buses are separate, but the CPU can read program memory as data using `LPM`. Stating "pure Harvard" is **half a mark lost**. > 2. **Forgetting the Stack Pointer is 16-bit:** SP is implemented as two 8-bit I/O registers (SPH, SPL). Students often write "8-bit stack pointer" – **wrong**. > 3. **Mixing word vs byte addresses for Flash:** The 14-bit PC is a *word* address. Use word addresses unless the question specifies byte. > 4. **SREG bit numbering:** SREG bits are numbered 7 to 0 (MSB to LSB). I-bit is bit 7, C-bit is bit 0. Reversing this loses a mark. > 5. **Misstating the ADC resolution:** ATmega32 ADC is **10-bit**, not 8-bit or 12-bit. > 6. **Confusing PORTA with PORTx registers:** Each port has 3 control registers (DDRx, PORTx, PINx) – not 2. Many students miss PINx. > 7. **Timer/Counter sizes:** T0 and T2 are **8-bit**; T1 is **16-bit**. This is a common board question. > 8. **Forgetting to subtract 1 in UBRR calculation:** The most common arithmetic error in Module 4. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Final 60-Second Revision – Memorize Before Exam:** - ✅ ATmega32 is an **8-bit AVR RISC** microcontroller by **Atmel/Microchip**. - ✅ **Architecture:** Modified Harvard, **131 instructions**, most single-cycle. - ✅ **CPU Throughput:** **16 MIPS @ 16 MHz** (1 MIPS per MHz). - ✅ **Program Counter:** **14 bits wide** → max Flash = **32 KB (16K × 16-bit words)**. - ✅ **Register File:** **32 × 8-bit GPRs (R0–R31)** directly coupled to ALU. - ✅ **Last 6 GPRs (R26–R31)** pair as **X, Y, Z pointer registers** for indirect addressing. - ✅ **SREG (Status Register):** 8 bits – I, T, H, S, V, N, Z, C. - ✅ **Stack Pointer:** 16-bit, in I/O space at `0x3D` (SPL) and `0x3E` (SPH). - ✅ **Memories:** **Flash 32 KB**, **SRAM 2 KB**, **EEPROM 1 KB**. - ✅ **SRAM data space:** `0x0060 – 0x085F` (linear, unified with GPRs and I/O). - ✅ **4 I/O Ports:** PORTA–PORTD, **32 I/O pins**, each with DDRx, PORTx, PINx. - ✅ **Pin multiplexing:** PORTA → ADC, PORTB → SPI, PORTC → JTAG/TWI, PORTD → USART/Timers/INT. - ✅ **Timers:** T0 & T2 are **8-bit**; **T1 is 16-bit**; supports PWM, CTC, Input Capture. - ✅ **ADC:** **8-channel, 10-bit**, with prescalers 2/4/8/16/32/64/128; first conv 25 cycles, then 13 cycles each. - ✅ **Communication:** **USART** (RXD/TXD on PD0/PD1), **SPI** (4-wire on PORTB), **TWI / I²C** (on PORTC). - ✅ **Operating Voltage:** **4.5 V – 5.5 V**; clock **0 – 16 MHz**; **40-pin PDIP**. - ✅ **Power:** Active ~1.1 mA @ 1 MHz / 3 V; **6 sleep modes** for low-power design. - ✅ **UART baud rate formula:** $UBRR = \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{16 \times BAUD} - 1$ (asynchronous, U2X = 0). - ✅ **Watchdog Timer + Brown-out Detector + JTAG Debug** are in-built on chip. - ✅ **Flash endurance:** 10,000 W/E cycles; **EEPROM endurance:** 100,000 W/E cycles. - ✅ **Internal pull-up resistors:** 20–50 kΩ typical, enabled via PORTx = 1 when DDRx = 0. - ✅ **Atmel Studio / AVR-GCC** are the typical development environments for ATmega32. - ✅ The **Arduino Duemilanove/Uno** board uses the ATmega328P, which is a direct **successor/pin-compatible variant** of ATmega32.

topics.title: CPU Core, Memory Organization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT404 - MICROCONTROLLER PROGRAMMING & INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** CPU Core, Memory Organization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 CPU Core – Formal Definition

The **CPU (Central Processing Unit) Core** of a microcontroller is the central computational engine that fetches, decodes, and executes program instructions, while coordinating all peripheral subsystems through a structured register set, an arithmetic logic unit (ALU), and a control unit. In modern embedded microcontrollers (such as those based on the ARM Cortex-M architecture studied in the KTU PBEBT404 syllabus), the CPU core is a 32-bit, load-store, RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) processor optimized for deterministic, real-time embedded applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** The CPU core is the datapath + control unit of the microcontroller. It is responsible for instruction cycle execution (Fetch → Decode → Execute), arithmetic/logic operations via the ALU, and data movement through general-purpose and special-function registers.

### 1.2 Memory Organization – Formal Definition

**Memory Organization** refers to the structured way in which program instructions and data are stored, addressed, and accessed within the microcontroller. It encompasses the memory map (the linear address space), memory sections (Code, SRAM, Peripheral, System), the bus matrix (which arbitrates access to these sections), the stack pointer mechanism, and the distinction between architectural memory models (Harvard vs. Von Neumann).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Highlight:** Modern ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers use a **modified Harvard architecture** — physically separate buses for instruction fetch (I-Code) and data access (D-Code), but logically present a unified 32-bit linear address space to the programmer.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the microcontroller as a **post office**:
- The **CPU Core** is the **postmaster** — receives letters (instructions), opens them, decides what to do, and dispatches workers.
- The **ALU** is the **calculation desk** — adds stamps, weighs parcels, performs simple decisions.
- The **Registers** are the **postmaster's personal desk drawers** — tiny, ultra-fast storage spots immediately at hand.
- The **Memory Map** is the **post office's building layout** — where the mail room (Flash), parcel room (SRAM), and specialized departments (peripherals) are physically located.
- The **Bus System** is the **internal elevator and corridor system** — carrying parcels between rooms.

When a "letter" (instruction) arrives, the postmaster (CPU) reads it from the mail room (Flash), opens it at the desk (decodes), acts on it (executes via ALU), and may temporarily put things in a drawer (register) or store them in a long-term locker (SRAM).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants/Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Word size:** 32 bits (4 bytes) for ARM Cortex-M
> - **Program Counter (PC) width:** 32 bits → address space = 2³² = **4 GB**
> - **Stack Pointer alignment:** 8-byte aligned (for AAPCS compliance)
> - **Reset vector location:** 0x00000000
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of the CPU Core

The CPU core of an ARM Cortex-M based microcontroller consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Register Bank (Register File)**
   - **R0 – R12:** General-purpose 32-bit registers, freely usable by the programmer.
   - **R13 (SP – Stack Pointer):** Banked in some architectures; points to the top of the active stack. Two physical SPs: MSP (Main Stack Pointer) and PSP (Process Stack Pointer).
   - **R14 (LR – Link Register):** Stores the return address when a subroutine call (BL/BLX) is executed.
   - **R15 (PC – Program Counter):** Holds the address of the next instruction to fetch (current instruction + 4 in Thumb-2 mode).
   - **xPSR (Program Status Register):** A composite register made of APSR (flags), IPSR (interrupt number), and EPSR (execution state).

2. **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)**
   - Performs 32-bit integer addition, subtraction, bitwise logic (AND, OR, XOR, NOT), shifts, and rotations.
   - Generates condition flags in APSR: **N (Negative), Z (Zero), C (Carry), V (Overflow)**.

3. **Barrel Shifter**
   - Performs any shift/rotate operation in a single clock cycle (e.g., LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR by any amount from 0–31).

4. **Control Unit & Decoder**
   - Decodes the fetched 16/32-bit Thumb/Thumb-2 instruction and generates the micro-sequenced control signals.

5. **Bus Interface Unit**
   - Issues bus transactions on the **AHB-Lite** system bus, separating instruction (I-Code) and data (D-Code) accesses via the **Code and System bus matrices**.

### 2.2 Memory Architecture Models

| **Architecture** | **Instruction & Data Buses** | **Memory Space** | **Example MCUs** | **Key Property** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Von Neumann** | Shared single bus | Unified | 8051 (older), general-purpose CPUs | Bottleneck: instruction fetch and data read cannot occur simultaneously |
| **Harvard** | Physically separate | Physically separate | Classic DSPs, some PIC MCUs | Simultaneous fetch + data access, but complex addressing |
| **Modified Harvard (used in ARM Cortex-M)** | Separate buses, unified logical map | Unified 4 GB linear | STM32, LPC1768, Tiva-C | Best of both worlds — performance + programmer simplicity |

### 2.3 The 4 GB Memory Map of ARM Cortex-M

The 32-bit address space is divided into well-defined regions. Memorize the following layout:

| **Region** | **Address Range** | **Size** | **Purpose** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Code (Flash)** | 0x0000 0000 – 0x1FFF FFFF | 0.5 GB | Program code, vector table, optional boot ROM |
| **SRAM** | 0x2000 0000 – 0x3FFF FFFF | 0.5 GB | On-chip SRAM, bit-band alias 0x2200 0000 |
| **Peripheral** | 0x4000 0000 – 0x5FFF FFFF | 0.5 GB | On-chip peripherals (GPIO, UART, timers) |
| **External RAM** | 0x6000 0000 – 0x7FFF FFFF | 0.5 GB | Off-chip memory (external bus, if present) |
| **External Device** | 0x8000 0000 – 0x9FFF FFFF | 0.5 GB | External memory-mapped devices |
| **System** | 0xE000 0000 – 0xFFFF FFFF | 0.5 GB | NVIC, SysTick, MPU, debug, vendor peripherals |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bit-Banding:** A unique Cortex-M feature allowing atomic bit-level read/write in the SRAM (0x2000 0000) and Peripheral (0x4000 0000) regions using an alias address formula.

### 2.4 The Stack & Memory Allocation

- **Stack:** A LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) memory region used for:
  - Saving local variables
  - Saving context during function calls (push of LR, R4–R11)
  - Saving context on interrupt entry (hardware pushes R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR)

- **Heap:** Used for dynamic memory allocation (`malloc`, `new`). Grows upward.
- **Stack direction in ARM Cortex-M:** Grows **downward** (full-descending model).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Rule** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| Address space size | $A = 2^{n}$ | $n$ = address bus bits; for $n=32$, $A = 4\,\text{GB}$ |
| Bit-band alias address | $A_{alias} = A_{base} + 0x02000000 + (8 \times n) + b \times 4$ | $A_{base}$ ∈ {0x20000000, 0x40000000}; $n$ = byte offset, $b$ = bit position (0–7) |
| PC increment | $PC_{next} = PC_{current} + I_{size}$ | $I_{size}$ = 2 (Thumb) or 4 (Thumb-2 32-bit) |
| Stack full-descending | $SP_{new} = SP_{old} - 4$ | On a 32-bit PUSH |
| Little-endian load | $\text{LSB at address } A, \text{ MSB at } A+3$ | Cortex-M is natively little-endian |
| Memory needed for variables | $M = \sum (\text{type\_size} \times \text{count})$ | Type sizes: char=1, short=2, int=4, float=4, double=8 |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Memory map awareness** is essential when writing **linker scripts** (`.ld` / `.scatter`) to place code in Flash and variables in SRAM.
- **Bit-banding** is used in **RTOS task synchronization** and **atomic GPIO manipulation** in safety-critical systems (automotive ECU, medical devices).
- **Stack management** directly impacts **interrupt latency** and **system reliability** — a stack overflow can corrupt SRAM and crash the firmware.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Addressable Memory from Address Bus Width

Given that the ARM Cortex-M CPU core uses a **32-bit** program counter, the maximum addressable memory locations is computed as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of addresses} &= 2^{n} \\
\text{where } n &= \text{width of address bus in bits} \\
n &= 32 \\
\text{Number of addresses} &= 2^{32}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to gigabytes (using $1\,\text{GB} = 2^{30}\,\text{bytes}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Addressable Memory} &= \frac{2^{32}\,\text{bytes}}{2^{30}\,\text{bytes/GB}} \\
&= 2^{2}\,\text{GB} \\
&= 4\,\text{GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 32-bit CPU core can address exactly **4 GB** of unique memory locations, divided architecturally as shown in the memory map table above.

### 3.2 Derivation: Bit-Band Alias Address Calculation

The Cortex-M bit-banding mechanism allows direct aliasing of every bit in the 1 MB bit-band region (0x2000 0000 – 0x200F FFFF for SRAM) to a unique 32-bit word in the alias region (0x2200 0000 – 0x23FF FFFF).

For a given **byte address** $A$ within the bit-band region and a **bit position** $b$ (0 to 7), the corresponding **alias word address** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{alias} &= A_{base} + (A - A_{bb\_region}) \times 32 + b \times 4 \\
&= 0x22000000 + (A - 0x20000000) \times 32 + b \times 4
\end{aligned}
$$

Equivalently:

$$
A_{alias} = 0x02000000 + 0x22000000 + (8 \cdot n) + (4 \cdot b)
$$

where $n$ is the byte offset within the bit-band region.

**Worked Example (KTU-style):**
Compute the alias address for bit 5 of the byte at SRAM address 0x2000 0100.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{base} &= 0x22000000 \\
A - A_{bb\_region} &= 0x20000100 - 0x20000000 = 0x100 = 256 \text{ (decimal)} \\
A_{alias} &= 0x22000000 + (256 \times 32) + (5 \times 4) \\
&= 0x22000000 + 8192 + 20 \\
&= 0x22000000 + 0x2000 + 0x14 \\
&= 0x22002014
\end{aligned}
$$

So writing to address `0x22002014` is equivalent to **atomically setting or clearing bit 5** of SRAM byte `0x20000100`, in a single, non-interruptible bus transaction.

### 3.3 Derivation: Stack Pointer Behaviour on PUSH/POP

The Cortex-M uses a **full-descending** stack. When a 32-bit register is PUSHed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SP_{new} &= SP_{old} - 4 \\
\text{Memory}[SP_{new}] &= \text{Register value}
\end{aligned}
$$

When POPed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Register} &= \text{Memory}[SP_{old}] \\
SP_{new} &= SP_{old} + 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** If `SP = 0x2000 0500` and we execute `PUSH {R0, R1, R2, R3}`, the new SP value is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SP_{new} &= 0x20000500 - (4 \times 4) \\
&= 0x20000500 - 0x10 \\
&= 0x200004F0
\end{aligned}
$$

R3 is stored at `0x200004F0`, R2 at `0x200004F4`, R1 at `0x200004F8`, R0 at `0x200004FC`.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Register Roles During a Subroutine Call

| **Step** | **Instruction** | **R0–R3 (args)** | **R4–R11 (vars)** | **R12 (scratch)** | **R13 (SP)** | **R14 (LR)** | **R15 (PC)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Before `BL func` | — | Caller's args | Caller's vars | — | Caller's SP | — | Caller's PC |
| 2. Execute `BL func` | Branch with link | — | — | — | — | `PC+4` (return addr) | Address of `func` |
| 3. Inside `func`, prologue `PUSH {R4–R11, LR}` | Stack save | — | Saved on stack | — | Decreased by 32 | Saved on stack | — |
| 4. Use of locals | — | Args passed | Restored later | Temp use | — | — | — |
| 5. Epilogue `POP {R4–R11, PC}` | Atomic restore | — | Restored from stack | — | Increased by 32 | — | Loaded with return address |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AAPCS (ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard)** mandates that **R0–R3 and R12 are caller-saved** (volatile), while **R4–R11 are callee-saved** (must be preserved across function calls).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: CPU Core Internal Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Instruction Fetch Unit] -->|I-Code Bus| B[Instruction Decoder]
    B --> C[Control Unit]
    C --> D[ALU]
    C --> E[Barrel Shifter]
    D --> F[Register File R0-R15]
    E --> F
    F -->|Read/Write| D
    C --> G[Bus Interface Unit]
    G -->|D-Code Bus| H[AHB-Lite System Bus]
    H --> I[Flash Memory]
    H --> J[SRAM]
    H --> K[Peripherals]
    C --> L[Exception / Interrupt Controller]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: 4 GB Memory Map of ARM Cortex-M

```mermaid
graph TB
    M[32-bit Address Space 0x00000000 to 0xFFFFFFFF]
    M --> N1[Code Region 0x00000000 to 0x1FFFFFFF 512 MB Flash + Vector Table]
    M --> N2[SRAM Region 0x20000000 to 0x3FFFFFFF 512 MB + Bit-band Alias]
    M --> N3[Peripheral Region 0x40000000 to 0x5FFFFFFF 512 MB On-chip Peripherals]
    M --> N4[External RAM 0x60000000 to 0x7FFFFFFF 512 MB]
    M --> N5[External Device 0x80000000 to 0x9FFFFFFF 512 MB]
    M --> N6[System Region 0xE0000000 to 0xFFFFFFFF 512 MB NVIC + Debug + Vendor]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Stack Push/POP Operation (Full-Descending)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Initial SP points to Top of Stack] --> B[Execute PUSH instruction]
    B --> C[Decrease SP by 4 for each register]
    C --> D[Store register value at new SP address]
    D --> E[Repeat for all registers in list]
    E --> F[SP now points to lowest stored register]
    F --> G[Later: Execute POP instruction]
    G --> H[Read value from current SP address]
    H --> I[Increase SP by 4]
    I --> J[Load read value into target register]
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture: Modified Harvard Bus System

```mermaid
graph LR
    CPU[CPU Core] --> BIU[Bus Interface Unit]
    BIU -->|I-Code Bus| IFLASH[Flash Instructions]
    BIU -->|D-Code Bus| DSRAM[SRAM Data]
    BIU -->|System Bus| PERI[Peripherals]
    BIU -->|APB Bridge| APIP[APB Peripherals]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight:** The I-Code and D-Code buses allow the CPU to perform a **simultaneous instruction fetch and data load** in the same clock cycle — this is the performance benefit of the modified Harvard design, even though the **programmer sees a unified address space**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] *Define the Program Counter (PC) and Link Register (LR) in the ARM Cortex-M CPU core. What value does the LR hold immediately after a `BL subroutine` instruction executes?*

**Model Answer:**
- The **Program Counter (R15)** is a 32-bit register that holds the address of the next instruction to be fetched by the CPU core.
- The **Link Register (R14)** stores the return address when a subroutine is called.
- Immediately after `BL subroutine` executes, **LR contains the address of the instruction following the BL** (i.e., `PC + 4`). This address is used by `BX LR` or `POP {PC}` to return to the caller.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] *List the four main regions of the 4 GB memory map of an ARM Cortex-M microcontroller. State the address range of the System region and name two components located in it.*

**Model Answer:**
The four main regions are:
1. **Code** (0x0000 0000 – 0x1FFF FFFF) – Flash/ROM for program storage.
2. **SRAM** (0x2000 0000 – 0x3FFF FFFF) – Working data memory.
3. **Peripheral** (0x4000 0000 – 0x5FFF FFFF) – Memory-mapped peripherals.
4. **System** (0xE000 0000 – 0xFFFF FFFF) – Private system bus.

**System region range:** 0xE000 0000 – 0xFFFF FFFF.
**Two components:** **NVIC (Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller)** at 0xE000 E000 and **SysTick Timer** at 0xE000 E010.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of an ARM Cortex-M CPU core. List the functions of the ALU, Barrel Shifter, and Bus Interface Unit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the 4 GB memory map of the ARM Cortex-M microcontroller. Discuss the purpose of the bit-band alias region and compute the alias address for bit 3 of the byte located at SRAM address 0x2000 0020. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Internal Architecture of ARM Cortex-M CPU Core:**
The CPU core comprises the following major blocks (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

- **Register File (R0–R15, xPSR):** Provides 16 general-purpose 32-bit registers and a composite status register. R13 is the Stack Pointer (SP), R14 is the Link Register (LR), and R15 is the Program Counter (PC). The file supports two simultaneous read ports and one write port for the ALU.

- **ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit):** Performs 32-bit addition, subtraction, bitwise logic (AND, OR, XOR, NOT, bit clear), and comparison. It updates the APSR flags (N, Z, C, V) after every data-processing operation.

- **Barrel Shifter:** Performs any shift (LSL, LSR, ASR) or rotation (ROR) from 0 to 31 positions in a **single clock cycle**, eliminating the need for iterative shift loops.

- **Bus Interface Unit (BIU):** Initiates instruction fetches on the I-Code bus and data accesses on the D-Code/System bus using the AHB-Lite protocol. It handles address alignment, byte/half-word sign extension, and endian conversion (little-endian default).

- **Control Unit & Decoder:** Decodes the 16-bit Thumb or 32-bit Thumb-2 instruction and orchestrates the datapath signals in the correct sequence for each instruction's micro-operation.

---

**(b) 4 GB Memory Map & Bit-Band Calculation:**

**Memory Map (recap):**
| Region | Range | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Code | 0x0000 0000 – 0x1FFF FFFF | Flash program memory, vector table |
| SRAM | 0x2000 0000 – 0x3FFF FFFF | Data RAM, bit-band alias starts at 0x2200 0000 |
| Peripheral | 0x4000 0000 – 0x5FFF FFFF | Memory-mapped I/O peripherals |
| External RAM | 0x6000 0000 – 0x7FFF FFFF | External memory (FSMC on STM32) |
| External Device | 0x8000 0000 – 0x9FFF FFFF | External memory-mapped devices |
| System | 0xE000 0000 – 0xFFFF FFFF | NVIC, SysTick, MPU, Debug |

**Purpose of Bit-Band Alias:**
The bit-band alias region allows **atomic, single-instruction read-modify-write** operations on individual bits in the bit-band region. This is critical in **multithreaded/RTOS environments** where two tasks might otherwise race on a shared flag. Each bit in the 1 MB SRAM bit-band region maps to a full 32-bit word in the alias region, so writing 1 to the alias word sets the bit, writing 0 clears it — interrupt-safe by hardware guarantee.

**Numerical Computation:**
Given: $A = 0x20000020$, $b = 3$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{base} &= 0x22000000 \\
A - A_{bb\_region} &= 0x20000020 - 0x20000000 = 0x20 = 32 \text{ decimal} \\
A_{alias} &= 0x22000000 + (32 \times 32) + (3 \times 4) \\
&= 0x22000000 + 1024 + 12 \\
&= 0x22000000 + 0x400 + 0xC \\
&= 0x2200040C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The alias address is **0x2200040C**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**(a)** Compare Von Neumann, Harvard, and Modified Harvard architectures. Which one is used in ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers and why? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the stack memory organization in ARM Cortex-M. What is the difference between MSP and PSP? Describe what happens to the stack and CPU registers when an interrupt is accepted by the NVIC. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture Comparison:**

| Feature | Von Neumann | Harvard | Modified Harvard |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bus structure | Single shared bus for code & data | Separate code & data buses | Separate physical buses, unified address space |
| Simultaneous fetch & data access | **Not possible** (bus contention) | **Possible** | **Possible** |
| Memory addressing | Single linear map | Two distinct maps | Single linear map from programmer view |
| Hardware complexity | Low | High | Medium |
| Performance | Limited by bus bottleneck | High | High |
| Example | 8051, x86 | DSPs, classic PIC | **ARM Cortex-M (STM32, LPC, Tiva-C)** |

**Used in ARM Cortex-M:** The **Modified Harvard** architecture is used. Reason: it provides the **simultaneous instruction fetch + data access** performance of Harvard, while keeping the **programmer-friendly unified 4 GB linear address space**. This simplifies compiler and linker design, while still allowing cycle-accurate deterministic execution for real-time embedded systems — a key requirement of PBEBT404 lab applications.

---

**(b) Stack & Interrupt Context Handling:**

**Stack Organization in Cortex-M:**
- The stack is a region of SRAM, configured as **full-descending** (grows toward lower addresses).
- There are **two physical Stack Pointers**:
  - **MSP (Main Stack Pointer):** Used by the kernel, exception handlers (ISRs), and after reset.
  - **PSP (Process Stack Pointer):** Used by application tasks in RTOS environments (Thread mode with CONTROL bit set).
- Only **one SP is active at a time**, selected by the **SPSEL bit** in the CONTROL register.

**Interrupt Acceptance Sequence (Hardware-Automated):**
When the NVIC accepts an interrupt request, the following actions occur **automatically in hardware** (no instruction overhead):

1. The CPU pushes the following 8 registers onto the active stack (MSP or PSP based on current mode):
   - **xPSR** (with the IPSR set to the interrupt number)
   - **PC** (return address — next instruction that was not executed)
   - **LR** (set to a special value `EXC_RETURN`)
   - **R12, R3, R2, R1, R0** (caller-saved registers)

2. The stack pointer decreases by 8 × 4 = 32 bytes:

$$
SP_{new} = SP_{old} - 32
$$

3. The vector for the ISR is fetched from the vector table (in Code region).

4. The ISR executes. The **LR** contains a special 32-bit value (`EXC_RETURN`) that encodes the return mode and stack selection.

5. On `BX LR` or `POP {PC}` in the ISR, the hardware:
   - Pops R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR from the stack.
   - The PC is loaded with the previously saved return address — resuming the interrupted code.
   - The stack pointer increases by 32 bytes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to save R4–R11 in ISRs:** Unlike R0–R3, the registers R4–R11 are **not auto-pushed** during interrupt entry. ISRs that use them **must** explicitly PUSH/POP, otherwise caller context is corrupted.
> 2. **Confusing MSP and PSP:** Many students write "SP" generically. Always state whether MSP or PSP is being used, and that the active SP is selected via CONTROL.SPSEL.
> 3. **Wrong PC increment:** In Thumb-2 mode, the PC reads as `current instruction + 4` (not +2, even for 16-bit instructions). Miscalculating LR leads to off-by-4 returns and crashes.
> 4. **Forgetting the EXC_RETURN mechanism:** Cortex-M uses `EXC_RETURN` in LR to trigger unstacking — students often think LR holds a normal return address during ISRs, which is wrong.
> 5. **Bit-band region bounds:** Bit-banding works **only** in SRAM (0x2000 0000 – 0x200F FFFF) and Peripheral (0x4000 0000 – 0x400F FFFF) bit-band regions — not the full SRAM region. Students often misapply the formula.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⭐ The **CPU core** of an ARM Cortex-M is a **32-bit RISC, load-store, modified-Harvard** processor with **16 registers** (R0–R15) plus the composite **xPSR**.
- ⭐ **R13 = SP**, **R14 = LR**, **R15 = PC**. R0–R12 are general purpose.
- ⭐ **R0–R3 and R12 are caller-saved** (volatile); **R4–R11 are callee-saved** (preserved across function calls — AAPCS).
- ⭐ PC always reads as **current instruction address + 4** in Thumb/Thumb-2 mode.
- ⭐ The 32-bit address bus provides **2³² = 4 GB** of addressable memory space.
- ⭐ The four primary regions are: **Code, SRAM, Peripheral, System**; plus External RAM and External Device.
- ⭐ The **System region (0xE000 0000 – 0xFFFF FFFF)** contains **NVIC, SysTick, MPU, and debug** components.
- ⭐ The stack is **full-descending**; **PUSH decreases SP**, **POP increases SP** by 4 bytes per register.
- ⭐ Two SPs exist: **MSP (kernel/exception)** and **PSP (application task)** — selected by **CONTROL.SPSEL**.
- ⭐ On interrupt entry, hardware **auto-pushes 8 words** (R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR) = **32 bytes**; on exit, `EXC_RETURN` in LR triggers unstacking.
- ⭐ **Bit-banding** maps every bit in 1 MB of SRAM and 1 MB of peripheral region to a 32-bit alias word, enabling **atomic bit manipulation**.
- ⭐ Endianness is **little-endian** natively in ARM Cortex-M.
- ⭐ Reset vector is at **0x00000000**; the **vector table** (initial SP + reset handler address) is at the start of the Code region.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to PIC Microcontrollers: Architectural Features, Core and Peripheral Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT404 - MICROCONTROLLER PROGRAMMING & INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to PIC Microcontrollers: Architectural Features, Core and Peripheral Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a PIC Microcontroller

A **PIC (Peripheral Interface Controller)** is a family of **Harvard-architecture**, **RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)**-based microcontrollers manufactured originally by **Microchip Technology** (formerly General Instruments). PIC microcontrollers are single-chip computers that integrate a CPU, RAM, ROM/Flash, I/O ports, timers, ADC, and communication peripherals into one IC package.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PBEBT404 – Module 4):**
> "PIC microcontrollers are 8-bit, 16-bit, and 32-bit RISC-based single-chip controllers employing Harvard architecture, characterized by separate program and data buses, a small fixed instruction set, and rich on-chip peripherals."

In the KTU 2024 scheme, the most studied variant is the **PIC16F877A** — an 8-bit Flash-based CMOS microcontroller belonging to the **Mid-Range PIC16 family**, which offers the right balance of complexity and pedagogical value for undergraduate engineering.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Office Building" Analogy — How a PIC Works**
> 
> Imagine a small office building with **two separate elevators**:
> - **Elevator A** carries *blueprints* (program instructions) from the **Architect's Office (Program Memory / Flash ROM)**.
> - **Elevator B** carries *working papers and files* (data) from the **Staff Room (Data Memory / RAM)**.
> 
> Because the elevators are physically separate, the architect (CPU) can read a blueprint **and** receive a working file **at the same time** — this is **Harvard Architecture**, the core of every PIC.
> 
> The **reception desk (ALU)** does all arithmetic and logical work. The **notice board (Register File)** holds temporary sticky notes (working registers like W). The **mailroom (Stack)** keeps track of who to return to after a meeting (subroutine calls). The **intercom system (Peripherals — ADC, UART, Timers)** talks to the outside world.

This dual-bus separation is what makes PIC microcontrollers **fast, deterministic, and predictable** — exactly why they dominate embedded real-time systems.

## 1.3 Classification & Family Overview

| PIC Family | Data Bus | Typical IC | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| PIC10 / PIC12 | 8-bit | PIC12F508 | ❌ Too simple for syllabus |
| **PIC16 (Mid-Range)** | **8-bit** | **PIC16F877A** | ✅ **Primary KTU Focus** |
| PIC18 (Enhanced) | 8-bit | PIC18F4550 | ✅ Referenced for advanced features |
| PIC24 / dsPIC33 | 16-bit | dsPIC33FJ | ❌ Outside 2024 B.Tech scope |

> [!NOTE]
> The "**F**" in PIC16**F**877A indicates **Flash program memory** (re-programmable up to **100,000 erase/write cycles**) — making it ideal for laboratory and academic use.

## 1.4 Why PIC? — The Engineering Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Reasons PIC is taught in KTU B.Tech ECE/EEE:**
> 1. **RISC architecture** → only **35 single-word instructions** (PIC16) → easier assembly learning.
> 2. **Harvard architecture** → simultaneous fetch + execute → deterministic timing.
> 3. **Rich peripherals** → on-chip ADC, PWM, USART, I²C, SPI eliminate external ICs.
> 4. **Low power** → CMOS technology, **< 2 mA @ 5V @ 4 MHz**.
> 5. **Wide tool support** → MPLAB X IDE, XC8 compiler, Proteus simulation.
> 6. **Industrial ubiquity** → used in washing machines, automotive dashboards, medical pumps, IoT nodes.

---

## 1.5 PIC vs. 8051 — A Quick KTU Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests the comparison between PIC and 8051 architectures.

| Parameter | **8051** | **PIC16F877A** |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Von Neumann (CISC) | **Harvard (RISC)** |
| Program Memory | 4 KB ROM (external) | **8 KB Flash (internal)** |
| Data Memory | 128 bytes RAM | **368 bytes RAM** |
| Instruction Size | 1–3 bytes (variable) | **14-bit fixed word** |
| Instruction Count | 111 | **35** |
| Pipeline | None | **2-stage (Fetch + Execute)** |
| ADC | External required | **8-channel, 10-bit internal** |
| Power Consumption | Higher | **Lower (CMOS)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Philosophy: The RISC + Harvard Pairing

PIC microcontrollers are designed around **two intertwined design philosophies** that govern every aspect of their behaviour.

### 2.1.1 RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)

- **Fixed instruction length:** Every instruction is exactly **14 bits** wide (in PIC16 mid-range).
- **Single-cycle execution:** Most instructions execute in **1 instruction cycle = 4 clock cycles (Tcy = 4 × Tosc)**.
- **Load-store avoidance:** ALU operates directly on the accumulator (**W register**) — no need to always load from memory.
- **Orthogonal-ish register model:** Limited register set, but highly regular addressing modes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Fact:** In PIC16F877A, 1 instruction cycle = **4 oscillator periods**.
> 
> $$\boxed{T_{cy} = 4 \times T_{osc} = \frac{4}{f_{osc}}}$$

For a crystal of **$f_{osc} = 20 \text{ MHz}$**:

$$T_{cy} = \frac{4}{20 \times 10^6} = 200 \text{ ns}$$

So each instruction takes **200 ns** — which is why PICs are prized for **real-time control loops**.

### 2.1.2 Harvard Architecture (Dual Bus)

The CPU is connected to **two physically separate memory spaces** via **two independent buses**:

$$\boxed{\text{Program Memory} \xleftrightarrow{\text{14-bit bus}} \text{CPU} \xleftrightarrow{\text{8-bit bus}} \text{Data Memory}}$$

| Bus | Width | Connects | Cycle |
|---|---|---|---|
| Program Bus | 14-bit | Flash ROM ↔ Instruction Register | Fetch phase |
| Data Bus | 8-bit | RAM + SFRs ↔ ALU | Execute phase |

> [!NOTE]
> This is why PIC can **fetch the next instruction while executing the current one** — a **2-stage pipeline** (Fetch → Execute). In a Von-Neumann 8051, fetch and execute are sequential, halving throughput.

## 2.2 Core Architectural Block Diagram — Walkthrough

The internal architecture of PIC16F877A can be decomposed into **6 functional blocks**:

1. **Program Counter (PC) — 13-bit:** Can address $2^{13} = 8192$ locations → matches the 8 KB Flash (1 word = 14 bits, so 8K × 14 bits).
2. **Instruction Register & Decoder:** Decodes the 14-bit opcode.
3. **ALU (8-bit):** Performs arithmetic, logic, bit, and shift operations.
4. **W (Working) Register:** The implicit accumulator. **All two-operand instructions** use W as one source/destination.
5. **Register File (RAM):** 368 bytes split into **General Purpose Registers (GPR)** and **Special Function Registers (SFR)**.
6. **Stack (Hardware, 8 levels × 13-bit):** Stores return addresses on `CALL` / interrupt.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall — Stack Size:** PIC16F877A has only an **8-level hardware stack**. Nesting more than 8 subroutines causes **stack overflow** → unpredictable program behaviour. This is a common viva question.

## 2.3 Pin Diagram of PIC16F877A (40-pin DIP)

| Pin Group | Pins | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **OSC1, OSC2** | 13, 14 | Crystal / Clock input |
| **MCLR** | 1 | Master Clear Reset (active low) |
| **RA0–RA5** | 2–7 | PORTA — 6-bit bidirectional (also analog AN0–AN4) |
| **RB0–RB7** | 33–40 | PORTB — 8-bit (RB0 has interrupt capability) |
| **RC0–RC7** | 15, 16, 17, 18, 23, 24, 25, 26 | PORTC — 8-bit (also serial I/O) |
| **RD0–RD7** | 19–22, 27–30 | PORTD — 8-bit (parallel slave port) |
| **RE0–RE2** | 8, 9, 10 | PORTE — 3-bit (also AN5, AN6, AN7) |
| **VDD, VSS** | 11, 12, 31, 32 | +5V and Ground |

## 2.4 Memory Organization — The Most-Tested KTU Topic

### 2.4.1 Program Memory (Flash)

- Size: **8 K × 14 bits = 14,336 bits**.
- Addressed by the 13-bit PC: `0000h` to `1FFFh`.
- **Reset Vector:** `0000h`
- **Interrupt Vector:** `0004h`
- User code resides in `0005h` to `1FFFh`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The interrupt vector at `0004h` is **shared by ALL interrupts**. The ISR must poll interrupt flag bits (e.g., `T0IF`, `INTF`) to identify the source.

### 2.4.2 Data Memory (RAM) — Banked Architecture

Total data memory = **512 bytes** logical, **physically 368 bytes** available.

| Bank | Address Range | Content |
|---|---|---|
| Bank 0 | `00h – 7Fh` | SFRs (top half `00h–1Fh`) + GPR (`20h–7Fh`) |
| Bank 1 | `80h – FFh` | SFRs for TRIS, OPTION, etc. + GPR |
| Bank 2 | `100h – 17Fh` | GPR (extended) |
| Bank 3 | `180h – 1FFh` | GPR (extended) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Critical Concept — Bank Switching:**
> Special registers **STATUS<RP1:RP0>** (bits 6 and 5) select the active bank.
> 
> - `RP1:RP0 = 00` → Bank 0
> - `RP1:RP0 = 01` → Bank 1
> - `RP1:RP0 = 10` → Bank 2
> - `RP1:RP0 = 11` → Bank 3

To write to **TRISB** (in Bank 1), the code must switch banks first.

### 2.4.3 Special Function Registers (SFRs) — The Control Panel

| SFR | Address (Bank 0/1) | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **W (Working Reg)** | Not addressable (implicit) | Accumulator |
| **STATUS** | `03h` / `83h` | Flags (Z, C, DC, RP0, RP1, IRP, TO, PD) |
| **PORTA** | `05h` | Read/Write Port A Latch |
| **TRISA** | `85h` | Data direction: `1`=Input, `0`=Output |
| **PORTB** | `06h` | Port B Latch |
| **TRISB** | `86h` | Data direction B |
| **INTCON** | `0Bh` / `8Bh` | Interrupt Control Register |
| **PCLATH** | `0Ah` / `8Ah` | Upper 5 bits of PC for GOTO/CALL |
| **OPTION_REG** | `81h` | Prescaler, TMR0 clock source, pull-ups |
| **EECON1** | `18h` | Flash/EEPROM read-write control |
| **ADCON0** | `1Fh` | ADC channel select + start conversion |
| **ADCON1** | `9Fh` | ADC pin configuration (analog/digital) |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Value | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Instruction Cycle Time | $T_{cy} = 4 \times T_{osc} = \dfrac{4}{f_{osc}}$ | Seconds |
| Max Clock Frequency | $f_{osc, \max} = 20 \text{ MHz}$ | $T_{cy} = 200 \text{ ns}$ |
| Program Memory Size | $2^{PC\_bits} = 2^{13} = 8 \text{ K words}$ | 14-bit words |
| Data RAM (physical) | 368 bytes | 96 SFRs + 272 GPR |
| Stack Depth | 8 levels | 13-bit return addresses |
| ADC Resolution | $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{1024}$ | Volts per LSB |
| ADC Acquisition Time | $T_{acq} \geq 2 \text{ μs (typical)}$ | Min wait before conversion |
| Baud Rate (USART) | $\text{SPBRG} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{16 \times \text{Baud}} - 1$ | Asynchronous mode |
| Timer0 Overflow | $T_{overflow} = 4 \times T_{osc} \times (256 - \text{TMR0}) \times \text{Prescaler}$ | Seconds |
| PWM Period | $T_{PWM} = (PR2 + 1) \times 4 \times T_{osc} \times TMR2\_prescale$ | Seconds |
| PWM Duty Cycle | $\text{Duty} = \dfrac{CCPR1L : CCP1CON<5:4>}{4 \times (PR2 + 1)}$ | Fraction (0 to 1) |
| Interrupt Latency | 3 instruction cycles | After flag is set |
| Reset Pulse Width | $\geq 2 \text{ μs}$ (MCLR low) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note (repeated mistake):** Students often confuse **Tcy** with **Tosc**. Always remember:
> - **Tosc** = one clock pulse period (e.g., 50 ns @ 20 MHz).
> - **Tcy** = one instruction cycle = **4 × Tosc** = 200 ns @ 20 MHz.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The architectural decisions of PIC make it the controller of choice for:

- **Automotive:** Dashboard clusters, ECU sub-modules, wiper controllers.
- **Medical:** Insulin pumps, BP monitors (deterministic timing critical).
- **Industrial:** PLCs, motor drives (PWM + ADC feedback loop).
- **Consumer:** Washing machines, microwave ovens, TV remotes.
- **IoT Edge Nodes:** Low-power sleep modes + ADC + serial comms.

> The Harvard + RISC + 2-stage pipeline combination is the same philosophy that later inspired **ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers** — so understanding PIC gives the student a strong foundation for **STM32, Tiva-C, and SAMD** platforms as well.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Clock, Instruction Cycle, and Throughput

The PIC's timing model is hierarchical. We derive the relationship between the external crystal frequency, the instruction cycle, and the effective instruction throughput.

### Given

- External crystal frequency: $f_{osc} = 20 \text{ MHz}$
- PIC16 instruction cycle consists of 4 internal clock phases: $Q_1, Q_2, Q_3, Q_4$
- One full instruction cycle: $T_{cy}$

### Step 1 — Period of one oscillator pulse

The period of one external clock cycle is the reciprocal of frequency.

$$T_{osc} = \frac{1}{f_{osc}}$$

Substituting $f_{osc} = 20 \text{ MHz}$:

$$T_{osc} = \frac{1}{20 \times 10^6} \text{ s}$$

$$T_{osc} = 50 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} = 50 \text{ ns}$$

This is the **finest time quantum** of the system.

### Step 2 — Period of one instruction cycle

PIC divides each oscillator period internally into **4 quadrature phases** ($Q_1$ through $Q_4$) to handle **fetch, decode, read, and execute** operations. Therefore:

$$T_{cy} = 4 \times T_{osc}$$

Substituting:

$$T_{cy} = 4 \times 50 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{cy} = 200 \text{ ns}$$

### Step 3 — Instruction Throughput (MIPS)

If **most** instructions execute in **1 instruction cycle** (a defining RISC property), then:

$$\text{IPS} = \frac{1}{T_{cy}}$$

$$\text{IPS} = \frac{1}{200 \times 10^{-9}} = 5 \times 10^{6} \text{ instructions/second}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Throughput} = 5 \text{ MIPS at } 20 \text{ MHz}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Question:** "Why can't PIC16F877A execute instructions in a single clock cycle even though it is RISC?"
> 
> **Answer:** RISC minimizes the *number of cycles per instruction* (CPI), not the *clock period itself*. The 4-phase internal clock is needed for the Harvard architecture to perform **simultaneous program fetch (Q1)** and **data read/write (Q2–Q4)**. So one instruction still requires one instruction cycle (= 4 clock pulses), but this cycle is much shorter than Von-Neumann multi-cycle fetches.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Timer0 Overflow Time Calculation

Timer0 is an 8-bit up-counter that increments on every instruction cycle (or external edge). On overflow from `0xFF` → `0x00`, it sets the `T0IF` flag.

### Given

- $f_{osc} = 4 \text{ MHz}$
- Prescaler assigned to Timer0 with ratio $PS = 256$
- Initial value loaded: `TMR0 = 100`

### Step 1 — Compute the instruction cycle

$$T_{cy} = \frac{4}{f_{osc}} = \frac{4}{4 \times 10^6}$$

$$T_{cy} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ μs}$$

### Step 2 — Effective Timer Increment Period

With prescaler $PS$, Timer0 increments only every $PS$ instruction cycles:

$$T_{inc} = T_{cy} \times PS$$

$$T_{inc} = 1 \text{ μs} \times 256 = 256 \text{ μs}$$

### Step 3 — Counts Before Overflow

Timer0 is 8-bit, so it overflows after $256$ total counts from `0x00`. If preloaded with $TMR0_{init} = 100$, then counts remaining:

$$N = 256 - TMR0_{init} = 256 - 100 = 156$$

### Step 4 — Total Overflow Time

$$T_{overflow} = N \times T_{inc}$$

$$T_{overflow} = 156 \times 256 \text{ μs}$$

$$T_{overflow} = 39\,936 \text{ μs}$$

$$T_{overflow} \approx 39.94 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 5 — Generalised Formula

$$\boxed{T_{overflow} = (256 - TMR0_{init}) \times 4 \times T_{osc} \times PS}$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the prescaler is **not** assigned to Timer0, set $PS = 1$ in the above formula. Also, remember that the prescaler can be assigned either to **TMR0** or to the **Watchdog Timer (WDT)** — never both simultaneously.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — ADC Digital Output from Analog Input

The PIC16F877A ADC is a **10-bit Successive Approximation Register (SAR)** type.

### Given

- $V_{ref+} = V_{DD} = 5.0 \text{ V}$
- $V_{ref-} = V_{SS} = 0 \text{ V}$
- Analog input: $V_{in} = 2.5 \text{ V}$

### Step 1 — Compute LSB (Step Size)

The ADC produces $2^{10} = 1024$ discrete levels (0 to 1023).

$$\text{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref+} - V_{ref-}}{2^n} = \frac{5.0 - 0}{1024}$$

$$\text{LSB} = 4.8828 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 4.88 \text{ mV}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Digital Output

$$D = \frac{V_{in}}{\text{LSB}} = \frac{V_{in} \times 2^n}{V_{ref+}}$$

$$D = \frac{2.5 \times 1024}{5.0}$$

$$D = \frac{2560}{5.0} = 512$$

### Step 3 — Verify the Range

$D$ ranges from `0` (when $V_{in} = 0 \text{ V}$) to `1023` (when $V_{in} = 4.995 \text{ V}$). For $V_{in} = 2.5 \text{ V}$, we get the midpoint value `512`, which matches physical intuition (half of full scale).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The maximum digital output is **1023, not 1024**. This is because 0 is a valid code. The transfer function is:
> 
> $$V_{in} = D \times \text{LSB} = D \times \frac{V_{ref}}{1024}$$
> 
> The 1024 in the denominator does **not** mean the max code is 1024.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — USART Baud Rate Register Value

The Enhanced USART in PIC16F877A in **asynchronous high-speed mode** uses the formula:

$$\text{Baud} = \frac{f_{osc}}{16 \times (SPBRG + 1)}$$

Rearranging for SPBRG:

$$\text{SPBRG} = \frac{f_{osc}}{16 \times \text{Baud}} - 1$$

### Given

- $f_{osc} = 20 \text{ MHz} = 20\,000\,000 \text{ Hz}$
- Desired Baud Rate: $9600$

### Step 1 — Substitute

$$\text{SPBRG} = \frac{20\,000\,000}{16 \times 9600} - 1$$

### Step 2 — Compute denominator

$$16 \times 9600 = 153\,600$$

### Step 3 — Divide

$$\frac{20\,000\,000}{153\,600} = 130.2083$$

### Step 4 — Subtract 1

$$\text{SPBRG} = 130.2083 - 1 = 129.2083$$

### Step 5 — Round to nearest integer

Take $\text{SPBRG} = 129$ (closest integer). The actual baud rate achieved will be:

$$\text{Baud}_{actual} = \frac{20\,000\,000}{16 \times (129 + 1)} = \frac{20\,000\,000}{2080}$$

$$\text{Baud}_{actual} = 9615.38 \text{ bps}$$

### Step 6 — Compute % Error

$$\%\text{Error} = \frac{|9615.38 - 9600|}{9600} \times 100 = 0.16\%$$

> Within the standard $\pm 2\%$ tolerance — acceptable for most UART links.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Reference:** For 20 MHz crystal, the canonical SPBRG values are:
> - 9600 baud → SPBRG = 129 (error ≈ 0.16%)
> - 19200 baud → SPBRG = 64 (error ≈ 1.36%)
> - 57600 baud → SPBRG = 21 (error ≈ -0.78%)

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Architectural Comparison for Exam

| Architectural Parameter | 8051 (Intel/MCS-51) | PIC16F877A | ARM Cortex-M0 (STM32F0) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Von Neumann | **Harvard** | Harvard |
| ISA Type | CISC | **RISC** | RISC (Thumb) |
| Bus Width (Data) | 8-bit | **8-bit** | 32-bit |
| Bus Width (Program) | 8-bit (shared) | **14-bit (separate)** | 32-bit (separate) |
| Pipeline | None | **2-stage** | 3-stage |
| Instruction Length | 1, 2, or 3 bytes | **Fixed 14 bits** | Fixed 16 bits (Thumb) |
| Instruction Count | 111 | **35** | ~60 |
| Register File | 4 banks × 8 = 32 | **W + file reg** | 13 GPR (R0–R12) |
| Program Memory | 4 KB ROM | **8 KB Flash** | 16–256 KB Flash |
| RAM | 128 bytes | **368 bytes** | 4–32 KB SRAM |
| Max Clock | 24 MHz | **20 MHz** | 48 MHz |
| ADC | External | **10-bit, 8-ch** | 12-bit, 16-ch |
| Power (active @5V) | ~10 mA | **~2 mA** | ~5 mA |
| Sleep Current | ~1 mA | **< 1 μA** | < 1 μA |
